He has seen it once, at dusk, when the light was bad: white, he says. Entirely white.

He is a lord's huntsman, skilled and unsentimental, and the white quality of the thing has unsettled him in a way that a lifetime of practical work has not prepared him for. He is not afraid of it. He is not sure what he is.

responses.label: You may join the hunt and help him track the white beast.
You may speak to him of what a white beast on the road might signify.

entries.body: The track is three days old but clear enough — the beast seems to make no effort to conceal itself, which is its own kind of concealment. The forest is quiet around the trail, the birds displaced upward, the undergrowth untouched on either side of the path.

resolutions.label: follow the track and come upon the white beast

success.body:
You find it at the edge of a clearing at dusk — a stag, entirely white, with antlers that catch the failing light and hold it. It looks at you for three full breaths. Then it walks into the wood and is gone, and the clearing is ordinary again.

The huntsman stands beside you and says nothing for a long time. Then: 'We will not speak of having found it.' You agree.

failure.body:
The track doubles back and crosses a stream and you lose it at the ford. You search for an hour. The huntsman sits on a stone and looks at the far bank with an expression that is not quite disappointment.

'Perhaps it does not wish to be found today,' he says. You think he may be right.

entries.body: The white beast in the old stories is always significant — a calling rather than a quarry, a direction rather than a destination. He listens to this with the practical scepticism of a man whose trade is the real, and the genuine openness of a man who has seen something that the real does not contain.

resolutions.label: draw on what you know of the old meanings and give him something to hold

success.body:
You tell him what the white beast means in the old tellings, and he receives it with a care that tells you it was needed — not that he believes it, exactly, but that it gives the unsettling a shape, and a shape is easier to carry than formlessness.

He goes back to the tracking, but with a different quality to the going.

failure.body:
What you know of the old meanings is thinner than the situation requires, and your explanation does not settle the unsettling. He thanks you and returns to the track, still carrying the formless thing.

Knowledge of the old meanings requires more than you currently have. Worth building.

entries.body:
His dog died this morning. That is all. It was an old dog, a good one, a working dog that he has hunted with for eleven years, and it lay down in the night and did not get up, and now he is sitting at the edge of the road with his hand on the wrapped bundle of the dog's body and he cannot quite make himself continue toward the hall where he must report it.

He is embarrassed to be grieving a dog. He is also grieving a dog.

responses.label: You may sit with him and be a witness to what he has lost.
You may speak to him of the dog without treating the grief as small.

entries.body: The grief is simple and without complication, and the embarrassment around it is the only complicated part. If you sit with him, you remove the embarrassment — the grief is given a witness who is not judging it, which is the only thing it needs.

resolutions.label: sit with him in honest witness to an honest grief

success.body:
You sit beside him for an hour. You do not speak of anything important. When he stands up, his face is still sad, but the embarrassment is gone — you have confirmed, simply by staying, that the grief is not ridiculous.

He buries the dog at the roots of an oak at the road's edge and marks it with a stone. Then he walks on.

failure.body:
The sitting becomes uncomfortable — you shift, you glance up the road, and he notices. He stands and says he must be going. The embarrassment returns, now shared.

Grief needs a witness who is entirely present. Today you were only partly here.

entries.body: A working dog's life is a working life — the same dedication, in its own terms, as any other. Eleven years in any calling is a long service, and a good service, and it deserves to be spoken of as such rather than diminished by the species of the one who gave it.

resolutions.label: speak of the dog's life and service with genuine respect

success.body:
You ask him about the dog — a specific question: what was it best at, what did it do that no other dog could do as well? He answers, and the answering is a kind of memorial, and by the end of it he has remembered eleven years of good work rather than only the morning that ended them.

He buries the dog under the oak. He goes on.

failure.body:
You say the right thing but at the wrong register — too formal, perhaps, or too casual — and it does not land as tribute. He hears it as condescension rather than respect.

The right thing in the wrong tone is not the right thing.

entries.body:
He saw something in the forest at dawn, and he has been sitting at this waypoint for two hours trying to decide what to do about it. Not a beast and not a person — something in between, or something that was either and became neither, which is the category the old stories cover but practical men are not equipped to navigate.

He is devout, in the practical way of men who spend their lives in the created world, and he believes what he saw was a sign. He does not know a sign of what.

responses.label: You may ask him to describe what he saw and help him understand it.
You may accompany him back into the forest to look for evidence of what was there.

entries.body: His description is a hunter's description — precise about size, movement, the quality of the light — and what emerges is something that does not fit any natural category but fits several spiritual ones. He has not read the relevant texts, but the pattern is there for someone who has.

resolutions.label: interpret what he describes through the spiritual knowledge you carry

success.body:
What he saw, in the old accounts, precedes a moment of significance in the life of the one who sees it — not immediately, but within a season. The meaning is preparatory rather than specific: something is coming that will require everything he has.

He receives this with the seriousness it deserves and asks what he should do. You say: keep doing what you do, but with more attention. He nods.

failure.body:
The description is precise enough, but the pattern it fits is not one you know well enough to read confidently. You offer what you have, knowing it is incomplete.

The spiritual world requires a different kind of tracking than you currently possess.

entries.body: The forest at the place he describes is different — you notice it before you can say what the difference is. The undergrowth grows in a direction that does not follow the light. The soil at the centre of the clearing is soft and unmarked, though a thing was there.

resolutions.label: read the forest and find what trace was left

success.body:
There is a print at the clearing's edge — not a hoof, not a foot, something that is both and neither, pressed into the earth with the weight of a presence rather than a mass. You measure it and find where it came from and where it went, and the path it walked does not follow the logic of any path you know.

You and the hunter look at each other. He says: 'I did not imagine it.' You say: 'No.' That is the whole of what can be said.

failure.body:
The clearing yields nothing — no trace, no mark, no bent stem. Whatever was there left nothing behind that ordinary reading can find.

Some presences are past the reach of the naturalist's art.

entries.body:
He has been hunting the same clearing for a week, though he has found it only today. He has seen himself there — not a reflection, not a vision, but himself, standing at the clearing's far edge, dressed as he is dressed today, doing exactly what he is doing. The image steps into the trees before he can reach it.

The enchantment is of the mirror kind, which is neither dangerous nor benign but always significant, placed by someone who wanted to show him something about himself. He cannot make out what.

responses.label: You may try to break the mirror-enchantment.
You may help him understand what the image is showing him.

entries.body: Mirror-enchantments are broken from the inside, not the outside — the person the enchantment is aimed at must refuse what it is offering. You can guide him to the refusal, but he must make it.

resolutions.label: help him find and make the refusal that breaks the mirror

success.body:
The mirror-enchantment is offering him a fixed self — himself as he always has been, doing as he always has done. The refusal is the recognition that he is not that, that he is also capable of something different. He says it aloud, at your direction, and the clearing goes ordinary.

He looks around with the expression of someone who has just set down something they were not aware of carrying.

failure.body:
You cannot find the right shape for the refusal — it is different for every person, and you have not yet understood this person well enough to find his. The image continues to appear.

The specific key for the specific lock. You did not yet have it.

entries.body: The image is him as he is — no distortion, no idealisation, just himself at this moment. The question is what about this moment the enchantment wants him to see. The answer is rarely the surface.

resolutions.label: probe carefully at what he is not seeing about himself

success.body:
Through careful questioning you arrive at it: he has been waiting for something to change — waiting for a better time, a better opportunity, a different circumstance — and the mirror is showing him himself waiting, which is the sum of what he has been doing for longer than he wants to admit.

He looks at the image one more time and then turns away from it deliberately. The clearing does not show it again.

failure.body:
What the image is showing is not something you can reach from the outside — it is something only he can see, and he is not yet ready to look at it directly.

The mirror shows only what the person is prepared to see. He is not there yet.

entries.body:
He is trying to teach a lady to hunt. She is the daughter of a minor lord, and she expressed an interest, and he is a huntsman, and what seemed like a simple enough task has become complicated by the fact that he has fallen in love with her somewhere in the teaching.

This is a problem. She is several social classes above him. She has noticed his feelings with the clear sight that ladies develop when they need it, and has said nothing, which is either encouragement or mercy and he cannot tell which.

responses.label: * You may counsel him on how to conduct himself in this impossible situation.
* You may offer to speak to the lady on his behalf.

entries.body: His options are limited in the obvious direction, but the situation is not without possibility — it depends entirely on what the lady has decided, which he does not know. What he can control is how he conducts himself while the not-knowing continues.

resolutions.label: counsel him on the conduct of an honourable impossible love

success.body:
You tell him: do nothing rash, say nothing he cannot take back, and let what is there be there without demanding that it resolve itself. If she has a position on the matter, she will find a way to make it known — ladies always do, when they choose to. His job is to remain worth choosing.

He receives this with the seriousness of a man who was hoping for a simpler answer and is instead getting the true one.

failure.body:
Your counsel is too practical for where he is — he is past the stage of practical, deep in the territory of feeling, and the map of right conduct does not yet have his location on it. He thanks you and goes back to the teaching.

Love, early, resists the map.

entries.body: Speaking to the lady on his behalf is either the most helpful thing possible or the most intrusive, and you will not know which until you have spoken. She is a day's ride away, and the question must be framed in a way that gives her a genuine choice rather than a pressure.

resolutions.label: find the lady and put the question in a way that leaves her free to answer truly

success.body:
She listens to you, and then she looks out the window for a long time, and then she says: the hunting lessons can continue. She says no more, but the tone in which she says it is not the tone of a woman speaking of hunting lessons.

You return to the huntsman with this, and his face does something that faces do not often do, which is become entirely open.

failure.body:
The framing, despite your care, arrives as pressure, and she closes the question with a courtesy that is not unkind but is final. You return to the huntsman with this, which is the harder errand.

Carrying the true answer is also a form of kindness, even when it is not the answer hoped for.

entries.body:
He is hunting a boar that he has been hunting for three years. Not this boar specifically — there have been several — but the boar that killed his dog and wounded him badly in the side, and every subsequent boar has become that boar in his mind, and the wound in his side has healed and opened and healed again with the seasons.

He is going deeper into the wood than is safe, alone, because the boar does not wait for companions, and the madness of the pursuit has the quality of something that will only end one way or the other.

responses.label: You may ride with him into the wood.
You may try to talk him out of the pursuit before he goes further in.

entries.body: Deep forest, late afternoon. The boar is real — you can hear it, further in — and the hunter is moving with the particular precision of a man who has tracked this animal enough times that the tracking has become something else, something that is about more than the quarry.

resolutions.label: hunt the boar with him and end the pursuit

success.body:
The boar turns, charges, and the huntsman takes it with a spear driven with three years' intention behind it. The boar falls. The huntsman stands over it for a long time, breathing.

He does not seem victorious. He seems emptied — which is, perhaps, the truest kind of victory. You help him carry it out.

failure.body:
The boar is injured but not taken, and it disappears into the deepest part of the wood where you cannot safely follow. The huntsman watches the direction it went with an expression that is not surprised.

Some pursuits outlast the pursuer's ability to complete them. Perhaps that is itself a lesson.

entries.body: He does not stop easily, and the argument against going must be strong enough to hold against three years of momentum. You have only the argument of safety, which a man in his state does not weigh heavily, and the argument of proportion, which he has already considered and rejected.

resolutions.label: find the argument that can actually hold him

success.body:
The argument that holds him is not about the boar or the danger or the three years. It is about what the hunt has cost that was not the boar: the side that aches every winter now, the dogs that died in the training for the pursuit, the seasons spent in a particular wood rather than a wider world.

He looks at you. He looks at the wood. He turns back. That is everything you were trying to accomplish.

failure.body:
Nothing you say is new enough or true enough to deflect three years of fixation. He goes into the wood.

Some pursuits are their own destination. Standing in their way only shows you where you are not able to stand.

entries.body:
He is tracking a man. Not an animal — the prints he is reading from are human, booted, heading east at a pace that tells you the man is not walking for pleasure. The huntsman has been hired by a lord whose servant absconded with something valuable — what exactly, he is not going to say, but the servant must be found before he reaches the city and disappears into it.

He could use a second pair of eyes and a sword arm if the finding becomes a catching.

responses.label: You may ride with him and help track and apprehend the servant.
You may ask him what was taken before you decide whether to help.

entries.body: The track is six hours old and leads into country that grows more complicated as you go east — more crossings, more roads, more ways to go wrong. The city is a day away, and the servant clearly knows the territory.

resolutions.label: cut off the servant's route to the city before he reaches it

success.body:
You know a shorter road east, and you ride it at pace while the huntsman follows the track. You catch the servant at a ford two miles from the city gate — not by outrunning him but by being there before him.

He is tired and surprised and does not resist. The huntsman arrives an hour later and regards the situation with professional satisfaction.

failure.body:
The servant knows the roads better than you do and finds a crossing you did not account for. By the time you reach the city gate there is no track and too many people.

Some quarries know their ground better than the hunters do. That is a lesson about home advantage.

entries.body: He pauses. The hesitation tells you something. He says it was a private document. Private documents are usually either evidence of something or embarrassing to someone, and either possibility changes the nature of what you are doing if you help.

resolutions.label: press him to say what the document is before you commit

success.body:
He tells you, because you have positioned yourself as someone who will not help without knowing, and he needs the help. The document is a deed of debt, held by the lord against the servant's family — not theft, then, but a man reclaiming what is his.

You decline to help and suggest a legal route. The huntsman looks at you, sighs, and continues alone. You have not helped him. You have done something better.

failure.body:
He will not say, and you cannot make him. You are left choosing between helping without knowing and refusing without reason.

Sometimes the question that needs asking is also the question that will not be answered. That, too, is information.

entries.body:
He is old enough to have seen Britain before Arthur's roads were safe, and he has the eyes of a man who has read the country for sixty years and still finds it interesting. He moves through the forest without sound, which is a thing that takes decades to learn, and he regards your approach with the amusement of a man who heard you coming from a quarter-mile.

He offers you nothing and asks nothing. But if you ask him, he knows the forest around here better than any map ever written, and what he knows includes things that are not on maps.

responses.label: You may ask him what he knows of the forest and what he has seen in it.
You may ask him to teach you something you cannot learn elsewhere.

entries.body: He speaks of the forest as a person speaks of a familiar: with knowledge that is also affection, specific observations mixed with longer patterns. He has seen the places where the old roads were, buried now under growth. He has seen things in this forest that he describes precisely without interpretation, leaving the interpretation to you.

resolutions.label: learn the forest from him with complete attention

success.body:
By the time you have listened for an hour, you know this forest in a way you did not before — not all of it, but enough that the trees are no longer background. You understand the drainage, the old paths, the places where something other than the ordinary has left a mark.

'Come back in spring,' he says. 'There is more to see.' You believe him.

failure.body:
You take in what you can, but the depth of what he knows exceeds the depth of what you can receive in a single sitting. You leave knowing more than you arrived with, but less than was available.

A lifetime of attention cannot be summarised into an afternoon. Come back.

entries.body: He is quiet for a moment, which is his way of deciding whether you mean it. Then he says there is one thing: the ability to walk through any terrain without disturbing what is there. He can teach you the beginning of it, if you have the patience for the beginning.

resolutions.label: learn the beginning of silent movement from a master

success.body:
Three hours of deliberate slow walking through difficult ground, corrected each time you do what you instinctively do rather than what the ground requires. By the end your legs are tired in muscles you did not know you used, and you have moved twenty yards with a quality of stillness you have never achieved before.

'That is the beginning,' he says. 'It takes a year to make it habitual.' You understand that you have been given the seed of something.

failure.body:
The teaching requires a quality of physical attention that you cannot sustain for three hours — your body reverts to its habits before the lesson can establish itself. He watches without comment.

Some skills require the body to be broken of what it already knows before the new thing can take hold.

entries.body:
He has a map that is not quite a map — part surveyed land, part legend, part something in between. He has been selling sections of it to lords in the area, each lord believing they are receiving a unique depiction of their territory. The sections are unique. They also overlap significantly.

This is not, he says, dishonesty — each section is accurate for its area, and the lords have what they paid for. He would simply prefer not to have all three lords compare their maps in the same room at the same time.

responses.label: You may help him avoid the comparison — for a consideration.
You may point out the flaw in his scheme and suggest a more honest approach.

entries.body: The comparison is likely to happen at a feast three days hence where all three lords will be present. If someone separates the lords' conversations at the feast, or arranges for the maps not to be displayed at the same time, the comparison can be avoided without any explicit interference.

resolutions.label: navigate the feast and prevent the comparison

success.body:
You manage the feast's social currents with some skill — a conversation redirected here, a minor distraction there, the maps displayed sequentially rather than together. The comparison does not happen.

The huntsman finds you afterward and gives you a quarter of what the maps earned, which is more than you expected and less than the scheme probably deserved.

failure.body:
One of the lords produces his map at the feast before you can redirect the conversation, and the other two notice their overlapping borders. The huntsman departs quietly through a side door.

The best schemes are those that do not require perfect execution. This one did.

entries.body: He listens to your objection with the expression of a man who has already heard it from himself and decided the other way. He wants the argument that will change his mind, not the argument that confirms his current discomfort.

resolutions.label: make the case for honesty in terms he can use

success.body:
The argument that lands is not about the lords but about the map itself: he made something accurate and useful, which is genuinely valuable, and the scheme treats it as less than it is. He could sell the overlap as a feature — a boundary survey, done properly — rather than hiding it as a flaw.

He looks at the map for a long time. 'That is worth more,' he says, meaning the honest version.

failure.body:
He has already weighed the options and decided, and your argument does not outweigh what he has already decided. He thanks you and continues on his way.

Honesty persuades those who are open to persuasion. He was not open today.

entries.body:
He has lost the trail and he knows it. He was tracking a deer that turned back on its own prints — a deliberate trick that deer learn in heavily hunted country — and now he is two miles into the forest with the light going and no clear sense of where either the deer or his camp might be.

He is not afraid of the forest — he has lived in forests — but he is cold and tired and the particular kind of lost that comes from knowing what you are doing and then having it not work is a harder lost than simple ignorance.

responses.label: You may help him find the trail again.
You may forget the trail and help him find his camp instead.

entries.body: The trail has doubled back, and a deer that doubles back does so in a predictable way — it circles wide and comes back to a point behind its own original position. The question is finding the right arc.

resolutions.label: work out where the trail reconvenes and find it

success.body:
You estimate the arc and walk it out, and there is the print you were looking for — the deer came back around and passed twenty yards south. The huntsman looks at it with the expression of a man who has done exactly this calculation and still finds it satisfying when the numbers come out.

'Good eye,' he says. The deer is another matter, but the trail is found.

failure.body:
The arc you walk finds nothing. The deer either made a wider circle than expected or left the forest entirely. The trail is gone.

A trail that is gone is gone. Knowing when to stop looking is also a skill.

entries.body: The camp is more findable than the trail — it is in a fixed location, and a fire, when he lit it this morning, would have left a smell in the canopy that has not fully dispersed. Smoke settles low in the evening; if you walk downwind you may catch it.

resolutions.label: find the camp by scent and the signs of morning smoke

success.body:
The smoke is faint but there — a quality in the air rather than an actual smell — and you follow it north-west. The camp is a hundred yards further than the smell suggested, tucked into a hollow.

The huntsman looks at his fire ring with the relief of a man who was further from his camp than he wanted to admit. He rebuilds the fire and does not say anything except thank you.

failure.body:
The smoke is too old or the wind wrong, and following the ghost of it takes you in a circle. You both make camp where you are, under a large oak.

The forest always has somewhere to stop. That is something.

entries.body:
His lord's forest has been poached bare, and he is the lord's huntsman, and the responsibility for this is on his shoulders in the way that a man's failures always land on him when he is the one responsible. The poachers are a village to the east, and the village is starving, and he knows both these things and has not yet reported the poaching because he does not know what the right thing to do is.

He is a practical man, not a philosophical one, and this is a philosophical problem. He would like help with it.

responses.label: You may help him think through the competing obligations.
You may ride to the village and find a way to address both the poaching and the hunger.

entries.body: The competing obligations are real and not easily ranked: loyalty to his lord, duty to report violations of the forest law, and the evidence of his own eyes that the people doing the poaching are doing it to survive rather than for profit or spite.

resolutions.label: help him find a course that honours all three obligations as well as possible

success.body:
The course you find is this: he reports the poaching to his lord, accurately, but simultaneously reports the village's condition and argues for a temporary licence rather than a punishment. His lord is a fair man — he has said so — and a fair man with the full picture may do better than a fair man with only half of it.

He nods. He had not thought of it as a full-picture problem.

failure.body:
The competing obligations are more evenly weighted than your counsel can resolve. He remains with the same impossible problem, better-articulated but no closer to a resolution.

Some problems are not puzzles to be solved but weights to be carried until circumstances change.

entries.body: Riding to the village is the direct approach — see the situation, speak to the people doing it, find out what is actually happening and why, and then carry an accurate picture back to the lord rather than a complaint.

resolutions.label: go to the village and understand the situation fully

success.body:
The village headman tells you plainly: a crop failure, a bad winter, a choice between poaching and watching children go without. He is not belligerent about it. He offers to make it good when they have enough again.

You carry this back to the huntsman and to the lord, together. The lord, given the full picture, grants a licence for the winter. The forest survives. The village survives.

failure.body:
The village is defensive and the headman will not speak freely to a stranger with a knight in tow. You leave with a partial picture.

Trust must be built before truth is shared. You arrived too directly.

entries.body:
He walks the road with a staff and a small pack, which is unusual — hermits do not, as a rule, walk roads. They dwell in their dwelling places and wait to be found. He is going somewhere, this one, which means he is either called or driven. His face does not say which.

He acknowledges you with the grave courtesy of one who is used to silence broken only by the right things. He does not ask where you are going. He asks, with the directness of his kind, whether you have recently made a confession.

responses.label: You may confess to him here, on the road.
You may ask him instead where he is going and why he walks today.

entries.body: Confession on the road is not unusual in this age — the hermits serve this function wherever they are, and the road is as sacred as any chapel, if the intention is right. The difficulty is not the location but the honesty.

resolutions.label: confess with full honesty and receive what is given

success.body:
He listens without interruption, and when you are done he is quiet for a time that feels longer than it is. His counsel is brief and exact: one thing you were avoiding naming, named. The penance he gives is small because he has found the real wound rather than its surface.

You continue on the road lighter than you came.

failure.body:
You confess, but not fully — there is something you circle around rather than name, and he knows it before you do. He gives you penance anyway, because partial confession is still confession, and sends you on. The thing you circled around remains with you.

Honesty, like most virtues, is practised rather than achieved all at once.

entries.body:
He tells you: he was called to leave his dwelling place by a voice that he does not describe as a voice but which he clearly heard, and the calling told him to walk the road until he found the person who needed him most. He has been walking for two days.

He looks at you with the expression of someone checking whether they have found that person.

resolutions.label: engage honestly with the possibility that the calling was meant for you

success.body:
You consider it seriously rather than deflecting it, which is unusual enough to hold his attention. The conversation that follows is the kind that a man walks away from changed by, though he could not easily say by what.

'You were not the one I was looking for,' he says eventually, 'but you were one who needed looking for, which is close enough.' He continues his walk.

failure.body:
You deflect the question with courtesy rather than engaging with it, and he accepts the deflection with the patience of a man who has seen many people deflect the question.

The question whether you are the person who most needs something is not usually one that answers itself comfortably.

entries.body:
He has been a hermit for thirty years, and for twenty-eight of those years he was at peace with the vocation. In the last two years something has happened — he does not say what — that has broken the peace without breaking the vocation. He is still a hermit. He is sorrowful in a way that hermits are not supposed to be, having made their peace with the world and its contents.

He does not ask you for anything. He is simply sorrowful in a way that is visible, and you are the first person to pass his dwelling in a week, and the week has been a long one.

responses.label: You may stay a while and be present with him.
You may ask him what has broken the peace.

entries.body: His cell is small and clean, and it has the quality of a place made purposeful by long habitation. He offers you water and bread, which is what he has, and you sit together in the late afternoon light.

resolutions.label: be present with him without attempting to fix the sorrow

success.body:
You stay through vespers, which he says alone and which you receive in silence. The sorrow does not lift, but it is companioned, which is different from being alone with it.

When you leave he blesses you, and the blessing has the weight of a thing given by a man who knows what blessing costs.

failure.body:
The silence becomes uncomfortable and you find yourself trying to offer something — comfort, counsel, distraction — and the trying takes you out of the quality of simple presence. He looks at you kindly, but the afternoon does not become what it might have been.

The hardest thing to give is often just your presence, without addition.

entries.body: He tells you: a man came to him two years ago and made his confession, and it was a confession of a great sin — a sin against a person the hermit once knew, long before the hermitage, in the life before the vocation. The man was absolved. The hermit did his duty. But the sorrow arrived after the man left and has not gone.

resolutions.label: help him understand the sorrow that came after the absolution

success.body:
The sorrow, you say, is not the absolution's failure — it is the hermit's own unfinished grief for the person who was sinned against, grief that the confession brought back to the surface. He cannot absolve the confessing man's guilt and simultaneously hold his own grief. He must grieve, not judge.

He is quiet for a very long time. Then he says: 'That is it.' Then he weeps, which is what was needed.

failure.body:
You see the shape of the problem but not its solution, and your attempt to name it comes out incomplete — close enough to be almost right, which is sometimes harder than being wrong.

Half-understandings can leave a person more alone than none.

entries.body:
He is writing when you find him — not copying scripture, but composing, which is unusual. He is writing about something he experienced last winter: a period of three days in his cell during which, he says, he was aware of a presence that he cannot describe without making it smaller than it was. He is trying to get it down before the shape of the memory changes further.

He looks up when you approach and regards you with the mild interest of a man who is not usually surprised and is slightly surprised now.

responses.label: You may ask him about the experience and receive what he is willing to share.
You may ask if there is anything he needs while he writes.

entries.body: He speaks of the three days in the careful language of a man who knows that language cannot quite reach what he is describing, and who uses it anyway because it is what he has. What he describes is not dramatic — no visions, no voices — but it has the quality of something that changed every subsequent experience without explaining itself.

resolutions.label: receive his account with full openness and engage with it honestly

success.body:
You listen without diminishing and without inflating, and you ask one question that he has not asked himself, and the question opens something he had not yet written. He writes it down while you wait.

'Come back in a year,' he says, 'and I will have a better account of it.' You think you may.

failure.body:
Your response gives too much or too little — either diminishing it to something ordinary or elevating it to something it cannot bear to be called. He thanks you and returns to his writing.

Some experiences speak to specific listeners, and you were not quite the specific listener today.

entries.body: He is out of candles. That is all he needs — candles, because the writing goes into the night when it is going well, and this writing is going well. He could ask the village, but the village is a half-day away, and losing the morning to the road would break something.

resolutions.label: find and bring him candles so the writing can continue

success.body:
You ride to the village and back with six candles, arriving before dark. He receives them with the gratitude of a man who knows exactly what they enable.

The practical service and the sacred work are not separate things. You have served both with one errand.

failure.body:
The village has no candles to spare — or the road back takes longer than expected. You return empty-handed, and he looks at the evening with the particular expression of someone watching an opportunity close.

'Tomorrow, perhaps,' he says. He has learned patience in thirty years. You are still learning it.

entries.body:
He is asleep, and has been asleep for three days, and his attendant — a young novice who brings him food from the village — cannot wake him. The sleep is not natural: he is warm, his breathing is steady, but he does not respond to sound or touch, and there is a quality to the air of his cell that is not the air of a sick man's room.

Someone has put him to sleep. The novice does not know who. The hermit, in his ordinary state, is a man of considerable spiritual reputation, and someone thought it worth the effort to remove him from availability.

responses.label: You may attempt to break the sleeping enchantment.
You may try to determine who placed it and why before you act.

entries.body: The enchantment is of the deep-sleep kind — not a surface glamour but something that reached into the dreaming. Breaking it requires offering the sleeper a path back that is more compelling than the sleep's continuing. The path back is usually an honest question that requires an awake person to answer.

resolutions.label: call him back with a question that requires his waking mind

success.body:
You speak into the sleep a question that is genuinely unanswerable without full waking — a theological puzzle of the kind he would not be able to leave unaddressed. After several minutes he stirs, and then he opens his eyes.

He looks at you for a long time. Then he says: 'The answer to your question is —' and gives you an answer you were not expecting.

failure.body:
The questions you ask do not reach him, or he is too deeply under for questions to carry this far. The sleep continues.

The working that placed him here was stronger than what you brought against it.

entries.body: The novice can tell you who has been to the cell in the last week. The list is short. One of the visitors is a name you recognise — a person of consequence whose interests and the hermit's are in a particular kind of conflict that you now understand.

resolutions.label: pursue the person who placed the enchantment and force them to undo it

success.body:
You find the person before they have left the district, and you present what you know in a way that makes it clear the undoing is less costly than the alternative. They undo it — reluctantly, with the particular grace of people doing things against their will. The hermit wakes.

He is not surprised, which tells you this has happened to him before.

failure.body:
The person is already gone, or denies responsibility with enough plausibility that you cannot force the issue. The hermit remains sleeping.

What you know and what you can prove are different tools, and you need the second one here.

entries.body:
He was not always a hermit. He was, before the hermitage, a man of the world — a lord's younger son, a tournament rider, a man who loved and was loved. He is not ashamed of the before, and he does not pretend it did not exist, but there is a letter on his desk that arrived three days ago from the woman who was the particular reason he stopped being that man.

She needs his counsel. Not his love — she is past that, as he is — but the specific counsel of someone who knew both her and the world she moved through, and who has had thirty years to think about what he knew.

responses.label: You may offer to carry his reply to her.
You may help him compose a reply — it is not yet written.

entries.body: He has written the reply. It is a page and a half, in the careful handwriting of a man for whom writing is still a formal act. He folds it and seals it without reading it back, which tells you he wrote it without revision, which tells you he knew what he wanted to say.

resolutions.label: carry his letter faithfully to where it needs to go

success.body:
She is three days south, in a house you find without difficulty. She receives the letter at the door and reads it there, in the afternoon light, without going inside. When she looks up her face has settled into something.

'Tell him,' she says, 'that it was the right answer.' You carry that back.

failure.body:
The road south is longer than planned, and you arrive a day after she has left — moved on to a sister's house, the household says, and the direction is uncertain. You leave the letter with a trustworthy member of the household and hope it reaches her.

Not all messages arrive in time for the answer to matter.

entries.body: He has not written it yet. The letter has been sitting on his desk for three days, which suggests the writing is not straightforward. What she asks for is counsel, and counsel requires knowing what the situation is, and to know the situation he must think about a life he has spent thirty years not thinking about.

resolutions.label: help him think through what she needs and how to give it

success.body:
The conversation that follows is one of the more unusual ones you have been part of: a hermit speaking of the past as though it is alive in the present, fitting thirty years of distance against a specific current need. By the end he has the shape of the reply and sits down to write it.

'Thank you,' he says. 'I needed someone to think against.'

failure.body:
You help as much as you are able, but the situation requires a particular knowledge — of her, of him, of the three decades between the letter and the life before it — that you cannot supply. He will write it without your help, which he was going to do anyway.

There are some things a person must do alone, and counsel only delays them.

entries.body:
He has gone past ordinary theology into something on the other side of it — not heresy, not madness exactly, but a place the orthodox mind does not follow. He speaks in fragments that are each separately true and together point toward something that may or may not be a destination. His cell is full of texts and the texts are full of marginal notes and the notes are full of connections that require great concentration to follow.

He has the eyes of a man who is looking at something the rest of the world cannot see, and he is not unhappy about it.

responses.label: You may try to follow where his thinking is going.
You may speak to him of ordinary things and see if it anchors him.

entries.body: Following his thinking requires leaving behind the assumption that the ordinary categories will hold. He is not reckless — each step is taken carefully — but the steps lead somewhere that does not have a name in ordinary usage.

resolutions.label: follow his thought to wherever it goes

success.body:
You follow as far as you can, and then you stop at a place that you cannot get further through by ordinary thought, and from that stopping point you can see something — briefly, imperfectly — that you could not see before.

He does not notice when you stop. He continues. You carry back what you found at the edge.

failure.body:
The thinking becomes undifferentiated — you cannot tell which connections are real and which are his system's own artifacts. You stop before you are lost.

Some territories require a map, and the map must be built before the territory can be entered.

entries.body: Ordinary things can be an anchor or an insult, depending on where a person is. If he is close to something, the ordinary will feel like a retrieval into the lesser. If he is lost in abstraction, the ordinary will feel like a rescue.

resolutions.label: speak of the ordinary world and see how he receives it

success.body:
You speak of the weather, the road, a small particular thing you noticed on the way here. He stops, and he listens, and something in him settles — not abandoning the thinking, but finding the ground under it. The thinking continues from a more stable position.

He says: 'It is important not to forget the weather.' You agree.

failure.body:
The ordinary arrives as interruption rather than anchor, and he looks at you with the gentle dismissal of a man whose attention is elsewhere.

Not everyone who appears lost wants to be found, or needs to be, or is lost in the first place.

entries.body:
He was a priest before he was a hermit, and before he was a priest he was a man who did something he has spent forty years making reparation for. He has not told anyone what it was, and he is not going to tell you. But there is a man who knows — the last surviving witness — and that man has begun, in his old age, to talk.

The hermit does not want the man silenced. He wants to be there when the man speaks, so that whatever is said is said with him present and not behind his back. He cannot travel. He would like someone to bring the man to him.

responses.label: You may find the man and bring him here.
You may ask the hermit whether meeting the man will truly serve the reparation he seeks.

entries.body: The man is old — older than the hermit — and he lives two days east, in a village where he is known as a teller of old stories, some true and some embroidered. He will come if asked clearly, but asking clearly requires being honest about who sent the invitation and why.

resolutions.label: bring the man honestly and prepare him for the meeting

success.body:
He comes. The meeting between the two old men is conducted in the hermit's cell, and you are asked to wait outside. After an hour, the hermit opens the door and says: 'Thank you.' His face is different from when you left.

You do not ask what was said. You were not meant to know.

failure.body:
The man declines — he is too old to travel, or he does not trust the invitation, or the conversation you have with him goes wrong in some way that closes the door. You return to the hermit empty-handed.

Some meetings cannot be forced. They can only be prepared for.

entries.body: This is the question he has not asked himself. He has been so focused on the practical necessity that the question of whether the meeting is truly needed has not been examined. It is worth examining.

resolutions.label: help him examine whether the meeting serves the reparation or only his own need for it

success.body:
The examination reveals: the reparation is already made, as fully as it can be. What he wants from the meeting is not reparation but witness — to be seen, once, by someone who knows the before and the after and can say whether the after was worth the before's cost.

The question of whether it was worth it is one the witness may not be able to answer. He sits with that for a long time. 'You are right,' he says at last. 'I will write him a letter instead.'

failure.body:
The question does not clarify things — if anything, it deepens the uncertainty. He is not sure what the meeting is for, and your examination has not found a better answer.

Some uncertainties cannot be resolved by thought. Only by doing and seeing what happens.

entries.body:
He is the hermit at the crossroads, exactly as the stories describe — sitting at the convergence of four ways, aged enough to have become part of the landscape, with the patient look of a man who has been here long enough that the crossroads itself has come to understand him.

He does not speak first. He waits. In his experience, people who arrive at crossroads know what question they have, and the question is usually not the first one they ask.

responses.label: You may ask him the question that is actually troubling you.
You may ask him what he has seen and heard from those who pass.

entries.body: He waits. Not impatiently — patience is his profession. He gives you the space to arrive at the real question rather than the safe one.

resolutions.label: ask the question you actually came with

success.body:
The real question, once you have found it and asked it, produces a silence, and then an answer that is not quite what you expected but is exactly what you needed. He does not say where the answer comes from. He says it simply, and you sit with it.

'The crossroads will be here when you return,' he says. 'If you return.' It is not a threat. It is an observation.

failure.body:
You ask the safe question, because the real one is too close to what you are afraid to hear. He answers the safe question with the same patience he would give the real one, but you leave carrying the real question unanswered.

The crossroads is always there. The courage to ask is the harder thing to find.

entries.body: He has seen many things pass this crossroads: knights going to quests they did not return from, ladies going to marriages they did not choose, merchants going to fortunes that became misfortunes. He has observed the pattern of these things for long enough that he can sometimes see the pattern before it is complete.

resolutions.label: receive what he has observed and find what is useful in it

success.body:
He tells you three things that have passed this crossroads recently, and in the telling a pattern becomes visible that you could not have assembled from the parts alone. The pattern tells you something about the road you are currently on that changes how you will ride it.

You thank him. He nods. He has been doing this for a long time.

failure.body:
What he has seen is rich but you cannot find the pattern — too much, too various, without a clear thread. You leave knowing more but understanding no better.

Knowledge without pattern is just more noise.

entries.body:
He knows something that he has not decided to share. You can see it in the quality of his silence — a silence that is holding something rather than empty — and in the way he watches you with a calculation that holy men are not supposed to have.

He is not malicious. He is strategic, which is a different thing. He knows something about your road, or your recent past, or your destination, and he will share it under the right conditions. The conditions have not yet been stated.

responses.label: You may ask what the conditions are.
You may offer him your confession in exchange for what he knows.

entries.body: He tells you: the condition is a service. Not a quest — a small thing, the sort of thing he cannot do himself because he does not leave the hermitage. A message to be carried, a task to be done, a word to be spoken to a specific person. The service and the knowledge are in proportion.

resolutions.label: agree to the service and do it faithfully

success.body:
The service is as described: small, specific, and completed without incident. You return, and he gives you what he knows — information about the road ahead that changes your planning considerably.

The exchange was fair. Not all wisdom is freely given, and not all prices are too high.

failure.body:
The service meets a complication — the person to be found is not where the hermit said, or the message is refused — and you return having done what you could but not what was asked. He gives you a partial account, which is what a partial service deserves.

The incomplete is still something.

entries.body: He considers this. Confession as payment for information is a theologically complicated arrangement — the confessor may not be offering absolution as a transaction — but he is a man who understands that sometimes the transactional form conceals a genuine need, and that serving the genuine need is worth the awkwardness of the form.

resolutions.label: confess genuinely and receive both absolution and what he knows

success.body:
You confess genuinely — more genuinely than you intended when you began — and the transaction disappears into the reality of what is actually happening, which is a man being heard by a priest. When it is done, he tells you what he knows.

The information is good. The absolution is better.

failure.body:
The confession begins as a transaction and remains one — you do not quite reach the genuine, and he knows it, and he gives you the information but not the absolution, which means you leave with useful knowledge and the same weight you came with.

You cannot buy the things that are not for sale.

entries.body:
He has not prayed in six weeks. This is not a confession he makes easily — he mentions it only because you ask why he seems as he seems, which is like a man maintaining the form of a thing while the substance has gone. He performs his offices, eats his meals, tends his small garden. He does not pray.

The absence is not anger and not doubt, exactly. It is more like a silence on the other end of a conversation he has been having for thirty years, and he cannot tell if the silence means the conversation has ended or if he is simply not hearing what was always there.

responses.label: You may sit in prayer with him and see what happens.
You may speak to him of your own experience of such silences.

entries.body: Not the prayer of offices — he can do that. The prayer of simply sitting in the intention of listening, which is a different thing entirely and the harder thing for someone who has practised the formal kind for thirty years.

resolutions.label: sit with him in the silence and be present in the attempt

success.body:
An hour of sitting, and nothing dramatic happens, and nothing needs to. At the end of the hour he looks different — not resolved, but present in a way he was not before. Something about not being alone in the attempt has changed the attempt.

'Try it again tomorrow,' you tell him. He says he will.

failure.body:
The sitting becomes restless — you find it harder than expected, and the restlessness communicates itself. The hour ends without the quality of presence that was needed.

Prayer is a practice, not a performance, and you are out of practice.

entries.body: He looks at you with more attention than you expected. He did not think a knight would have had such silences. He would like to know what they were and what became of them.

resolutions.label: speak honestly of your own experience of spiritual silence

success.body:
You tell him what you know of it — not as counsel, but as one person's experience offered to another. He listens with the full attention that only people who spend most of their time alone can give.

'You came at the right time,' he says. 'That is not an accident.' Whether it is or not, the visit has been useful to both of you.

failure.body:
Your experience is thinner than his need, and what you offer does not quite reach the depth of where he is. He receives it graciously.

To be honest about the limits of your experience is itself a form of help, even if it is not the help that was needed.

entries.body:
His cell is on fire. Not large — a beam in the roof, caught by an ember from his cooking fire that he did not catch in time — but the fire is real and spreading, and he is standing outside it looking at the work of forty years burning with an expression that is not panic but something quieter and harder to look at.

He has already pulled out the most important things — the relics, the manuscripts, a few objects of practical necessity. He is not asking for help. He is watching his home burn.

responses.label: You may help him fight the fire and save what can be saved.
You may stand with him and witness what cannot be saved.

entries.body: The fire is manageable — not yet at the walls, and a quantity of water from the nearby stream might still save the structure if applied immediately. It requires speed and sustained effort rather than skill.

resolutions.label: fight the fire with everything you have

success.body:
Two hours of work, and you save most of the cell — the roof at one end is lost, but the walls stand, and the manuscripts survive, and there is enough remaining that the hermit can continue. He sits in the surviving portion and looks at the char on the ceiling.

'You did not have to,' he says. You say someone had to. He nods.

failure.body:
You fight, but the fire is further along than it appeared and the wind turns against you. The cell comes down. You get out in time, and so does the hermit, and the manuscripts are safe.

Some things cannot be saved by effort alone.

entries.body: He has chosen to watch rather than fight, which tells you the fire is either too far along for fighting or he has made his peace with the burning. Standing with him is a different kind of service from fighting — it requires the capacity to witness loss without flinching away.

resolutions.label: stand with him and witness the burning without retreating from it

success.body:
You stand beside him until the cell is ash. He does not weep. He watches with the attention of a man who is committing something to memory — the shape of the thing as it goes, the quality of the light through the smoke.

When it is done, he says: 'Forty years of accumulated things, gone in an hour. I feel lighter.' He sounds as though he means it.

failure.body:
The watching becomes too much and you intervene — not from cowardice, but from the instinct to help that is not always what is needed. He watches you work and then helps you, because you need it, and the fire takes more than it would have if you had simply stood.

Not all help is helpful. The art is knowing which.

entries.body:
He is on the road ahead of you, walking at the pace of someone who is going somewhere but in no hurry to arrive, and he has the sideways quality of a man accustomed to knowing where the nearest exit is. When you catch him up he turns without surprise and regards you with the professional assessment of one who reads people for a living.

He is not a thief, he says — not exactly, or not only. He carries messages and packages for people who find it inconvenient to use official channels. The current package is sewn into his boot lining. The current destination is two days east.

responses.label: You may travel with him and learn more about his trade.
You may offer to carry the package yourself, through the official roads.

entries.body: The two days reveal a man who is more complicated than his opening suggested: careful, principled in his own way, and in possession of a map of Britain's unofficial roads that is more comprehensive than anything a lord's cartographer has produced. He is also being followed.

resolutions.label: help him lose the tail before it becomes a problem

success.body:
Between your knowledge of the official roads and his knowledge of the roads that do not appear on any document, you construct a route that the followers cannot anticipate. The package reaches its destination. The tail does not.

He looks at you afterward with the genuine respect of one professional for another. 'You have a talent for this,' he says. It is not entirely a compliment.

failure.body:
The followers are more capable than either of you estimated, and they box you in at a crossroads two miles from the destination. The package is taken. Your rogue shrugs with the philosophical air of a man for whom things going wrong is a professional constant.

'Lesson,' he says. 'Always have two packages.'

entries.body: He considers this offer with the same sideways calculation he applies to everything. The official roads are faster and safer by most measures. They are also watched. Whether watched helps or hinders depends entirely on what is in the package.

resolutions.label: convince him the official route is the better risk

success.body:
He agrees, after some negotiation about what you will and won't say if stopped. The official route is indeed faster, and you encounter no difficulty — the very visibility that he feared turns out to be protection. Nobody stops a knight.

The package arrives. He receives his payment. He looks at the coin and then at you. 'You are useful,' he says, 'in a legal sort of way.'

failure.body:
He declines the official route for reasons he declines to fully specify, and you are left to choose between letting him go and going with him on terms you haven't agreed to.

Some decisions belong to the person who carries the package.

entries.body:
He is sitting at the edge of the road with his pack beside him and no particular intention of moving, and the reason — when he decides you are worth telling — is that his partner is dead. Not his business partner, though that too. His partner in everything, for eleven years, a woman who was as capable a rogue as he was and considerably more charming.

She died of a fever three weeks past, in a town neither of them had intended to stop in, and he has been sitting at various points on the road ever since, not precisely going anywhere.

responses.label: You may sit with him a while.
You may ask where she would have wanted him to go.

entries.body: He is not the sort of man who usually receives sitting without suspicion, and the first few minutes have a quality of him deciding whether this is a con. When he decides it is not, the quality changes.

resolutions.label: sit with him until he is ready to stand

success.body:
An hour passes. He talks some, is quiet some, and by the end he has the look of a man who was carrying something he could not set down until someone else arrived to hold it briefly.

He picks up his pack. He says: 'I need to go north.' He goes north.

failure.body:
You sit, but the restlessness comes over you before he is ready, and he notices, and he picks up his pack before he means to — not because he is ready, but because your readiness to leave has made staying feel like a burden.

Waiting for someone else's moment requires patience you are still building.

entries.body: He is quiet for a long time after you ask this, and you think perhaps it was the wrong question. Then he says: north, probably. She had a sister north of the river. She spoke of visiting, in the someday way that people who live on the road speak of the things the road keeps them from.

resolutions.label: help him find the direction worth riding toward

success.body:
The north is not a solution, and you do not offer it as one. But it is a direction, which is what he needed — a next place that has a reason to it, and a person in it who was part of her life, which means part of his.

He stands. He adjusts his pack. He says: 'The sister will not like me.' You say: 'Go anyway.' He nods.

failure.body:
The north is where she would have wanted him to go, but it is not where he is ready to go, and the question has only made the gap between wanting and readiness more visible. He sits back down.

Some directions must be chosen in the person's own time.

entries.body:
He has been robbing travellers on this road for three years, and yesterday a traveller he robbed gave him back his own knife. He does not know what to do with this. The traveller did not fight him, did not run, did not plead — he simply took the knife when it was at his throat, held it a moment, and handed it back.

He has been sitting in the wood since yesterday trying to understand what happened, and he cannot get back to the road because he does not know what he is anymore in relation to it.

responses.label: You may help him understand what was given back to him with the knife.
You may take the opportunity to speak with him about another way of living.

entries.body: The traveller gave back the knife, which means the traveller decided not to use it, which means the traveller saw something in him that made the knife unnecessary. This is a mirror being held up, and the man in the mirror is not the man he has been performing for three years.

resolutions.label: help him see what the traveller saw

success.body:
You work it out together, carefully: the traveller gave back the knife because the robbery was performed without conviction, because what the robber was doing was not what he was. The knife was returned as information.

He sits with this for a long time. Then he asks: 'What do I do now?' Which is the right question. You do not have a complete answer. But you have the beginning of one.

failure.body:
The interpretation you offer does not quite land — he deflects it, or cannot quite let it mean what it means. He takes the knife and looks at it.

The mirror was offered. Some people are not yet ready to look into it.

entries.body: The opportunity is real. He is in a state of genuine openness — the kind that does not come from argument but from an event that cannot be argued with. The question is whether what you can offer is genuinely better than what he has, or only better by your measure.

resolutions.label: describe a life that is genuinely available to him and worth having

success.body:
You think before you speak, and what you offer is specific rather than abstract: a lord two days north who is known for taking on capable men without asking too many questions about their recent past. It is not a transformation — it is a step, taken in the right direction.

He listens. He says: 'That is not much.' You say: 'It is a beginning.' He nods.

failure.body:
What you offer is the life you know, which is not the life available to him, and he can see the gap without your pointing it out. He thanks you and retreats further into the wood.

A genuine alternative must be genuinely available.

entries.body:
He is trying to steal from you. Not aggressively — he is working through the social exchange of a traveller meeting another traveller, his hands busy at the edge of your perception while his eyes and voice occupy your attention. He is good at it. You notice because you were looking.

What is interesting is that he is not stealing for himself. The child at the road's edge is watching him with the concentrated attention of someone for whom the outcome of this matters considerably.

responses.label: You may let him know you noticed and see what happens next.
You may give him what he was trying to take.

entries.body: He goes still when you name what he was doing — the particular stillness of a person caught who has caught people himself and knows the various ways this goes. He does not run. He is watching to see what kind of caught this is.

resolutions.label: offer him a way forward that is better than the catching

success.body:
You ask what the child needs. He tells you, which was not expected — the telling is either a manipulation or a genuine response to being asked plainly, and you decide it is the latter. You give them both what was needed, which is more than what he was trying to steal.

He looks at you with the expression of a man who has just had his assumptions recalibrated. The child is already eating.

failure.body:
The catching produces a standoff — he is not afraid of you but he is not going to simply yield, and the child is watching, and neither of you wants the situation to escalate in front of the child. You release the impasse and he departs.

Some confrontations have no clean resolution.

entries.body: He was trying to take a piece of bread and a few coins. You give him those things, and he looks at you with an expression that starts as professional wariness and arrives somewhere much less professional.

resolutions.label: give generously and without comment

success.body:
He takes what you give with the hands of a man who has not been given things without conditions for a long time. The child runs to him. He does not say thank you, exactly, but he says something — a word that serves the same purpose in whatever language he comes from.

You ride on. The giving was simple. The receiving was not.

failure.body:
The giving comes with a look, or a pause, or some quality that attaches a condition even though you intended none. He takes what is offered with the careful distance of someone not quite sure of the price.

Generosity without reservation is harder than it looks.

entries.body:
He is in love with a woman who is in love with him, which sounds like the resolution rather than the problem, and would be except that she is the wife of a man who is also his employer. The situation has the particular trapped quality of something that happened in the space between decisions, where people do not usually look.

He has come to the conclusion that he must leave the employ and the city, which is the right conclusion, and he does not know how to leave without making it worse by the leaving.

responses.label: * You may help him find a way to leave that does the least harm.
* You may speak to him of what he owes the woman and what she owes herself.

entries.body: The leaving is the right move, but leavings have shapes, and the wrong shape creates a story that follows both people. A clean departure is possible; a dramatic one is not.

resolutions.label: help him construct a departure that closes the situation honestly

success.body:
You work out the form together: a reason for leaving that is true, a timing that does not coincide with any obvious event, and a word to the woman that says what it needs to say without saying what would be worse to say.

He departs three days later. The situation closes behind him without explosion. That is the best anyone could have hoped for.

failure.body:
The departure plan is good but the execution has a flaw you did not anticipate — a timing that coincides with something that gives the wrong impression. The story that follows him is not the worst possible, but it is not the clean one you aimed for.

Planned departures are still departures, and departures create their own stories.

entries.body: He has been thinking about what he owes and has not clearly thought about what she owes herself, which is a different calculation and arguably the more important one. She is not an object in his situation — she is a person in her own situation, and her choices are her own.

resolutions.label: help him see her situation clearly and separately from his own

success.body:
The shift happens when you ask what she would choose if she were choosing purely for herself. He has not asked her. He has been making decisions on her behalf as well as his own, which is the kind of thing people do when they are more comfortable with decisions than conversations.

He says: 'I need to talk to her.' You agree. That was the thing that was needed.

failure.body: The distinction between his situation and hers is one he is not yet ready to make — the two are too entangled in his thinking for clarity to arrive from the outside. He thanks you and continues carrying both.

entries.body:
He has convinced himself that he is in possession of a secret that will change everything, and the secret, as far as you can determine, is that the third mill in a particular village is not paying its full tithe to the lord, and the shortfall has been ongoing for eight years. This is true. It is not, however, the world-altering revelation he believes it to be.

He is going to the lord to deliver this information, and he believes the delivery will change his fortunes dramatically. He has calculated the reward wrong by an order of magnitude.

responses.label: You may tell him gently that his calculation is wrong.
You may go with him to see how it plays out and be there if it goes wrong.

entries.body: He does not want to hear that the calculation is wrong. But he needs to hear it before he stands in a lord's hall and makes claims that will be received as either trivial or impertinent.

resolutions.label: give him the honest recalculation before he embarrasses himself

success.body:
The recalculation, laid out plainly, does deflate something — the grandiosity that had built up around a small true fact. He sits with it. He is not grateful, exactly. But he says: 'How do I make it worth more?'

Now that is the right question. You have three suggestions.

failure.body:
He has invested too much in the value of the secret to receive the recalculation. He argues with your numbers and continues to the lord's hall.

Some mistakes must be made in order to be learned from. You hope the consequences are not too severe.

entries.body: The lord receives the information with the bored attention of a man who already suspected this and would have attended to it eventually. The reward discussed is a fraction of what was expected. The rogue's expression cycles through several stages that you watch with the particular interest of someone standing outside a door during a difficult conversation.

resolutions.label: intervene at the right moment to salvage something useful

success.body:
You enter the hall at the moment the conversation is at its lowest point and reframe the information as the beginning of something rather than the end — there is a larger pattern here, you suggest, that deserves an investigation the lord might want to commission. The lord's interest revives.

The rogue does not understand how this happened. He gets paid.

failure.body:
The moment you choose is the wrong one, or the framing does not land, and the lord's dismissal extends to you as well. You both leave the hall with less than you came with in terms of standing.

The right intervention requires the right moment, and the moment passed before you found it.

entries.body:
He is tracking the man who cheated him: a merchant who sold him a shipment of goods that were represented as one thing and were another, and took the payment and departed the city before the shipment was opened. The merchant has a day's head start and a horse.

The rogue does not have a horse, but he knows the roads, and he knows where a man going south would stop for the night, and he is prepared to walk through the night to get there before the merchant does.

responses.label: You may lend him your horse and your speed.
You may offer to handle the merchant on his behalf through official channels.

entries.body: With your horse, the day's head start can be overturned by nightfall. The question is what happens when he catches the merchant — whether this is a recovery or a confrontation.

resolutions.label: ride ahead and intercept the merchant before the rogue arrives

success.body:
You reach the merchant at the inn where the rogue expected him, and you hold him — politely, your horse blocking the exit, your presence explaining itself — until the rogue arrives an hour later. The recovery is made. The merchant argues. Your presence stops the argument from being physical.

The rogue gets his goods back, or their equivalent value. He looks at you with an expression that is not quite gratitude and is close enough.

failure.body:
The merchant took a different road, or moved faster than expected, and the inn is empty. You wait for the rogue to arrive and give him the news.

A head start and a changed road are sometimes enough. The merchant got away.

entries.body: Official channels require evidence, a lord willing to hear the case, and time. None of these is what the rogue wants. But what he wants — confrontation and recovery — carries risks that official channels do not, and the merchant is not without connections.

resolutions.label: convince him the official route serves his actual interests better

success.body:
He listens because he is tired, and because you are standing between him and the road south, and because some part of him knows the official route is more reliable. He gives you the documentation — he has it, carefully kept, which tells you he half-intended this all along.

The case is heard. The merchant is fined. The recovery is made, minus costs, over two weeks rather than one night. He says: 'Slower.' You say: 'Safer.' He shrugs.

failure.body:
He does not accept the official route, and goes south anyway. You hope he finds the merchant before he finds trouble.

Some grievances have their own momentum. You can only get out of the way.

entries.body:
He has been on the road for forty years, and his face is a map of what forty years on the road produces: lines that are some grief and some weather and some laughter, and eyes that have seen the world through its window rather than its parlour. He is not going anywhere in particular today, which he says is his greatest achievement.

He asks if you have ever been truly free of an obligation, even briefly. He is not asking to be difficult. He seems genuinely curious.

responses.label: You may answer him honestly.
You may ask him instead what freedom, in his experience, actually looks like.

entries.body: The honest answer requires examination, because the question is not about temporary holidays from responsibility but about the deeper thing he seems to be asking: whether you have ever existed, even briefly, without something pulling on you.

resolutions.label: answer with genuine honesty after genuine examination

success.body:
The examination produces an honest answer, and it is not the one you expected — there was a morning, or an hour, or a particular quality of some road you cannot name now, where the obligations briefly lifted without having been resolved. You describe it.

He nods slowly. 'That,' he says, 'is what I am looking for most days. Sometimes I find it.' He seems neither happy nor unhappy about this.

failure.body:
The honest answer, when you try to find it, is that you are not sure — the question goes somewhere you have not examined closely before. You say so.

He nods. 'That is the most honest answer,' he says. 'I did not expect it.'

entries.body: He looks pleased by the return question. He has been asked for advice before, but not often asked what he has actually experienced, which is different and more interesting.

resolutions.label: receive his account of freedom with full attention

success.body:
He describes three moments — specific, located, years apart — when the road became itself rather than a means of getting to somewhere else. They are very ordinary moments, described with precision, and together they say something that no single one of them could say.

You do not know what to do with what he has described. That seems to be the point.

failure.body:
You listen, but the particular quality of attention required to receive what he is saying escapes you today. You hear the words and not the thing underneath them.

Some things can only be heard when you are ready to hear them.

entries.body:
He is going to sell you something you do not want. He has not yet decided what, but the decision will be made in the next few minutes based on what he reads from your face and your gear and the way you hold your horse. He is very good at this, and he is enjoying the assessment.

He opens with flattery that is precise enough to be interesting — he has identified something real about you, not just a generic compliment — and the precision is itself the tell.

responses.label: You may engage with him on his own terms and see who comes out ahead.
You may name what he is doing before he makes his offer.

entries.body: His terms favour him: he knows the game better and has had more practice. But you know you are in the game, which is the minimum requirement for not losing entirely.
