He tells you. And because you held the position, what he tells you is the truth of it rather than the edited version — the illegal prisoner, the object moved, the guards confused — and you can make an informed decision. You agree, because the intervention was just.

The knowing made the helping better.

failure.body:
He will not tell without your agreement, and you will not agree without telling, and the standoff ends with him going elsewhere for help.

The principle was right. The outcome is the outcome.

entries.body:
He is looking for a specific knight — not you — who wronged his mistress in a matter he declines to specify. The description of the knight he is looking for is a description of someone you know slightly, a knight of unremarkable reputation who was at court last winter.

He asks you to tell him where the knight is. He does not say what he will do when he finds him. He has the air of a being who expects the question not to be asked.

responses.label: You may tell him what you know of the knight's whereabouts.
You may ask what was done before you direct him anywhere.

entries.body: The knight was at a manor two days west as of last week. Whether he is still there is uncertain. Sending a dwarf of this bearing in that direction is a decision with consequences that depend entirely on what he plans to do when he arrives.

resolutions.label: tell him truly and offer to accompany him to ensure the encounter is fair

success.body:
He accepts the direction and, after a pause, your company — which changes the nature of what the encounter will be. With you present, it cannot be summary; there will be words and testimony and a reckoning rather than a retribution.

What the knight did was real, it turns out. What follows is difficult but not unjust.

failure.body:
You direct him without accompanying him, and the encounter that follows is not one you witnessed. You hear about it afterward, from a different direction, and the account is not comfortable.

Directing someone toward a confrontation carries some responsibility for the confrontation.

entries.body: He tells you: the knight made a promise in the presence of his mistress — a specific promise with a specific consequence — and has not kept it. The promise was freely made. The consequence was understood. His mistress does not ask for more than was promised.

resolutions.label: find the knight and remind him of what he owes

success.body:
The knight remembers the promise and knows he has not kept it. Your arrival, accompanied by the knowledge that a fey court's messenger is looking for him, provides the urgency that conscience alone had not. He makes arrangements to keep the promise.

The dwarf receives your report with the satisfaction of a professional whose case has been handled correctly.

failure.body:
The knight disputes the terms of the promise, or denies the consequence, or is simply not present. The debt remains outstanding.

Unkept promises do not resolve themselves. They accumulate.

entries.body:
He knows the road ahead better than you do. Not just the physical road — the relational road, the web of obligations and enmities and alliances that stretches from here to wherever you are going. He has been a messenger for the fey courts long enough to have passed through this territory many times, and territory, over time, becomes information.

He offers to tell you what he knows. He does not specify a price. That is the most expensive form of offer.

responses.label: You may accept his offer and hear what he knows.
You may ask what the offer costs before you accept it.

entries.body: He tells you four things. Three of them concern the road ahead — enmities to avoid, an alliance that has shifted, a lord who is not what he appears. The fourth concerns you specifically, and it is the fourth that he has been saving.

resolutions.label: receive all four things fully, including the fourth

success.body:
The fourth is not comfortable but it is accurate, and accuracy about oneself has a particular value that comfort cannot provide. You receive it in the spirit in which it was given — as information, not judgment — and ride forward with all four things in your consideration.

He departs without specifying a price. That, too, was information.

failure.body:
The fourth arrives and you deflect it — not consciously, but defensively — and what should have been information becomes a source of irritation. He watches this happen with the neutral interest of a professional.

Deflected information does not disappear. It waits.

entries.body: He says: no price that he knows of. Which means there is a price he does not know of, which is either the honest truth of how fey-court offers work or a deflection designed to produce the acceptance. Both are possible.

resolutions.label: engage with the uncertainty and accept on reasonable terms

success.body:
You accept on terms you name yourself: you will receive the information, you will not be obligated by it beyond the immediate use you make of it, and any consequence that follows is yours to bear as a free agent. He receives these terms with the slight amusement of one who has seen this negotiation attempted before and found it adequate.

The information is good. The terms held.

failure.body:
The terms you name are not quite complete — they leave a gap that he notes without filling, and the gap is where the price lives. You accept without fully closing it.

Fey-court offers are designed to be accepted. They are not designed to be declined. The difference is in the terms.

entries.body:
He is playing both sides of a transaction. You can see this in the careful precision of how he describes both parties to you, each description shaped to produce a specific impression that serves a specific outcome. He is not lying — the descriptions are accurate — but the angle of the telling is the lie.

He is managing a dispute between two lords and has calculated that the most favourable outcome for his court requires a particular resolution. You are, apparently, one of the factors he is planning to use.

responses.label: You may name what he is doing and decline to be a factor.
You may play the factor consciously, extracting what you can from the position.

entries.body: He receives the naming with the composure of someone who has been named before. He neither confirms nor denies. He waits to see what you do with the naming.

resolutions.label: decline the role and name what a fair resolution would actually look like

success.body:
You describe what a fair resolution between the two lords would look like without the fey court's preferred angle, and the description is accurate enough that he has to engage with it rather than dismiss it.

The fair resolution is less advantageous to his court but not entirely without benefit. He takes it back to consider. You have introduced an alternative that was not on the table before.

failure.body:
Your declination and description produce no visible effect — he nods and continues on his way, carrying the plan intact. Your refusal removed you from the game without changing the game.

Declining a role in something does not stop the something.

entries.body: Being a conscious factor is different from being an unwitting one. If he needs you to do a specific thing, and you know he needs it, you can decide what the price of doing it is. The price must be something real enough to be worth the negotiation.

resolutions.label: negotiate your role consciously and get something real in return

success.body:
The negotiation is good — you are asking for something he can give, in exchange for something he actually needs, and both things are calibrated correctly. He agrees, because it is still better than his alternative.

You do what was asked. The resolution proceeds. The price is paid.

failure.body:
Your price is either too high or too low — too high and he finds another way, too low and you have undersold yourself. Either way, the negotiation does not land where you meant it to.

Pricing information requires knowing the market. This market is unfamiliar.

entries.body:
He has lost the knight he was assigned to guide. This is a significant failure: dwarf-guides do not lose knights, as a rule, because losing the knight is losing the purpose, and purpose is the central fact of a dwarf's professional existence.

The knight went south when he was supposed to go east, and did so with such conviction that the dwarf could not redirect him, and has now been going south for two days. The dwarf is going to find the knight and resume the guidance. He would like your help finding where a very determined knight who went south two days ago might currently be.

responses.label: You may help him track the knight.
You may ask why the knight went south instead of east.

entries.body: A knight who went south for two days with conviction has gone in a specific direction at a specific pace, and the pace and conviction together narrow the possibilities considerably. He was going somewhere, not wandering.

resolutions.label: track the knight to wherever he went

success.body:
You find him — at the manor he was apparently heading to all along, attending to a piece of personal business that the dwarf's assignment was interrupting. He is surprised to be found, mildly embarrassed, and willing to continue east now that the personal business is concluded.

The dwarf receives him with the professional irritation of a guide who has been made to wait. He does not say anything. He resumes the guidance.

failure.body:
The knight's trail goes cold at a ford — he crossed and the far bank is too busy for individual tracks. He is south of the ford, somewhere, and that is all you can determine.

Some quarries know they are being followed and arrange not to be found.

entries.body: He is reluctant to say, because the reason reveals something about the guidance that he would prefer not to have revealed. After some persistence, he says: the knight was told to go east, but what waits east is something the knight knew about and was avoiding, and knowing this, the knight went south instead.

resolutions.label: help him understand whether the knight should be returned east at all

success.body:
The question of whether the guidance should be resumed depends on what waits east. He tells you — reluctantly — and you assess it honestly: the thing east is something the knight was sent toward for reasons that were good, and the avoidance was understandable but not the right choice.

You give him that assessment, and he nods, and the guidance resumes with a clearer understanding of what it is for.

failure.body:
The thing east is something you cannot assess without more information, and without assessment you cannot help. He continues the tracking without the benefit of your counsel.

Some decisions cannot be made without information that is not available.

entries.body:
He has been pursued for three days by something he will not name. Whatever it is, it has the quality of a working aimed specifically at him, and it is gaining rather than losing ground. He has been running — in the dignified, rapid way of his kind — and is now at the end of his capacity to run further.

He asks for sanctuary. Not protection — he is past believing protection is available — but the formal sanctuary that a knight can offer: a name invoked, a presence given, a claim made on his behalf before whatever is coming.

responses.label: You may give him sanctuary and stand against what follows.
You may help him understand what he carries that is being pursued.

entries.body: Giving sanctuary means standing between him and what is coming, in the formal sense: your name and your presence as the claiming party. What is coming will have to decide whether your claim is worth contesting.

resolutions.label: stand as his sanctuary with your full name and presence

success.body:
What arrives is not simple — it is a working of considerable specificity, aimed at him and not at you, and it pauses before your claim. The pause is the important thing. It considers.

Then it withdraws. Not permanently — it will return with a different approach — but for now your claim has bought him time.

He looks at you with the gravity of someone receiving a gift of uncertain final cost.

failure.body:
Your claim is real but not sufficient — whatever is pursuing him has calculated the cost of your presence and found it acceptable. You slow it, but do not stop it.

You gave what was required. That it was not enough is the measure of what he carries.

entries.body: He carries something — not physically, but informationally: he knows something that something does not want known. The pursuit is not about him but about what he knows, which means the pursuit can be ended if what he knows is known by enough people to make the hiding of it impossible.

resolutions.label: help him distribute what he knows before the pursuit catches him

success.body:
You spend an hour together determining who needs to know and how quickly they can be reached. Between your connections and his speed, the information is with five people before the pursuing working arrives.

The working arrives, assesses the situation, and withdraws. A secret known by six people is not a secret that can be retrieved by force.

He sits down for the first time in three days.

failure.body:
The distribution is incomplete before the working arrives — two people instead of five, which is not enough. The working takes what it came for and departs.

Speed and reach were both required. You had one and not the other.

entries.body:
She stands at the side of the road in the full light of noon, and the light seems to treat her differently than it treats everything else — it moves around her rather than on her, as though the air itself is making an accommodation. She is watching you approach with an interest that is not quite human in its quality, neither predatory nor friendly, simply other.

She is curious, she says, about you specifically. Not knights in general. You. She has been watching your road for some time and has questions.

responses.label: You may answer her questions.
You may ask your own question before you answer any of hers.

entries.body: Her questions are not what you expected. She does not ask about your deeds or your lineage. She asks about the colour of a dream you had last week, the last thing you said to someone you will not see again, and what you think of in the moment before sleep. The questions have the quality of someone conducting a very specific research.

resolutions.label: answer all three honestly

success.body:
She receives each answer with the full attention of one for whom nothing is trivial, and at the end she nods as though something has been confirmed. She gives you, in return, a piece of information about your road that you did not have and could not have obtained by any ordinary means.

She is gone before you have finished thinking about what she said.

failure.body:
One answer is not fully honest — the second one, which is the one that mattered most to her. She receives it with an expression that is not disappointment but its equivalent, whatever that looks like in her kind.

She gives you something anyway — a small thing, imprecise, the version of what she would have given if the answer had been true.

entries.body: She pauses. She considers your request with the quality of someone translating between very different operating systems. Then she says: you may ask one question. It must be a question she is able to answer. She will tell you if it is not.

resolutions.label: ask what you most want to know from one who sees differently

success.body:
She is able to answer it. The answer is in a form that will take you some time to understand completely — not obscure, but layered in the way that genuine answers are layered. She gives it fully and without embellishment.

Then she asks her questions. You answer. She nods. She departs. The exchange was equitable.

failure.body:
The question you ask is not one she is able to answer — or it is, but in a form so different from what you were asking that the answer is not useful. She tells you this plainly and offers an alternative.

The alternative is useful. The question you came with was the wrong one.

entries.body:
She is weeping, which is unusual enough to stop you entirely. Not the weeping of a human face — the tears are the right kind, but the expression around them is wrong, too still, too complete, as though the sorrow is her whole self rather than something happening to her.

She has lost something. Not an object — she is past objects. She has lost access to a place she cannot go back to, and the loss is permanent in a way that her kind is not built to accommodate.

responses.label: You may sit with her in the grief.
You may ask whether the place she has lost can be reached by any living means.

entries.body: Sitting with a faerie's grief is different from sitting with a person's. The sorrow has a quality of depth that human grief reaches toward and does not quite achieve — not more valuable, but different in kind, like the difference between a river and an ocean.

resolutions.label: remain present with the grief in its full depth

success.body:
You remain. The grief does not diminish — it cannot, in the time you have — but it is companioned. She looks at you eventually with an expression that has something grateful in it, though the word is not quite right.

She gives you a gift that she had been carrying for another purpose: a small piece of the place she has lost, preserved in the way that her kind preserves things. You will understand what it is in time.

failure.body:
The depth of the grief is more than you can remain present with — it pulls at you in ways that human grief does not, and you find yourself retreating before you mean to. She does not reproach you.

'Your kind cannot hold this long,' she says. It is not unkind. It is simply true.

entries.body: The place she has lost is one of the old places — a valley between two hills that is no longer accessible because the hills have been changed, their arrangement altered by a working that was not aimed at her but had her place as its casualty. The hills are three days west.

resolutions.label: ride west and find what was done to the hills

success.body:
You find the working — human in origin, a boundary-working intended for something entirely unrelated, which displaced a geographical element that had been in place since before the working's creator was born. The working can be modified without defeating its original purpose.

You do not know how to modify it. But you know who does, and you ride back to tell her what you found.

failure.body:
The hills are as they are. Whatever was done to them is done too deeply for the kind of reading you can perform. You return to her with nothing useful.

Not all losses can be traced to their cause, let alone reversed.

entries.body:
She radiates something that is not quite light and not quite warmth but encompasses both — a quality that the road has more of after she passes through it than before. She is going to a holy place, she says, for reasons that are not, in her frame of reference, religious, but which involve the same crossing between the ordinary and the more-than-ordinary that holy places are built on.

She does not need your help. She is, nevertheless, pleased by your company.

responses.label: You may walk with her to the holy place.
You may ask her what she believes is more-than-ordinary.

entries.body: The holy place is two hours away, and the two hours in her company have a particular quality that the rest of the day does not. She does not do anything to produce this quality. It simply occurs.

resolutions.label: walk with her and receive what the walk offers

success.body:
At the holy place she does something you cannot watch directly — not because it is hidden, but because looking at it requires a kind of attention that produces a pressure behind the eyes. When it is finished, the place feels different.

She thanks you for the company with a sincerity that her kind is not usually credited with. You believe it.

failure.body:
The two hours are harder to receive than expected — you are too watchful, too ready to interpret, and the quality of the walk requires something simpler. You arrive at the holy place having walked past much of what was available.

Presence is not the same as watchfulness.

entries.body: She considers the question with the pleasure of one who enjoys being asked. What she believes is more-than-ordinary is: the edge of anything — the edge of a wood, the edge of sleep, the edge of a season. The edges are where the two orders of existence are closest.

resolutions.label: engage with this fully and test it against your own experience

success.body:
You think of the edges in your own life — moments of threshold, of crossing — and you find, when you examine them, that she is right: the ordinary and more-than-ordinary were closer in those moments than at any other. You tell her this.

She says: 'Yes. That is what I came here to confirm.'

failure.body:
Your examination of your own experience does not produce what she described, or produces it but in a form you cannot quite say. The conversation ends without the connection it was reaching toward.

The truth of the edges is easier to experience than to articulate.

entries.body:
She is bound. Not to a tree or a stone — to a decision, which in the economy of faerie law is the same as being bound to a place: she made a choice a century ago and the choice has a consequence, and the consequence requires her presence in this spot until a condition is met. The condition, she says, involves a question being asked that she cannot ask herself.

She looks at you with the particular expression of someone who has been waiting for exactly the person they are looking at.

responses.label: You may ask the question — whatever it is — and see what happens.
You may ask her first what will happen when the condition is met.

entries.body: The question comes to you without effort, which tells you it was always there: it is the question of why she made the choice, not what it was or what it cost, but why — what she was for, in the moment she chose. This is the question she cannot ask herself because the self that would answer it is the self the choice created.

resolutions.label: ask her why she made the choice

success.body:
She answers it. The answer is long, and some of it is in a language you do not have, but what reaches you is: she chose from love rather than interest, which cost her everything and was still right.

The binding dissolves. She stands in the ordinary air of the road with the expression of someone who has not done so for a very long time, and looks at the sky.

failure.body:
The question that comes to you is not quite right — close but off by something small, and in faerie law small is everything. The binding holds. She looks at you with an expression that is not quite patience and not quite resignation.

The right question is still out there. It may be yours to find, or someone else's.

entries.body: She tells you: when the condition is met, she returns to where she came from, which is a place that does not appear on any map because it predates the category of map. She has been in this spot for a hundred years and will have no memory of it once she goes.

resolutions.label: ask what makes her situation bearable, and receive what she says

success.body:
She says: certain travellers, over the hundred years, asked her this question, and the asking confirmed that the waiting had been witnessed. She had not expected to find it comforting and did.

You ask the question then, the real one. The binding considers it. The binding releases.

failure.body:
Your question is sincere but it is not the specific question the condition requires — the difference is architectural rather than moral, a matter of form rather than intent.

You have given her something. The condition waits for someone else.

entries.body:
She has fallen in love with a mortal man, which is the beginning of one of the world's oldest stories and rarely ends well for the mortal. She knows this. He does not. She is trying to decide whether to continue the acquaintance or withdraw before the story goes in the direction it always goes.

She asks you, with the directness of one for whom social convention is a foreign language, what you think she should do.

responses.label: * You may give her your honest opinion.
* You may ask her what she hopes for, before you answer.

entries.body: The honest opinion requires knowing the story well enough to say something true about it. The story of a faerie's love for a mortal is old, and its endings are known, and none of them is straightforward.

resolutions.label: give her your honest and considered opinion on the situation

success.body:
What you say is: the question is not whether she should love him but whether she can love him in a way that does not require him to become less human. Some loves of this kind have been given well. Most have not. The difference is in whether the giver can hold back the tide.

She is quiet for a long time. 'I do not know if I can,' she says. You say that is the right question to have arrived at.

failure.body:
Your opinion comes out as a warning rather than counsel — the endings of the story, the costs, the patterns — and she receives it as you already know what she should do, which is not what counsel is.

Advice that knows the answer in advance of listening is not always advice.

entries.body: She tells you what she hopes for: not possession, not even permanence — just the experience of being fully known by someone in the mortal world, once, before she returns to a place where being fully known is different. This is a small and modest hope by the standards of the story she is in.

resolutions.label: help her find the form that might make the small hope possible

success.body:
The form exists — there are stories of it, though they are not the famous ones. You describe what it requires: complete honesty from both parties about what they are and what they cannot give the other, and the willingness to hold the meeting within those limits.

She listens. She says: 'That is harder than the other thing.' You say: 'Yes. But it is real.'

failure.body:
The form you describe requires a quality of mutual honesty that the situation does not currently contain — the man does not know what she is — and beginning with that particular disclosure is its own enormous problem.

Some hopes require a great deal of preliminary work before they can be attempted.

entries.body:
She is changing the weather. Not dramatically — she is making it rain in a very specific way over a very specific field, with a precision that tells you this is intentional and a peculiarity that tells you the intention is not rational. The rain falls in a perfect square, and the field is empty, and she has been doing this for some time.

Her court has sent two others to collect her, and both times she has sent them away. She is working out something in the weather that she has not yet resolved.

responses.label: You may ask her what she is working out.
You may speak to her court on her behalf and buy her more time.

entries.body: She turns to look at you and the rain turns with her, which is both alarming and strangely graceful. She is working out, she says, whether a specific thing is possible. She will not say what the thing is. But the working out requires weather, and weather requires time.

resolutions.label: find the question she is working out and offer what you know of the answer

success.body:
The question, when you find it, is a question about whether something that was sundered can be made whole again. Not physically — she is not a mender of broken objects — but structurally, in the way that certain relationships are broken.

You know the answer, or a version of it: sometimes yes, and the yes requires a specific action on both parts, and the action is possible but not guaranteed. She receives this and stops the rain.

The field is ordinary again. She looks at it with an expression that might be satisfaction.

failure.body:
The question she is working out is beyond your reach to name, or you name it wrong, and she continues the rain without engaging with your attempt.

Some things cannot be reached from the outside. The working must conclude itself.

entries.body: Her court is a day's ride in a specific direction that she indicates without words — a gesture that describes a direction that is not north, south, east, or west as you understand them, but which you can follow if you watch carefully. Speaking to a faerie court on behalf of one of their own requires a particular kind of courtesy that is not human courtesy but adjacent to it.

resolutions.label: speak to her court with the appropriate courtesy and make her case

success.body:
The court receives you with the detached interest of beings for whom a human advocate is novel but not unprecedented. What you give them is time — not indefinite, but enough. They agree to a specific interval within which she may conclude her working uninterrupted.

The interval is three days, which she appears to know without being told.

failure.body:
The courtesy required is more specific than you possess, and the court dismisses your advocacy with the politeness of beings who do not need to be impolite to be unmoved. They send a third collector.

The languages of different courts require their own study.

entries.body:
She has a grievance with a mortal lord — a specific grievance, two centuries old, involving a promise his ancestor made and his family has never kept. She is not violent. She is simply persistent in the way that beings with unlimited time are persistent: she will be there at the end, however long the end takes.

She asks you to carry her grievance to the current lord as a formal statement of the obligation. She does not require action, only acknowledgment.

responses.label: You may carry her grievance to the lord as requested.
You may ask her what the promise was before you agree to carry it.

entries.body: The lord is a man of middle years who has heard about the faerie grievance from his grandfather and has been expecting exactly this encounter at some point, which makes him more prepared for it than most lords in similar situations.

resolutions.label: deliver the formal statement and facilitate the acknowledgment

success.body:
The lord acknowledges the obligation — formally, in the presence of witnesses, in the specific terms the faerie's statement requires. The acknowledgment is not the keeping of the promise, but it is a step toward it, and in faerie law acknowledgment has its own weight.

When you return to her with the acknowledgment, she regards it with the expression of one who has been patient for two centuries and finds one more small step in the right direction adequate for the present.

failure.body:
The lord acknowledges the obligation verbally but will not do so in the formal terms required. The acknowledgment is insufficient.

Faerie law has specific requirements, and what fails the specificity fails the requirement entirely.

entries.body: The promise was: the first son of each generation of the family would learn the old language, which is the language in which the world's original names were given. The family kept the promise for four generations and then stopped. The language is now dead in that family.

resolutions.label: help her think through what satisfaction is actually available for this loss

success.body:
The promise cannot be retroactively kept — the four generations are gone, and the language was not learned. What remains possible is: the current lord could begin again, for his own son, if he chose. That is not the same as the promise kept. It is the promise renewed.

She considers this for a long time. Then she says: 'That would be acceptable.' The word 'acceptable' carries a century of accommodation in it.

failure.body:
The available satisfaction is not available to be articulated in terms that bridge the faerie and human frames of the promise — what she lost and what can be restored do not map onto each other cleanly. The conversation ends without resolution.

Some obligations have no adequate remedy. They can only be held.

entries.body:
She has something to tell you. Not as a riddle, not as a bargain, not as a warning obscured in metaphor — she has something specific and true to tell a specific and true person, and she has been waiting on this road for the right person to arrive. She believes you are that person.

She will tell you without precondition. She asks only that you receive it fully, because half-receiving it will make the giving pointless.

responses.label: You may receive what she has to tell with full attention.
You may ask first if what she tells will be welcome or unwelcome.

entries.body: She tells you. It is about yourself — not your deeds or your future, but your nature: something that is true about you that you have been around the edges of but not inside. The telling requires several minutes and an attention from you that is the equivalent of a physical exertion.

resolutions.label: receive the telling with your full self, nothing withheld

success.body:
You receive it completely. The thing she tells is not dramatic — it is not a prophecy or a secret — but it is accurate in the way that very few things said about a person are accurate, and the accuracy is the gift.

She nods when it is complete. 'It was received,' she says. Which is the whole of what she came to say.

failure.body:
You receive most of it. The part you do not receive is the central part — the thing you could not quite let in — and what you carry away is the true account with a hole in its middle.

The hole is shaped like the thing you were not ready to know.

entries.body: She says: it is neither welcome nor unwelcome. It is simply true. Truths of this kind — about oneself — can be received as welcome or unwelcome depending entirely on the state of the receiver.

resolutions.label: prepare yourself honestly to receive what follows

success.body:
The preparation changes the receiving: you arrive at the telling without a preferred version of what it will be, which gives it space to be what it is. She tells it. You hold it.

Later, alone, you will find yourself returning to it as to something that was given rather than said.

failure.body:
The preparation produces a vigilance that is the opposite of openness — you arrive at the telling braced, and bracing prevents full reception. What reaches you is partial.

Preparation and readiness are different things. You prepared but were not ready.

entries.body:
She has a game she would like to play with you. She explains the game clearly, which is itself unusual, and the game as described seems fair — a test of a specific quality, with a specific reward and a specific forfeit. Both are things she has the authority to give and take.

The game is not a trap. It is a genuine game. She prefers genuine games. But she is very good at them.

responses.label: You may accept the game and play.
You may ask why she plays games with mortals.

entries.body: The game is this: she will make three statements, and you will determine which is true, which is false, and which is true in a different language than the one it is stated in. The third category is the difficult one.

resolutions.label: play the game with full attention and find all three

success.body:
You find all three. The first is the easy one. The second requires patience. The third requires a particular kind of lateral thinking — holding the statement at an angle until its truth in a different register becomes visible.

She nods, and gives you the reward: a piece of knowledge about a road ahead that cannot be obtained by any ordinary means, and that is genuinely useful.

failure.body:
You find two of the three. The third eludes you — the true-in-a-different-language one is beyond your current range. She takes the forfeit, which is small: something you will not miss until you need it, which is when you will miss it most.

The game was fair. You were not quite ready for the third.

entries.body: She considers the question with genuine interest. She plays games with mortals, she says, because mortals are the only beings she has encountered who play games and mean something by them — whose games have stakes that they actually feel. Her own kind plays games that are more like choreography.

resolutions.label: engage with her answer and offer what games mean to you

success.body:
What games mean to you — the articulation of this, against her question — produces something neither of you expected: a genuine exchange about the nature of risk and meaning that goes further than the question reached.

She proposes the game afterward, but on terms that she describes as more interesting. You accept. She does not cheat.

failure.body:
Your answer is thinner than her question, and the exchange does not produce the mutual understanding it was reaching for. She proposes the game anyway, on the original terms.

Some conversations require a fuller preparation than the moment allows.

entries.body:
She is separated from her court, which is not the same as being lost — she knows exactly where her court is, and she knows exactly how to return to it. What she lacks is the capacity to return: something in the mortal world has attached to her in a way that prevents the crossing back, and she does not know what.

She has been stranded for three weeks and is running out of the things that faeries carry for mortal-world use: patience, primarily.

responses.label: You may help her find what has attached to her.
You may help her find a different means of crossing.

entries.body: The attachment is something she received rather than something she sought. In the mortal world, things attach to faeries through gifts, obligations, or feelings — any of which can create a tie that prevents returning.

resolutions.label: review her last three weeks with her and find the attachment

success.body:
The attachment is a feeling — not love, but something adjacent: the particular regard of a mortal child who watched her pass and looked at her as though she were the most beautiful thing in the world. That look attached.

You describe what you have found. She says: 'That is an easy one to release.' She releases it with something that looks like, but is not quite, regret. She crosses back.

failure.body:
The attachment is present but invisible to your searching — whatever tied her here was subtle enough to pass your reading. She remains stranded.

Some ties between worlds are finer than the instruments that might find them.

entries.body: The normal crossing-points between her court and the mortal world are the old places — standing stones, particular fords, the angles of certain hills at certain times. If one crossing is blocked, another may be open.

resolutions.label: find an alternate crossing-point she can use

success.body:
You know of a place — two hours south, a ford at the crossing of three streams — that has the quality of a crossing-point even if you could not have said why before now. She goes there with you, and the quality of the ford is exactly what she needed.

She steps into it and is not on the other side. She is simply gone, which is a different thing.

failure.body:
The places you know of that might serve as alternate crossings are either not what they appear or not accessible in the form required. The stranding continues.

The landscape of crossing-points is its own study, and you have only the edge of it.

entries.body:
She is fading. This is not metaphorical. She is becoming less present in the mortal world in a way that is not death but is the direction of death — a withdrawal of whatever makes her visible and real from this side of the divide. It will complete itself in a week if nothing changes.

Her court knows. They sent someone — a dwarf, probably — but the dwarf did not arrive in time, or arrived and could not help. She does not have another week for another attempt.

responses.label: You may try to slow or reverse the fading through your knowledge of such workings.
You may help her complete something before the fading is done.

entries.body: The fading is not a working that was done to her — it is a natural process, accelerated by something: possibly the attachment issue, possibly a change in the land, possibly something in her own choice. Reversing it requires finding the acceleration and removing it.

resolutions.label: find what is accelerating the fading and remove it

success.body:
The acceleration is a grief — not for a person, but for a place that no longer exists, a quality of the world that is gone. The grief is holding her in a state of between: not fully present in the mortal world, not fully withdrawing from it.

You give her, precisely and carefully, the thing that grief needs: acknowledgment without resolution. The fading slows. Slowing is not stopping, but it gives her court time to arrive.

She looks at you with the full attention of a being who is now more present than she was.

failure.body:
The acceleration is deeper than your working can reach — it is in the structure of her relationship with the mortal world, not in anything that can be addressed from outside it.

You cannot stop this. What you can do is stay with her while her court finds another way.

entries.body: She has one thing she wanted to do in the mortal world that she has not done. Not a grand quest — a small thing, specific, that she had been putting off for a century and now will not have the time to put off further.

resolutions.label: help her complete the thing she came to do

success.body:
The thing is this: to stand at a specific place at a specific time and see the light in a particular way. You find the place, you bring her there, and the light does what she said it would.

She is more present at the end of it than she was at the beginning. Whatever the seeing gave her, it was enough to strengthen her hold on the world, at least briefly.

She says: 'That was all I came for.' There is no loss in it.

failure.body:
The place is found but the light is wrong — cloud, or the timing off, or something the seeing required that the day does not provide. The thing goes undone.

You remain with her. That is what is left to give.

entries.body:
Beneath the city's streets, where the old Roman stonework gives way to older stonework still, a portion of the underground passage has collapsed. A small crowd has gathered at the opening in the cobbles — someone went down an hour past and has not come up. A rope hangs into the dark.

The passage is narrow and the air that rises from it is cold. Something is wrong below, beyond the simple wrongness of a fall.

responses.label: You may go down the rope and find who is below.
You may scout the surrounding streets for another entrance before descending.

entries.body: The passage below is a tangle of fallen stone and old timbers, and somewhere in it a man is pinned and conscious and trying not to show how much pain he is in. Getting him out requires moving things in the right order.

resolutions.label: work quickly to free him from the fallen stone

success.body: You find the load-bearing pieces and shift them correctly — a thing easier to say than to do in a dark space with bad air and limited room. He comes free. You get him up the rope before the passage settles further.

failure.body:
The stone shifts wrong, and you retreat before more falls. He is still below, still alive, calling up to you. You will need more hands and better light.

You have learned the shape of what is needed.

entries.body: The streets here are Roman-built over something older, and older means there may be access through a cellar, or through the drainage culverts that still run below the market quarter. You have an hour before the trapped man's situation becomes worse.

resolutions.label: find the secondary entrance and reach him from below

success.body:
The culvert entrance is exactly where old city logic suggests it should be. You come up below the collapse, brace it from underneath, and the man is freed and walking within the hour.

The crowd above does not know how it was done. The man does.

failure.body:
The culvert is blocked with decades of accumulated debris, and clearing it would take longer than the man has. The rope descent remains the only way.

You have lost the time the scouting cost. Go down.

entries.body:
The cave's mouth is half-hidden by roots and old ivy, and the deer-track that leads to it suggests animals have been avoiding this place for some time. Something lives here, or lived here — the bones at the entrance are too old and too large to be recent, and too numerous to be coincidence.

A woodcutter's child went in three days past on a dare. She has not come back.

responses.label: You may enter the cave directly and find the child.
You may listen at the cave mouth before entering, reading what is within.

entries.body: The cave goes deeper than the hillside should allow. Your torch makes the dark retreat but does not eliminate it, and the sound of your breathing comes back to you changed, as though the rock has its own opinion of your presence.

resolutions.label: press forward to find the child

success.body:
You find her at the back of the cave, unharmed but unable to move — some quality of the place had fixed her there, a mild holding-enchantment that releases when a person of good intent approaches. She comes back to herself as you take her hand.

The cave is empty of its inhabitant. It has moved on.

failure.body:
The thing in the cave is aware of you before you are aware of it, and the encounter goes badly — not mortally, but enough that you retreat without the child, marked by something you would rather not have met.

You must try again, differently.

entries.body: The silence at the cave mouth has a quality. You listen to it, reading the specific kind of still — whether it is the still of emptiness or the still of something holding itself quiet. A hunter's skill applied differently.

resolutions.label: read the cave by sound and air before entering

success.body:
The cave is occupied but the occupant is asleep — a creature of territorial habit rather than malice, presently dormant in the deeper section. The child is in the middle portion, beyond the creature's immediate reach.

You enter quietly and have the child out before anything wakes.

failure.body:
The listening is inconclusive — the cave is too deep and the creature too still for the information to become certain. You must enter without the advantage you were hoping for.

You go in anyway.

entries.body:
The cave is high in the mountain, and its danger is not a creature but the mountain itself: a warming day has set the snow moving, and the cave that seemed like shelter this morning is now the only safe path — around it the slopes have become unreliable. But something is already in the cave.

A pilgrim who went before you is sheltering in the cave mouth. He cannot move forward and cannot go back. Neither can you.

responses.label: You may enter the cave fully and find a way through.
You may wait out the danger with the pilgrim and conserve your strength.

entries.body: The cave goes through the mountain — or appears to. The passage is narrow and descends steeply, and the pilgrim watches you go with the expression of someone who has weighed the same option and decided the other way.

resolutions.label: navigate the cave passage and find the way out the far side

success.body:
The passage is real and passable, and it opens on a sheltered slope below the avalanche line. You come back for the pilgrim, and together you reach safety before dark.

The mountain is indifferent to your survival. You survived anyway.

failure.body:
The passage narrows to nothing and you must back out — the way through is not here, or not this way. You return to the pilgrim having confirmed only what the cave is not.

You wait. The mountain allows it.

entries.body: Waiting is its own discipline: conserving warmth, reading the snow's movement on the slope, knowing when the moment comes to move and being ready for it. The pilgrim is older than you and better at stillness.

resolutions.label: wait out the danger with patient skill

success.body:
The snow settles. The slope hardens. You read the moment correctly and you both move, quickly but not rashly, through the window of safety. The cave is behind you.

The pilgrim says a brief prayer at the far edge. You add your own.

failure.body:
You misjudge the moment and the slope moves again when you are in the middle of it. You make it — barely, a scramble that leaves your hands cut — but the pilgrim is ahead of you and has already reached safe ground.

Close is not the same as comfortable. But it is alive.

entries.body:
The plains here have given way: a sinkhole, twenty feet across, has opened in the earth, and at its bottom is a passage going further down. A cart has gone in — you can see the wheel marks at the edge — and the carter is nowhere visible. The hole is fresh; the walls are raw clay.

Something about the passage below is not entirely natural. The air rising from it is too warm for the depth.

responses.label: You may lower yourself into the sinkhole and investigate.
You may follow the cart tracks backward to find someone who knows this land.

entries.body: The passage below is not what you expected: it is worked stone, old enough to predate any lord's holding, leading in a direction that should be under the river. The cart is here, the horse standing patiently in the dark, the carter nowhere visible.

resolutions.label: follow the passage to its end

success.body:
The passage ends in a chamber with a door and the carter sitting before it in a state of motionless wonder, unable to explain why he stopped. You lead him back; the wonder departs as he reaches the surface.

Whatever is behind the door remains behind the door.

failure.body:
The passage fills with water — an underground spring disturbed by the sinkhole's opening — and you must retreat quickly with nothing but wet boots.

The carter surfaces separately through a different way you did not find.

entries.body: The local farming family who works this land has seen the sinkhole appear before — thirty years ago, the same place, the same warmth in the air. The grandmother remembers it. She says the hole closes by itself in three days and nothing that goes in comes to harm, but those who go in bring something back.

resolutions.label: learn what was brought back before and act on that knowledge

success.body: The previous visitor brought back a changed quality of attention — he could find water in dry ground after, the grandmother says, by feel. Armed with this, you go in without fear, find the carter, and bring him back. You cannot tell what you have brought back yet. You will know in time.

failure.body:
The grandmother's account is useful but not specific enough — the pattern differs this time in some way you cannot account for. You act on incomplete information and it is not quite the right action.

The knowledge was partial. Partial knowledge is still better than none.

entries.body:
The swamp has a cave at its margin — visible only because you are on horseback and can see above the reed-line. Something is in it that is not an animal: a light, small and unsteady. A person is in that cave, and the only path to the cave involves crossing forty yards of ground that is not reliably solid.

The swamp has closed behind you. You know the general direction of safety, but not the specific path.

responses.label: You may cross to the cave directly, reading the ground as you go.
You may make your way around the swamp's edge to approach the cave differently.

entries.body: Forty yards of uncertain ground, each step tested before weight is committed. The light in the cave has not moved. Either the person is unconscious or they have learned the wisdom of staying still.

resolutions.label: cross the marsh ground to the cave

success.body:
You read the ground correctly — the darker patches are sound, the lighter ones false — and you reach the cave. An old woman is inside, unhurt, a torch burning from a piece of her clothing. She has been there since yesterday.

Getting her back is the easier problem.

failure.body:
You go in to your knee at the third step and must extract yourself and begin again. The sound ground is not where you read it. Try a different path.

The swamp knows more than you do. Listen to it.

entries.body: Around the swamp's edge is a longer route, but the ground is firmer and the angle of approach lets you see more of the cave's interior. The light inside has a familiar quality — a person who knows how to make a fire last.

resolutions.label: make the longer crossing and reach the cave safely

success.body:
The longer route takes an hour, but you arrive dry and she is still there, still burning what remains of her torch. She is a midwife from a village to the north, caught by the swamp at dusk.

You take the same long way back. It is as safe the second time.

failure.body:
The longer route has its own uncertainties, and you lose an hour to a dead end before finding the correct line. The woman's torch is nearly out when you arrive.

You made it. That is the whole of what matters now.

entries.body:
The sea cave is accessible for two hours at low tide, and the low tide is now. Inside, fishermen from the village say, there is wreckage from a ship that came apart on the rocks two seasons past, and within the wreckage there may still be goods worth recovering — and perhaps a survivor who found shelter there and was never found.

The tide waits for no recovery.

responses.label: You may enter the cave and investigate the wreckage.
You may speak first with the fishermen about what the wreckage contains.

entries.body: The cave smells of salt and old wood, and the wreckage is deeper in than the entrance suggests. The sound of the sea is louder here, carried through passages in the rock, a constant reminder of what the tide will do when it returns.

resolutions.label: search the wreckage before the tide returns

success.body:
The wreckage yields: a sealed chest of modest value and, on a ledge above the waterline, a man who has been living on shellfish for two seasons and is very thin and very grateful.

You get both out with twenty minutes to spare.

failure.body:
The cave is larger than the time allows, and the tide comes back faster than the fishermen warned. You get out with the chest but not the man — if he is there, he is where you could not reach him in the time.

The chest you brought back is something. It is not enough.

entries.body: The fishermen know the wreck well — they have made their own recoveries on previous tides. They can tell you where the waterline is, which passages are dead ends, and where the remaining goods are likely to be. They cannot tell you about the survivor because they do not believe there is one.

resolutions.label: enter with the fishermen's knowledge and use the time correctly

success.body:
Armed with the layout, you move efficiently — the dead ends skipped, the likely locations checked in order. The survivor is where a man who knew his tides would go: high, dry, and visible from the cave mouth to anyone who looks at the right angle.

You were the first to look at the right angle.

failure.body:
The fishermen's knowledge is good for goods but not for people — they did not look for the human signs, and neither does your search, following their pattern. You come out with goods but without the person.

Some searching requires a different kind of eye.

entries.body:
The collapse beneath the city has revealed something older than the city: a passage lined with symbols that are not Roman and not Christian, carved when the land was named differently. A priest who entered to assess it has not come back.

The symbols, those who saw them say, have a quality of the old religion — not evil, but demanding of a particular respect that the priest may not have been equipped to offer.

responses.label: You may enter the passage with full awareness of its spiritual significance.
You may find someone who knows the old symbols before you go in.

entries.body: The passage receives you with the gravity of something that has been waiting. The symbols on the walls are not a warning and not a welcome — they are a condition. You are in a place that asks something of its visitors.

resolutions.label: proceed with full reverence for what the passage asks

success.body:
The passage accepts you. The priest is at its end, kneeling before something he cannot name and cannot leave, held not by force but by wonder. You take his hand and lead him back, speaking quietly of ordinary things.

The passage releases him when he has someone to return to. It did not release him when he was alone.

failure.body:
The reverence you bring is of the wrong kind — not insufficient, but shaped by a tradition the passage does not recognise. You pass through it, but it does not receive you. The priest is not where you expected.

This requires a different understanding.

entries.body: There is, in the quarter where the old families live, a woman who maintains the old knowledge alongside the new. She does not advertise this. She receives you with the careful attention of someone deciding whether your interest is genuine.

resolutions.label: earn her trust and receive what she knows

success.body:
She tells you what the symbols mean and what they ask: acknowledgment rather than worship, the willingness to enter without claiming the place as conquered. You go back with this understanding, and the passage opens its logic to you.

The priest is found. He will not say what he saw.

failure.body:
She does not find you trustworthy enough for the full knowledge and gives you only what is safe to give a stranger. It is not enough to navigate the passage.

The trust must be earned before the knowledge.

entries.body:
A hermit at the forest's edge directs you to a cave half a mile in, speaking of it as a place of testing rather than danger — a distinction he makes carefully, as though the difference matters. The Grail questors who have passed this wood have been advised to enter; several have not come back.

He does not say what the cave tests. He says: you will know when you are in it.

responses.label: You may enter the cave prepared to meet its test.
You may ask the hermit more before you decide.

entries.body: The cave tests you by showing you something. What it shows is specific to you and cannot be described in general terms. What you do with what you are shown is the test.

resolutions.label: face what the cave shows you without looking away

success.body:
You look at it fully. It is not comfortable and it is not quick, and when the cave is done with you it releases you into the ordinary air of the forest with a quality of exhausted clarity.

The hermit looks at you when you return. He says: 'Good.' Nothing else.

failure.body:
You look away. Not from cowardice — from the reasonable response to seeing something that ought not be seen. The cave releases you, having registered the refusal without judgment.

The hermit says only: 'Perhaps another time.'

entries.body: He will tell you one thing more: the cave shows each person what they most need to see in order to be worthy of the Grail, and it is not always what they expected to need. He adds that the questors who did not return chose to stay, which is also a possible outcome.

resolutions.label: weigh this fully and decide whether to enter

success.body:
The weighing produces a clear decision: you enter knowing it may change you, and that the changing is the purpose. The cave receives you. What follows is yours.

You return changed, as expected. The change is useful.

failure.body:
The weighing produces uncertainty rather than clarity, and you enter uncertain, which is not the right state. The cave shows you something, but the showing is blurred by the state you brought to it.

Clarity is a precondition for some kinds of seeing.

entries.body:
The mountain cave is in the vision given to a Grail questor — she described it precisely, the crack in the left-hand wall, the sound of water within, the quality of the light at the entrance at noon. She could not enter it herself because her quest led another way. She gave you the description.

The cave is exactly as she described. Whatever is inside is waiting for someone.

responses.label: You may enter in the questor's name and bring back what is there.
You may enter and let what is there speak for itself.

entries.body: Entering in someone else's name is a kind of agency: you go as their representative, which changes what is inside. The cave is not indifferent to whose name you carry.

resolutions.label: carry the questor's name faithfully into the cave and receive what is there for her

success.body:
The cave receives the name. What is inside — a sealed vessel, a wound stone, a piece of light that holds its shape — is given to you for her, and you receive it as her agent.

The transfer will require finding her. That is another quest.

failure.body:
The cave does not recognise the proxy arrangement — or it does and finds the proxy insufficient for what it holds. You leave with nothing but the experience of having been in a place that was waiting for someone else.

Some things can only be received by the person they are meant for.

entries.body: Entering without a name — simply as yourself, without proxy — is another kind of offering. What is inside may receive you or it may not. The cave does not announce its preferences.

resolutions.label: enter as yourself and let what is there decide

success.body:
What is inside receives you — not as the questor's proxy but as yourself, which apparently it is also prepared to do. What you are given is not what was meant for her but something that was waiting for a person of your particular quality.

You had not known you were the person.

failure.body:
The cave was waiting for a specific person, and it turns out you are not that person, or not yet. You leave with your integrity intact and the cave's patience undiminished.

Some places hold what they hold for longer than a lifetime.

entries.body:
A passage has opened in the plains earth — not a sinkhole, but something deliberate-seeming: the edges are smooth, the descent regular. A group of local people stands around it refusing to go near. An old man at the edge says it opened this morning and something came out of it and went, and the something left no footprints.

The passage goes down into a darkness that does not feel like ordinary underground.

responses.label: You may descend into the passage.
You may speak with the old man about what he saw before you decide.

entries.body: The descent is real enough — stone, earth, root — but the light below is wrong. Not dark: there is light, sourceless and steady, and it has a quality that the surface light does not.

resolutions.label: descend into the light and find what is below

success.body:
Below is a place that is not quite this world and not quite another: a chamber where something rests that was placed here before living memory, something whose purpose is not finished. You touch it. The purpose you receive will become clear in time.

You ascend. The passage is sealed behind you.

failure.body:
The light and the chamber receive you, but you are not yet what they require, and you return to the surface having been given only the evidence of something significant without the content of it.

The passage remains. Others may find it.

entries.body: The old man saw: a shape, human in form but not quite in proportion, that emerged before dawn and went north-west without hurry. It left nothing behind. He does not say what he believes it was. He says what he saw.

resolutions.label: use his description to understand the passage before entering

success.body:
The shape's direction and the passage's location together suggest a specific relationship to the old landscape — a thing that goes out from here and returns, regularly, on a cycle you cannot calculate from this single data point.

You descend prepared for that possibility. Preparedness helps.

failure.body:
The old man's description is too strange to usefully interpret without a framework you do not have. You descend without the understanding you sought.

Knowledge without interpretation is still data. You carry it forward.

entries.body:
The swamp cave is accessible — just. The water level is lower than it should be, which the local people find alarming rather than convenient. Something has diverted the water, or absorbed it, and the cave that was previously flooded is now open.

A questor passed through three weeks ago and said the cave contained a question. He came out unable to say what the question was, only that he could not answer it.

responses.label: You may enter the cave and face the question.
You may find the questor and learn what he could not say.

entries.body: The cave is dry underfoot and the walls are crystalline — mineral formations that hold the light in unexpected ways. The question arrives not as a voice but as a state: you know, when you are inside, that a question is being asked of you.

resolutions.label: answer the question the cave is asking

success.body:
You answer it. Not verbally — there is no receiving mechanism for words — but in the way that you exist in the space, what you bring of yourself to the asking. The cave accepts the answer.

What you receive is permission — for something you had not known you needed permission to do.

failure.body:
The question is there and you cannot find the answer in yourself — not because the answer does not exist, but because the question touches something you are not yet ready to address.

The cave waits. It is patient. Come back when you are ready.

entries.body: The questor is at a monastery a day east, where he has been since he emerged from the cave. He is not ill and not mad. He is thinking, which the monks have accommodated with unusual grace.

resolutions.label: speak with him and understand what the cave asked

success.body:
He can say it now, a week's distance having given the question a shape he can speak. The question the cave asked was: what do you serve that you have not named? He could not answer because he had not examined the naming.

You carry this back to the cave, better prepared.

failure.body:
He is not yet ready to say it, and your visit disturbs the thinking rather than assists it. You leave knowing the question exists but not its content.

Preparation sometimes requires other people's time as well as your own.

entries.body:
The sea cave is known in the local stories as a place where the Fisher King's influence reaches — where the land's wound and the sea's healing are closest together. A boat has been left at its mouth for any knight who comes on the Grail quest.

The boat is there. The tide is going out. The cave's interior is visible and the interior is changing as the light changes: showing different things at different angles, none of them exactly what they appear.

responses.label: You may enter the cave in the boat and follow where it leads.
You may watch the cave from outside and learn its patterns before entering.

entries.body: The boat enters the cave on its own current — you have only to keep it steady. Within, the cave walls show scenes: things that have happened, things that are happening elsewhere, and things that will happen if specific choices are made. Which is which is not labelled.

resolutions.label: read what the cave shows and receive it completely

success.body:
You read it correctly — or correctly enough. The vision of what will happen if a specific choice is made is the one meant for you, and you carry it forward with enough clarity to act on it.

The boat returns you to the cave mouth. The vision remains.

failure.body:
The three registers of showing are not as separable as you needed them to be, and what you carry back is a mixture that will take time to sort. The clarity required was greater than what you could bring.

Visions require a quality of stillness that is earned, not assumed.

entries.body: The patterns visible from outside tell you something: the cave shows different things at different tidal states. At the lowest ebb, the innermost chamber is visible for perhaps thirty minutes before the water returns. That chamber shows only one thing, which is the same at every tide.

resolutions.label: enter at the lowest ebb and see the thing the innermost chamber shows

success.body:
The innermost chamber shows the Grail — not as vision, but as fact: a vessel of light that is present and real and does not come closer when you approach. You look at it for the full thirty minutes.

What you learn from looking is not what the Grail is, but what you are in relation to it.

failure.body:
The ebb is shorter than you calculated, and the water returns before you reach the innermost chamber. You see it from a distance and must retreat.

The glimpse was real. It was not the full thing.

entries.body:
Beneath the city's older quarter, people are sheltering. Not from a battle — from the aftermath: soldiers loyal to Mordred are conscripting in the streets above, and those who do not wish to serve, or cannot, have gone below. A passage leads to a network of cellars where perhaps forty people are hiding.

They have food for three days. After that, the choice becomes starve or surface.

responses.label: You may go below and help them plan a safe exit.
You may negotiate with the conscription officers above before going down.

entries.body: Below, the people are quiet and frightened in the particular way of those who have made a decision and are waiting for the consequences. The eldest among them has mapped the other passages. She believes there is a way east that does not surface until outside the conscription zone.

resolutions.label: scout the eastern passage and confirm the route

success.body:
The eastern passage is real and passable, surfacing in a tanner's yard east of the conscription zone. You move the forty people through in three groups over six hours. No one is taken.

The tanner asks no questions. He makes room.

failure.body:
The eastern passage is flooded — a recent development, probably a burst water-pipe from the fighting above. The route is impassable.

The alternative must be found. You have one day.

entries.body: The conscription officers are not monsters — they are men doing an unpopular job under orders from a lord they may or may not believe in. There is a space in that situation for negotiation, if it is approached correctly.

resolutions.label: negotiate exemptions for those below before they need to be used

success.body:
You secure exemptions for the women, the old, and the young — which is most of the forty. The remaining men make their own decision with that information in hand, and most surface on their own terms rather than as conscripts.

The officers' commander does not look pleased, but he has his quota elsewhere.

failure.body:
The officers have orders that do not accommodate negotiation at this level. The exemptions are not available.

The underground route remains the only option.

entries.body:
The forest cave is occupied by men who were once soldiers — you can see it in the way they hold themselves, the discipline of the camp they have made inside. They left whichever side they were on when the orders became ones they could not follow, and now they are here, dangerous to travellers not because they choose to be but because they are hungry and afraid.

They have not attacked you. They are watching you from the cave mouth, deciding.

responses.label: You may approach them as a knight and hear what happened to them.
You may offer them food before you say anything else.

entries.body: They hear you out with the wariness of men who have been betrayed by authority before. Their story is not simple: they served one lord, their lord changed sides, the new orders were ones that required them to harm people they had been protecting. They left.

resolutions.label: help them find a way back from the edge they are on

success.body:
You know of a lord who is accepting men of integrity regardless of their previous service, one who is holding a line that is about something worth holding. You describe him. Three of the men leave the cave that day.

The others follow over the next week, in ones and twos.

failure.body:
The line you draw between what they did and what they might do does not land — their distrust of authority, any authority, is too well-founded to be addressed by a stranger with a good name.

The conversation was worth having. Trust is slower to rebuild than it was to break.

entries.body: Hunger is the first thing. Before loyalty, before strategy, before anything else: food. If you have provisions, offering them is the gesture that resets the dynamic.

resolutions.label: give them food and let the giving speak first

success.body:
You give what you have. They take it without hostility and without ceremony, which is its own form of acceptance. After they eat, the watching-quality departs and something else replaces it: the posture of men who have been reminded they are still human.

The conversation after the food is entirely different from what it would have been before.

failure.body:
What you have is not enough — you have eaten today, and the remainder is insufficient for twelve men. The gesture is visible but insufficient. It is received as what it is: partial.

Partial gestures are still gestures.

entries.body:
The mountain cave is serving as a field hospital and refuge for survivors of a battle two days past — a skirmish that went wrong, the details varying by who is telling it. Perhaps forty wounded men are here, tended by two surviving physic-men and a nun who was caught by the fighting on her way to an abbey.

The cave is too cold, the supplies are nearly gone, and the path down the mountain is watched by hostile forces.

responses.label: You may try to break through the forces watching the path.
You may find another route down the mountain for the wounded.

entries.body: The force watching the path is small — six men, posted to prevent exactly this scenario. They are cold and bored and the boredom has made them careless. A distraction from one direction while the wounded move from another might work.

resolutions.label: create the distraction and cover the wounded's descent

success.body:
The distraction works. The six men come to investigate you, and the wounded make the path while the investigation is ongoing. You disengage before it becomes a fight you cannot afford.

The last group of wounded are through before the force realises what happened.

failure.body:
The distraction is not sufficient — the force splits, some coming for you and two remaining on the path. The wounded cannot descend while two armed men watch the path.

A different approach is needed.

entries.body: The nun knows the mountain. She was raised in a village on the other face and she remembers a shepherd's route that comes down the south side, emerging well below the force's position. It has not been used in twenty years.

resolutions.label: use the nun's knowledge to bring the wounded down the shepherd's route

success.body:
The route is hard going for the wounded, but it is there and it is clear. You take it in stages through the day, and by evening the worst cases are down and in shelter at the village below.

The nun leads the prayers that evening. You do not object.

failure.body:
The route has changed in twenty years — a rockfall blocks the key section, and you cannot get the wounded through without equipment you do not have.

The path through the watchers remains. You return with that information.

entries.body:
The plains below a contested ridge have an old network of underground passages — pre-Roman, used by the local people to move livestock in dangerous times, now repurposed. A scout who went in carrying a vital message for Arthur's remaining forces has not emerged.

Both sides know about the network. The scout's message cannot wait.

responses.label: You may enter the network and find the scout.
You may carry the message yourself if you can reconstruct it from those who sent the scout.

entries.body: The network is dark and branching, and sound carries strangely — you can hear men moving in parallel passages without being able to locate them precisely. The scout went in two hours past. The message was simple, which means he should have been through in thirty minutes.

resolutions.label: find the scout in the dark network

success.body:
The scout is trapped — not by enemy forces but by a cave-in in the central passage, unhurt but stuck. You find the alternate route the cave-in did not block and lead him out. The message is delivered.

The message was worth the finding.

failure.body:
The network is larger than the entrance suggested, and you cannot find him — he has either gone further in or emerged from another exit. You return without him.

The message must go another way.

entries.body: The people who sent the scout know what the message contains. If the message is simple enough, reconstructing it and carrying it yourself bypasses the problem of the missing scout entirely.

resolutions.label: carry the message yourself through the contested ground

success.body:
You go above ground — faster, more dangerous, but direct. You reach Arthur's forces before the information becomes useless. The scout emerges from a different exit three hours later, having found an alternate path.

Two messengers, two routes. One message delivered.

failure.body:
The contested ground claims more time than you had. The message arrives after the moment it was needed for has passed.

Timeliness is itself a form of truth.

entries.body:
The swamp has a cave known to the locals as the Wader's Rest — a high-ground pocket within the swamp that is always dry, always cool, and smells of old rushes rather than marsh. It is being used by soldiers wounded at a river crossing three days past. The battle moved on. They were left.

There are eight of them, from both sides of the current war, sharing the cave with the agreement of men who are past the point where loyalty matters more than survival.

responses.label: You may bring what help you can to the cave.
You may go to the nearest settlement and organise proper aid.

entries.body: What you can bring immediately: whatever medicines you carry, your knowledge, your hands. What the eight need: more than that. But more than nothing is the thing that changes the calculation.

resolutions.label: use what you have to stabilise the worst cases

success.body:
You work through the night. Three cases were dire; one dies despite your effort; two are stable before dawn. The remaining five are walking wounded who have been stabilising themselves.

You have done what was possible. One man is past what was possible.

failure.body:
The worst cases are beyond what your knowledge can address, and the effort costs more than it produces. You do what you can, which is less than what is needed.

You stayed. That was not nothing.
