entries.body: The nearest settlement is two hours through the swamp. They have a healer, or someone who serves that function, and a cart, and the willingness to help if the help is organised correctly — which means explaining the situation without alarming anyone into inaction.

resolutions.label: bring aid from the settlement to the cave

success.body:
The healer comes and the cart comes and by evening the eight men are in the settlement rather than the swamp. Three will recover fully. Two will not. Three are uncertain.

The healer does not ask which side they were on. Neither do you.

failure.body:
The settlement is unwilling to commit resources to soldiers on an unclear side — they have their own losses from the fighting and their own fears. You bring back what you can persuade them to part with, which is not enough.

Fear makes cowards of communities as well as individuals.

entries.body:
The sea cave is hiding a boat. Not hidden from the weather — from the war. A lord who refused to swear for Mordred has loaded a small vessel with his household's most important documents, records, and a few irreplaceable objects, and concealed it in the cave while he determines whether there is anywhere safe to send it.

His people are exposed. He needs the boat moved now, before Mordred's coastal patrols find it, and he needs a knight willing to captain a night crossing.

responses.label: You may captain the boat across to safe harbour.
You may find a more experienced sailor to captain while you provide escort.

entries.body: The crossing at night, in a small boat, with a cargo that must not be lost: this is a specific kind of difficulty that is about endurance and attention rather than skill with water.

resolutions.label: captain the night crossing and deliver the cargo safely

success.body:
The crossing takes four hours and the darkness is its own navigation challenge, but you read the stars and the current and arrive at the harbour before dawn. The cargo is intact. The documents and the objects will outlast the war.

The lord receives word three days later. He sends thanks by a route that cannot be intercepted.

failure.body:
The crossing goes wrong — a current, a patrol closer than expected, a miscalculation of the tide. You arrive at the wrong harbour with a damp but intact cargo and begin the work of finding an overland route instead.

The boat is lost. The cargo is not.

entries.body: The local fishing village has a woman who knows these waters in the dark better than anyone. She is willing, for a price, to captain the crossing. The price is reasonable and the service is real.

resolutions.label: arrange the crossing with the fisherwoman and provide the escort

success.body:
The negotiation is clean and the crossing is clean. She knows the patrol routes as well as the currents, and the cargo arrives safely in a port that is not technically controlled by either side.

You provided the sword arm and the silver. She provided the knowledge. Between you, it was enough.

failure.body:
The negotiation hits an unexpected obstacle — her price, or her conditions, or a dispute about the destination — and the arrangement falls through with the tide already turning.

Time was the casualty. Find another way before dawn.

entries.body:
A gate stands in the middle of a city street where no gate was this morning — the neighbours confirm it, unsettled and curious in equal measure. The gate is old and fine, more fine than anything in this district, and beyond it there is a courtyard that is larger than the building surrounding it should allow.

Smoke rises from within. Someone, or something, is home.

responses.label: You may knock at the gate and wait for it to be opened.
You may climb the wall and look before knocking.

entries.body: The gate is answered. The person who answers it is neither what you expected nor what you could have anticipated: a steward of such antiquated courtesy that the courtesy itself is a language you must read carefully. He invites you in as though the invitation has been long-standing.

resolutions.label: enter and engage with the castle on its own terms

success.body:
The castle's terms, when you understand them, are fair: it shows you a thing you needed to see, accepts a thing you had been carrying, and sends you back to the street with both a gift and a changed perspective.

The gate is not there an hour later.

failure.body:
The terms are subtler than you read them, and you accept something you should not have in exchange for something you needed. The bargain is technically fair. It does not feel fair.

The castle's courtesy is a kind of weapon for those who do not read it correctly.

entries.body: The wall is not high but the climbing of it produces a view that does not correspond to the wall's height: you can see into the courtyard as though from a great height, and the courtyard contains something you cannot describe precisely — only that it is there.

resolutions.label: read what the courtyard contains from this elevated vantage

success.body:
The something in the courtyard is, when you have looked long enough, recognisable: it is the quality of a question that has been waiting a very long time for the right person to ask it. You climb down and knock.

The gate opens. You ask. It is answered.

failure.body:
The view from the wall shows something, but the something requires a frame of reference you do not have. You climb down with the image but not its meaning.

Meaning often comes later, if you carry the image faithfully.

entries.body:
The castle is in the forest where no castle was on any account. It is old — the stonework has weathered into the hillside — and it is lit: candles in every window, and the sound of music from within, music played by instruments you cannot identify from outside.

A knight in arms stands at the gate, not hostile, watching you approach with an interest that is entirely calm.

responses.label: You may challenge the knight as custom requires.
You may simply ask him what this place is.

entries.body: He accepts the challenge with a gravity that suggests challenges here are always accepted and always serious. He is skilled — the way of this kind of knight always is — and the bout will tell you something about the castle before you enter it.

resolutions.label: joust with the castle's guardian knight

success.body:
You unseat him. He rises, removes his helm, and nods. 'Enter,' he says. Inside, the castle receives you as a guest of honour, and the evening is one of the more remarkable of your life: the music, the food, the conversation — all of it bearing a quality of the otherworldly that is difficult to account for afterward.

You leave in the morning. The castle is gone.

failure.body:
He unseats you, cleanly. He replaces his helm and returns to his post. The gate is closed.

The castle was available to someone of greater martial quality than yourself today. Come back when you are better.

entries.body: He considers your question with the seriousness it apparently deserves. He says: it is a place that appears when there is someone who needs to enter it. He is the one who determines whether you are that person. He asks you a question in return.

resolutions.label: answer his question honestly and completely

success.body: Your answer satisfies whatever criterion the question was measuring. He steps aside. The interior of the castle is what you needed it to be — which is different for every person, and impossible to describe to another — and you return to the forest changed in a way you cannot specify for a week.

failure.body:
Your answer is honest but incomplete — you withheld something not from deception but from the habit of withholding, and the knight knows the difference. The gate remains closed.

The castle will be where you need it to be when you have learned to answer fully.

entries.body:
The mountain keep was not on your map because it cannot be on any map: it appears with the fog and the fog is its castle walls, and when the fog lifts you see that the keep is real and the walls have windows.

A light burns in the highest window. From this distance, you cannot tell if it is moving.

responses.label: You may approach the keep and ask for shelter.
You may watch the keep from a distance until you understand it better.

entries.body: The approach is steep and the fog reduces as you climb — paradoxically, the castle becomes more real as you near it, not less. The gate is open. There is food set on a table in the main hall, and no one visible.

resolutions.label: accept the hospitality and see who joins you

success.body:
Someone joins you: a figure who is neither clearly alive nor clearly otherwise, who speaks of the mountain and its history in a way that suggests they have seen it all. What they tell you has practical value — the passes, the old routes, the places to avoid. The knowledge is gift enough.

In the morning, the keep is mist.

failure.body:
No one comes. The food is cold. The hall fills with the quality of a place that expected someone other than you, and the expectation is palpable and uncomfortable.

You sleep by the fire. In the morning, the keep is mist and you are on the mountainside.

entries.body: Watching from distance, you notice: the light in the window moves on a pattern — not randomly, but with the rhythm of something being communicated. The pattern repeats three times before the fog begins to come back.

resolutions.label: read the pattern and respond to it

success.body:
The pattern is an invitation, specifically — a signal in the old language of fires and lights used by mountain communities before roads made language unnecessary. You respond in kind, using your own torch.

The keep's gate opens. You enter.

failure.body:
The pattern is not one you can read — it belongs to a knowledge you do not have. The fog returns. The keep is gone.

Some invitations are in languages that must be learned.

entries.body:
The castle stands on the plain where all accounts say there is no castle — and all accounts are recent enough that the discrepancy is alarming rather than simply curious. The castle looks inhabited and inhabited recently. Banners fly that are not any house's banners, bearing devices of the old type.

The gates are open. The drawbridge is down. It has the look of a place expecting visitors.

responses.label: You may ride in as the open gates invite.
You may circle the castle before entering to understand its situation.

entries.body: The courtyard is occupied: a household of extraordinary courtesy, all dressed in the fashion of a hundred years past, going about the business of a castle with quiet efficiency. They receive you as though you were expected, which you may well have been.

resolutions.label: engage with the household and understand what this place is

success.body:
The household's lord receives you in the great hall and tells you, with perfect directness, that this castle exists in a different relation to time than you do — that it appears on the plain when it is needed, which it currently is, because of you. He gives you something that has been kept for you here since before your birth.

The something is small and exactly right.

failure.body:
The household's courtesy is impenetrable — perfectly hospitable, perfectly uninformative. You eat, you sleep, you leave in the morning having been treated beautifully and learned nothing.

Some hospitality is its own conclusion.

entries.body: Circling the castle reveals: no tracks around the foundation, no evidence of construction or recent occupation, and a shadow that falls at the wrong angle for the sun's position. Also: a small postern gate at the back, slightly ajar, through which you can hear someone weeping.

resolutions.label: enter through the postern and find who is weeping

success.body:
Inside, you find the castle's secret: a person who has been here without means of leaving, kept not by force but by a condition they cannot break alone. What they need is a witness — someone from outside the castle to confirm that they exist, in this age, and can choose to leave.

You confirm it. They leave.

failure.body:
The postern leads into the castle's logic before you find the person — you become briefly lost in the castle's impossible geography and emerge back at the front gate having gone in a circle.

The postern route requires something you did not bring.

entries.body:
Rising from the swamp on what appears to be solid ground is a castle that should not be here: too large for any minor lord, too well-maintained for abandonment, and too impossible to reach without crossing ground that will not hold a horse. You have left your horse at the swamp's edge.

The castle's single door is open. A light burns within.

responses.label: You may cross to the castle on foot.
You may call to the castle from the swamp's edge and wait for a response.

entries.body: Crossing on foot requires reading the ground precisely — each step tested, the weight committed slowly, the pattern of safe ground followed through the unreliable marsh. The castle does not get closer as fast as your pace suggests it should.

resolutions.label: cross the swamp and reach the castle door

success.body:
You reach it. Inside, the castle is warm and dry and contains a library of extraordinary breadth — texts you have no name for, in languages that are nonetheless readable if you let your eye move naturally across the page. You spend two hours there.

The crossing back is easier. The castle is gone when you look over your shoulder.

failure.body:
You go in to your waist before accepting that this particular path does not reach the castle. You extract yourself and return to your horse, cold and no closer.

The castle's ground has its own rules.

entries.body: Your call crosses the swamp and something hears it: a figure appears at an upper window, looks at you for a long moment, and makes a gesture you cannot read at this distance. Then they are gone. A minute later, a path appears across the swamp — stones rising to the surface, one by one, from the water's edge to the door.

resolutions.label: follow the risen path to the castle

success.body:
The path holds your weight and the door receives you and what is inside is exactly what you needed — not what you wanted, which is different. You return across the path before it sinks again.

The castle does not disappear. It simply becomes impossible to find.

failure.body:
The path begins to sink before you cross half the distance, and you retreat before the swamp reclaims it. Whatever the figure saw in you, it was not sufficient for the full crossing.

The call was heard. The answer was not yes.

entries.body:
The sea cliff has a castle carved into it — or growing from it — that the fishermen say appears with certain tides and is gone with others. This tide it is here: full-formed, flags moving in the sea wind, a staircase cut in the cliff face leading up to its gate.

You have until the tide turns.

responses.label: You may climb to the gate before the tide changes.
You may speak to the fishermen about what the castle has contained on previous visits.

entries.body: The stair is cut for someone of your size and takes ten minutes to climb. The gate is open. The castle is real — solid stone, cold salt air, every detail correct — and it is empty, except for a single room where something has been placed on a table.

resolutions.label: take what is on the table and descend before the tide

success.body:
You make it back down the stair with minutes to spare and the thing from the table in your hands. When you look back, the castle is gone, and the cliff face is unmarked.

The thing from the table is real. What it is for will become clear.

failure.body:
You reach the table but the descent takes longer than the climb, and the stair is covered before you are fully off it. You make it to the base wet and shaken. The thing from the table is in your hands but the tide took your boots.

The castle was worth it. You can replace the boots.

entries.body: The fishermen have stories — three generations' worth. The castle contains different things on different tides: once a feast, once a room full of weapons, once a library, once a man who would not say where he had been or how long he had been there. Each visitor brought away something specific to them.

resolutions.label: climb knowing what to look for and how to use the time

success.body:
Armed with the pattern of what the castle provides, you know to look for the thing that is specific to you rather than everything. You find it immediately — it is the most obvious object in the room, for someone who knows to look.

You are down and dry before the tide.

failure.body:
The pattern the fishermen described does not apply this tide — the castle contains something they have not encountered before, and their framework does not help you read it.

You bring back something anyway. Whether it is the right something, you cannot yet say.

entries.body:
A castle within the city that no one remembers building has acquired a reputation among Grail questors as a place of temptation — not of the obvious kind, but the subtle kind, the kind that offers the questor exactly what they want rather than what they need. Three questors have entered. Two left when they realised what the castle was offering. One has not come out.

The missing questor went in a week ago. His companions are outside, trying to decide what to do.

responses.label: You may enter to find the missing questor.
You may speak with his companions to understand what he is likely to have been offered.

entries.body:
Inside, the castle offers you something immediately. The offering is personalised — it addresses a specific lack in your life, with a specificity that suggests the castle knows you.

This is how it works.

resolutions.label: refuse the offering and navigate to the questor

success.body:
The refusal is the key. With the offering declined, the castle's logic becomes navigable — it offers nothing further and allows you to move through it freely. The questor is in the deepest room, sitting with the thing that was offered to him, unable to refuse it.

You help him find the refusal. He takes it. You both leave.

failure.body:
The offering is more precisely targeted than you expected, and the refusal takes longer than it should — you linger at the edge of acceptance for a moment too long. The castle uses that moment to complicate your navigation.

You find the questor. You do not come out immediately.

entries.body: The companions know the missing questor's weakness — they have been companions long enough. His is the longing for a settled life, for a hall and a wife and children and an ordinary future. The castle will have shown him that life.

resolutions.label: enter knowing what he was offered and find the argument that reaches him

success.body:
You find him in the castle's vision of his desired future, happy in a way he was not before he entered. You speak to him not of duty — that argument has failed — but of what the vision is and is not: a real life, or a beautiful lie.

He chooses. He comes out.

failure.body:
The argument you find does not reach him — the vision is too precisely what he wanted, and the true life outside the castle too uncertain to compete. He stays.

You report to his companions. They go in next.

entries.body:
The forest hermit directed you here: a castle in the deep wood that holds the question you need to be asked, not the question you have been asking. He was specific about the direction and specific about the warning — you must not ask the castle anything, only receive what it asks of you.

The castle is exactly where he said. It appears to be waiting.

responses.label: You may enter according to the hermit's direction — receiving, not asking.
You may test the hermit's warning by approaching with your own question ready.

entries.body: The castle's interior rearranges itself, very slightly, as you pass through the gate — rooms you can see from one angle are gone from another, as though the castle is orienting itself to you specifically. You do not ask it anything. You wait.

resolutions.label: wait in the castle for the question it will ask

success.body:
The question comes: not as a voice but as a situation presented — a scene in one of the rooms that asks something specific of you by requiring a choice. You make the choice truly. The castle receives it.

What you receive in return is a clarity about something that had been obscured for a long time.

failure.body:
You wait, but the waiting becomes restless — you begin to look, to explore, to approach the castle's rooms with the wrong kind of attention — and the question, which was forming, withdraws.

The receiving posture is harder than the asking posture. Practice it.

entries.body: You test the warning by bringing your question to the gate. The castle admits you. Your question shapes everything that follows — the rooms it shows you are answers to your question rather than the question it would have asked. The result is useful but limited.

resolutions.label: work within the limits of what your question produced

success.body:
The answers to your question are real and you use them correctly. The hermit's warning was right — you received less than you would have — but what you received was what you were able to receive at this stage.

Perhaps the castle's question will be available to you later, when you are ready for it.

failure.body:
The answers to your question are not what you thought they would be, and you cannot use them as intended. The castle gives you what your question deserved.

Better questions yield better answers.

entries.body:
The castle appeared in a vision given to the Grail questors: a mountain castle whose lord holds one of the sacred questions — the specific question that, if asked at the right moment, advances the Grail's healing. The lord does not know he holds it. The questors must find the moment.

The castle is real and the lord is real, an ordinary lord of the old type, puzzled to be receiving strange visitors.

responses.label: You may speak with the lord and search for the moment when the question might be asked.
You may seek the castle's chapel, where the visions have suggested the question will be visible.

entries.body: The lord is welcoming and unremarkable, a man who has managed his mountain lands competently for thirty years. Something in him carries the question without knowing it — it shows in certain pauses, certain topics that produce a quality of arrest in his face.

resolutions.label: find the moment the lord's question surfaces and help him ask it

success.body:
The pause comes, and the topic behind it is one you have guided toward through careful conversation. In the pause, you ask on his behalf the question he has never been able to form: 'Whom does the Grail serve?'

He does not know he has said anything significant. The question has been asked.

failure.body:
The moment comes but you press too directly and it closes. The lord gives you his hospitality and his puzzlement, and the question remains unasked.

The guidance must be more subtle.

entries.body: The chapel is old — older than the castle, the chaplain says, the castle built around it rather than the chapel within the castle. The walls are painted with figures you do not recognise, doing things that are not quite what they appear to be doing.

resolutions.label: read the chapel's paintings until the question becomes visible

success.body:
The question is in the central panel, encoded in the figures' posture: 'What ails thee?' — not as a question about the painting, but as the question to be asked of the wounded king. You carry it forward.

The paintings remain. The question is now in you to be used.

failure.body:
The paintings resist your reading — they require a visual language you have not been taught. You carry away the image but not the meaning.

The chapel waits. Come back with the right preparation.

entries.body:
The plains castle is the one the questors have been warned about: the false Grail castle, beautiful and welcoming, run by an enchanter who has created a simulacrum of Corbenic to test the questors' discernment. Those who cannot tell the false from the true lose a day inside it and come out confused.

You can see the castle from the road. Its towers are exactly right, its banners appropriate, its gates open.

responses.label: You may approach with discernment, looking for what marks it as false.
You may pass by and not enter, having been warned.

entries.body: The false castle is made with craft and care, and its creator is skilled enough that the errors are small: a shadow that falls wrongly, a servant whose eyes hold nothing behind them, a smell in the great hall that is very close to but not quite the smell of old stone.

resolutions.label: identify three marks of falsehood before engaging with its inhabitants

success.body:
You find them: the shadow, the servants' eyes, and a third — the food, which is perfect but without texture, beautiful but without sustenance. Armed with these, you engage the enchanter directly.

He releases the questors he has been holding when you demonstrate you know exactly what his castle is. He is not entirely without shame.

failure.body:
You find one mark and miss the others, which is not sufficient — the castle's enchanter requires three confirmations before he acknowledges his work. You lose a day inside before he allows you out.

One hour of the day was genuinely instructive.

entries.body: Passing by is a legitimate choice, and you have been warned. The castle's magic does not force entry. But as you pass, you see a figure at one of the windows who is clearly a real person, not a servant of the enchanter — a questor caught within.

resolutions.label: decide whether to enter to free the captive, knowing the risk

success.body:
You enter with your eyes open, which is the protection — the castle can only hold those who mistake it for real. You find the questor, name the illusion to them, and lead them out. The enchanter watches but does not intervene when the deception fails.

The questor thanks you. They had begun to forget.

failure.body:
The castle's design accounts for people who enter knowing it is false. The enchanter has a second level — a deeper illusion that catches those who believe they are above catching. You lose several hours sorting yourself from it.

The questor is still inside. Try again, differently.

entries.body:
The swamp castle has been there long enough that the swamp has grown up around it — the moat is the swamp now, and the drawbridge is a path of raised ground that the rushes have partially reclaimed. It shows no sign of having been abandoned. It shows no sign of having been built.

A light in one high window. A quality in the air around it that the swamp does not have elsewhere.

responses.label: You may walk the raised path and enter.
You may sit at the path's beginning and wait to see what approaches.

entries.body: The path holds and the gate holds and the interior is a contradiction: entirely maintained, entirely dusty, entirely lived-in, entirely abandoned. All four things simultaneously. Something about the Grail quest has brought you here, and the somewhere you have arrived is its own answer.

resolutions.label: follow the castle's interior logic to its centre

success.body:
The centre is a room that holds one thing: the certainty that you are in the right place at the right stage of the right life. Not a vision. Not a gift. A certainty.

You return to the path. The certainty does not fade.

failure.body:
The castle's interior logic is not available to you at this stage — it requires a quality of surrender that you have not yet found in yourself. You walk the corridors and find them empty in the wrong sense.

The castle will wait.

entries.body: You wait. The swamp is quiet in the particular way of places that are listening. After a time — it is difficult to say how long — someone comes along the path from the castle side, walking toward you rather than away.

resolutions.label: receive whoever is coming toward you

success.body:
The person is a knight you knew — have not seen for a year, believed dead at a crossing you heard about secondhand. He is not dead. He has been here, and the time inside the castle has not been the same as the time outside it.

You ride on together. Whatever the castle gave him, it shows.

failure.body:
The person who comes is not someone you can receive — a figure whose nature you cannot determine, whose approach produces alarm rather than openness. You step back from the path.

Some comings-toward require more preparation than you have made.

entries.body:
The island castle is visible at dawn: a sea castle rising from a rock two miles out, towers clear against the morning sky, and a boat tied at the harbour wall that has no apparent owner. The fishermen say it is not always visible, and when it is, the questors who go out do not always come back.

The boat is sized for one person.

responses.label: You may take the boat and row to the castle.
You may wait for the morning to advance and observe whether the castle changes.

entries.body: The rowing takes an hour, and the castle gets closer more quickly than the distance suggests once you have committed to the approach. The dock is ready. The gate is open. The castle is real.

resolutions.label: row out and enter the castle

success.body:
Inside, the castle holds a library of accounts by those who have been on the Grail quest — not a trophy collection, but a resource, placed here for those who need it. You read for two hours. The boat returns you when you are ready.

The accounts you read change the shape of your own quest.

failure.body:
The rowing out is fine; the castle admits you; but the return rowing goes wrong — the current is different, the distance greater than it was coming, and you arrive back at the harbour cold and later than you meant to be.

The library remains. Whatever you read, you read well.

entries.body: Watching through the morning hours shows you: the castle's relationship with the light changes as the sun moves. By noon, the castle is at its most solid. By afternoon, it begins to recede. The boat's availability is not unlimited.

resolutions.label: row out at the optimal moment and use the window well

success.body:
You time it correctly — mid-morning, the castle at maximum reality — and the rowing is direct, the admission immediate, and you have three hours inside rather than one. The library the castle contains is extensive.

You bring back notes and a changed understanding.

failure.body:
The optimal window is narrower than your observation suggested, and you arrive at the castle just as its solidity begins to degrade. The library is thinner than it should be and the return is uncertain.

Precision requires a longer study period than you took.

entries.body:
A city building that was Mordred's administrative centre has been abandoned so quickly that the departure left things behind: documents half-burned, a steward who refused to leave, and a locked cellar that the retreating soldiers were told not to open. They did not.

The steward has the key. He will not use it without a knight to stand with him.

responses.label: You may stand with the steward and open the cellar.
You may ask the steward what he believes is in the cellar before opening it.

entries.body: The cellar, when opened, contains documents — not administrative records, but personal ones: Mordred's own papers, including correspondence that has bearing on the current disposition of forces, and several things that are clearly of personal significance to him and were not expected to be found by anyone.

resolutions.label: assess and preserve what is useful, properly

success.body:
You sort the documents with care: what is useful to Arthur's cause, what is personal and irrelevant, what is damaging in ways that go beyond this war. The useful goes to Arthur's messengers. The rest you seal.

The steward witnesses. He seems relieved to have done something constructive.

failure.body:
The documents are too numerous to assess quickly and some are written in cipher. You secure the cellar with the steward as guard and send for someone with the relevant knowledge.

The finding was not the finding's end.

entries.body: The steward believes the cellar contains the list of those who informed for Mordred — a document whose existence he has suspected for months. If true, its handling requires extreme care: names that could destroy families, or save them depending on use.

resolutions.label: open the cellar with full understanding of what you may find

success.body:
The list is there. You read it once, carefully. The steward does not read it — you judge that to be safer. What you do with the knowledge is a decision you make over the next two days, and the decision you make is one you can defend.

The list is then burned.

failure.body:
The list exists and you see it, and the seeing before you have decided how to use it means the decision is made under the weight of specific knowledge you did not choose to carry.

Some doors, once opened, change you before you can decide how to be changed.

entries.body:
The forest castle is one that was Gawain's during the Golden Age — a posting, a gift, a northern outpost he never lived in long enough to know well. The war has reached it in the form of a lord who has claimed it for Mordred, displacing the small household that served there.

The household has gone a mile into the forest. They do not know whether to fight or accept. They are not soldiers. The lord who displaced them brought six.

responses.label: You may challenge the lord and take back the castle.
You may find a legal remedy through Arthur's remaining authority.

entries.body: Six men and their lord against one knight: not impossible, but requiring the kind of approach that makes the numbers less relevant — surprise, terrain, the moral weight of the right side.

resolutions.label: retake the castle through force and legitimacy

success.body:
The lord's men do not fight well for a cause they know is questionable. Three leave when you make the stakes clear. The lord himself stands longer than wisdom required and then yields.

The household returns. The castle is Gawain's again — or what Gawain stood for.

failure.body:
The lord's men are better than expected, and the castle's walls work as well for defenders as for any fight. You are forced to withdraw.

The castle remains taken. The household remains in the forest.

entries.body: Arthur's authority extends where his messengers can reach, and there is a lord two days east who still holds a commission from Arthur's chamberlain. A written order through him would be legal and enforceable in the eyes of those who still recognise the law.

resolutions.label: ride for the lord's commission and return with the legal remedy

success.body:
The commission is obtained in two days and the ride back is hard but passable. Presented with an order that carries the king's legal remnant, the occupying lord reassesses. He leaves.

Law, in its final days, can still move men who believed in it.

failure.body:
The commission requires longer to obtain, or the occupying lord has no remaining regard for Arthur's authority, and the legal remedy has no purchase.

Law requires both belief and enforcement, and one is failing.

entries.body:
The mountain castle is one of the last loyalist holds — a place that declared for Arthur and has been holding since Camlann. Its small garrison has been there for two months, cut off by Mordred's forces on the slopes below.

You can see the castle from the ridge. Below, between you and the pass, six men watch the approach. The garrison does not know you are here.

responses.label: You may signal the castle and work with the garrison from both sides.
You may find the unguarded way through the watching force.

entries.body: Signalling the castle is possible — light from the ridge, in simple patterns. The garrison, if alert, will read it and act. The six men below will also see the signal, but they cannot cover both directions at once.

resolutions.label: signal the castle and time the approach with the garrison's response

success.body:
The garrison is alert — they have been watchful for two months — and the signal is read correctly. They make a distraction from the walls while you come down through the six men. The distraction and the descent coincide. The watching force cannot cover both.

The pass is open. The garrison is relieved.

failure.body:
The signal is misread, or the timing is off, and the distraction comes at the wrong moment. The six men are looking your way when you need them looking elsewhere.

Try the other approach.

entries.body: Six men cannot watch every angle. The mountain has gullies, ridges, and blind spots, and men who have been watching the same pass for weeks develop habitual attention — they look where things have come from before, not where things have never come from.

resolutions.label: find the blind approach and reach the castle unseen

success.body:
The blind approach is a gully that runs close enough to the castle's wall to allow you to reach the postern gate without being visible from the watchers' posts. The garrison opens it the moment they hear you knock.

You are inside with supplies and news from the world below. Both were needed.

failure.body:
The gully is more exposed than it appeared from the ridge, and one of the watchers is alert enough to catch movement. You go to ground and wait.

Waiting is itself a form of progress.

entries.body:
The castle in the open plains has been a neutral ground since the war began — held by a lord who has refused to declare for either side and whose neutrality has been reluctantly respected because the castle is too defensible to take without disproportionate cost. Both sides want him.

He has sent word that he will receive a knight who can tell him which side deserves his loyalty. You have been recommended as that knight.

responses.label: You may go to him and make the case for Arthur honestly.
You may go to him and help him reach his own conclusion rather than making the case.

entries.body: The case for Arthur is not the case for a king — the king is ailing and his cause is in doubt. It is the case for what Arthur represents: a way of governing that is worth preserving even in its defeat.

resolutions.label: make the case for Arthur's cause without overstating what remains of it

success.body:
He receives your honest case, which does not pretend Arthur is winning, and respects the honesty more than he would have respected a confident prediction. He declares for Arthur.

The castle's gates are open to Arthur's messengers from that day. It matters.

failure.body:
The case is strong but not strong enough to overcome the practical consideration of which side is more likely to be standing in a year. He thanks you and remains neutral.

Neutrality, in this case, is a form of wisdom you cannot quite argue against.

entries.body: He has already decided — you can see it in him — but he needs to say it to someone who will not try to redirect it. What he needs is a witness, not an advocate.

resolutions.label: be the witness he needs rather than the advocate he asked for

success.body:
You say: tell me which side deserves your loyalty, in your own judgment. He says it. It is Arthur. He has known it for weeks.

The declaring, once done, is done cleanly. He needed the clean.

failure.body:
You try to be the witness and find yourself advising despite yourself — the situation calls for advocacy and the advocating instinct is too strong. He hears the advocacy and retreats from the decision he was ready to make.

Witness requires the complete suppression of your own preference.

entries.body:
The swamp castle has changed allegiance twice in the last month. Its lord originally declared for Arthur, then for Mordred when threatened, then back to Arthur when Mordred's forces retreated. He is not cowardly — he is genuinely uncertain which side is right, and the uncertainty has produced a pattern of choice that serves neither.

He wishes to speak with a knight of Arthur's camp who will hear his account without judgment.

responses.label: You may hear his account without judgment.
You may hear his account and give him the judgment he is avoiding.

entries.body: His account, once he trusts you enough to give it, is coherent: he has people dependent on him, and the choice of sides has been a choice about which danger to his people is the lesser. Both sides have threatened his people. Neither has protected them.

resolutions.label: hear the account in full and reflect it back accurately

success.body:
You receive it without judgment, which is what he asked for, and when you reflect it back accurately he hears it differently than he heard it in his own head. The problem is clearer. The path through it is not yet clear, but clarity about a problem is a precondition for finding the path.

He commits to Arthur, not because you argued for it, but because he sees his own position more clearly now.

failure.body:
You hear the account but your reception is not entirely without judgment — something in your posture or phrasing conveys a position, and he closes the account before it is finished.

Witness without judgment requires the complete management of your own reaction.

entries.body: He asked for no judgment, but the absence of judgment has left him circling the same question indefinitely. Sometimes a person needs to be told what they already know, stated simply by someone outside the situation.

resolutions.label: give him the honest judgment he is asking for obliquely

success.body:
The judgment, given plainly, is this: his people's safety is not well-served by repeated switching, regardless of which side is switched to, because reliability is itself a form of protection. A man known to switch provides no one with certainty.

He receives this. It is not what he wanted to hear. It is what he needed.

failure.body:
The judgment comes out as criticism rather than analysis, and he defends himself. The conversation becomes an argument. Arguments are not what was needed here.

Judgment requires precision of tone as well as truth.

entries.body:
The sea castle was held for Arthur by a lord who died at Camlann. His widow holds it now, with a garrison of twelve and the knowledge that two of Mordred's ships are approaching the coastline. She has sent for help. You are what arrived.

The ships will reach the harbour at dawn. She needs a plan that twelve people can execute.

responses.label: You may defend the harbour and deny the ships their landing.
You may evacuate the castle and deny the ships a prize.

entries.body: Twelve people and a harbour wall against two ships' crews: the numbers are against you, but harbour defence has its own advantages — the ships must come to you, and the approaches are narrow, and fire is a maritime weapon.

resolutions.label: hold the harbour against the landing

success.body:
The fire does the work that the numbers could not. The lead ship takes fire at the harbour mouth and turns; the second ship turns with it. They do not land.

The castle stands. The widow does not celebrate — she has seen too many such victories to trust them.

failure.body:
The fire falls short, and the ships land. You make the landing cost them, but you cannot hold the harbour against both crews. The castle has an inner gate.

You fall back to the inner gate.

entries.body: The castle's value is primarily symbolic. If evacuated and denied to the ships, they gain a building but not a prize — the widow's household, the garrison, the stores, and whatever Arthur's cause is worth in practical terms can be moved inland in the hours before dawn.

resolutions.label: evacuate the castle completely before the ships arrive

success.body:
You work through the night. Everything that matters is gone before the ships dock. The garrison is three miles inland by dawn. The ships take a castle that is empty of everything except the decision to leave it.

The widow looks back once. Then she does not look back.

failure.body:
The evacuation is not complete before the ships arrive. Three of the garrison are still in the castle when the first men off the ships reach the gate.

Three is fewer than twelve. The choice was still the right one.

entries.body:
A bear has come down from the hills into the city — driven from its territory by fire or flood — and it is in the marketplace now, overturning stalls and scattering the crowd with the panicked violence of a creature that does not understand where it is. Three people have been hurt. The beast is not malicious. It is terrified.

The city guard has ringed the square with spears but no one will advance. They are waiting for someone to tell them what to do.

responses.label: You may drive the bear out of the square toward the city gate.
You may work with the crowd to calm the situation before anyone is further hurt.

entries.body: Driving a panicked bear requires noise, fire, and someone willing to be the thing it runs from. The gate is sixty yards east. The bear must go in that direction rather than into the crowd on the other three sides.

resolutions.label: drive the bear through the marketplace and out the city gate

success.body:
You come at it from the right angle with enough noise and your horse moving hard, and the bear goes east, then through the gate, then gone into the fields beyond the wall. The crowd exhales.

The stalls are wrecked. The bear is alive. Everyone who was hurt will live.

failure.body:
The bear goes north instead of east when you push it, toward the denser part of the city, and you are forced to change the approach entirely. Someone else takes a hit before the beast is redirected.

The bear eventually finds its own way out. The cost was higher than it needed to be.

entries.body: Panic in a crowd amplifies every danger. The bear reacts to movement and noise; if the crowd stills, the bear may still itself. The challenge is persuading several hundred frightened people to do a counterintuitive thing simultaneously.

resolutions.label: still the crowd and let the bear find its own way

success.body:
Your voice carries and your authority in it is sufficient. The crowd stills. The bear, in the sudden quiet, stops and looks around, and after a long uncertain moment it walks to the eastern gate and through it.

No one is hurt in the ending. That is the measure of it.

failure.body:
The crowd does not still completely — there are always a few — and the incomplete quiet is worse than full noise, producing an eerie uncertainty that the bear responds to badly.

You must drive it out after all.

entries.body:
The boar stands in the forest road with the ancient authority of a creature that predates the road and knows it. It is enormous — a veteran of many seasons, its hide scarred from encounters that should have killed it. It has been here since morning, blocking the only route through, and it will not be shouted away.

Two merchants have tried and given up. Their carts are where the boar left them.

responses.label: You may hunt the boar and clear the road.
You may find a way around through the forest.

entries.body: The boar has lived long enough to be shrewd, and the straightforward approach will not work without skill and preparation. It will charge when pressed, and a boar's charge is its own kind of argument.

resolutions.label: hunt and take the boar to clear the road

success.body:
You read the boar and find the approach that accounts for its intelligence as well as its strength. The take is clean. The road is open. The merchants will have meat as well as their route.

The boar was a fine animal. You say a word for it.

failure.body:
The boar is wiser than you gave it credit for and turns the approach against you — you are unhorsed and the boar is gone into the trees, having made its point. The road is still blocked.

Try the forest route while you recover.

entries.body: The forest offers a parallel way — slower, wetter, but not impossible. Someone who knows the forest could find it; someone who does not will spend the better part of the day going wrong.

resolutions.label: navigate the forest and find the way around

success.body:
The parallel route exists and you find it — a deer-track widened by seasons of use, passable by carts if the carts are willing to accept a muddy morning. You lead the merchants through.

The boar is still on the road when you rejoin it from the far side. You leave it in peace.

failure.body:
The forest route goes wrong at a stream crossing that has more water than it should after recent rain, and you spend the day trying alternatives that do not work. The boar, when you return to the road, has gone of its own accord.

The day is gone. The road is open.

entries.body:
Something has been taking livestock from the mountain villages — not wolves, which are known, but something larger that leaves tracks too big for any wolf and burns where it touches. Three villages have lost animals. The people are frightened and the description grows with each telling.

The tracks lead up the mountain. You have been asked by the village elder to go and see.

responses.label: You may follow the tracks and confront what made them.
You may set a careful trap rather than a direct pursuit.

entries.body: The tracks lead to a high cave, and the thing in the cave is smaller than the stories — a young creature, not yet full-grown, separated from whatever context produced it. It is hungry. It is afraid. It is still genuinely dangerous.

resolutions.label: drive the creature away or deal with it directly

success.body:
Young or not, the encounter is serious enough, and you acquit yourself well. The creature retreats further up the mountain than it is comfortable pursuing. The villages will have some peace.

You report to the elder: the thing is real, it is gone, and it may return when it is grown.

failure.body:
The creature is not as young as it appeared, and the encounter does not go your way. You are singed and bruised and the creature is still in the cave.

A different strategy is needed. Possibly fire of your own.

entries.body: A trap requires knowing what the creature's pattern is: when it comes down, which paths it uses, what draws it. The village women who tend the remaining animals have this knowledge — they have been watching.

resolutions.label: work with the villagers to trap or dissuade the creature

success.body:
The trap you design with the villagers uses the creature's pattern against it — not to kill it, but to make the mountain easier than the village as a food source. Guided by the village elder's granddaughter, who knows every goat-path on the mountain, you establish a situation in which the creature has better options than the village.

It takes them. The raids stop.

failure.body:
The trap is well-designed but the creature is cleverer than expected and avoids the most predictable elements. A modified approach with the same principle eventually works, but it costs another week and another flock.

Patience in problem-solving is its own skill.

entries.body:
The white stallion has been running wild across the plains for three weeks, too fast and too intelligent to be caught, its presence disrupting the farming settlements it passes through — not from malice but from the sheer energy of a creature too large for the landscape it is in.

It is beautiful. It is also destroying crops. The farmers want it caught or killed. A lord two days east claims it is his; if it is, the killing option is more complicated.

responses.label: You may attempt to catch and tame the horse.
You may investigate the lord's claim before acting.

entries.body: Catching a wild horse of this quality requires patience, skill, and the willingness to let the horse come to you rather than pursuing it — pursuit only drives it further and faster.

resolutions.label: work patiently to bring the horse to hand

success.body:
Three days of reading the horse's patterns, presenting yourself repeatedly at the edges of its territory, and waiting with perfect stillness at the third appearance — it approaches on its own terms. You take its halter gently.

It is a remarkable animal. The lord's claim, when you investigate, is false.

failure.body:
The horse is too clever and the plains too large. After three days you are no closer and the crops are still being damaged.

A different strategy is needed, or more time.

entries.body: The lord's claim is worth investigating before any action is taken — if the horse is his, its return is a matter of courtesy; if the claim is false, that fact changes the resolution considerably.

resolutions.label: investigate the claim and establish the truth

success.body:
The lord's claim is false — his horse, which ran away, was brown. The white stallion is a creature from nowhere that anyone can account for, which means it belongs to whoever catches it lawfully. The lord withdraws his claim when you present the evidence.

You catch the horse on the following day.

failure.body:
The claim cannot be clearly confirmed or denied without more information than is available at this distance. The legal ambiguity complicates every subsequent action.

Ambiguity is itself a kind of obstacle.

entries.body:
Something in the swamp has taken three travellers who used the eastern path in the past month. The locals describe it as a serpent — very large, patient, and specific in its ambushes. The eastern path is the most direct route through, and without it the journey to the next settlement doubles.

You need to use the eastern path. The question is how.

responses.label: You may go through the path and deal with the serpent directly.
You may hunt the serpent on its own terms before attempting the path.

entries.body: The serpent's ambush point is predictable, according to those who have survived near-encounters — a particular section where the path narrows between two water-channels. You can prepare for it.

resolutions.label: prepare for the ambush and defeat the serpent at its own point

success.body:
You are ready when it comes. The preparation makes the difference between a fight and a slaughter — the outcome is not in doubt once you are prepared. The path is clear.

You mark the spot so those who follow will know the danger is gone.

failure.body:
The serpent does not ambush at the expected point — it has a second position, which the locals did not know. The encounter is harder than prepared for.

You survive. The path remains dangerous.

entries.body: Hunting the serpent in the swamp is approaching the problem on different terms — finding it before it finds you, in terrain that favours the serpent. The advantage is that you choose the ground rather than it.

resolutions.label: track the serpent through the swamp and end the threat

success.body:
You read the swamp correctly — the disturbed water-grass, the smell of something dead and recent, the particular quality of the mud near the water's edge. You find the serpent before it knows you have found it.

The path is open.

failure.body:
The swamp reads you more easily than you read the swamp, and the serpent is not where the signs suggested. You withdraw with the information that the serpent is real and larger than the accounts.

The path must wait.

entries.body:
The sea creature has overturned two fishing boats this week — not to feed, the fishermen believe, but as a territorial response to the boats' presence in a particular bay. The bay is the best fishing ground in three days' travel, and the fishing community cannot afford to lose it.

You are on the coast. The creature has been seen from the cliff.

responses.label: You may take a boat into the bay and confront the creature.
You may find a way to discourage the creature from the bay without direct confrontation.

entries.body: The creature, seen from the water, is a long-bodied thing — not monstrous, exactly, but large enough and determined enough to be genuinely dangerous to a small boat. You are in a small boat.

resolutions.label: face the creature in the bay and drive it off

success.body:
The creature is territorial, not aggressive — once you demonstrate that the bay's visitors will not be passive, it reassesses the cost of its claim. Three passes and it retreats to deeper water.

The fishermen will watch carefully for some weeks. The bay is theirs again.

failure.body:
The boat takes a hit and you are in the water and the creature is between you and the shore. The fishermen on the cliff are already in their boats.

You are retrieved. The creature is not confronted today.

entries.body: A creature defending a territory is responding to signals — sound, smell, the pattern of intrusion. If the signals can be changed, the creature's response may change without requiring combat.

resolutions.label: find what signal is triggering the creature and change it

success.body:
The problem is the particular net the fishermen have been using — it produces a sound underwater that mimics the territorial call of the creature's own kind. A different net, or a different entry angle, produces no conflict.

The solution is embarrassingly practical. The fishermen are not embarrassed.

failure.body:
The triggering signal is not visible from outside the water, and your analysis produces several candidates but no certainty. The fishermen try one modification; it does not help.

More observation is needed.

entries.body:
A beast has appeared in the city that answers no natural description — not monstrous, but composite, combining features of creatures that do not go together. It has been moving through the city's streets for three days without harming anyone, but its presence stops everyone it passes. The Grail questors who are in the city say: this is a test of discernment.

The beast is in the street ahead of you now, looking at you with an attention that is entirely intelligent.

responses.label: You may regard the beast in return and see what it communicates.
You may ask the questors what manner of test this is before engaging.

entries.body: The beast's attention is legible to someone willing to look carefully and without assumption. It is not threatening. It is showing something — a quality of attention itself, a way of seeing, a demonstration of what it means to look at a thing fully.

resolutions.label: look at the beast fully and receive what it is showing

success.body:
You look, and the looking does something: the composite quality of the beast resolves into a coherent image, not of what the beast is but of what it represents — the aspect of the divine that does not fit any single shape. You understand something you had only approached before.

The beast departs. The street is ordinary.

failure.body:
The looking is sincere but not quite complete — you look at the beast rather than through it, and the thing it was showing remains just beyond what you received.

The beast departs anyway. The partial is still something.

entries.body: The questors have been debating the beast for three days without agreement. Their collective wisdom is: it is an image of the divine composite — the aspect of God that is beyond any single attribute. The test is not of knowledge but of reception.

resolutions.label: approach the beast prepared to receive rather than classify

success.body:
The questors' preparation helps — you know not to reach for a category. The receiving is cleaner for the knowing. What you receive is a quality of sight that the Grail quest requires and that cannot be taught, only given.

You have been given it.

failure.body:
The preparation produces its own obstacle — you arrive with the questors' framework, which is better than nothing but is still a framework, and frameworks limit receiving. You take what the framework allows through.

The beast departs. The questors argue about what it meant.

entries.body:
The unicorn was seen this morning at the forest's edge, and those who saw it cannot agree on what they saw except the essential: white, horned, unlike anything. The questors in this wood know the old accounts — the unicorn is a sign, a mirror, a creature that appears to those of particular quality.

It is still at the forest's edge. Sitting, not grazing. Waiting.

responses.label: You may approach the unicorn alone.
You may watch from the treeline to understand what it is waiting for.

entries.body: The approach must be made without pretence — the old accounts are consistent: the unicorn will flee from artifice and come to honesty. What you bring to the approach is what it will respond to.

resolutions.label: approach in full honesty, nothing concealed or performed

success.body:
It does not flee. It watches you come and then lowers its head, which the accounts describe as the gesture of acceptance. You stand with it for a time — you cannot say how long — and something passes between you that is not communication in any ordinary sense but is nonetheless real.

When you step back, it walks into the forest.

failure.body:
It reads something in the approach that is not quite honest — not a lie, but a presentation rather than a being — and it retreats, step by step, until it is in the trees.

The honesty required is complete, not partial.

entries.body: From the treeline, watching without approaching, you can read what the unicorn is doing: it turns toward specific people as they approach and away from others, with a consistency that suggests a criterion. You can learn the criterion if you watch long enough.

resolutions.label: read the criterion and present yourself accordingly

success.body:
The criterion is not virtue, exactly — it is something more specific: the quality of not wanting anything from the encounter. Those who approach wanting to see the unicorn are turned away. Those who approach without agenda are received.

You approach having genuinely let go of the wanting. It waits for you.

failure.body:
The criterion you identify is not quite right, and your approach — calibrated for the wrong thing — is turned away. The unicorn goes into the forest.

The criterion was subtler than you found from the outside.

entries.body:
The creature at the mountain pass is not a beast in the ordinary sense: it has the shape of a dragon but the eyes of something much older, and it does not attack the questors who approach — it asks them something and then either steps aside or does not. The questors who have been turned away are below, debating what the question was.

It is your turn to approach.

responses.label: You may approach the creature and answer its question as fully as you can.
You may speak first with the failed questors and learn from their attempts.

entries.body: The creature's question, when you are close enough to hear it, is in words you understand but feel strangely weighted: 'What do you carry that you have not acknowledged?'

resolutions.label: answer the question with complete honesty

success.body:
You name it — the thing carried and unacknowledged, the weight you had been managing rather than facing. The creature regards you for a long time.

Then it steps aside. The pass is open.

failure.body:
You name something, but it is not the thing it asked for — it is the acceptable version of the thing, presented rather than confessed. The creature does not step aside.

The question remains open.

entries.body: The failed questors have been honest with each other about their attempts. They cannot agree on the question's exact wording, but they agree on the shape of it: it asks something about what the questor has not yet admitted to themselves.

resolutions.label: use their accounts to prepare your answer before approaching

success.body:
The preparation helps you find the thing before the creature asks for it — you have it ready, acknowledged and named, when you approach. The creature hears the readiness and does not need to ask.

The pass opens before the question is spoken.

failure.body:
The preparation points you toward the wrong thing — you find something, acknowledge it, but it is not what the creature is asking for. The question is still required.

Honest preparation, but in the wrong direction.

entries.body:
A white hart has been following one of the Grail questors across the plains for three days. Not hunting her — following, at a fixed distance, appearing at dawn and departing at dusk. She is disturbed by it. The other questors say it is a sign. She says she does not want a sign.

She has asked for a knight's opinion on what she should do.

responses.label: You may counsel her to engage with the hart and discover what it signifies.
You may speak to the hart on her behalf, if that is possible.

entries.body: Her reluctance to engage with the sign is itself significant — it tells you something about the quality of the test. The hart follows someone who is running from what it represents rather than toward it.

resolutions.label: help her understand why turning toward the hart may be the right choice

success.body:
You find the argument that reaches her: the hart is not a demand but an offer, and offers can be declined; but declining without looking at what is being offered is not the same as a considered refusal. She agrees to look.

She turns to face the hart. It approaches. She is changed by the morning that follows in ways she cannot describe.

failure.body:
She has reasons for her reluctance that go deeper than the argument can reach, and your counsel does not shift her. The hart follows for another day and then is gone.

Some signs do not wait indefinitely.

entries.body: The old accounts suggest that a hart of this kind can be spoken to — not in words, but in the language of attention and intention. Whether this is true in your experience is another matter.

resolutions.label: approach the hart and speak to it in intention

success.body:
The hart receives your approach. What you communicate to it — not in words but in the quality of your attention — is: she is not ready, but she is not unwilling. The hart accepts this and moves closer, which is its own form of accommodation.

She sees the accommodation and finds it less frightening than the following.

failure.body:
The hart regards you with the gentle incomprehension of something operating in a register you cannot enter. Your approach is received but not understood in the way you intended.

Some communications require a fluency you do not yet possess.

entries.body:
The swamp serpent does not attack — it observes. Coiled on the highest piece of dry ground in the swamp's centre, it watches the questors who try to pass through, and the watching has the quality of an evaluation. Three questors have turned back. One passed through, and she will not say how.

You must cross the swamp. The serpent is watching you from the moment you enter the reeds.

responses.label: You may cross without acknowledging the serpent.
You may acknowledge the serpent directly and meet its gaze.

entries.body: Ignoring the watcher is its own kind of statement — not quite pretending it is not there, but refusing to give it the power of your attention. The cross is then about the swamp itself, which is enough of a challenge.

resolutions.label: cross the swamp without acknowledgment, on your own terms

success.body:
The cross is difficult and the serpent watches without intervening. Your refusal to be evaluated — or your refusal to perform for the evaluation — is itself a kind of passing. You reach the far side.

The serpent lowers its head when you look back.

failure.body:
The swamp defeats you on its own terms — mud, water, uncertain ground — and you must retreat before the cross is complete. The serpent has not moved.

The cross requires better preparation.

entries.body: Meeting the serpent's gaze is meeting the evaluation directly — presenting yourself to be assessed rather than trying to slip past the assessment. The old accounts of such encounters suggest this can go either way.

resolutions.label: meet the serpent's gaze and submit to the evaluation

success.body:
The gaze is held. It is not comfortable — the evaluation is real, and what it looks for is real — but it concludes. The serpent uncoils, very slowly, and descends into the water. The path across the swamp's centre is clear.

You cross quickly, before it reconsiders.

failure.body:
The evaluation concludes in your disfavour — not judgment, not condemnation, but a turning away. The serpent regards you for a long time and then looks elsewhere, which is itself an answer.

You are not what the crossing requires today.

entries.body:
The creature in the sea — something large and old, surfacing at dawn near the Grail questors' ship — has been taken by the ship's captain as an ill sign. The questors aboard are divided: an omen of failure, or a sign of the deep's blessing.

The creature has not sounded. It keeps pace with the ship, surfacing and diving in a pattern that is not random.

responses.label: You may read the pattern of the creature's surfacing.
You may speak with the questors about what this kind of sign means in their tradition.

entries.body: The pattern of surfacing has a rhythm — three short, one long, a pause, repeated. A pattern is an attempt at communication, or a behavior so consistent that it becomes one.

resolutions.label: decode the pattern and find its meaning

success.body:
The pattern is a direction — the creature surfaces on the ship's left side when heading north, on the right when heading east. It is guiding.

You tell the captain. She changes course toward the east. The creature sounds at last, and the dive takes it deep. The course it set is the one that avoids the rocks.

failure.body:
The pattern is real but its meaning is not recoverable with what you know. You carry the observation without the interpretation.

The creature sounds eventually, without guidance given or received.

entries.body: The questors have a tradition of reading such signs, and they are willing to share it — they debate with the vigour of people who have been on a ship for some days with not enough to do. Their collective reading is: the creature is testing the ship's direction, not its occupants.

resolutions.label: apply the questors' tradition and correct the ship's direction

success.body:
You synthesise the questors' debate into an actionable reading: the creature's consistent surfacing on the starboard side when sailing north-east suggests the north-east course is wrong. The captain accepts this reluctantly. The creature dives when the course changes.

The rocks that the north-east course would have found are visible an hour later, from a safe distance.

failure.body:
The questors' tradition provides several equally plausible readings, and you cannot synthesise them into a single actionable course. The ship continues on its original heading.

The creature dives.

entries.body:
The bear was displaced by the burning of its forest — a fire set deliberately as part of the war's logic of denial, the lord's woodland destroyed so it could not shelter the enemy. The bear is in the city now, wounded from the fire and bewildered, and the city is frightened.

The animal is not a problem of war. But it is a consequence of war, and someone must deal with it.

responses.label: You may calm the bear and guide it out of the city.
You may find the bear shelter outside the city while it heals.

entries.body: The bear is in pain from the burns, which makes it more dangerous and also more pitiable. Calming it requires patience and the willingness to approach a wounded animal slowly — the slowness communicating what words cannot.

resolutions.label: approach the wounded bear slowly and guide it to safety

success.body:
The bear lets you near when you are near enough, slowly enough. It does not charge. You walk with it to the city gate and through it, and it finds a stand of trees at the river's edge and lies down.

It is not a victory. It is a kindness. The distinction matters.

failure.body:
The pain makes the bear unpredictable, and it charges when you are almost close enough. You are not hurt, but the approach is ended, and the bear retreats to the market square.

A different strategy is needed.

entries.body: The city's infirmarian — who tends to livestock as well as people — can treat the burns if the bear can be confined long enough. A disused yard at the city's edge might serve, if the bear can be persuaded to stay in it.

resolutions.label: arrange treatment for the bear and confine it safely

success.body:
Three days in the yard, the infirmarian's poultices applied with the determination of a woman who does not consider species a barrier, and the bear is well enough to be released into the river country.

It is the kind of small good that large times make invisible. You make it visible here.

failure.body:
The bear will not stay confined — it is an outdoor animal and confinement is its own injury — and you must let it go before treatment is complete. It goes on its own terms.

Partial healing is still healing.

entries.body:
The wolf pack has grown bold with the forest's emptying — the settlements are abandoned, the hunters gone to war, and the deer are fewer because the war has disturbed their ranging too. The wolves are hungry and the highway travellers are easier targets than deer.

Three merchants have been attacked in the past week. You have caught up with the pack's trail.

responses.label: You may hunt the pack directly and scatter them.
You may find what is drawing them to the highway and remove that draw.

entries.body: A pack of wolves scattered by a mounted knight will usually avoid that stretch of road for some days. It is a temporary solution, but temporary solutions have their place.

resolutions.label: ride through the pack's hunting range and scatter them

success.body:
The pack breaks before you and does not regroup quickly. The highway is safe for the next several days, which is long enough for the merchant traffic to pass.

The wolves will return when you are gone. That is the nature of the solution.

failure.body:
The pack is larger than you estimated and they do not break — they circle and harry, and you are forced to stay in motion rather than scattering them. The highway is no safer than before.

A larger effort is required.

entries.body: Something is drawing the wolves to the highway specifically — an abandoned cart of provisions, a dead horse left unburied, something that has made this stretch of road smell like regular food. Find the draw and remove it.

resolutions.label: find and remove whatever is drawing the wolves

success.body:
The cart is half a mile off the highway — an abandoned supply wagon from a retreating force, its contents still intact and clearly visited by the pack regularly. You burn what will burn and scatter what will not.

The wolves shift their range. The highway is safer for weeks.

failure.body:
The draw is not visible on the highway's surface — whatever is attracting the wolves is in the deep wood, beyond the range of quick investigation. The highway remains threatened.

The draw is there. You have not found it.

entries.body:
A great wolf — alone, not a pack — has been following the armies through the mountain passes for three weeks, taking the wounded who fall behind and the dead who are left. It is not unusual behaviour for wolves in wartime. This wolf is unusual: it is enormous, it has not been driven off by any means tried, and a soldier who looked at it closely said its eyes were the wrong colour.

It is on the ridge above you now.

responses.label: You may climb to the ridge and face the wolf.
You may find out more about the wolf from those who have seen it before you approach.

entries.body: On the ridge, the wolf is larger than the soldier's account suggested and the eyes are indeed wrong — pale, with a quality of attention that ordinary wolves do not have. It does not flee when you approach.

resolutions.label: face the wolf on the ridge

success.body:
You stand your ground and the wolf sits, which is not wolf behaviour. You regard each other for a time that is difficult to measure. Then it rises and goes north, over the ridge, and does not appear again on this section of the pass.

What it was, you do not say. What you know, you keep.

failure.body:
The wolf does not leave when you arrive, and it does not sit, and the standoff produces a quality of unease rather than resolution. You descend without clarity.

The wolf watches you go.

entries.body: The soldier who saw its eyes is still in the mountain camp, recovering from a wound. He describes the wolf with the precision of a man who has been thinking about what he saw for three weeks: the colour of the eyes, the way it moved, the fact that it did not take anyone who was alive.

resolutions.label: use his account to understand what you are dealing with

success.body:
The account points toward something that is not simply a wolf — a guardian aspect, perhaps, that walks with armies in the old accounts and takes the dead as its due. Not a threat to the living if acknowledged properly.

You go to the ridge and acknowledge it. It goes.

failure.body:
The account is too strange to yield to analysis — every interpretation leads to the next question. You go to the ridge without a framework, which is what you would have done without the soldier's account.

Some things require experience rather than understanding.

entries.body:
The horse herd stampeded through the battlefield two days past and is still running — or some of the herd is. Three horses that were someone's destriers are now running with wild horses across the scarred plains, and the knights who owned them are alive but on foot, which matters considerably in the current situation.

The herd is visible from the road: thirty animals moving together.

responses.label: You may ride after the herd and cut the destriers out.
You may set up a position ahead of the herd and turn it.

entries.body: Cutting individual horses from a herd in motion requires riding faster than the herd and getting ahead of the specific animals, then separating them without triggering the full herd to scatter. It is difficult, skilled work.

resolutions.label: ride into the herd and cut the destriers free

success.body:
You read the herd's internal structure — the destriers are on the eastern flank, recognisable by their build — and you peel them off cleanly over three passes. The wild horses continue without them.

The three knights have their horses. The march continues.

failure.body:
The herd splits at your approach rather than allowing the separation, and the destriers go with the larger group. You follow but cannot recover them today.

The herd is heading north. Try ahead of them.

entries.body: Getting ahead of the herd and turning it requires knowledge of the ground ahead and the speed to reach it. If you can turn the herd toward a natural enclosure — a valley, a river bend — the destriers can be separated in the slower ground.

resolutions.label: get ahead of the herd and guide it to enclosed ground

success.body:
The river bend two miles north creates the natural pen you needed. You reach it ahead of the herd, take position, and turn the lead horses. The herd mills in the bend.

The destriers are recovered with help from the knights themselves, who had been following at a distance.

failure.body:
The ground ahead offers nothing suitable within range, and the herd moves faster than expected. You cannot get ahead of it.

The chasing approach must be tried after all.

entries.body:
