The sea pirates blockading this harbour are former navy — men from Arthur's own fleet, unpaid since Camlann, who have taken to piracy with the professional efficiency of people who know what they are doing. They have been selective: they take from Mordred's supply ships and leave civilian vessels unmolested.

They want to speak with a representative of Arthur's remaining authority. You are what is available.

responses.label: You may meet with their captain as Arthur's representative.
You may determine what they actually need before the meeting.

entries.body: The captain receives you aboard his vessel with the courtesy of a professional meeting a professional. His situation is this: he has men, ships, and skills, and no one to serve. He would stop the piracy if he had a legitimate alternative.

resolutions.label: represent Arthur's remaining cause and offer them a legitimate role

success.body:
You offer what Arthur would offer: recognition of their service, back pay as debt rather than promise, and a commission to continue blockading Mordred's supply ships as a legitimate act of war rather than piracy.

He accepts. The harbour is open. The blockade of Mordred continues, legally now.

failure.body:
The offer is good but the authority behind it is uncertain — you represent what remains of Arthur, and what remains may not be enough. The captain defers his answer.

The answer may come later. The meeting was not wasted.

entries.body: Their actual need, before the meeting, can be understood by speaking with the harbour master, who has been in regular quiet communication with the fleet — they are former colleagues and the harbour master knows what the captain needs versus what he asks for.

resolutions.label: learn their real need and go into the meeting prepared

success.body:
The real need is: a letter from Arthur's remaining seal, addressed to them by name, acknowledging their service and their current situation. A letter, they believe, will be enough to preserve their honour regardless of what happens to Arthur.

You can produce this. The meeting is short and the result is clean.

failure.body:
The harbour master's information is out of date — the captain's position has changed in the past week. You go into the meeting prepared for the wrong thing.

Preparation for the wrong need is still preparation. You adapt.

entries.body:
The inn's back room has been engaged for a private meeting, but the privacy is imperfect — through the wall, with sufficient attention, you can distinguish voices if not words. The meeting began an hour ago and a servant in the corridor has the expression of someone who would rather not be here.

Three lords, from what you can determine by the horses outside — lords who should not, by their competing interests, be in any room together. The thing they are conspiring about is not visible from here.

responses.label: You may find a way into the room or a better listening position.
You may ask the servant what he has heard and offer him protection for speaking.

entries.body: The inn's rooms share a wall, and the room adjacent to the meeting room may have been let to someone else or may be empty. The innkeeper will know.

resolutions.label: find a vantage point that lets you hear the meeting

success.body:
The adjacent room is empty. Through the thin wall, the meeting resolves into comprehensible speech — three lords agreeing to withhold military support from Arthur at a specific moment in exchange for land concessions from the party they will favour instead.

You have something worth carrying to Camelot.

failure.body:
The wall is thicker than the inn's construction suggested, and the voices stay voices rather than words. You hear a tone — agreement, conclusion, something settled — but not what was agreed.

The knowledge that something was settled is itself something.

entries.body: The servant has been carrying food and wine throughout the meeting. He has heard things he cannot unknow and is frightened by the knowing. Protection, honestly offered, might open what fear has closed.

resolutions.label: offer him protection and hear what he knows

success.body:
He tells you, quickly, in the corridor: the three lords are planning to absent themselves from a specific engagement Arthur is counting on, having received assurances of safety from the other side.

You ride within the hour.

failure.body:
The protection you offer is not compelling enough for the risk he perceives. He thanks you and goes back inside.

The information stays behind the door.

entries.body:
The forest clearing at midnight — you saw the lights from the road and left your horse at a distance. A group of twenty people in a ring, speaking something. Not ritual exactly; more like a shared rehearsal of common purpose. They have not posted guards, which means either they are confident they have not been followed or they want to be found.

You are at the clearing's edge.

responses.label: You may step into the clearing and introduce yourself.
You may observe from the edge until you understand what this gathering is.

entries.body: Twenty people who were expecting not to be interrupted receive your appearance differently depending on what the gathering is. They stop and watch you come.

resolutions.label: introduce yourself honestly and explain your presence

success.body:
They are the families of men taken for a lord's forced labour — meeting to plan a petition to Arthur, since the lord's own courts are the lord's own courts. They receive a knight with the specific wariness of people who have been failed by knights before and the cautious hope of people who haven't given up.

You offer to carry the petition. They consider. They accept.

failure.body:
Your arrival produces flight — they scatter before you can explain, and the clearing is empty in thirty seconds. Whatever they were meeting about, the secrecy was more important than the meeting.

You find a dropped document. It is the beginning of the answer.

entries.body: From the edge, the gathering resolves: twenty farmers, roughly, dressed for cold, speaking in turns. The subject is serious — you can tell by the posture — and the ring formation suggests a tradition of shared decision-making rather than a leader speaking.

resolutions.label: observe until you understand, then approach with that understanding

success.body:
The understanding comes before you step forward: these are people deciding whether to go to Arthur or to stay where their lord will find them. They have not decided. You step forward with that knowledge and enter the decision rather than interrupting it.

The conversation that follows is different from the one that would have happened without the understanding.

failure.body:
The observation is insufficient — the language they use is local and specific, and you understand less than you thought. You step forward with a misread of the situation and must correct it on the fly.

A partial misread is better than a full one.

entries.body:
The mountain meeting can be seen from the opposite ridge: four riders at a high point that can only be reached by specific paths, and those paths were watched — you were seen coming and turned away. Now you are on the opposite ridge and the meeting is visible.

The four are noble, from their bearing. Their conversation, whatever it is, has been going for an hour.

responses.label: You may find the watching-path they used and observe what that tells you.
You may wait for them to descend and intercept one on the way down.

entries.body: The watching-path reveals: who they were watching for (not you — you came from the wrong direction) and what they were prepared for (more riders, armed, which tells you this meeting had a threat attached).

resolutions.label: read what the watching-path tells you and act on the information

success.body:
The watching-path was watching for someone coming from the east — a specific threat, a specific person who did not arrive. The meeting's subject was contingent on that arrival. You know enough now to guess the subject.

The guess will be confirmed when you speak with the right person.

failure.body:
The path tells you less than you hoped — it was watched by someone good at not leaving signs of watching. You learn the general direction of the threat they expected, which is something.

General direction is a beginning.

entries.body: The descent from the high point uses three paths, and only one comes past a location where you can speak privately with a single rider. Getting there before the meeting ends requires moving now.

resolutions.label: intercept one of the four on their descent

success.body:
You reach the intercept point ahead of the descending riders and take a position. The last of the four comes past alone — the other three took different paths — and he recognises a knight on the road as a normal thing.

The conversation you have with him, carefully directed, tells you most of what the meeting was about.

failure.body:
They descend faster than expected and in a group, which removes the possibility of a private conversation. You observe them pass.

Four faces, four horses, remembered well. That is something.

entries.body:
The meeting on the plains is disguised as a horse fair — an effective disguise, because it actually is a horse fair for half the people there. But six of the attendees have not looked at a horse since their arrival, and they have been stationary at the back of the grounds, which is where you are now.

They are clearly negotiating something. The horses are cover.

responses.label: You may approach them as a horse buyer and work into their conversation.
You may watch them complete the negotiation and intercept the result.

entries.body: A knight at a horse fair is entirely unremarkable. You approach as a buyer, examine the nearest horse (which is fine), and allow the conversation to exist around you as background until you are close enough for it to become foreground.

resolutions.label: work your way into position to hear and understand the negotiation

success.body:
The negotiation concerns the disposition of grain stores in three border villages — something that matters considerably if the villages are about to become a battlefield. You are close enough to hear the terms.

You buy the horse. It is a good horse. The information is better.

failure.body:
One of the six recognises a knight who is not quite browsing and the conversation moves away from you before you get close enough. The horse is purchased. The information is not.

At least you have a good horse.

entries.body: The negotiation will produce a document, a handshake, or a parting that has meaning. The meaning will be legible to someone watching the conclusion of a serious negotiation between strangers.

resolutions.label: read the conclusion of the negotiation and understand what was agreed

success.body:
The parting is read: they depart in pairs, each pair going a different direction, which means the agreement has distributed aspects. Not a single transaction but a coordinated response to something. The coordination is what you bring back.

The something it is responding to takes another day to work out.

failure.body:
The parting is too efficient to be read — they were trained in this, and the conclusion gives nothing away. Six people leave in various directions and the fair continues.

Some negotiations are concluded for exactly the audience that is watching.

entries.body:
The swamp meeting is held where it is because there is literally no other way to reach it than to want to be there — the path is invisible to anyone who is not following someone who knows it, and the anyone is you, following the second of the three riders who went in this morning.

The meeting is on a dry island. Three people and a fire.

responses.label: You may approach the island openly.
You may observe from the swamp's edge before deciding.

entries.body: An open approach gives them time to see you coming and the choice of how to receive you — not necessarily a disadvantage, because it gives them time to decide you are not a threat.

resolutions.label: approach openly and identify yourself

success.body:
They watch you come — there is enough time for that — and something about the openness of your approach reassures them. They receive you at the island's edge.

The three are healers meeting to share knowledge of a specific ailment that the war has produced in large numbers. The knowledge they share will save lives. You are welcome to listen.

failure.body:
Open does not mean unthreatening to people who have been meeting secretly out of necessity. They douse the fire and go into the swamp before you reach the island.

The fire's site tells you something about the meeting's duration. It was long.

entries.body: Observing from the swamp's edge — from behind a screen of reed — gives you the shape of the meeting before you enter it. Three people, equal authority, sharing something in turns.

resolutions.label: observe until the right moment to approach

success.body:
The right moment comes when one of them steps to the island's edge to look at the swamp — a habit, a check — and sees you in the reeds and makes a decision not to alarm the others. You step out and they speak quietly.

The meeting continues with you as a participant.

failure.body:
The reed is not as complete a screen as it appeared, and a sound you make alerts them. They do not run — they wait for you to emerge, which you do, and the meeting becomes something else entirely when you do.

A different kind of conversation than you intended.

entries.body:
The sea cove has a boat in it that was not there yesterday, and the boat has come from somewhere south that it should not have come from without a reason. Three men are on the beach, not unloading, not eating — talking, which means the boat is the cover and the conversation is the cargo.

The cliffs above the cove are accessible from the coastal path.

responses.label: You may descend to the cove and identify yourself.
You may stay on the cliff and observe from above.

entries.body: Descending changes the situation from surveillance to encounter. The three men will hear you coming and will have composed themselves before you arrive.

resolutions.label: descend and engage them directly

success.body:
The composure they have adopted does not fully conceal what they were discussing, and your directness gives them less time than they needed. Two of them tell you by what they don't say; the third is better at concealment.

You have two-thirds of what was discussed, which is usually enough.

failure.body:
Three men who know what they are doing have composed themselves completely before you arrive at the beach. They are sailors stopped for water and rest. They are possibly even telling the truth.

You let them sail. The boat is distinctive and will be seen again.

entries.body: From the cliff, the conversation's body language is readable even if the words are not — who is leading, who is receiving, whether there is agreement or contention. The boat's direction of arrival tells you its origin.

resolutions.label: read the conversation from the cliff before the meeting concludes

success.body:
The body language: two lords of some kind (carriage, posture) meeting a third who came by boat from the south. The south lord is delivering something — verbally, no document — and the two are receiving it with the quality of confirmation rather than surprise.

You know what they were doing even if not what they agreed to.

failure.body:
The cove's acoustics throw the words up to the cliff but not coherently, and the body language is harder to read with the sun at the wrong angle. You have impressions rather than information.

Impressions, carefully held, sometimes mature into information.

entries.body:
The city tavern's back room has a group of Grail questors in it — not your company, but others — and they are meeting in secret because the lord of this city has declared the Grail quest a destabilising influence and has forbidden public gatherings of questors.

The questors inside are debating something serious. The street outside has one of the lord's men watching the door.

responses.label: You may create a distraction that allows the questors to continue or disperse safely.
You may negotiate with the lord's man to withdraw his watch.

entries.body: A distraction that occupies the watcher's attention for ten minutes is sufficient for the questors to conclude and disperse through the back. The distraction can be almost anything plausible.

resolutions.label: create a distraction for the watcher

success.body:
The distraction works — a small dispute nearby that requires the watcher's attention for exactly the right duration. The questors disperse by the time he returns to the door.

The meeting is concluded. Its content is its own.

failure.body:
The watcher is better trained than the average street observer and returns to his post before the questors have finished. You have bought them less time than you needed.

The negotiation approach must try now.

entries.body: The watcher is a man doing a job he may not entirely believe in. A knight's authority — and the specific argument that the lord's order may exceed his legal jurisdiction given the Grail quest's royal sanction — might persuade him to find something more important to watch.

resolutions.label: persuade the watcher to abandon his post without direct conflict

success.body:
The argument about jurisdiction is one he cannot refute on his feet, and the knight making it is one he does not want to directly contradict. He decides he has been at this post long enough and moves on.

The questors are safe to finish.

failure.body:
His orders are specific enough that no jurisdictional argument moves him. He stays.

The distraction approach was always available.

entries.body:
The forest gathering at dawn is one that the Grail questors describe as the Vision Circle — questors who have experienced partial visions meeting to share what they have seen, in the hope that the partial visions together make a complete one. They are not secretive about their meeting, but the location is remote enough to have the quality of the hidden.

You have experienced a partial vision and you have been directed here.

responses.label: You may join the circle and share your vision.
You may observe first and determine whether your vision belongs in this circle.

entries.body: The circle receives you with the openness of people who have all arrived at the same place by different roads. Each person speaks in turn: what they saw, as precisely as possible, without interpretation.

resolutions.label: share your vision with complete precision and receive the others' in return

success.body:
Your vision completes something in the circle — fills a gap that the others could feel but not describe. In return, the circle's combined vision gives your partial something to attach to: a context, a direction, a meaning.

You ride away with a clearer sense of where the Grail is to be found.

failure.body:
Your vision is received but does not complete what the circle was reaching toward. The gap remains. You have added your piece, which is still something.

The incomplete is still part of the whole.

entries.body: Observing before joining is a form of discernment — you want to understand whether your vision belongs in this circle rather than another, and whether the circle is what it claims to be.

resolutions.label: observe the circle and determine its quality

success.body:
The circle is genuine. The precision of the speech, the absence of embellishment, the genuine quality of attention when one person speaks — these are not performed. You join with confidence.

Your vision belongs here. The joining is clean.

failure.body:
The observation is not sufficient time to determine the circle's quality — spiritual matters require a different kind of reading. You join anyway, uncertain.

Uncertain is still joining.

entries.body:
The mountain meeting is a gathering of hermits — seven of them, from seven different locations, who meet once a year on this high place to share what they have learned in the previous year. It is not secret, exactly; they have simply found a place where no one else comes.

You arrived on this mountain by a different route and found the meeting already underway.

responses.label: You may ask to join the hermits' council for this session.
You may sit at the meeting's edge and listen without asking.

entries.body: Seven hermits receiving a request to join their annual council will discuss the request. One of them is likely to be in favour, one against, and five observing which way the debate resolves.

resolutions.label: make the case for your inclusion and receive their decision

success.body:
Your case is received — not by argument but by the quality of your asking, which they evaluate with more precision than ordinary social situations allow. They admit you for the afternoon's portion of the council.

What is shared in that afternoon is not easily described, but it changes the shape of what you understand about Britain's spiritual state.

failure.body:
The council does not admit you — not from unkindness, but from the sense that your presence would change what is shared. You are given a space at the fire instead.

The fire, and the conversation during the meal that follows the council, are not nothing.

entries.body: Sitting at the edge without asking is its own kind of statement — it demonstrates that you do not require the formal admission, that you can receive whatever is available without demanding more.

resolutions.label: sit at the council's edge and receive what filters through

success.body:
The council does not exclude you — they have noticed you and made the decision to continue regardless, which means your presence has been accepted at the level of the edge. What filters through is considerable.

Seven hermits sharing a year's worth of hard-won knowledge is a remarkable thing to sit near.

failure.body:
The council notices you and adjusts what it shares — not excluding you, but modifying to what is appropriate for an observer rather than a participant. The adjustment is subtle. You notice it.

Some knowledge is appropriately not available to everyone.

entries.body:
The plains meeting is a questors' supply exchange — a practical gathering where those on the Grail road share provisions, information, and news of the road ahead. It has the informal structure of something that grew rather than was planned.

A questor recently returned from a significant encounter is speaking when you arrive. She is describing something she saw in a castle three days east.

responses.label: You may join the circle and hear the full account.
You may speak privately with the returned questor rather than in the group.

entries.body: The group's reception of the account shapes it — questions from twelve people produce more detail than one person's questions, but the detail is also more diffuse and harder to carry forward whole.

resolutions.label: receive the account in the group and contribute your own knowledge

success.body:
The exchange is genuine. You give information about the road west, receive information about the road east, and the castle three days ahead becomes clearer from the questor's account.

The group is better equipped after the sharing than before. So are you.

failure.body:
The group discussion becomes imprecise — too many questions, some well-intentioned wrong readings — and the account loses its shape. You carry something from it but less than the questor had to give.

Groups produce quantity; individuals sometimes produce quality.

entries.body: A private conversation with the returned questor gives her account without the shaping that group dynamics impose. The fuller account may contain things she would not say in the group.

resolutions.label: speak with her alone and receive the full account

success.body:
The full account contains what the group received and three things she did not say aloud: something she saw that she is not yet sure of, something she experienced that she has not yet named, and a specific detail about the castle that the group did not ask about and she did not volunteer.

The detail will matter later.

failure.body:
She gives you the same account she gave the group — the private setting does not produce additional disclosure. Either she has said everything she knows, or what she is holding back is not for you.

The full account is the group account.

entries.body:
The swamp gathering has been held here annually since before any living person can remember — the old families of this district meet to share the old knowledge, the kind that is not written because writing was never available to these people.

You were brought here by accident, following a guide who turned out to be going to the gathering. He is embarrassed. The gathering is uncertain.

responses.label: You may ask permission to stay and witness.
You may offer to leave and keep the gathering's location private.

entries.body: Asking permission is the honest acknowledgment that you are where you should not have been. The gathering's response will depend on what they see in you.

resolutions.label: ask honestly and accept whatever is decided

success.body:
The eldest of the gathering speaks for a few minutes with the others, and then she says: you may stay for the first portion and not the second. The first portion is the sharing of the year's events — ordinary, though told with a precision that the ordinary rarely receives. The second portion is theirs.

You stay for the first. You leave for the second. Both feel exactly right.

failure.body:
The gathering cannot agree on your presence in time, and the uncertainty of the debate is worse than your leaving would be. You are asked to go.

Honestly asked is honestly answered.

entries.body: Offering to leave — and to keep the location private — is a gift that may produce more than your presence would have. What you give in restraint sometimes opens more than what you take by staying.

resolutions.label: offer to leave and give your word of discretion

success.body:
The eldest looks at you for a long time after you give your word. Then she says: stay for the meal. Not the gathering, but the meal that follows it. The meal, it turns out, is where the gathering's real conversation happens.

You leave knowing the gathering's people and their current situation in the world. This will matter.

failure.body:
Your word is accepted and you leave. The discretion is appreciated. The door that might have opened does not.

Discretion is its own reward.

entries.body:
The sea meeting — four boats anchored together in a fog-hidden cove, the people on them speaking across the gunwales — is a gathering of sea-merchants trading information rather than goods. They exchange news, route intelligence, and warnings that the overland merchants do not have.

One boat has a man who just came from a port that holds news worth having.

responses.label: You may approach in a borrowed boat and offer something in exchange for news.
You may listen from the cove's rocks before deciding whether to approach.

entries.body: The merchants receive a stranger with caution and commercial openness in equal measure — a stranger is a potential source of information, which is the currency here. What you have to offer determines how the exchange goes.

resolutions.label: trade information with the merchants and get what you came for

success.body:
You offer what you have — road intelligence, news of the inland, a specific piece of recent information about a port they will visit — and it is valued correctly. In return, you receive the news from the recent port: a fleet's disposition, a supply route, something that will matter in the next week.

The exchange was fair.

failure.body:
What you have to offer is not valued as you valued it — they have the inland information already, from another recent visitor. The exchange is uneven and the news you receive is less complete than the news you gave.

Value is determined by scarcity you do not always control.

entries.body: From the rocks, the conversation between the four boats is partially audible — the water carries sound in unexpected directions in fog. What you hear informs what you offer when you approach.

resolutions.label: listen from the rocks and approach with the knowledge you gain

success.body:
Twenty minutes of listening gives you the shape of what they value and what they lack. You approach with exactly what they need and the exchange is simple.

The man from the recent port receives you directly when you show you already know the broad shape of what he is carrying. He fills in the detail.

failure.body:
The fog and the water distort the conversation enough that what you hear is misleading — you approach with what you believed they needed and they have it already.

Surface reading in conditions that distort surface is unreliable.

entries.body:
The city meeting in the back room of an inn is a resistance council — people who have not declared for Mordred and are organising the means of not declaring. They are neither armed nor political; they are ordinary people planning ordinary help for those who need it.

They are being watched by two of Mordred's agents, who you saw follow them in.

responses.label: You may deal with the agents before they can report.
You may warn the meeting and help them move before the report reaches anyone.

entries.body: Dealing with the agents — not harming them, but removing their ability to report — requires either disabling them physically or removing them from the vicinity long enough for the meeting to conclude and disperse.

resolutions.label: remove the agents from the vicinity before they can report

success.body:
A manufactured emergency, a false order, and the agents are occupied elsewhere for long enough. The meeting concludes and disperses before either agent returns to their post.

They will report something. But what they report will not lead anywhere useful.

failure.body:
One agent is removed; the other is too alert to the same approach. The second agent gets a message out before you can intervene.

The meeting must move now.

entries.body: Warning the meeting requires getting into the room without the agents knowing you have gone in. The inn's kitchen entrance, if it exists, would allow a passage that does not pass the front room where the agents are sitting.

resolutions.label: reach the meeting room without the agents knowing and warn them

success.body:
The kitchen entrance exists and the cook is sympathetic. The warning reaches the meeting with ten minutes to spare. They disperse through the kitchen and out through the yard while you sit down in the front room where the agents can see you.

The meeting never happened, as far as anyone can prove.

failure.body:
One of the agents follows you through the kitchen and sees the meeting dispersing. The dispersal is complete before any names are fixed in his memory, but the fact of the meeting is not deniable.

Fact without detail is difficult to prosecute. You hope.

entries.body:
The forest meeting has been arranged by a lord who refused to swear for Mordred — he has called together those in this region who share his position, to coordinate what resistance they can manage.

He has asked for a knight with Arthur's authority to attend and confirm that the king knows of their loyalty. You carry a letter that satisfies this requirement, if you choose to attend.

responses.label: You may attend the meeting and deliver the king's acknowledgment.
You may assess the lord's gathering before committing the king's name to it.

entries.body: The gathering is thirty people — lords, knights, and the households of each — who have gathered at some personal risk. The king's acknowledgment, delivered by a knight who carries his seal, will mean something specific to them.

resolutions.label: deliver the king's acknowledgment to the assembled loyalists

success.body:
The letter is read aloud. The thirty people hear the king's acknowledgment of their loyalty and his hope for their continued support. The acknowledgment is real — you carried it honestly.

The gathering is strengthened. Thirty people with a king behind them are not the same as thirty people alone.

failure.body:
The delivery is right but the moment is wrong — some division in the gathering has preceded your arrival, and the acknowledgment lands in a room that is already arguing about what loyalty requires. It does some good. Not the good it would have done an hour earlier.

Timing is a form of truth.

entries.body: The lord's gathering deserves assessment before the king's name is committed to it — if the gathering is genuine, the acknowledgment strengthens good people; if it is compromised, the acknowledgment could be used against Arthur.

resolutions.label: assess the gathering's loyalty before delivering the acknowledgment

success.body:
The assessment: genuine, uncompromised, and operating at considerable personal risk. You deliver the acknowledgment with confidence.

The king's name, given cleanly, serves the people it was given to.

failure.body:
The assessment is inconclusive — you cannot determine whether one element of the gathering is trustworthy or not. You deliver the acknowledgment with a private caveat to the lord about the element you are uncertain of.

Inconclusiveness handled honestly is the right response.

entries.body:
The mountain meeting is one that both sides of the war believe they have organised. Each side has sent an envoy to meet secretly with the other, under the impression that the meeting is their initiative. Someone has arranged for both to believe they are the instigator.

You have stumbled into this because the third party who arranged it has disappeared, and without them the two envoys will discover the manipulation before the meeting can produce anything useful.

responses.label: You may manage both envoys through the meeting as though the arrangement was legitimate.
You may explain the situation to both and see whether they will meet honestly.

entries.body: Managing both envoys requires sustained performance across both conversations — keeping each convinced that the meeting was their initiative while steering toward an outcome worth having.

resolutions.label: manage the meeting and produce a genuine outcome from a manipulated beginning

success.body:
The manipulation succeeds in producing something real — a specific agreement about a specific matter that both sides actually want and that only required someone to get them in the same room. The room was manufactured. The agreement is genuine.

You do not tell either of them about the third party. That story is for another day.

failure.body:
One envoy begins to suspect the arrangement is not what they believed, and the suspicion spreads before the meeting can conclude. The manipulation unravels.

Honesty was always the harder but more reliable path.

entries.body: Both envoys, told the truth, have a choice: leave, or meet on honest terms. The honest meeting is a different meeting from the manipulated one, but it may produce something the manipulated one would not have.

resolutions.label: tell both the truth and let them decide whether to meet honestly

success.body:
Both envoys, told the truth simultaneously in each other's presence, make the same calculation: the truth is embarrassing for whoever arranged this, and both of them would rather proceed than admit to having been manipulated.

They meet. Honestly, with all the awkwardness of people who were manipulated into the right room. The outcome is smaller than the manipulated meeting might have produced. It is also real.

failure.body:
One envoy leaves when told the truth — they would rather leave than meet in compromised circumstances. The meeting fails.

Honesty clears the room. Clearing the room is not always the desired outcome.

entries.body:
The plains meeting is a parley — two small groups of armed men, one from each side, meeting in the middle of the contested ground to discuss the return of specific prisoners. The parley has been going for two hours and has not concluded.

You have no official role. But you have been waved forward by both sides, which suggests both sides want an outside opinion.

responses.label: You may join the parley as an independent voice.
You may speak privately with each side before joining the parley.

entries.body: An independent voice in a deadlocked parley needs to identify what the deadlock actually is — usually it is not the stated issue but something adjacent to it.

resolutions.label: find the actual deadlock and propose a solution to it

success.body:
The stated issue is the prisoners' condition; the actual issue is each side's fear that releasing prisoners first signals weakness. You propose simultaneous release at a specific point — a solution so simple it had not been said aloud because neither side could propose it without looking weak.

The parley concludes in twenty minutes.

failure.body:
The actual deadlock is not visible from outside the two sides' respective constraints — they have not explained everything, and what is not explained is where the deadlock lives.

The parley continues.

entries.body: Speaking privately with each side first gives you the fuller picture that the parley format prevents — what each side cannot say in front of the other.

resolutions.label: hear both sides privately and carry the fuller truth into the parley

success.body:
Each side tells you, privately, what they cannot say: the real constraint, the real fear. Armed with both, you enter the parley and propose the exact solution that addresses both private concerns without requiring either to state them publicly.

The parley concludes.

failure.body:
One side declines the private conversation — they did not wave you forward to learn more, they waved you forward to break the other side's position. The private approach reveals this, which is itself useful information.

Knowing the dynamic is worth the failed attempt.

entries.body:
The swamp meeting has been arranged to transfer a specific prisoner — not through an official exchange, but privately, because the official channels are compromised. The prisoner is someone Arthur's remaining forces need and Mordred's forces believe they have adequately secured.

The transfer point is this island. Both parties are due to arrive within the hour. You are the third party who arranged it.

responses.label: You may manage the transfer directly.
You may ensure the approach routes are safe before either party arrives.

entries.body: Managing the transfer directly means being the guarantee — the person both sides have agreed to trust with the prisoner in the moment between their respective hands. The prisoner's safety and the exchange's completion rest with you.

resolutions.label: receive the prisoner and complete the transfer

success.body:
Both parties arrive, make their respective calculations, and the transfer is made through you. The prisoner — who has not spoken since their extraction — looks at you with the expression of someone who had given up and is revising that assessment.

The swamp path takes you all out before anyone changes their mind.

failure.body:
One party arrives late, which shifts the dynamic — the other party becomes nervous and the transfer stalls. You manage the stall but not the completion; the prisoner remains in transit, which is neither safe nor resolved.

The stall must be resolved before someone makes a bad decision.

entries.body: Securing the approach routes before both parties arrive gives the transfer the best possible conditions. If either approach is watched, the transfer will not succeed regardless of the principals' best intentions.

resolutions.label: scout the approach routes and clear any watchers

success.body:
One approach route has a watcher — Mordred's man, insurance against exactly this kind of transfer. You redirect him through a conversation about a false threat elsewhere.

Both routes are clear when the parties arrive. The transfer completes.

failure.body:
The watcher is found but not successfully redirected — he sees through the false threat and you must physically remove him from the route. The delay means the parties arrive before you have confirmed all routes are clear.

The transfer proceeds under uncertainty, which is a different situation than the one intended.

entries.body:
The sea meeting is a council of neutral harbour masters — men who have kept their harbours open to both sides and are now discovering that both sides want them to close to one. They are meeting to decide collectively what a single harbour master cannot decide alone.

Their conclusion will determine the coastal supply situation for Arthur's remaining forces.

responses.label: You may speak before the council on Arthur's behalf.
You may find the harbour master most sympathetic to Arthur and work through them.

entries.body: Speaking before the council is speaking to a room of practical men whose primary consideration is the continued existence of their harbours and their livelihoods. Arthur's cause must be framed in those terms to have any effect.

resolutions.label: make the practical case for keeping the harbours open to Arthur

success.body:
The practical case is this: Arthur's defeat would consolidate power in Mordred's hands, and consolidated power is bad for neutral harbour masters in the long term. The council hears this and votes to maintain current arrangements for another month.

A month is longer than Arthur might have, but it is real.

failure.body:
The practical case is sound but the council's risk assessment puts Mordred ahead in the long run, which makes the practical case for maintaining neutrality rather than choosing Arthur. The vote goes toward neutrality.

Neutrality is not the closure Arthur cannot afford.

entries.body: One harbour master in particular has been keeping his harbour open to Arthur's ships without Mordred's agents noticing — he has done this because he believes in the cause. Working through him gives Arthur a committed advocate in the room.

resolutions.label: work through the sympathetic harbour master and amplify their voice

success.body:
With specific information and specific arguments, you equip him to make the case more effectively than he could have made it alone. The council hears from one of their own and votes differently than they would have voted after your speech.

Three harbours are specifically committed to Arthur's cause. Not neutrality — commitment.

failure.body:
The harbour master's voice carries, but his position on the council is weaker than you believed — two others actively oppose him and the vote is split in ways that leave the situation unchanged.

The split is its own kind of stasis.

entries.body:
The old building at the city's centre — Roman, by the construction, and something before that — has been sealed since anyone can remember, but the recent rain has disturbed the earth at its base and the entrance is visible for the first time. The city's children have already begun daring each other to enter.

The ruin is more than it appears. The stones at the threshold have carvings that are neither Roman nor Christian.

responses.label: You may enter the ruin and see what lies within.
You may study the carvings before entering and understand what you are entering.

entries.body: Within, the ruin has survived better than the outside suggested — the ceiling holds, the air is dry, and the walls are painted in pigments that have not faded. The paintings are of a Britain that does not exist anymore.

resolutions.label: explore the ruin fully and bring back what you find

success.body:
The inner chamber holds a cache — not treasure in the ordinary sense, but documents on durable material, records of a lord's covenant with this place that predates the Roman occupation. The covenant has implications for land rights that the city's current lord has not been aware of.

You bring the documents to the light.

failure.body:
The inner chamber collapses when you reach it — the disturbed earth has made the ruin less stable than it appeared. You retreat with a bruise and a memory of the paintings.

The paintings alone are worth the bruise.

entries.body: The carvings are in the old language — the language before Latin, the one that is not entirely lost because the hermits who know the land remember pieces of it. What they say is: this is a threshold, and thresholds require acknowledgment.

resolutions.label: acknowledge the threshold and enter with the correct respect

success.body:
The acknowledgment, made in whatever form you can manage, produces a quality of receptiveness in the ruin — not supernaturally, but in the practical sense that entering with the right attention produces different things than entering without it.

What you find inside is more than the cache. It is the sense of the place as it was.

failure.body:
The acknowledgment is made imprecisely — the right form but the wrong spirit — and the ruin receives it neutrally. You enter and find what the ruin contains, without the additional quality that the right acknowledgment would have provided.

You still find the cache.

entries.body:
The forest stones are in a clearing that the trees avoid — a circle of tall stones, older than anyone can date, with a quality that makes the clearing feel larger than its dimensions suggest. Moss on the north side of each stone; no moss elsewhere. The clearing has the character of a place that has been doing something for a very long time.

A raven sits on the tallest stone and watches you approach.

responses.label: You may enter the stone circle.
You may study the circle from outside and read its character before entering.

entries.body: Inside the circle, the quality changes — the forest sounds diminish and the light is the same but seems differently placed. The raven has moved but is still visible on the edge of the clearing.

resolutions.label: spend time in the circle and receive what it offers

success.body:
The circle offers something specific: a clearing of whatever has accumulated since your last significant rest. Not a vision, not information — a quality of fresh beginning, as though the intervening road has been assessed and set in order.

You ride away lighter.

failure.body:
The circle does nothing perceptible. Either you are not what it requires, or the time is wrong, or the offering is simply not there today.

Not every old place is active every day.

entries.body: Reading the circle from outside — its orientation, the wear patterns on the stones, the way the clearing holds sound — produces a working understanding of what the circle is for, before you commit to entering it.

resolutions.label: read the circle from outside before entering

success.body:
The orientation tells you: the circle is positioned for dawn of the winter solstice, which means its active quality is strongest at that specific moment. Today is not that moment, but you understand what the circle is and why it was built where it was.

You carry this understanding forward. It will matter.

failure.body:
The circle's character is not readable from outside it — the knowledge required to read it is precisely what being inside it would give you. The question and the answer are the same door.

Entering without preparation was always the option.

entries.body:
The mountain fortification was a Roman garrison post, abandoned when the legions left and not reoccupied because the mountain is too difficult for the local lords and too exposed for meaningful shelter. The walls still stand; the interior is roofless but intact.

Something was left in the commander's quarters — you can see it from the gate, a shape that is not stone or furniture.

responses.label: You may cross the courtyard and investigate what was left.
You may search the perimeter first and ensure the ruin holds no present danger.

entries.body: The commander's quarters hold: a cache of Roman military documents sealed in oilskin, a ring that is too fine for a soldier, and a small carved deity of the pre-Roman type that was clearly placed here by someone after the Romans left.

resolutions.label: assess and take what is useful from what was left

success.body:
The documents are interesting but local — a surveyor's notes on the mountain passes that is still accurate. The ring has a value. The carved deity has a significance you recognise from an older account.

You take the map notes and leave the rest as found.

failure.body:
The assessment is inconclusive — you take too much or too little, disturbing the arrangement without clear benefit.

Ancient things have their own logic about being touched.

entries.body: The ruin is large enough to hold occupants who would not be visible from the gate. A systematic perimeter check before committing to the interior is the cautious approach.

resolutions.label: check the ruin's perimeter before exploring its interior

success.body:
The perimeter reveals: the ruin is currently unoccupied, but it has been occupied recently — the ashes of a fire in the western tower, food scraps in the barracks. Outlaws or travellers, gone a day ago.

Knowing this changes how you explore the interior. You find something they left behind in haste.

failure.body: The perimeter check takes longer than it should on the difficult mountain ground, and what you find confirms only that the ruin is not obviously dangerous. The interior holds what the interior holds.

entries.body:
The ruins on the open plain are the remains of a hall that burned two generations past — the family that owned it died out, or fled, and the stones have been used for a generation of boundary walls and foundations. What is left is the hearth and the hall's north wall, which bears a carving.

The carving is a record — genealogy, or a deed, or some form of testament — and it is in a language you can read.

responses.label: You may read and copy the carving.
You may find out who the hall belonged to before reading it.

entries.body: The carving, copied carefully, is a claim — a lord's record of a covenant made with the land itself, witnessed by the names of people long dead, marking a specific promise that his line would keep this particular valley in good faith.

resolutions.label: copy the carving and carry it to where it can be properly understood

success.body:
The copy is made carefully and the carving is understood at least partially: the covenant was made for a specific purpose that is still relevant, with a specific promise that was kept through three generations and then lost with the family's end.

The covenant and the valley's current situation are now in your hands to decide about.

failure.body:
The language is older than you thought and the reading is incomplete — some of the most specific portions of the covenant are in a dialect you do not know well.

The copy is made. The reading must wait for a better scholar.

entries.body: The local farming family half a mile south has been here longer than the ruin — their grandparents knew the hall's family. What they remember may give the carving a context.

resolutions.label: learn the hall's history before reading the carving

success.body:
The farmer's grandmother remembers: the hall's lord was a man of unusual integrity who refused to bend his valley's use to any lord above him, holding it for the people who lived there. The carving, read with that context, is the record of exactly that refusal.

What the valley needs now is the same thing it always needed. You know what to carry forward.

failure.body:
The farming family's memory of the hall is general rather than specific — a good lord, a long time ago, a fire that was deliberate but whose origin is not known. The context is insufficient to give the carving its meaning.

The carving must stand on its own.

entries.body:
The sunken ruin in the swamp's centre is visible at low water — a stone hall, or what remains of one, with its upper stories above the waterline and its lower courses accessible only by swimming or wading. It sank over decades as the swamp expanded.

Something in the ruin is still generating light at night — local accounts are consistent about this — and you are here in the late afternoon.

responses.label: You may wade in and find the source of the light.
You may wait for darkness and approach when the light is visible.

entries.body: Wading in requires navigating the sunken courtyard's floor, which is under two feet of swamp water and is uneven with fallen stones. The upper stories are accessible by a staircase that is partially submerged.

resolutions.label: wade through the flooded lower level to reach the upper stories

success.body:
The upper chamber is dry — miraculously so, given the water below — and the source of the light is a brazier that burns without apparent fuel, tended by nothing visible. The brazier has a quality of things that have been burning for a very long time.

You do not extinguish it. You note what it illuminates.

failure.body:
The staircase gives way at the second step and you are in the water before you can correct. The upper stories are not reachable today without better equipment.

The light continues its work without you.

entries.body: The light at night is a warm gold that reaches further across the swamp than any ordinary lamp — perhaps a quarter mile. Approaching it from the darkness gives you the quality of the light before you see the source.

resolutions.label: approach the ruin in darkness and read the light

success.body:
The light from the ruined upper chamber illuminates the swamp in a specific direction — east, toward a ford that is not on any map but that is visible in the light's path on the water.

You mark the ford. It will matter.

failure.body:
The swamp at night is difficult to navigate without the light as a false friend — you approach but misjudge distance in the dark.

Dark and water together require more preparation than you brought.

entries.body:
The coastal ruin is a sea-tower — built to watch the approaches, abandoned when the threat it watched for changed direction. The sea has battered three of its four walls but one remains, and at the base of the remaining wall there is a sealed door that the sea has not yet reached.

The tide is going out.

responses.label: You may open the sealed door and see what the tower holds.
You may work out why the tower was abandoned before opening what it sealed away.

entries.body: The door is sealed with iron that has rusted to a single piece — sealed by accident of time rather than intention. The opening requires force rather than skill.

resolutions.label: break the seal and open the door

success.body:
The chamber behind the door is the tower's archive — sealed before abandonment, preserved by the same iron that sealed it. Logs of every ship that passed this coast for forty years, in the distinctive shorthand of the watchmen.

The logs end on a specific date and the final entry says: the thing we were watching for has arrived from the north, not the sea.

failure.body:
The rust is complete — there is no door anymore, only a wall of corroded iron. The seal has become its own permanence.

The archive stays inside.

entries.body: Why a watchtower was abandoned tells you what it was watching for and what changed — and that context may change what opening the sealed door means. The village a mile inland has accounts going back further than the tower's abandonment.

resolutions.label: learn the tower's history and then open it with that knowledge

success.body:
The village elder's great-grandfather built the tower to watch for a specific fleet — a fleet that arrived from the north, not the sea, making the tower obsolete. They sealed it and left.

Armed with this, you open the door and the archive is instantly legible. The fleet's arrival is the context for everything.

failure.body:
The village's history is not clear enough to provide the specific context — several conflicting accounts, an old dispute about what actually happened. You open the door without the full context.

The archive provides its own context, partially.

entries.body:
The ruined chapel in the city was once a place of significant spiritual activity — the Grail questors say that the spiritual residue of active faith does not depart quickly from a place of prayer, and this chapel burned in a fire thirty years past. The walls stand; the roof is gone; the altar is exposed to the sky.

A questor has been sitting at the altar for three hours and has not moved.

responses.label: You may approach the questor and speak to her.
You may also sit in the chapel and receive what it holds.

entries.body: She is not in distress — you can see this from the doorway. She is in prayer of a specific kind, the kind that looks like absence from the outside and is presence from the inside.

resolutions.label: wait until she is ready to be spoken to, then speak

success.body:
She speaks when she is ready, which is fifteen minutes later, and what she shares of her experience in the chapel is something she has been carrying for the three weeks of the quest and could not put down alone.

The chapel gave her the setting down.

failure.body:
You approach before she is ready and the approach disturbs the thing that was almost settled. She is gracious about it.

Patience for other people's processes is its own practice.

entries.body: The questors say that ruined sacred places hold something the occupied ones do not — the prayer of thirty years of use, with the distraction of organisation removed. An open-air altar is not worse than a roofed one; it may be better.

resolutions.label: sit in the roofless chapel and receive what it holds

success.body:
What the chapel holds is a specific quality of accumulated prayer — not a vision, not information, but a quality of being heard that the active quest sometimes lacks. You sit for an hour. The sky is the ceiling.

You rise different in a way you cannot yet articulate.

failure.body:
The open sky is a distraction rather than an invitation — the mind goes to the clouds rather than the prayer. The hour passes in something that is not quite the thing.

Not every place works for every person at every moment.

entries.body:
The forest monastery was abandoned a generation ago when its monks were scattered by a local dispute — not dissolved, just scattered, its people gone different directions. The buildings remain, overgrown but sound, and the scriptorium's books were walled up before the last monk left.

A hermit nearby says the walled scriptorium holds texts that bear directly on the Grail quest.

responses.label: You may find and open the walled scriptorium.
You may speak with the hermit about what specifically the texts contain.

entries.body: The walled section of the scriptorium is in the north building — you can see the newer stonework, lighter in colour, that marks the sealed section. Opening it requires careful work with the right tools.

resolutions.label: open the walled scriptorium and recover the texts

success.body:
Three hours of careful removal and the section opens: twelve bound texts in remarkable preservation, wrapped in treated cloth. The hermit was not wrong — these texts are among the rarest accounts of the Grail's history outside of Corbenic itself.

You carry them carefully, knowing you are carrying something worth more than you are.

failure.body:
The wall comes down, but the condition of the texts inside was not as well-preserved as the outer wall suggested — moisture had entered somewhere, and half the texts are damaged beyond recovery.

The remainder is still significant.

entries.body: The hermit was one of the monastery's monks before he became a hermit — he knows what was sealed and why. Knowing the specific content before opening the wall changes how you handle what you find.

resolutions.label: receive the hermit's account of the texts' content and significance

success.body:
He tells you: three of the twelve texts are accounts of Grail sightings that predate the current quest by centuries. The others are commentary. The three accounts specifically contradict a common assumption about where the Grail can be found.

You open the wall knowing what to look for. The three texts are the first things you retrieve.

failure.body:
The hermit's memory of the texts' content is vaguer than his certainty about their significance — he remembers the significance without the specifics. You know they matter without knowing what they say.

The opening reveals it.

entries.body:
The mountain ruin was a chapel, not a fortress — a small sacred building at a high point, placed there to mark a specific event in the old accounts. The Grail questors say: the chapel's location marks where something was set down that was later taken up again.

You have climbed to the chapel and you can see in three directions.

responses.label: You may meditate in the chapel's remains on what was set down and taken up.
You may read the view and understand what the chapel was built to mark.

entries.body: The meditation the chapel invites is specific: what was once set down and then taken up, in the old accounts, was the weight of a particular grief. The chapel was built to mark the setting-down, the temporary relief that happened here before the weight was resumed.

resolutions.label: meditate on what the chapel marks and receive its gift

success.body:
The meditation is real — the kind that produces something, rather than the kind that only passes time. What it produces is a quality of restored perspective: the weight you have been carrying is set down briefly, assessed, and resumed with clearer knowledge of what it is and what it is for.

The setting-down was the gift.

failure.body:
The meditation does not quite settle — the view distracts or the wind is too insistent or something in you resists the specific gift this place offers.

Not every gift can be received the first time.

entries.body: The view from the chapel reveals what was here before the chapel: a convergence of roads, visible from this height, that no longer exists as roads but exists as the slight variations in the valley's farming strips. The chapel marked the convergence.

resolutions.label: read the view and understand the convergence the chapel marked

success.body:
The convergence, understood from the height, is also the convergence of three valley drainage systems — a place where the old roads were determined by the water's logic. The chapel marked not the roads but the water's pattern.

Knowing this changes how you read the landscape going forward.

failure.body:
The convergence is legible in theory but too old and too overgrown for your specific reading skills. The pattern is there; you cannot see it clearly enough to use it.

Aerial knowledge requires patience to build.

entries.body:
The plains ruin is a questors' station — a house maintained for those on the Grail road, now abandoned when the road's traffic thinned. The questors' accounts say that the station was maintained so long by people of good will that the building absorbed something of that good will and holds it still.

The roof is partial; the fire can be lit; the threshold has its old blessing-mark.

responses.label: You may stop here for the night and receive what the station offers.
You may speak with the questors about what this station means for those on the road.

entries.body: A night in a blessed building — even a ruined one — is a different experience than a night in an ordinary building. The partial roof is enough; the fire is sufficient; the walls hold something.

resolutions.label: spend the night in the station and receive what it holds

success.body:
The night is a good night — the quality of sleep is different, the dreams are cleaner, and the morning brings a clarity that the previous week has not offered. Whatever the accumulated good will of the station's maintenance holds, it was given.

You ride better after a good night.

failure.body:
The partial roof is insufficient against the specific weather of this night and the fire cannot compensate. The sleep is poor and the morning is cold.

The station was a good idea. The night was not cooperative.

entries.body: The questors who maintained this station — people who never met each other, across fifty years — created a tradition of specific acts of maintenance: the threshold mark, the fire prepared, the water drawn. Each act was intended for the next questor. Learning the tradition is learning what to leave for those who come after.

resolutions.label: learn the tradition and fulfil it before moving on

success.body:
You spend the morning fulfilling what the station requires: re-marking the threshold, drawing fresh water, preparing the fire, adding your name to the book of those who maintained this station.

You are part of the tradition now. The questor who comes after you will find it kept.

failure.body:
The tradition's specific requirements are more detailed than you were told, and your fulfillment is approximate rather than exact. The station is maintained but not wholly maintained.

Approximate care is still care.

entries.body:
The sunken ruin in the swamp is, according to the questors' accounts, one of the places the Grail was held between its appearances — not the Grail itself, which is elsewhere, but the vessel's impression, the spiritual weight of its former presence. The ruin is partially accessible at low water.

A questor who entered it a week ago said she could feel where it had been.

responses.label: You may enter the ruin and seek what the questor found.
You may speak with the questor before entering.

entries.body: The ruin at low water is accessible to the upper level — the lower is still submerged. The upper level has a chamber whose ceiling is lower than the rest, its stones arranged with a care that the rest of the building does not show.

resolutions.label: enter the careful chamber and receive what it holds

success.body:
The chamber holds what the questor described — not the Grail, but the quality of a space that was used for something of immense importance. The impression is real. What it tells you about the Grail's nature is not easily said, but it is understood.

You carry it forward.

failure.body:
The chamber holds what it holds, but you are not in a state to receive it — too tired, too distracted, too much of the world still in you. You emerge knowing you were in a significant place.

Significance is not always receivable.

entries.body: The questor who found the chamber can describe what she experienced, and the description can prepare you — or it can set up an expectation that the experience cannot meet.

resolutions.label: decide whether to receive her account or enter without it

success.body:
You ask her to describe only the practical aspects — the chamber's location, the conditions required — and not the experience itself. The preparation without the expectation is the right balance.

You enter prepared without being predetermined.

failure.body:
The distinction between practical preparation and experiential expectation is harder to maintain in conversation than in theory, and you leave with more expectation than was useful.

Expectation is a form of obstruction.

entries.body:
The coastal ruin is an old signal tower, and its significance for the Grail questors is this: it was built by a king who saw the Grail in a dream and believed it would come from the sea. He watched from this tower for twenty years. His account of those twenty years is carved into the tower's inner wall.

The account, if the questors' tradition is right, contains the specific question that heals the Fisher King.

responses.label: You may read the account on the tower wall.
You may read the account only after purifying yourself in the sea below.

entries.body: The account is in an old script but legible with patience. The tower's inner wall is well-preserved — sea air has been the enemy and the inland-facing wall has escaped it.

resolutions.label: read the account carefully and in full

success.body:
Twenty years of watching, recorded with the precision of a man who had nothing to do but watch and was not distracted from it. The question he arrived at — the one that the watching produced — is the question the tradition says heals the king.

You have it now. The responsibility is yours.

failure.body:
The account is legible but the specific question — the last entry — is in a damaged section. You have the twenty years and not the conclusion.

The conclusion was the whole point.

entries.body: The questors' tradition says: the account should only be read by those who have made themselves available through an act of purification. The sea below the tower is the specific act the tradition describes.

resolutions.label: enter the sea in purification and then read the account

success.body:
The purification is cold and thorough and real — not a ceremony but a genuine preparation, the sea attending to what it attends to. You climb back to the tower wet and cleansed and you read the account from a different state than you would have before.

The question on the wall is the question you were prepared to receive.

failure.body:
The tradition's requirements are more specific than simply entering the sea — there is a quality of the entering that you do not quite achieve, and the reading afterward is clear but not transformed.

The account is read. Its full weight is not received.

entries.body:
The city's ruins are war ruins — a hall burned last month, its family fled or dead, its contents scattered or taken. The building's shell is still standing and someone has been sheltering in it: a family from the district, dispossessed by the same violence that burned the hall.

They are in the ruin's most intact section, cooking over a fire that will eventually attract notice.

responses.label: You may help them find better shelter or leave the city safely.
You may ensure the ruin is safe enough for them to stay in the short term.

entries.body: Better shelter or safe passage out of the city — both require knowledge of what is available and what the current situation allows. The family has three children and a grandmother and they cannot move quickly.

resolutions.label: find them a safer place or a way out

success.body:
You find a situation that works: a religious house at the city's west end that is taking families in, no questions asked, at the direction of a prior who has decided this is what a religious house is for. The family reaches it before dark.

The ruin holds no one now.

failure.body:
The options are either unsafe or inaccessible to a family moving slowly with children. You find nothing adequate and return to them with that news, which is the worst kind of news to bring to people who need better news.

The ruin is not the worst place, after all.

entries.body: Securing the ruin as temporary shelter requires fixing what can be fixed — the roof section that lets rain in, the wall that is unstable, the fire that needs moving before it does what fire does to damaged buildings.

resolutions.label: make the ruin safe enough for the family to shelter in

success.body:
Three hours of work makes the ruin's best section adequately weatherproof and the fire safely positioned. The family's situation is not resolved. They are safer while they work on resolving it.

Sometimes you can only fix the immediate problem. That is still something.

failure.body:
The ruin is more damaged than it appeared, and what you fix reveals something else needing fixing. After two hours you have improved things but not secured them.

Improved is what was possible.

entries.body:
The forest ruins are a monastery burned in the early weeks of the war — its monks scattered, its library burned, its church walls still standing over a floor of ash. One monk remained: too old to flee, too committed to leave. He lives in the undercroft.

He has been here through the war and he knows things about the region's movement of forces that scouts have not managed to gather.

responses.label: You may speak with the old monk about what he has observed.
You may help him first before asking anything.

entries.body: He speaks readily, having had no one to speak to for some time. His observations are a scout's information gathered from a different vantage: the sounds of movement, the types of supplies being transported, the directions of messengers.

resolutions.label: receive his intelligence and assess its value

success.body:
His intelligence is surprisingly useful — the specific detail about a supply route that has been moved, visible from his undercroft's narrow window, is information that changes the situation's map. He did not know it was useful. He was simply paying attention.

You ride with the map changed.

failure.body:
His observations are real but not translatable into actionable intelligence without a broader context that you cannot provide in the conversation's duration.

The data exists. The framework to use it is incomplete.

entries.body: He needs practical things: food, a repaired section of the undercroft where water comes in, and a letter to a specific person who may not know he is alive.

resolutions.label: help him with what he needs before asking for anything

success.body:
The helping comes first and the conversation comes after, and the conversation is entirely different in quality from what it would have been if you had asked before helping. He gives you more than the intelligence — he gives you a context for the intelligence that makes it actionable.

The help bought something worth more than the help.

failure.body:
The help takes longer than expected — the undercroft repair is more complex than it appeared — and by the end of it there is no time for the conversation.

The help was still the right choice.

entries.body:
The mountain ruin was a signal post in Arthur's communication network — one of a chain of fire-beacons that could relay a message across Britain in a matter of hours. The chain is broken now: too many posts abandoned or destroyed. This one is intact.

With one person here and someone at the next post, a message could be relayed. Both posts are intact. Neither is staffed.

responses.label: You may staff this post and signal the next one to see if it is still watched.
You may ride to the next post and determine its condition before committing to the chain.

entries.body: The fire-basket is intact and the signal code is carved into the post's wood — a simplified version, sufficient for the most urgent messages. Whether the next post is watched is the unknown.

resolutions.label: light the beacon and send a test signal

success.body:
The signal is sent: the emergency pattern, which requires no interpretation. The next post — two miles east — responds with the acknowledgment. Someone is there. The chain is partially intact.

You have restored one link. The chain has value again.

failure.body:
The test signal goes out and receives no answer. Either the next post is unwatch, or the signal was sent incorrectly, or conditions are wrong. No acknowledgment comes.

The next post must be investigated directly.

entries.body: Riding to the next post — two miles through mountain terrain — gives you direct knowledge of its condition before committing to the chain strategy. If the post is unusable, the strategy fails regardless.

resolutions.label: ride to the next post and assess its condition

success.body:
The next post is intact and, it turns out, staffed — by a boy who has been tending it out of habit because his father tended it before him. He did not know if anyone would ever signal again.

You have found a keeper for the chain. The signal network lives.

failure.body:
The next post's fire-basket is destroyed — too damaged to repair quickly. The chain cannot be restored through this link.

The assessment was worth the ride.

entries.body:
The plains ruins are what is left of a village. Not abandoned — destroyed, two months past, in an engagement that the war's accounts will not record because both sides prefer not to acknowledge what happened here. The village's remaining people are in the ruin, living in its shells.

They do not want to be found by anyone who might be from either side.

responses.label: You may identify yourself clearly and offer what help you can.
You may approach slowly and let them see you before you reach them.

entries.body: Clear identification — who you are, whose cause you serve, what you have — gives them information they need to make a decision about whether to receive you. The risk is that a side association makes them more afraid rather than less.

resolutions.label: identify yourself clearly and offer help without condition

success.body:
The identification produces wariness and then, slowly, a kind of relief — a knight from Arthur's remaining cause is not, to these people, the worst possible arrival. They receive you.

What they need is simple and specific. You provide what you can.

failure.body:
The side association is the wrong one for these specific people — Arthur's cause is associated, in their recent experience, with something painful. They close the ruin's entrance.

Identification was honest. The honesty was not what they needed.

entries.body: Slow approach, visible, hands open — giving them maximum time and maximum information about your intentions through your body rather than your words. People who have been hurt by the war read bodies before they read speech.

resolutions.label: approach slowly and let them set the terms of the meeting

success.body:
They see you before you reach them and they do not hide, which means they have decided you are not a threat. One person comes forward when you stop. You do not speak first.

They speak. The conversation is entirely theirs, at their pace, and it produces an understanding and a small amount of help that they will carry forward.

failure.body:
They hide before you stop — the slow approach was not slow enough, or the approach angle was wrong. You arrive at an empty ruin that is not empty.

You leave provisions at the ruin's centre. Someone will find them.

entries.body:
The swamp ruin is a recent one — a lord's hunting lodge burned in the first week of the war to prevent Mordred's forces from using it. The burning was done badly: the lodge's lower level is intact, including a lockbox that the lord's steward sealed before they fled.

The lord's steward has asked you to recover what the lockbox contains, and has given you the key.

responses.label: You may enter the ruin and recover the lockbox.
You may determine what the lockbox contains before entering the damaged building.

entries.body: The lower level's entrance is accessible but the building above it is damaged — it has survived so far but it will not survive indefinitely, and 'indefinitely' may or may not include the time you need to reach the lockbox.

resolutions.label: enter the damaged ruin and recover the lockbox

success.body:
You reach the lower level, find the lockbox where the steward described it, and extract it before anything further comes down. The lockbox is heavy — deeds and silver and something that clinks.

The lord will be glad of its recovery, whatever the war's outcome.

failure.body:
The upper level shifts as you reach the lower one and you must exit before you reach the lockbox. The building is not safe to re-enter today.

The steward's instructions did not account for structural instability.
