entries.body: The steward described the lockbox's contents as 'the lord's private documents and a portion of silver.' What specifically the documents are would change how urgently the recovery needs to be attempted at what risk.

resolutions.label: learn what the documents are before risking the damaged building

success.body:
The steward, pressed gently, specifies: the documents include a land grant that was disputed before the war and would be more disputed after it. The recovery is genuinely urgent — without the document, the land is vulnerable.

You enter the ruin knowing exactly what to prioritise.

failure.body:
The steward cannot say more specifically without revealing things he was asked not to reveal, and the attempt to find out produces more confidentiality than information.

You enter without the specific knowledge. It is enough to know the urgency.

entries.body:
The coastal ruin is recent — a harbour fort destroyed in the opening naval engagement of the war, its garrison either dead or fled. The harbour below it is navigable and Mordred's ships know this; Arthur's people do not, because the charts showing the deep channel were kept in the fort.

The charts may still be in the fort's chart room, which was in the lower level.

responses.label: You may descend into the ruined fort and find the charts.
You may find a local fisherman who knows the channel from experience.

entries.body: The lower level is accessible but dangerous — the fort's collapse has left the lower chambers partially exposed to the sea, and the tidal action has been working at the structure for months.

resolutions.label: descend and recover the charts

success.body:
The chart room is intact — its position in the lower level has protected it from the elements that took the upper structure. The charts are oilskin-wrapped and undamaged.

Arthur's ships will know this harbour now.

failure.body:
The tidal action has filled the chart room with water. The charts, if they survived, are submerged. The recovery would require diving equipment you do not have.

The fisherman approach is the remaining option.

entries.body: The fishing families who have worked this harbour for generations know every depth by feel — they do not need charts because the knowledge is in their hands and their boats' keels. One old fisherman, specifically, knows the deep channel the charts describe.

resolutions.label: find the fisherman and have him describe the channel for a written record

success.body:
He describes it exactly — the specific bearings, the depth at the critical points, the rocks that are under the surface at high water and visible at low. You write it down in the format a ship's navigator can use.

The charts existed in his head. They exist on paper now.

failure.body:
The fisherman's knowledge is real but not translatable to the navigational format required — the knowledge is bodily, not verbal, and the translation loses too much to be trusted.

The charts in the submerged room remain the authoritative version.

entries.body:
The city's eastern quarter held a skirmish two days past — a lord's men against a merchant coalition that had grown large enough to field swords. The skirmish is over; the quarter is not. Men from both sides remain in the streets, watching each other, and the truce that ended the fighting is being maintained by nothing stronger than mutual exhaustion.

The wounded have not been fully tended. The dead have not been fully collected. Both of these facts are making the truce more fragile.

responses.label: You may organise the care of the wounded and collection of the dead.
You may speak with the leaders of both sides and formalise the truce.

entries.body: Organising practical care — healers, stretchers, water — changes the quality of the situation more than speeches do. Both sides have wounded they want tended and dead they want honoured, and these are shared needs.

resolutions.label: organise the practical aftermath of the skirmish

success.body:
The organising works — both sides accept the same practical framework, because the wounded don't ask for ideological sorting before they receive care. By evening the quarter is quieter. The practical has done what the political cannot yet do.

The truce is still fragile. It is less fragile than it was.

failure.body:
The two sides accept care for their own wounded and resist the shared framework. The collection of the dead becomes a dispute about which side's dead go where. The organising produces argument rather than coordination.

The shared need is real. The willingness to share is not yet there.

entries.body: The leaders of both sides are in separate buildings watching each other through windows. A formalised truce requires both to commit to something specific — duration, terms, consequences — rather than the current informal exhaustion.

resolutions.label: bring the leaders together and formalise the truce

success.body:
The formalisation is achieved: a written truce, witnessed by you, committing both sides to three days of peace while their respective lords are petitioned. Three days is enough for tempers to cool and alternatives to be found.

You are the guarantee. You accept that.

failure.body:
The meeting produces more heat than agreement — the grievances behind the skirmish are closer to the surface than the exhaustion suggested. The truce remains informal.

Informal truces end when the exhaustion does.

entries.body:
The forest ambush site is two days old — you can tell by the birds, which have returned, and by the state of the blood on the leaves. A column was attacked here, and the column's survivors went in two directions: those who fled toward the road and those who were driven into the forest.

Of the second group, some are still in the forest. You can hear them.

responses.label: You may find the survivors still in the forest.
You may secure the road side of the ambush site before going into the forest.

entries.body: The sounds in the forest are the sounds of injured people trying to be quiet, which is specific and recognisable. They are not far in.

resolutions.label: find and recover the survivors from the forest

success.body:
Four survivors, two with wounds that needed tending a day ago. You find them and begin the tending and the leading back. By the time you reach the road all four are mobile.

They are quieter than gratitude usually produces. They have been in the forest with the sound of the ambush for two days.

failure.body:
The forest is larger than the sounds suggested, and the sounds stop when you enter — a survival response that works against you. You find two of the four. The other two are further in.

Two is better than none.

entries.body: The ambushers may still be in the area — the road side of the ambush site shows signs of recent movement that is not from the ambush itself. If they are still watching, the recovery of the forest survivors becomes a more complicated task.

resolutions.label: clear the road side before attempting the recovery

success.body:
The recent movement was from a single watcher left to see whether any survivors would emerge. You deal with the watcher efficiently and the road is secure.

The forest recovery is now possible. You go in.

failure.body:
The watcher was not alone — the ambush party has regrouped nearby. The clearing operation becomes a confrontation and you must withdraw.

The forest recovery must wait for more support.

entries.body:
The mountain siege ended three weeks past, and the victors left the defenders' dead on the slope as a lesson. The slope is visible from the road below and the lesson has been learned by everyone who uses the road.

A woman at the road's edge has been standing there for two hours. Her son was in the garrison.

responses.label: You may climb the slope and bring back what she is looking for.
You may speak with her before you decide whether to climb.

entries.body: The slope is steep and the dead on it are three weeks old in mountain weather. What she is looking for may or may not be findable, and finding it requires searching ground that is not comfortable to search.

resolutions.label: climb the slope and search for her son

success.body:
You find him. The identification is possible from the description she gave — a specific device on his belt, a specific height and age. You bring him down.

She does not speak when you reach her. She takes your hand once. That is the whole of the exchange.

failure.body:
The slope has been disturbed by the weather and the bodies have moved from where they fell. You cannot find him with certainty, and certainty is what she needs.

You bring back what you believe may be him. The uncertainty is its own cruelty.

entries.body: She has been standing there long enough that standing is itself an act — not hope, exactly, but the refusal to leave without the thing she came for. Speaking with her first ensures you know specifically what she needs and what will and will not serve.

resolutions.label: hear what she needs and then give your answer

success.body:
What she needs is not the body — the body is gone beyond recovering cleanly. What she needs is confirmation that he died as a soldier rather than as a prisoner, which is recoverable: the survivors of the garrison are in a nearby town and one of them was with him at the end.

You find that survivor. The confirmation is given. She leaves.

failure.body: The conversation makes clear that what she needs is not something you can give — only the body, specifically, will do — and the slope search is the only remaining option.

entries.body:
The battlefield on the plain is two days old and still being worked: the heralds identifying the dead, the physicians attending the wounded, the quartermasters accounting for what the battle consumed. You are here on the winning side's authority, which gives you access.

Something is wrong. A section of the field is being avoided by the workers, and the avoiding has the quality of orders rather than instinct.

responses.label: You may investigate the avoided section of the field.
You may find out who gave the order to avoid that section before going there.

entries.body: The avoided section is on the western edge, near a drainage ditch. The workers give it a wide berth and none of them will say why when asked.

resolutions.label: investigate the avoided section directly

success.body:
The section holds something the winning side does not want found: prisoners killed after surrender, in a way that the rules of the field forbid. The evidence is clear and the avoiding is the victors' instinct to conceal rather than account for it.

You have the evidence. What you do with it is a question of the kind that follows you for some time.

failure.body:
The section is as harrowing as any battlefield section and you search it thoroughly but cannot determine what specifically it was ordered to avoid — the evidence, if there was evidence, has been moved.

The avoiding was real. The reason has been removed.

entries.body: The order came from a specific officer — the quartermaster's assistant, who is not the kind of person who makes operational decisions unless directed to. Finding who directed him reveals whether this is administrative or something more serious.

resolutions.label: find who ordered the avoidance and understand why

success.body:
The direction came from a senior knight who was involved in the western edge's final fighting. His name is known and his reasoning becomes clear when you put the chain together. What happened in the western edge is now on record — your record.

The truth, once found, cannot be unfound.

failure.body:
The chain of direction is broken deliberately — each person knows only their part of it. The origin of the order is not reachable from the outside.

Some things are concealed with professional care.

entries.body:
The swamp ambush cost a column its baggage train and its rearguard — twenty men, gone into the swamp in confusion, and now the swamp holds them somewhere between the ambush point and the far edge. They may be alive. They may not.

The column's commander has sent you because you are the only person available who knows this swamp.

responses.label: You may enter the swamp and find the survivors.
You may determine the ambushers' route first and work backwards from that.

entries.body: The swamp still holds the ambush's aftermath: broken equipment, disturbed mud, the sounds of what happened. Working from the ambush point toward the far edge gives you the route the survivors would have taken if they were trying to escape.

resolutions.label: track the survivors through the swamp from the ambush point

success.body:
Eleven survivors, in two groups. The first group had kept together and found higher ground; the second group had scattered and needed finding one by one. Three hours of work in the swamp and all eleven are out.

Nine more were not found. The swamp keeps its accounting.

failure.body:
The swamp has obscured the trail beyond reading — too much disturbance, too much water movement since the ambush. You find three survivors by chance rather than tracking.

Three is not eleven. Three is not zero.

entries.body: The ambushers would have had a route in and out — they knew this swamp and they used it systematically. Following their route in reverse might lead to the survivors faster than tracking the confused trails of panicked men.

resolutions.label: find the ambushers' route and follow it to the survivors

success.body:
The ambushers' route is clear, marked by their discipline rather than their confusion. Following it in reverse takes you to the pocket of high ground they had used as an observation post — and the survivors, who had the same instinct for high ground.

Thirteen survivors at the observation post, some wounded.

failure.body:
The ambushers' route is professional enough to be invisible — they left nothing behind. The reverse tracking finds nothing useful.

Direct tracking remains the only option.

entries.body:
The naval battle's wreckage has come ashore across two miles of coastline — timber, sail, bodies, and cargo, mixed without order. Two ships were involved and they came from opposite sides, which means the living who survived the sinking are from two opposing forces and are now sharing a beach.

The coast's people have come down to salvage and found a situation more complicated than salvage.

responses.label: You may take charge of the beach and manage the survivors and the salvage.
You may focus on the wounded first and let the political situation resolve itself.

entries.body: Managing the beach means managing three things at once: the survivors' mutual hostility, the coastal people's salvage rights, and the practical care of the wounded. A knight's authority, clearly established, can hold all three.

resolutions.label: establish authority on the beach and manage all three elements

success.body:
The authority is established and held. The survivors from opposing sides are separated with sufficient distance. The coastal people's salvage is directed toward the cargo rather than the bodies. The wounded are tended.

By evening the beach is a managed situation rather than a disaster.

failure.body:
The three elements do not all accept the authority at the same rate. The salvage dispute becomes a fight before you can redirect it, and the energy spent there means the survivors are less carefully managed.

Improved rather than resolved.

entries.body: Wounded people from opposing sides, treated with the same care on the same beach, produce a different atmosphere than wounded people abandoned to their respective politics. The tending is the argument for peace more effectively than any speech.

resolutions.label: tend the wounded from both sides with equal care

success.body:
The tending goes where it is needed without stopping to check allegiance, and the beach watches this. By the time the most urgent cases are stable, the salvage dispute has resolved itself — the watching has had an effect on the watchers.

Wounding and care are older than war.

failure.body:
The wounded are too numerous for one person to address adequately, and the triage decisions you make are questioned by both sides as politically motivated. The good work is there; its reception is complicated.

The intention was right. Perceptions are their own problem.

entries.body:
The city's battle site is a place the questors' tradition marks as spiritually significant — a place where many people died and the weight of that dying has not been absorbed by the earth. The Grail quest passes through places like this because the questors must know what the Grail heals.

The site smells of old iron and new growth, the specific combination of death and the continuation that follows it.

responses.label: You may sit with the site's weight and receive what it teaches.
You may perform a specific rite for the dead of this place.

entries.body: Sitting with a place of concentrated death is not comfortable and is not meant to be — the questors' tradition says it teaches something that comfortable places cannot teach: the actual weight of what the Grail's healing must address.

resolutions.label: sit with the weight of the site and receive what it teaches

success.body:
An hour of sitting with it, without softening what is there, produces what the tradition describes: not sorrow, exactly, but a specific understanding of what is at stake in the quest. The Grail heals this — this specific accumulated weight — and knowing that changes what seeking it means.

You ride away knowing more about what you are seeking and why.

failure.body:
The weight is more than you can sit with for long enough — you soften it, or retreat from it, or find yourself thinking of other things. The hour passes without the teaching.

Not every site can be received by every person at every moment.

entries.body: The questors' tradition includes a specific rite for battle sites — a form of prayer that acknowledges each dead person as an individual rather than a number, which requires time and attention but which the tradition says has a measurable effect on the site's spiritual weight.

resolutions.label: perform the rite for the dead of this site

success.body:
The rite takes three hours. By the end, the site feels different — not clean, but addressed, the way a wound feels different after it has been properly tended even if it is not yet healed. The questors say this is real. After three hours you believe them.

You leave the site behind you as a tended thing.

failure.body:
The rite requires a completeness of attention that you cannot sustain for three hours in this site's conditions. You complete what you can.

Partial tending is still tending.

entries.body:
The forest battle took the lives of six Grail questors, ambushed on the road by men who believed — incorrectly — that the questors were political agents rather than spiritual seekers. The survivors have buried their companions but cannot continue — the grief and the injustice have combined to stop them.

You are the first knight they have seen since.

responses.label: You may sit with the survivors and be present with their grief.
You may help them find the way forward from this stopping place.

entries.body: Three survivors, all of them at the edge of what grief can hold without breaking them. Not broken — holding. The holding requires witness.

resolutions.label: be present with the survivors' grief for as long as they need

success.body:
You stay through the evening and the night and part of the next morning, doing what is needed — no more and no less — and at some point the grief has been witnessed enough for the survivors to stand again.

They ride when they are ready. You ride with them for a day. Then they go their way.

failure.body:
The staying becomes restless — your own discomfort with the grief interferes with the being present for it. They notice. The witness is imperfect.

Imperfect witness is still witness.

entries.body: The forward path is not clear to them because the ambush happened in the context of the road they were following, and the road now has a different quality. They need both the practical direction and the answer to the question the ambush raised about whether the quest should continue.

resolutions.label: help them find the forward path in both the practical and spiritual sense

success.body:
The practical: the road east, which is safer, which takes longer, and which has two other questors who were separated from a different group and need companions. The spiritual: the ambush was an injustice, not a sign — the quest continues.

They receive both and they ride.

failure.body:
The spiritual answer to the ambush — whether it is a sign or an injustice — is not one you can settle for them from outside their experience of it.

The practical path you give them anyway. Sometimes practical is what is needed while the spiritual settles itself.

entries.body:
The mountain battlefield is still active — the siege is ongoing, with the besiegers below and the besieged in the keep above, and you are on the approach road that runs between both. You have been given safe passage by the besieging lord, which means neither side will fire on you immediately.

Both sides believe you are going to negotiate on their behalf.

responses.label: You may negotiate between the two sides and attempt to end the siege.
You may determine which side's cause is just before committing to negotiation.

entries.body: Negotiating without a declared position gives you maximum flexibility but also maximum ambiguity — both sides will hear what they want to hear unless you are precise about your role.

resolutions.label: negotiate between the two sides and find a resolution

success.body:
The resolution requires giving each side something they can call a victory, even when the actual terms are a draw. The besieged garrison surrenders with honours; the besieging lord receives the keep but acknowledges the garrison's right to depart freely.

A siege ended without massacre is the measure of a successful negotiation.

failure.body:
One side's position is inflexible enough that no settlement is reachable without concessions the other side will not make. The siege continues.

Negotiation fails when one party has decided to win rather than resolve.

entries.body: The justice of the two sides' positions is relevant: if one side is clearly wrong, negotiation that treats both sides equally produces an unjust result. The accounts of how the siege began are accessible from both sides.

resolutions.label: hear both sides and form an honest judgment before negotiating

success.body:
The judgment comes down clearly on the besieged side — the siege is illegal under Arthur's law, mounted on false pretences. You negotiate not as a neutral party but as a representative of that judgment, which gives you leverage the neutral position would not have.

The siege lifts. The besieging lord will answer to Arthur's court.

failure.body:
The justice of the situation is genuinely unclear — both sides have legitimate claims and the conflict between them is real rather than manufactured. Neutrality is the only honest position.

Neutral negotiation is harder. You attempt it.

entries.body:
The plains battlefield is one where Grail questors have been gathered — not to fight, but because the battle below them has produced, in the questors' accounts, something visible only from the field's edge: a quality of light over the dying that the questors say represents the Grail's presence in its healing aspect.

The battle is ending. The light is there, if you know to look.

responses.label: You may look for the light the questors describe.
You may go into the field and help the dying while the light is present.

entries.body: Looking for a described light in a chaotic aftermath requires the specific quality of attention that receives rather than searches — the light, in the questors' accounts, appears to those who are not looking for it because they are doing something else.

resolutions.label: look for the light with the right quality of attention

success.body:
You stop looking and you help a dying man nearby — and in the helping you see it, peripheral and complete. The questors are right: it is the quality of the Grail's healing that is there, not the Grail itself, and it is unmistakable to those who encounter it.

You understand something about the Grail that the questors said could only be experienced here.

failure.body:
The looking is too direct and misses what the questors described. The light, if it is there, is not visible to the searching kind of attention.

The helping, at least, was real.

entries.body: Going into the field to help the dying — not to fight but to tend — is its own form of presence. The questors say the light is most visible to those who are in service to the dying rather than observing them.

resolutions.label: go into the field and tend the dying

success.body:
You work through the worst of it. Those who can be helped are helped; those who cannot be are accompanied. The light the questors described is visible, briefly, in the moments between one dying person and the next — not dramatic, but real.

You leave the field knowing you were in a significant place.

failure.body:
The field is more than one person can address, and the vastness of the need defeats the tending before the light can be found. You help who you can and leave exhausted.

What was done was real. The transcendent was not reached today.

entries.body:
The swamp battle has left the ambushers and the ambushed intermixed in the swamp's interior — neither side able to extricate itself cleanly, both sides too depleted to continue fighting. They have reached an armed standoff in three feet of water and no one knows how to end it without someone moving first.

Both sides would accept a neutral withdrawal facilitation. You are neutral.

responses.label: You may facilitate a simultaneous withdrawal from the swamp.
You may negotiate for the surrender of one side before withdrawal.

entries.body: Simultaneous withdrawal requires both sides to trust a sequence: one side turns its back on the other in order to move, which requires the other to refrain from attacking during that vulnerability.

resolutions.label: facilitate the simultaneous withdrawal through careful sequencing

success.body:
The sequencing works: you stand between both sides while each withdraws, which means the attacking during withdrawal would require going through you. No one wants that complication. Both sides exit the swamp.

The standoff ends without further casualties.

failure.body:
One side breaks the sequence — not aggressively, but anxiously, moving before the agreed moment — and the withdrawal becomes complicated. Both sides make it out, but not cleanly.

Anxiety and trust are incompatible. You needed more of the second.

entries.body: If one side is more depleted than the other, a conditional surrender — one that preserves the surrendering side's dignity and lives — may be achievable and would resolve the standoff more completely than a withdrawal.

resolutions.label: negotiate a dignified surrender from the more depleted side

success.body:
The terms are: the depleted side surrenders their weapons but not their persons — they are released, not taken prisoner. The other side accepts this because taking prisoners from a swamp standoff is logistically impractical.

The standoff ends. Both sides go home.

failure.body:
Neither side is depleted enough to accept surrender on any terms currently available. The standoff continues.

The withdrawal approach is the remaining option.

entries.body:
The sea battle's survivors have come ashore at a sacred cove that the questors know from their accounts — a place of the old religion that is said to receive the shipwrecked with particular mercy. The survivors from both ships are here, many wounded, and the cove's quality has produced a truce more durable than the swamp or the city produced.

But one ship carried something that the questors need to find.

responses.label: You may search the surviving wreckage for what the questors need.
You may ask the surviving sailors what their ship carried.

entries.body: The wreckage that came ashore includes sealed containers from both ships — cargo that the sailors rescued from the sinking. The questors' need is specific enough that the right container will be identifiable if found.

resolutions.label: search the wreckage for what the questors need

success.body:
The right container is identifiable by a specific mark — a seal that the questors described in their accounts. You find it in the third pile of salvage, unclaimed because the sailors who brought it ashore did not know its significance.

The questors receive it.

failure.body:
The mark, if it survived the sinking, is on a container that went down rather than ashore. The wreckage does not contain what is needed.

The sailors may know more.

entries.body: The sailors know their cargo — they loaded it. One ship's manifest included something that matches the questors' description, and the sailor who managed that cargo is alive and in the cove.

resolutions.label: find the right sailor and learn what became of the cargo

success.body:
The cargo was sealed and placed in a specific location in the hold — and that specific location, the sailor says, was in the section that was sealed before the sinking. It is underwater but accessible to someone willing to dive.

You dive. The cargo is there.

failure.body:
The sailor who managed that cargo did not survive the battle. The knowledge went with him.

The wreckage search is the remaining approach.

entries.body:
The city's current battle has produced a street by street situation — neither side holding the whole, both sides claiming different districts, and the civilians caught between them occupying whatever space is currently unclaimed. You are in one of those unclaimed spaces.

A group of civilians — twenty of them — need to cross three streets to reach the city's safe quarter, and those three streets are not currently safe.

responses.label: You may escort them through the three streets directly.
You may find a route through the city that avoids all three streets.

entries.body: Three streets, twenty civilians, whatever forces currently occupy those streets. The escort requires reading each street before committing the group to it.

resolutions.label: escort the civilians through the three streets

success.body:
The three streets are navigated one at a time, waiting at each corner for the right moment. The right moments come. All twenty civilians reach the safe quarter.

You return through the three streets alone, which is a different kind of calculation.

failure.body:
The second street is occupied before you can clear it, and the group must back to the starting position and hold there. The direct route is closed today.

The alternative route must be found now.

entries.body: The city has alleys, yards, and passages that connect districts without using streets. A knight who knows the city's back routes — or can find someone who does — might navigate twenty people from one district to another without touching any of the three streets.

resolutions.label: find the back route and lead the civilians through it

success.body:
A local woman among the twenty knows the yard connections between this district and the safe quarter — she has lived here thirty years. She leads; you guard. The back route is slower but clean.

All twenty arrive. The woman looks at you and says: 'I knew these yards would matter one day.'

failure.body:
The back route you find is blocked by a recent collapse — the battle has changed the city's interior. The street route is the only remaining option.

Route-finding in a changed city requires local knowledge you do not have.

entries.body:
The forest fighting in the Final Wars is a guerrilla affair — small groups, quick engagements, no clean lines. A group of Arthur's men have been pinned by a larger group in a defensive position they cannot hold and cannot leave without being caught in the open.

You are an hour from them. They are not an hour from running out of defensive capacity.

responses.label: You may reach them and help them hold until they can withdraw safely.
You may circle the pinning force and create a distraction from the rear.

entries.body: Reaching them and adding your sword changes the defensive equation. A fresh knight in a defensive position matters more than their numbers suggest.

resolutions.label: ride to them and reinforce the defence

success.body:
You arrive with enough time to matter. The reinforcement is not a victory — it is a stalemate, which is what they need. By nightfall the pinning force withdraws rather than continue an engagement that has stopped being cheap.

Arthur's men withdraw in good order.

failure.body:
The road to them is more complicated than the hour suggested, and you arrive after they have been forced to withdraw under worse conditions than they would have had with your arrival.

You were where you were needed. The timing was not yours to control.

entries.body: A distraction from the pinning force's rear changes their calculation — they are now between two threats rather than surrounding one. A sufficient distraction at the right moment allows Arthur's men to withdraw while the force addresses the rear.

resolutions.label: create a distraction from the rear to break the encirclement

success.body:
The distraction is sufficient. The force turns to address it and Arthur's men use the moment to withdraw. You disengage before the force can consolidate against you.

Three forces, three directions, none of them intersecting. That is the measure of the operation.

failure.body:
The distraction is identified too quickly as a distraction, and the force splits to address both threats simultaneously. Arthur's men make a harder withdrawal than they would have.

The attempt was real. The force was larger than its numbers.

entries.body:
The mountain is Camlann's shadow — not the battle itself, but its consequences working their way into a mountain hold that declared for Arthur. The hold's garrison is now without their lord, without clear orders, and aware that the war is effectively over. They are considering whether to surrender, fight, or simply dissolve.

The captain has asked for a knight's counsel.

responses.label: You may counsel the captain on the most honourable course.
You may help the garrison understand the actual state of the war before they decide.

entries.body: Honour in defeat has its own logic: surrender to legitimate authority, not to whoever happens to arrive first, and with terms that preserve the garrison's dignity and lives.

resolutions.label: counsel the captain on an honourable conclusion

success.body:
The counsel is received: the garrison should send to the nearest legitimate authority and surrender formally, with terms, rather than waiting to be overwhelmed or fragmenting into individual decisions. The captain agrees.

The garrison negotiates a formal surrender with terms that Arthur would have approved. That is the measure of honour in defeat.

failure.body:
The counsel is sound but the captain has a specific grievance that complicates his acceptance of any general principle. The specific grievance must be addressed before the principle can be applied.

General principles and specific situations require each other.

entries.body: The garrison's decision requires accurate information about what has actually happened — where Arthur is, who holds power, what Mordred's position is. Some of what they believe is wrong.

resolutions.label: give them an accurate account of the war's current state

success.body:
The accurate account changes their calculation. Two of their three options evaporate when the actual situation is clear — fighting cannot change the war's outcome and dissolving simply leaves them without the protection of formal status.

Surrender with terms is the only rational option. They arrive at it on their own.

failure.body:
Your information is not complete enough to fully clarify the situation — some of the most relevant developments are from the last three days and you do not have them. The garrison's uncertainty remains.

Incomplete information produces incomplete decisions.

entries.body:
Camlann is done, and the plains hold its aftermath. You are moving through the edge of the field — not the centre, which is beyond approach — and the edge holds: the wounded who made it this far, the riderless horses, the equipment left by men who are not coming back for it.

A herald who knew the battle's participants is moving through the field making the formal identification of the dead. He needs a witness for the most significant names.

responses.label: You may serve as witness for the herald's identifications.
You may search for specific people you need to account for.

entries.body: The witness role is simple and important: the herald's word alone is not sufficient for the formal record; a knight of good standing who witnesses the identifications gives the record legal weight.

resolutions.label: serve as witness through the field's identifications

success.body:
The field produces names that will define the age's end. You witness each one, formally, and the record is made correctly. It is harrowing work and it matters: the widows and the heirs and the historians will use this record.

You have helped the age end with the dignity of accurate accounting.

failure.body:
The field produces more names than the available time, and the witness becomes rushed. Some identifications are uncertain. The record is partial.

A partial record is better than no record. Barely.

entries.body: You have specific names to account for — people you knew, or were commissioned to find — and Camlann's field either holds them or has already given them up by other means.

resolutions.label: search the field for the people you need to account for

success.body:
You find what you were looking for: some alive, some not. Each finding is its own moment, and the accumulation of them is the weight of the age's ending made specific and personal.

You carry the accounting forward. Some of it will be welcome. Some of it will not.

failure.body:
The field does not give up what you were looking for clearly enough. The people you sought are either not here or past clear identification.

Uncertainty, in this context, is its own kind of answer.

entries.body:
The swamp at the Final Wars' edge has been a skirmish ground for two months, and its particular feature is this: soldiers who fall in the swamp are not always recovered. Families of those who fell here come to the swamp's edge looking for signs, for news, for anything.

A woman has been at the swamp's edge for three days. She has a name and a description and the certainty that her husband came through here.

responses.label: You may enter the swamp and search for what she is looking for.
You may find the soldiers who were with him and learn what they know.

entries.body: The swamp holds many things — two months of skirmishes leave their marks. What she described is specific enough to search for, if the swamp has not moved it.

resolutions.label: search the swamp for the specific evidence she needs

success.body:
You find him — or what the swamp has preserved of him — and you find with him his ring, which she described, and a knife with a specific mark she mentioned. You bring these back.

She takes them and stands for a long time. Then she thanks you. Then she leaves.

failure.body:
The swamp has taken what it has taken. The specific things she described are not findable — moved, or sunk, or gone.

You return to her with the honest knowledge that he was here, which is the thing she needed most and you can give.

entries.body: His unit passed through this area — the local village keeps track of military movements, since their survival depends on it. The unit's survivors are a day north, regrouping after their last engagement.

resolutions.label: find his unit and bring back word of what happened to him

success.body:
His sergeant is alive and willing to speak. He knows what happened — the specific moment, the specific place, the specific person who was with him. He has been carrying the account because he had no one to give it to.

You bring it back to the woman. She receives it completely.

failure.body:
The unit is further than a day, or has fragmented beyond finding, or the sergeant who knew is not among the survivors. The specific account is not available.

You bring back what you can, which is general rather than specific.

entries.body:
The sea battle off this coast was part of Camlann's naval dimension — Mordred's ships against Arthur's last fleet. The fleet is gone, most of it, and the survivors who made it ashore are at this coastal village, trying to understand what they survived into.

The village is sheltering them. It cannot shelter them indefinitely. And several of the survivors carry information about what the fleet saw in its last hours — where ships went, who survived, the route Arthur's barge took.

responses.label: You may speak with the survivors and gather what information they carry.
You may ensure the survivors are safely passed inland before the coast becomes contested.

entries.body: Fleet survivors who have been through Camlann's naval phase carry specific and irreplaceable information: who is alive, who is not, where the ships went, what was seen. This information needs to be gathered before it disperses with the survivors.

resolutions.label: speak systematically with the survivors and gather what they know

success.body:
The systematic gathering produces a picture: the fleet's last hours, the direction of Arthur's barge, the names of those who survived and those confirmed not to. You write it down in the form that will survive you.

This is the historical record of the age's ending, made by you, in a coastal village. It matters.

failure.body:
The survivors are too dispersed in their state of shock to be gathered systematically. You collect fragments, some reliable and some not.

Fragments, carefully held, sometimes compose into truth.

entries.body: The coast will become contested in the next several days as the war's remaining forces sort themselves out. The survivors need to be inland before that happens — both for their own safety and because their information is more useful at Arthur's remaining court than in a coastal village.

resolutions.label: organise and lead the survivors inland to safety

success.body:
The movement inland takes two days and the survivors include several who are not well enough to walk easily. You manage it — the pace is slow and the route is chosen for the pace.

The survivors reach the inland road before the coastal situation changes. Their information and their lives are preserved.

failure.body:
The movement is slower than the situation allows, and the coastal situation changes before you have everyone inland. Some survivors make it; others must be left with the village's protection.

Partial success in a race against time is still partial.

entries.body:
The great hall of the city lord is laid for a feast that has been seven years in the making — the peace treaty signed this morning between the house of the valley and its northern rivals, enemies since before Arthur's coronation. The mead flows freely and the music is loud enough to cover the silence between men who were killing each other a season past.

At the high table, the lord of the valley and the northern chieftain drink from the same cup, but their eyes do not meet. The feast is real. The peace is not yet.

responses.label: You may speak between the two lords and press the peace into something that will hold past the wine.
You may draw out the northern chieftain apart from the hall and understand what will truly seal his commitment.

entries.body: You seat yourself between them in the natural lull after the third cup, when the music rests and both lords are full enough to listen. The lord of the valley watches you with gratitude and suspicion in equal measure. The northern chieftain watches you with professional assessment — he has survived long enough to know when a man is sincere.

resolutions.label: speak to each man in turn and draw from the feast itself what they share

success.body:
The words come right — not eloquent, but honest, and a feast responds to honesty more readily than to eloquence. You remind the northern chieftain that his son learned to ride horses bred in the valley below; you remind the valley lord that the northern passes kept his merchants safe through three winters of lowland war. The cup passes between them again, and this time they look at each other when they drink.

The hall feels different after. The music is the same music, but the silence beneath it has changed its quality.

failure.body:
The words are not quite right — the valley lord takes the northern chieftain's history as grievance rather than common ground, and the moment of connection passes before you can retrieve it. They drink separately after, and the feast continues in its surface warmth without the deeper accord.

You have learned the precise shape of what is missing between them, which is knowledge of value if you find another occasion.

entries.body: The chieftain follows you to the outer gallery without reluctance — he is a man who prefers the dark to candlelight for conversations that matter. The feast noise falls behind you, muffled by stone. He looks out over the city walls at the northern road and says nothing, waiting.

resolutions.label: read the chieftain's silence and offer him what the feast cannot offer in public

success.body:
The thing he needs is not land or gold — it is a guarantee, witnessed by someone outside both houses, that the valley lord will not use this peace to consolidate against him during the summer. He has been outmanoeuvred by feasts before. You make the guarantee in the only form that will hold: your own name, spoken to him directly, as surety for the peace's terms.

You are the guarantee. You accept that. He returns to the feast a different man.

failure.body:
What you offer him is close but not quite the shape of what he needs, and the gap between what you have read and what is true is wide enough that he does not trust the reading. He returns to the feast politely, and the peace holds for now on the strength of the mead and the music.

But it has no deeper roots than that, and roots are what the summer will test.

entries.body:
The clearing in the forest's eastern reaches has been laid for a feast that should not be possible — long tables dressed in white linen, candles burning without wind to trouble them, and a host you cannot place by house or device who greets each arriving knight by name without introduction. The food is real food. The wine is wine.

Your companions eat freely and with pleasure. You notice that no one has asked the host his name.

responses.label: You may ask the host directly who he is and what this feast marks.
You may study what the host offers each guest and discern the nature of this gathering.

entries.body: The host receives your question without surprise — he has the quality of a man who has been asked this question many times in many forms and has a considered answer for each form. He sets down his cup with care. The candles do not flicker.

resolutions.label: press the question further and require a true answer rather than a considered one

success.body:
He is a lord of this forest — not a faerie lord in the old sense, but a man whose family has kept this wood for eleven generations and whose authority in it is the authority of deep knowledge and long tenure. The feast is his offering: to the forest's health, which has been troubled of late, and to any knight willing to take an interest in troubles that are not easily named.

He tells you what troubles the wood. It is a thing you can address.

failure.body:
The host gives you an answer that is technically true — a name, a lineage, a claim on this land — but something in it is a surface answer, and the pressing has made him careful rather than honest. The feast continues. The wood's trouble remains unnamed.

You leave knowing less than you might have, but no worse for the eating.

entries.body: The host offers each guest something different: bread to some, salt to others, a small carved token to one knight who pockets it without comment. The pattern is not random. You watch through the first three cups of wine and begin to understand the grammar of it.

resolutions.label: interpret the pattern of gifts and receive the one that is meant for you

success.body:
The host offers you nothing, and you understand that nothing is the gift — the gift is absence, the empty hand, the invitation to ask for what you actually need rather than accept what is given by convention. You name it aloud, and the host smiles with the satisfaction of a teacher whose student has understood at last.

What you receive is harder to hold than bread or token, but more lasting in its effect.

failure.body:
The pattern is there, but the final interpretation eludes you — you read it two ways and choose the wrong one, taking the offered bread when what was meant was the salt. The host does not correct you. The feast ends well enough, and the forest receives you kindly on the road out.

Something was there that you did not quite see.

entries.body:
The mountain fortress of Lord Geraint above the snow line holds a feast for the first clear night in a month — the passes have been closed, provisions have been thin, and the celebration of a hunting party's success has grown into something larger. The great hall is low-ceilinged and warm, and every fire in the fortress has been lit.

Geraint's daughter sits at the high table with the look of a woman who has made a decision that the feast has not yet been told about. The musicians play, but she is not listening to the music.

responses.label: You may speak with Geraint's daughter and learn what decision she has made.
You may watch the hall's political currents and understand who at this feast already knows what she has decided.

entries.body: She speaks to you directly, without the usual courtly circling — she has been in this fortress through a month of closed passes and has lost patience with indirection. She intends to ride for the lowlands when the passes open, to take service with the queen's household. Her father does not know. The feast is her last in this hall, and she means to make it a good one.

resolutions.label: counsel her on the approach to her father that will let her go with his blessing rather than against his will

success.body:
The counsel is practical and the timing is right — Geraint is in good humour from the hunt and the wine, and a daughter who comes to him with a plan rather than a demand is a daughter he can speak with. You frame it as service to the crown rather than departure from home, and the framing holds because it is true.

By the end of the feast, Geraint is telling his neighbours of his daughter's appointment with the pride of a man who believes it was his idea from the beginning.

failure.body:
The timing is not quite right — Geraint hears the plan before the wine has done its work, and the proud father surfaces before the reasonable lord. The conversation becomes difficult. His daughter's departure is not prevented, but it is made harder than it needed to be.

She thanks you anyway, as she rides out in the morning, for the attempt.

entries.body: Three men at the feast already know: her father's steward — who knows everything in this hall and is weighing it; a young knight who watches her with the unmistakable expression of a man who is about to do something inadvisable; and an older woman near the fire who watches both of them with the calm assessment of a person who has seen this before.

resolutions.label: align with the older woman and manage what she sees before it becomes a scene

success.body:
The older woman — Geraint's aunt, as it turns out, who has been managing this household's social weather for thirty years — accepts your assistance without condescension and deploys it with precision. The young knight is redirected; the steward is given something else to weigh; and the daughter reaches the end of the feast without the hall knowing her decision before she has told her father herself.

The aunt tells you, on parting, that you have good instincts. From her, it is a considerable compliment.

failure.body:
The young knight moves before you do, and the scene he makes is not catastrophic but it is public. Geraint learns of his daughter's decision from the wrong direction, with the wrong faces watching, and the conversation that follows is harder than it needed to be.

The aunt meets your eyes across the hall with the expression of someone who has witnessed a salvage operation fail and is not surprised.

entries.body:
The army that has held this plain through the summer is celebrating its final victory before the march home — a field feast, campfires joined into something resembling a great hall, with the knights seated according to their deeds rather than their birth. It is a Camelot custom and it pleases the common soldiers to see it done.

The feast has a complication: two knights are being seated at positions they both believe themselves to have earned, and they cannot both be right, and the herald who would normally resolve this is conspicuously absent.

responses.label: You may judge between the two knights' claims and seat them rightly before the army.
You may find a third place of honour that makes the question moot and preserves both dignities.

entries.body: Both knights state their cases to you with the precision of men who have been rehearsing them for two hours. One held the river crossing; one broke the enemy's flank. Both claims are true. The question is which deed was the more decisive — and the army is watching.

resolutions.label: render judgement on the deeds fairly and speak it to the army without equivocation

success.body:
The judgement you render is a careful one — you trace the sequence of the battle and demonstrate that the river crossing made the flank possible, and therefore the first knight's deed was the foundation on which the second was built. You seat them accordingly, with the explanation given in full to the army.

Both knights accept it. The one who sits lower does so with a visible grace that earns him more regard than the seat would have.

failure.body:
The judgement you render is fair but not persuasive — you lack the specific knowledge of the battle's sequence that would make it inarguable, and both knights leave the exchange with residual grievance. The seating is settled by your authority rather than your argument, which is a weaker settling.

The feast proceeds. The two knights do not speak to each other through it.

entries.body: The field feast has a gap — the position at the junction of the two longest tables, which is neither high nor low but is the point through which every man at the feast must pass at some point in the evening. It is a position of movement rather than station. The herald would never have thought of it.

resolutions.label: propose the junction seat to both knights and frame it as a new honour for the celebration

success.body:
The framing works because it is genuinely true — a feast does not have a fixed geography, and a position that puts you in contact with every man is a form of honour the old seating arrangements did not provide for. You offer it to the higher-claiming knight first, as a new thing rather than a consolation, and he accepts it as a thing worth having.

The feast moves with an ease it would not have had otherwise.

failure.body:
The junction seat is a good idea, but the higher-claiming knight sees through the framing to the fact that you are managing him, and a man who has just proved himself in battle is not in a mood to be managed. He declines the seat with cold courtesy. You settle the question by the old method after all.

The idea was sound. The execution needed more preparation.

entries.body:
The manse at the swamp's heart is older than the village that remembers it, and the lord who holds it keeps the old customs — a feast for any knight who comes to his door, torchlight reflected in the standing water beyond the windows, a table long enough for twenty with only six seated tonight. He is a hospitable man. He is also a man with something to ask.

The food is good and the fire is warm and the smell of the swamp does not quite reach the inner hall. By the second course, you know the asking is coming.

responses.label: You may invite the asking openly, before the wine makes the host believe you have not noticed.
You may discern what the asking is before he frames it and prepare your answer.

entries.body: The lord receives your directness with visible relief — he has been managing the patience for this conversation since you arrived, and being released from the management lets him speak plainly. The swamp has been changing. Something that was contained in its eastern reaches is contained no longer, and he cannot send his own people against it without risking more than the thing is worth.

resolutions.label: commit to addressing the swamp's eastern trouble and negotiate what you will need to do so

success.body:
The negotiation is honest on both sides — he knows what he is asking and you know what you are agreeing to, and the agreement made at his feast table has the quality of a thing that will hold because both parties understand its cost. He gives you what he can: guides, provisions, the specific knowledge of the eastern reaches that his people have gathered.

You leave the feast with a clear commission and better preparation than you would have had without the asking.

failure.body:
The negotiation unravels at the detail — not from bad faith, but from the lord's reluctance to admit how much he does not know about the eastern trouble's precise nature. What you agree to is vague enough that the commitment does not fully take. You leave with a direction rather than a mission.

Something was held back that needed to be given.

entries.body: The signs are not subtle once you attend to them: the lord's steward watches the east-facing window whenever the fire gutters; the lord himself eats facing the door; two of the six guests are not guests at all but men-at-arms seated at table for the sake of company in numbers. Something is wrong in the swamp's eastern reaches, and the lord of this manse is afraid of it.

resolutions.label: name the fear for him and offer the framing that will let him ask what he needs

success.body:
You name it quietly, between the third and fourth courses, in a way that makes no public scene of his fear. The relief on his face is genuine and immediate — a man who has been carrying a thing alone is most grateful for the person who can see it without being told. He tells you everything then, in full, without the managed version.

What is in the eastern swamp is real and nameable and something you can act against.

failure.body:
You name it close but not exactly right, and the slight discrepancy gives the lord enough room to retreat back into the managed version of his fear. The feast ends warmly; you leave with the sense of something unspoken that the night's end has sealed in.

The eastern swamp will remain what it is until someone asks the right question.

entries.body:
The coastal lord's feast is held aboard his great ship, anchored in the bay where the river meets the sea — a tradition of his house when a voyage is completed safely, and the voyage completed this time was the most dangerous in a generation. Merchants, sea-captains, and three knights of the Round Table who sailed with him are among the guests.

The lord's harbourmaster takes you aside between courses. There is a man at this feast, he says, who was not on the voyage and should not know that it succeeded.

responses.label: You may identify the man who has no rightful place at this feast.
You may watch the feast's currents and learn what this uninvited man hopes to take from the evening.

entries.body: There are forty guests aboard. You have the harbourmaster's quiet description — a man of middle years, well-dressed but not expensively, seated toward the ship's bow end of the main table with the sea-captains, with whom he converses with the fluency of a man who knows the trade. He is very comfortable for someone who should not be here.

resolutions.label: move through the feast naturally and identify the man by elimination and observation

success.body:
He is found by the inconsistency of his knowledge — he knows the voyage's destination precisely but speaks of the route with a vagueness that no one who actually sailed it would have. When you sit beside him and speak of the route's particular difficulty at the third headland, his answer is the answer of a man working from a merchant's report rather than memory.

You have him identified before he has identified you as a threat.

failure.body:
There are three plausible candidates and you narrow to the wrong one — your approach alerts the actual intruder without identifying him, and by the time you have ruled out your first choice, the man has found a reason to leave the ship with the tide.

The harbourmaster does not say anything. He does not need to.

entries.body: The uninvited man watches the coastal lord most when the lord is not watching anyone. He watches the lord's chart-case — a leather tube bound to the mast near the stern — with the regular attention of a man reminding himself of what he came for. The feast is cover. The chart-case is the purpose.

resolutions.label: protect the chart-case without revealing that its danger has been seen

success.body:
You position yourself between the case and the intruder's line of movement without making the positioning obvious — engaging the lord in a conversation that draws him toward the stern, making the case difficult to reach without passing through two witnesses. The intruder recognises the arrangement by the end of the second hour and slips from the ship with nothing.

The lord thanks you in the morning for a vigilance he only understood in retrospect.

failure.body:
The intruder is more patient than you are — he waits until the feast reaches the hour when all attention is on the singers, and in that window he has been at the chart-case and away from it before you can move. What he took was one chart of many. The lord will not know which one until he needs it.

The feast ends in good spirits, unaware.

entries.body:
The city's great lord holds his Midsummer feast, but of the forty places laid, twenty are empty — the knights who held them have ridden on the Grail quest and may not return. The lord does not remove the empty chairs. He leaves them as they are, settings undisturbed, as though absence could be honoured by an undisturbed place.

The feast is good food eaten in a hall made strange by its own missing half. At the high table, the lord stares at the empty chair beside him — the chair of his son, who rode three months past — and does not speak.

responses.label: You may sit with the lord and offer what presence can offer in the face of an absence.
You may speak to the feast's assembled guests and give the missing knights a proper acknowledgement.

entries.body: The lord of the city receives you at his side without words — he gestures to the empty chair opposite, meaning sit across from it, and you do. The feast continues around you. He is a man trying to determine whether grief is a weakness or simply the cost of love, and he has not decided.

resolutions.label: sit with the lord in his silence and speak only when the words will carry weight

success.body:
You do not rush the silence. When you speak, it is not comfort — it is witness: you name the son's deed at the tournament two summers past, which you saw, which was a genuinely fine thing, and the naming of a specific deed is more use than any consolation. The lord receives it the way a man receives water after a long road — not gratefully but necessarily.

The feast finds a different quality after that. The empty chair is the same chair, but the lord is a different man in relation to it.

failure.body:
You speak too soon — your words, meant well, arrive before the lord is ready to receive them, and they land as intrusion rather than comfort. He thanks you with the courtesy of a man who has had practice at courtesy in pain, and you both fall back into silence.

The silence was the right thing; only the timing was wrong.

entries.body: The assembled guests — lords, ladies, merchants, the city's knights who did not ride on the quest — receive your intention to speak with the mixed attention of a room that has been trying not to look at the empty chairs all evening. The lord of the city watches you from the high table. He has not given you permission and he has not withheld it.

resolutions.label: speak of the departed knights and frame their absence as deed rather than loss

success.body:
The speech finds its form around the empty chairs themselves — you speak of what it means to answer a call that cannot be refused, and you speak of the feast they do not share as the feast they are worthy of, given what they seek. The Grail's light, you say, is in the empty place at every table.

The room is quiet in the way of people who have heard something that reorganises a feeling they did not know how to hold.

failure.body:
The speech is sincere but it resolves the wrong tension — it comforts the absent knights' families but unsettles those who chose to stay, who hear in your words an implicit judgement of their own choice. The room is appreciative on the surface and uncomfortable beneath.

Sincerity without precision is still a form of clumsiness.

entries.body:
A hermit in the forest's western clearing has done an extraordinary thing — he has laid a feast, and the feast is attended by knights of the Round Table who have arrived separately from their various roads, drawn by something they cannot quite name. The hermit moves among them with the ease of a man who expected every arrival.

The bread on the table is ordinary bread. But at the feast's centre — where a cup should be, in the old placement — there is nothing. The hermit has left the place empty, and every knight at the table has noticed, and none has spoken of it yet.

responses.label: You may ask the hermit directly about the empty centre of his table.
You may prepare yourself in prayer before the feast and see what the preparation reveals.

entries.body: The hermit hears the question with the careful attention of a man who has been waiting to hear it asked well. He considers you before he answers. The other knights at the table have fallen silent, and the forest around the clearing has fallen silent with them, in that particular way that forests do when something is at stake.

resolutions.label: ask the question in its plainest form and receive whatever answer that deserves

success.body:
The hermit tells you that the empty centre is a waiting — not for the Grail itself, which cannot be compelled, but for the kind of readiness that makes a vessel worth filling. He has laid this feast as a question. The question is whether those who gather here can feast in the presence of an absence without diminishing the absence by filling it.

You understand what is being asked. You do not reach for the cup that is not there.

failure.body:
The question you ask is close to the right question but not exactly it, and the hermit gives you the answer the wrong question deserves — a teaching about patience, a reference to scripture, true and not unhelpful but not the specific thing the moment was offering.

The feast continues. The empty centre remains empty. You suspect the asking mattered in ways you have not fully accounted for.

entries.body: The prayer in the forest clearing, before the feast begins, is not the prayer of a chapel — it is an outdoor thing, with the sound of the forest around it and the smell of the hermit's fire, and it asks not for strength but for sight: the ability to see what this gathering actually is.

resolutions.label: make the prayer honestly and remain with what it shows you

success.body:
The prayer opens something — not a vision, precisely, but an awareness of the clearing's quality that is different from ordinary awareness. The empty centre of the table has a presence; the bread has the quality of bread in a specific way it did not have before. The hermit watches you from across the clearing with the satisfaction of a teacher whose student has understood the lesson before it was spoken.

You go to the feast knowing what kind of feast it is.

failure.body:
The prayer is sincere but the sight does not open — the clearing remains what it is, a cleared patch of forest with a table and bread. The hermit does not seem disappointed; some prayers are the right prayer at the wrong moment.

You eat the bread, which is good bread, and the company is good company, and that is not nothing.

entries.body:
The high mountain fortress of the bishop of the northern passes has opened its hall for a feast that is also a council — the Grail quest has created administrative chaos in the church's mountain parishes, as hermits multiply and pilgrims travel routes the local clergy cannot manage. The bishop has invited every knight passing through his territory to sit at his table and speak of what they have seen.

The feast is genuinely good — the bishop keeps a better table than the landscape suggests should be possible. But between every two knights, there is a priest or a canon with a notebook, and the questions are as systematic as they are hospitable.

responses.label: You may speak honestly with the bishop about what you have witnessed on your journey.
You may navigate the feast's questions to learn as much as you give and understand the bishop's true purpose.

entries.body: The bishop is an intelligent man, not merely an administrator — when you speak honestly, he responds in kind. He tells you things in exchange: the pattern of what the Grail-seeking knights have reported, which suggests a geography of experience that no single knight has seen whole. He is assembling a map of something that cannot be put on a map.

resolutions.label: contribute your experience to the bishop's account and help him understand the pattern more fully

success.body:
What you offer is not dramatic — a conversation overheard near a forest hermitage, a direction three knights took that the bishop has not had accounted for, a detail of the light at a particular crossroads that sounds small and is not. The bishop's canon writes it all with the care of a man who understands that small things become large in the right map.

The bishop gives you, in return, a piece of the pattern you did not have. You leave the mountain fortress knowing more about the shape of the quest than you arrived knowing.

failure.body:
What you offer does not fit the pattern as the bishop has assembled it — either your experience is genuinely anomalous, or the bishop's pattern has a flaw you cannot identify from where you sit. The exchange is good-willed but not productive.

You leave with the good food in you and a sense of questions that do not yet have the right shape.

entries.body: The bishop's purpose, read from the feast's structure, is not merely administrative. He is looking for something in the knights' accounts — a specific description, a specific location — and the canons' notes are not the record he is building; they are the sifting apparatus for the record he is building elsewhere. This is a harvest, and the feast is the threshing floor.

resolutions.label: move through the feast's questions with awareness of what is being sought and offer only what you choose

success.body:
You give the canons the ordinary things and keep the extraordinary thing — the hermit's description of a valley that fits no geography you have been told, which you mention only to the bishop himself, privately, over the last cup of wine. His reaction tells you that it is exactly what he has been sifting for.

The exchange you make with this information is a fair one, and what he gives you in return is equally withheld from the canons.

failure.body:
The bishop is more skilled at this than you are — he draws the extraordinary thing from you in the third canonical question, framed as a request for direction, and by the time you understand what you have given, it is given. He thanks you with genuine warmth.

You have learned the shape of a cleverer man's harvest, which is its own education.

entries.body:
The pilgrimage camp on the open plain has grown into an accidental feast — a group of Grail-seeking knights has stopped for the night at the same water source, and between them they have provisions enough for a proper meal, and the night is clear, and someone has started a fire large enough to call a gathering. A wandering friar has appeared from the direction of nowhere and is already saying grace.

There are seven knights at this fire, each from a different road. The friar asks each of them, in turn, what they have learned since they last sat at a proper table.

responses.label: You may answer the friar's question honestly and with full consideration.
You may listen to the other knights' answers first and understand what this gathering is before you speak.

entries.body: The friar receives your honest answer with the attention of a man who has heard seven hundred answers to this question and knows which ones are the real ones. He says nothing when you finish. He waits. The fire pops; the plain is dark beyond its light; the other knights are quiet.

resolutions.label: remain with the honesty and go deeper when the friar's silence opens the space

success.body:
The deeper answer is the one that the question was built for, and the friar receives it with the quiet of a man who has been given what he came to receive. He speaks after — not in response exactly, but in the way of a man who offers something because something has been offered to him. What he says about the Grail's nature is not scripture but is not less than scripture.

The fire burns lower; the stars are bright; the seven knights sit without moving for a long time after the friar finishes.

failure.body:
The deeper answer does not come — what surfaces in the friar's silence is not the truth of the matter but a more polished version of the honest answer, which is not quite the same thing. The friar nods and moves to the next knight.

You have said true things. You have not said the thing.

entries.body: The six knights who speak before you are not all honest and not all self-deceived — there is a spectrum, and the friar's silence after each one calibrates the quality of the answer without pronouncing on it. You have learned, by the time your turn comes, what the question is really asking and what an answer sounds like that the friar's silence receives well.

resolutions.label: give the answer that the observed pattern suggests the friar is listening for

success.body:
The answer you have prepared is true — what the observation allowed was not the fabrication of an answer but the stripping away of what was not true, leaving what was. The friar's silence after your answer is the same silence that followed the two honest knights who spoke before you.

Afterward, the friar speaks equally to all — but there is a quality in the way he looks at the honest three that is different from the way he looks at the others.

failure.body:
The pattern you read leads you to a formulation that is technically accurate but strategically constructed, and the friar's silence after it has a quality that is different from the silence he gave the two honest knights. He is not deceived. He does not comment.

You have learned the precise difference between honesty and the performance of honesty, which is a painful lesson.

entries.body:
The dark manse at the swamp's centre holds the strangest feast you have found on the Grail road — the host is a woman of middle years who knew your name before you gave it, whose manse has more rooms than its exterior suggests, and whose table is set with food that you cannot identify by sight but which, when eaten, tastes of things you recognise from your childhood.

She does not eat. She watches. At the meal's end, she says — as though continuing a conversation you did not know had started — that you are almost ready, and she will show you what you lack.

responses.label: You may accept her offer and learn what she believes you lack.
You may question her claim of knowledge before accepting the lesson.

entries.body: She takes you to a room in the manse that has no door — you enter it through the act of looking for it, and you are in it. The room contains a mirror and, in the mirror, not a reflection. The manse is stranger than it appeared. She is stranger than she appeared.

resolutions.label: look clearly at what the mirror shows and receive the lesson without flinching

success.body:
What the mirror shows is not monstrous — it is the gap between what you believe yourself to be and what you are. The gap is not vast, but it is real, and the specificity of seeing it without the usual mediations of memory and self-regard is an experience that does not end when you leave the room.

She says nothing when you return to the feast. The food on the table has changed. You have changed.

failure.body:
What the mirror shows you cannot hold — the image shifts or your attention shifts, and you come out of the room with a sense of something seen that will not stay in memory. The woman of the manse receives you back with neither disappointment nor reassurance.

Almost ready, she said. The almost remains.

entries.body: She receives the challenge with the equanimity of a woman who has had it before and found it reasonable. She does not prove her knowledge; she demonstrates a piece of it — she tells you something about your road that you did not tell her, something specific to the road's eastern branch where the three standing stones mark the boundary between parishes.

resolutions.label: test her further claim and determine whether her knowledge warrants the lesson she offers

success.body:
The testing is not cruel but it is thorough, and she passes it — what she knows is real knowledge, not the performance of knowledge, and the things you ask about that she does not know, she says she does not know, which is itself a sign of the genuine thing. You accept the lesson.

What you learn from her is something that will not be learned from a chapel hermit or a tournament victory.

failure.body:
The testing reveals a limit in her knowledge that she explains but which you cannot fully accept — the explanation is plausible, but the gap is larger than you expected from the opening demonstration. You take a partial lesson, cautiously received.

Partial wisdom is not no wisdom, but it is a different thing from the full account.

entries.body:
The island monastery at the sea's edge holds a feast for the Feast of the Apostles — a modest feast by court standards, bread and fish and wine that has been watered, but the monks' hospitality is entire and the hall is warm and the brothers sing between courses with a quality that fills the room differently than music played for entertainment.
