A knight is here who has been here for three months — stranded by weather, or by something else. He sits apart from both the monks and the visiting knights, eating in the particular isolation of a man who has decided something and not yet acted on the decision.

responses.label: You may go to the isolated knight and learn what he has decided.
You may speak with the prior about the knight and understand what three months in this place has done to him.

entries.body: The knight — Sir Elias of the Western March, a man you know by reputation if not by sight — looks at you when you approach with the expression of a man who has been hoping for an interruption. He has made his decision. He has decided to stay. He intends to take holy orders at this monastery. He is looking for someone to tell him that this is not a failure.

resolutions.label: tell him honestly whether this is a failure or something else

success.body:
You tell him that you cannot speak for the Grail's judgement, but you can speak for the quality of his service in the Western March, which you witnessed in part. A man who served well in the field and chooses to serve differently has not failed the service; he has found a different form of it. The monastery is a kind of vigil.

He receives this with the grateful attention of a man who needed to hear it from someone who had been in the field rather than from a man of God. The prior, nearby, hears it too and is satisfied.

failure.body:
You try to offer him what he needs, but what surfaces is comfort rather than honesty, and he recognises the difference immediately — he is a man who has been sitting with the question for three months and knows the shape of the evasive answer. He thanks you and returns to his food.

The comfort he needed was the harder kind.

entries.body: The prior of the monastery is a man who chooses his words with the care of a craftsman choosing tools — he says what he means and nothing adjacent to it. He tells you that Sir Elias arrived in a storm, that the storm passed, that the knight chose to remain when the sea opened again. What changed him, the prior says, was the offices — the daily rhythm of prayer, which the knight had never experienced without interruption.

resolutions.label: ask the prior what the monastery has given the knight that the quest could not

success.body:
The prior considers before he answers — not from evasiveness but from precision, because the question is a good one and deserves a precise answer. What he says is that the quest is a moving thing, and movement and contemplation are not the same gift; some men need to stop moving before they can see what they have been riding toward.

You carry the prior's answer with you when you leave the island, and it changes how you look at the road.

failure.body:
The prior answers the question but the answer is a theological one, true but at a distance from what you actually asked. The gap between what a man of the church can say and what the question needs is a gap you have encountered before on this road.

You leave the island with the monks' blessing and Sir Elias's wave from the quay, which is not nothing.

entries.body:
Mordred holds his court feast in the hall that was Arthur's hall, and the feast is magnificent — the food is better than in the golden years, the musicians are the finest that gold can purchase, and the lords of half of Britain have come to be seen at the table of the new power. The old devices have been taken down. New ones hang in their place.

You are here alone, and you are known as a man of the old fellowship. The feast's hospitality is real. The danger beneath it is also real.

responses.label: You may navigate the feast's social currents and preserve your position without giving Mordred what he wants.
You may find the lords at this feast who have not yet fully committed to Mordred and speak to them while the wine lasts.

entries.body: Mordred watches you from the high table with the calm interest of a man who is collecting information about everyone in the room. He has sent his steward to invite you to the seat nearest the high table — a place of honour that is also a position of visibility. He wants to know whether you will sit there.

resolutions.label: navigate the seating without accepting the high visibility position and without openly refusing

success.body:
The management of this is a careful piece of social geometry — you accept the invitation in principle, then find yourself in conversation with a lord further down the table, then are seated before the steward can redirect you, then make the distance from Mordred's eye seem like the natural result of the feast's organic arrangement rather than a choice. Mordred watches this with an appreciation that is not quite approval.

You have passed through his feast without paying his price, and he knows it, and you both know that the knowing is part of the transaction.

failure.body:
The geometry fails at the steward's second approach — the insistence is more forceful than anticipated, and refusing it openly would make the refusal a declaration. You take the seat near the high table and spend the feast under Mordred's interested regard, giving nothing but unable to claim the distance you sought.

You leave the feast unharmed. Unharmed is not the same as uncompromised.

entries.body: Three lords at this feast are not Mordred's men yet — you read it in the way they hold their cups and the way their eyes move toward the old devices' empty hooks on the wall. They are men of the old allegiance who came to Mordred's feast because absence would have been a statement and they are not yet ready to make the statement. They are deciding.

resolutions.label: speak to the three lords quietly and give them the language for what they are deciding

success.body:
The language you give them is not rhetoric — it is simply the naming of what they already know: that the feast's magnificence is real, that Mordred's power is real, and that eating at a table does not make a man its host. They can be here without being of here. The distinction matters.

One lord thanks you with his eyes only. The other two say nothing. All three ride for Arthur's position in the morning.

failure.body:
The words do not find the right form — the lords you speak to hear the appeal and weigh it, and the weighing goes the wrong way for at least one of them, who is closer to Mordred's side than his expression suggested. Your approach to the other two is now known to Mordred's man.

You leave quickly. Two of three remaining uncertain is better than zero.

entries.body:
A lord of the eastern forest who has kept his oath to Arthur holds a last feast for a company of knights preparing to ride north to join the king's final muster. The feast is held in a forest clearing because the lord's hall is no longer safe — Mordred's men have been seen on the road. The fire is smaller than it ought to be and the toasts are spoken quietly.

The lord's wife serves the wine herself. There is no retinue to spare for ceremony.

responses.label: You may speak to the company of knights and give them what a farewell feast should give before they ride.
You may speak privately with the lord and help him plan the ride north to keep the company intact.

entries.body: The company of knights — eleven men, none young, all carrying the accumulated wear of a campaign that has been losing ground — eat in the particular silence of men who have said what they mean to their families and are now past that kind of speech. They need something else. You have been in feasts before that were also farewells.

resolutions.label: speak to them of the worth of the riding, not the likely outcome

success.body:
The words that matter are not about Arthur's chances — they are about the quality of the oath and the dignity of keeping it when keeping it is costly. You speak of a knight you rode with fifteen years ago in the golden age, a name most of them know, who once said that an oath was worth most at its most expensive moment.

The fire seems larger after. The eleven men eat differently — with more presence, less distance. They ride in the morning without looking back.

failure.body:
The words do not quite reach — they are the right kind of words, but the eleven men are further gone into the interior silence than oratory can reach. They receive your speech with courtesy and without the spark you were reaching for.

They ride in the morning regardless. The riding was never in question; only the quality of the last night.

entries.body: The lord of the eastern forest knows his roads — he has been riding them for thirty years — but the northern route to Arthur's muster has three points where Mordred's patrols have been sighted, and the eleven knights are a company large enough to be noticed. There is an alternate way through the forest's deeper tracks. It adds three days and loses the road's advantages.

resolutions.label: work through the route with the lord and determine the safest line through the three patrol points

success.body:
The route you plan together is not the alternate forest path — it is a combination of roads, using the patrols' own predictable timing and two smaller tracks that bypass the most exposed sections without the full three-day addition. The lord looks at it on his map with the nod of a man who would have found it himself given another hour.

The company rides out the next morning on a road that will hold. That is the best the planning can provide.

failure.body:
The patrol timing proves harder to read than expected — the pattern you identify has an exception you did not account for, and the route you recommend exposes the company at the second patrol point more than the alternate forest path would have. The lord takes the longer road instead.

Three days added is three days arrived later. Later is not impossible.

entries.body:
The mountain fortress that belongs to neither Mordred nor Arthur has become a refuge — a lord of old family who refused all oaths in the Final Wars holds a feast for anyone who arrives at his gate regardless of allegiance, a last act of the old hospitality that the age of last things has not yet extinguished. Mordred's men are at his table. Arthur's men are at his table. They eat in the same room.

You have arrived alone. The lord watches the room with the exhausted attention of a man maintaining a peace that costs him something every meal.

responses.label: You may support the lord's peace by making yourself useful at the fault lines of the feast.
You may speak with the lord himself and understand what sustains him in this impossible position.

entries.body: The fault lines are visible by the third cup — Mordred's man and a grey-bearded knight of Arthur's household are seated two places apart and have been speaking through the man between them with an escalating courtesy that is not courtesy at all. The man between them has the look of someone who has been trying to step out of the road of two carts.

resolutions.label: interpose yourself in the exchange and redirect it before the courtesy breaks

success.body:
You take the seat beside Mordred's man — which has just been vacated by the man between them, who has found somewhere else to be — and open a third conversation that draws Mordred's man's attention. The grey-bearded knight takes the opening and turns to the person on his other side. The fault line closes without a scene.

The lord, from the high table, raises his cup in your direction by a fraction. It is sufficient.

failure.body:
The opening is too late — the courtesy breaks while you are still in motion toward it, and the scene that follows is not violent but is definitional: the two men have stated their allegiances publicly, in the mountain lord's hall, and the neutrality of the feast has been marked. The lord rises and separates them with a word.

He holds the peace. But it has cost him more than it should have.

entries.body: The mountain lord speaks to you in the cold outer corridor between the feast's third and fourth courses — a man who needs five minutes outside the room in which he is maintaining a peace that may not be maintainable. He is not despairing. He is calculating. He wants to know if the old hospitality has any value left or whether it is a gesture he is making alone.

resolutions.label: tell him plainly what his feast means to the knights who are arriving at his gate

success.body:
You tell him what it meant to you — arriving at his gate, finding a table that asked no oath, eating bread in a room that still held the old courtesy. In the age of last things, a feast that requires no allegiance is not a gesture. It is the last practice of something that Britain will need to remember it once had.

He is quiet for a moment. Then he goes back into the room and sits again at the high table with a different quality of resolution.

failure.body:
What you offer him is true, but it is not what he needs — he needs practical argument, and you give him feeling, and a calculating man in a difficult position does not find feeling sufficient. He thanks you with courtesy and returns to the hall.

He maintains the feast regardless. He does not need your words for that. He was looking for something more than he found.

entries.body:
The last camp feast before the great muster — three dozen of Arthur's remaining knights gathered on the plain south of the final battle's position, eating together for what may be the last time. The provisions are dwindling. What has been assembled is the best of what remains: good enough, by men who have known better.

A young knight — barely into his first year of arms — sits at the camp fire's edge. He is frightened. He has not yet learned to conceal it, and the older men are pretending not to see it, which is a kindness that is also an abandonment.

responses.label: You may sit with the young knight and give him what the older men's silence cannot.
You may address the camp as a whole and give the last feast its proper frame.

entries.body: The young knight — Sir Petyr, from a family of the northern borders, nineteen years old — does not try to conceal his fear when you sit beside him. He is past that. He asks you, without preamble, whether what they are riding into is what it looks like.

resolutions.label: give him an honest answer about what lies ahead and what that honest answer means for how he should ride

success.body:
You tell him: yes, it is what it looks like. And you tell him what that means for how to ride — not recklessly, not defensively, but with full attention, because the last battles require more presence than the easy ones, not less. The fear is information, not weakness; the information is that he is paying attention to the actual stakes.

He eats after with a different quality — still frightened, but with the fear in a useful place. He thanks you the next morning, on horseback, which is the last time you see him.

failure.body:
The honest answer, delivered without enough care for how a nineteen-year-old receives honesty, lands harder than you intended. He does not break — he has enough of the knight's training for that — but he retreats to the older men's silence, which is the silence of men who are not yet ready for the kind of honesty you offered.

You have given him a true thing. The giving was not the mistake; only the timing.

entries.body: Three dozen men eating together in the plain's dark have the quality of a gathering that knows itself to be a gathering of consequence. No one will say what the occasion is. Someone needs to say it, or the meal ends with the weight of the unsaid heavy on every man who rides tomorrow.

resolutions.label: speak to the camp and name what this feast is without diminishing it

success.body:
The naming is simple: you say that men who eat together before a hard road do something worth doing, and the eating together is itself the purpose, not preparation for a purpose. The feast is the thing. The fellowship is the thing. Whatever comes tomorrow, this meal is real and complete.

The camp receives it — not with cheering, but with the particular quality of a room that has been given the right words for what it is feeling. Three dozen men eat their dwindling provisions with more presence than they had before you spoke.

failure.body:
The naming reaches for too much — the words try to make the moment more than it is, and the men on the plain can feel the reaching, which undermines the naming. They appreciate the intent. They eat their provisions in the same silence they were in before.

The attempt was not wrong. Only the scale was wrong.

entries.body:
The dark manse at the swamp's centre has new occupants — the lord who kept it died in the first year of the Final Wars, and what has inherited his hospitality is a company of women who fled the city's fall and have established the old custom with the resources available to them. The feast they hold is small and honest.

You have arrived alone and wet from the swamp crossing, and the welcome is genuine but measured. The women of this manse have learned to read strangers carefully. One of them — the eldest — has something she wishes to give you that she has been keeping since the lord's death.

responses.label: You may receive whatever the eldest woman has been holding and hear its account.
You may earn the trust of the household first, before accepting what they have held in trust.

entries.body: The eldest woman brings out a sealed letter from a chest near the fire — the old lord's seal, addressed to a name you recognise: a lord of the western reaches who has been dead these two years, though the letter's writer did not know this when he wrote it. The letter contains something that is now yours to decide what to do with.

resolutions.label: read the letter and determine what its contents require of you

success.body:
The letter is a land covenant — the old lord's agreement with the lord of the western reaches, witnessed and sealed, specifying the disposition of the manse and its grounds if the line of either family failed. The old lord's family has failed; the western lord's family has not. The covenant has heirs who do not yet know they are heirs.

You understand what the letter requires: not legal action, but a journey — to the western reaches, to find the heirs, and to put the covenant in their hands before Mordred's administration makes it moot.

failure.body:
The letter's legal language is denser than you can fully unravel in the manse's firelight — you understand its general import but not the specific obligation, and acting on the general import without the specific is a way of acting wrongly with good intent. You take the letter for later consideration.

The women of the manse receive your caution with respect. Caution with other people's covenants is not a fault.

entries.body: The eldest woman watches your movement through the manse's evening with the assessment of a person who has spent two years reading strangers. The household's courtesy is complete but its trust is not automatic. You have a meal and a fire and a dry place to sleep. The letter stays in the chest until the trust is established.

resolutions.label: demonstrate through the evening's actions that you are a person fit to carry what the household holds

success.body:
The demonstration is not dramatic — you eat what is given without complaint, you speak of where you have come from without inflation, you help move wood for the fire without being asked, and you ask the elder woman a question about the manse's history that shows you are interested in the place and not merely in what it can provide. By the end of the evening, the chest is open.

Trust given for good reason is the only trust worth having.

failure.body:
Something in the evening's manner reads wrong — not dishonestly, but with the automatic courtesies of a man who has learned courtesy as performance rather than practice. The elder woman sees the difference. The chest stays closed.

You sleep warmly and leave in the morning with her blessing and without her letter. Some things are rightly guarded.

entries.body:
The island fortress off the southern coast has kept the old ways — the sea lord who holds it never swore to Mordred, never sent his fleet north for Arthur's wars, and holds his little island as a remnant of a Britain that operated by different rules. He has invited you to his feast across a narrow strait of difficult water.

The feast is excellent — the sea provides, and the sea lord knows what to do with what it provides. But the invitation has a purpose, and the purpose is a thing he waits until the last cup to name.

responses.label: You may hear the sea lord's purpose and consider it on its merits.
You may name the purpose before he reaches it and give him the answer before the asking.

entries.body: The sea lord's purpose, named at last over the final cup, is this: he has a ship ready and a route south across the Channel that Mordred cannot intercept. He is offering passage to anyone who needs it — a way out of Britain before the last battle closes the roads and the sea becomes the last option. He is asking whether you know people who should take the passage.

resolutions.label: think carefully through who in Britain should know of this passage and how to reach them

success.body:
The thinking is a careful inventory of lives and circumstances — who among Arthur's circle has cause to flee rather than fight, who can be reached before the roads close, who has the kind of knowledge or the kind of family that makes their survival a thing worth the sea lord's risk. The names you give him are the right names.

He writes them in his logbook with the care of a man who intends to find each one. Whether he succeeds is beyond the island's reach. The attempt is real.

failure.body:
The names you think of are the obvious ones, and the obvious ones are already beyond reach — already taken, already committed to the fight, already gone. The sea lord receives the list with the patience of a man who expected this answer but hoped for a better one.

He will watch for opportunities the list did not provide.

entries.body: The sea lord watches you speak his purpose before he reaches it with the expression of a man who is unsure whether to be surprised or impressed — he has prepared this conversation carefully, and you have arrived at its destination before him. He sets down his cup. He waits for the answer that follows the naming.

resolutions.label: give the sea lord your answer and make it a commitment rather than a consideration

success.body:
The answer you give is yes — not to taking passage yourself, but to serving as the link between the island and the people in Britain who should take it. You commit to carrying his offer back through the roads before they close, and you give him a timetable you can keep. He accepts it with the handshake of a man who has learned to read promises accurately.

The last cup of wine at this feast tastes like a thing worth drinking.

failure.body:
The commitment you offer is real but its terms are vague — you have anticipated his request but not prepared your answer, and the gap between the two is wide enough that the sea lord is not certain whether he has a partner or a well-meaning guest. He holds his plan with less confidence than he arrived at the evening with.

You leave the island knowing you gave less than the occasion required.

entries.body:
The mist comes down without warning — not weather, but something with intention — and through it you hear water, though you know of no lake in this direction. A woman in a grey mantle stands at the edge of your sight, neither beckoning nor retreating, watching you with the composure of one who has all the time that exists.

She carries a basket of herbs that have no names you recognise, and the smell of them is like sleep without dreaming. She is one of Avalon's own, and she is measuring you — for what purpose, she has not yet said.

responses.label: You may ask what Avalon's sisters require of a knight who would approach their isle.
You may offer to carry the herbs and walk with her a while without pressing questions.

entries.body: The woman in grey considers your question with the patience of one accustomed to being asked the wrong thing. Then she speaks — not in answer, but in correction. What Avalon requires, she says, is not what any knight has yet been able to give. But she will tell you what the isle is willing to receive, and that is a different matter.

resolutions.label: answer her conditions honestly and accept what the isle will offer

success.body:
The conditions are not difficult — they are simply exact, and exactness is its own kind of difficulty. You speak truthfully of what you carry: your failures as readily as your deeds, the oaths you have kept and the one or two you have not. The woman nods, once.

She gives you a vial of water from Avalon's well. Its virtue is its own, and you will know when to use it.

failure.body:
The conditions expose you — not to her judgment, which is neither kind nor cruel, but to your own. You answer as well as you are able, but the answers do not satisfy the exactness the isle requires. The woman withdraws into the mist without cruelty.

The mist lifts, and you are on a familiar road again. The encounter has its own teaching.

entries.body:
You fall into step beside her, carrying the basket, and say nothing. She does not seem surprised by this. She moves through the landscape as though the landscape were simply a room she knows, and after a time she begins to name the herbs she has gathered — their uses, their seasons, and what each one costs to collect.

She is testing you in the oldest way: not with riddles but with attention. To listen well here is itself a form of skill.

resolutions.label: attend to her knowledge and remember what she teaches

success.body:
You listen with your whole attention, and the names settle into you as names do when they are the right ones — not memorised but understood. When she stops at last and takes back the basket, she looks at you with something that might be approval, in the way an isle accustomed to disappointing knights registers a pleasant deviation.

She leaves you a dried cutting of a plant whose name you now know, and with it a knowledge of what it heals.

failure.body:
The names blur together — the conditions of her naming are too precise, the distinctions too fine for a knight's education. She sees it happen and does not fault you; she simply adjusts, giving you the broadest lessons only. These are still lessons.

You remember one plant's name, and that is one more than you knew.

entries.body:
A rider in Benwick colours — azure field, three gold lions — overtakes you on the road at midday and reins in with the crisp courtesy of a knight who has been trained in a tradition stricter than most. He carries a sealed dispatch, and his business is not with you, but his business will take him the same road for two days, and the offer of company comes with an assessing look.

Benwick's knights are among the finest in the world and they know it, which makes their fellowship conditional. He is deciding whether you are worth travelling with.

responses.label: You may match his formal manner and make clear you know the courtesies of the road.
You may demonstrate your quality plainly, as a knight rather than as a diplomat.

entries.body: The formal courtesies of the road between knights have been the same in every Christian kingdom since the first tournaments, and Benwick follows them precisely — the exchange of precedence, the acknowledgment of retinue, the careful etiquette of rank among equals. He is watching to see whether you know the forms as well as he does.

resolutions.label: conduct yourself with exact courtesy throughout the two days' riding

success.body:
The two days are conducted like a long formal dance — each exchange precise, each courtesy answered in kind — and at the end the Benwick knight loosens into something approaching warmth, which for a man of his training is a significant concession. He speaks frankly of his dispatch and what it means for the politics of the continent.

You part with a promise of welcome should you ever reach Benwick's gates, and that promise, from such a man, is worth holding.

failure.body:
You manage the forms well enough for the first day, but by the second a small error — a question asked before the right moment, a precedence misjudged — produces a perceptible cooling. He does not rebuke you. He simply becomes less available.

You have learned where the edges of Benwick's courtesy lie.

entries.body: The Benwick knight has assessed your bearing and found it potentially sufficient, but his tradition prizes tested skill above all self-recommendation. He proposes a friendly passage of arms — mounted, blunted lances — at a clearing ahead. No prize except the satisfaction of knowing. He says this as though it were entirely ordinary.

resolutions.label: meet him in the lists with full commitment and good cheer

success.body:
Three passes — the first an exchange of measure, the second a full test, the third a decisive moment — and you acquit yourself in a way that earns his unambiguous respect. He remounts without ceremony and continues the road with you as though you have always known each other.

He tells you, as you ride, which of Benwick's gates are always open to a knight who rides as you do.

failure.body:
He unseats you cleanly on the second pass, and the third is a formality. He helps you up without condescension — a mark of his own quality — and the road continues companionably enough, though you ride in the position of the student rather than the equal.

There is instruction in the fall, if you are willing to take it.

entries.body:
You are three days north of anywhere that has a name on any map when the stones appear on the horizon — enormous, older than memory, standing in their circle on the moor as though they arrived there before the land itself was finished. The wind around them is different from the wind elsewhere: not colder, but older, as though it has been circling those stones since before the first human word.

A woman is seated at the circle's edge — not a hermit, not a maiden in distress. She is something else, dressed in undyed wool, watching you approach with the equanimity of one for whom all arrivals are equally interesting.

responses.label: You may ask her what the stones are and what purpose they still serve.
You may enter the circle without asking and see what it shows you.

entries.body:
The woman at the circle's edge does not answer your question directly — this seems to be a quality of those who keep such places. She answers a different question: not what the stones are, but what they remember. She speaks of a covenant between this land and its people that was made before any name was given to either, and the keeping of that covenant is the stones' purpose.

She asks what you, a knight of Arthur's age, know of covenants made before Arthur's time.

resolutions.label: speak honestly of what the land has asked of you and what you have given

success.body:
What you say is not sophisticated — the land's demands on a knight are simple: to ride through it honestly, to take what you need and no more, to treat what is alive with the respect owed to what was alive before you. The woman nods at this. It is, she says, what the stones would have said if they spoke plainly.

She gives you a piece of the moor's grey stone, worked into a small shape by hands long dead. It is old enough to carry weight beyond its size.

failure.body:
The question exposes the distance between a knight's world and the world the stones remember. You answer with what you know, but what you know is not enough — the covenants you have kept are too recent, too legible, too Christian to satisfy something this old. The woman does not dismiss you. She simply closes that particular door.

You leave the circle knowing the gap better than before.

entries.body:
Inside the circle the wind stops. The silence is not empty — it has texture, weight, the quality of a very long patience. The stones do not acknowledge you, but the space between them seems aware of you in the way that deep water is aware of a stone dropped into it: not hostile, not welcoming, simply responsive.

Something in the centre of the circle is asking to be understood. Not in words. In whatever sense lies beneath words.

resolutions.label: open yourself to what the circle shows and endure what that requires

success.body:
What the circle shows is not the future — it is the past made present, layer beneath layer, the land's own memory stretching back to a time before roads and names and kingdoms. You see Britain as it was, which is also, in some sense, as it still is beneath all the building and breaking.

You carry something of that seeing with you when you step out of the circle. It does not leave you entirely.

failure.body:
The showing is too much — not hostile, simply too vast and too old for a knight's education in such things. You step out of the circle after a time with no clear memory of what you experienced, only a sense of having been in the presence of something very large.

That sense has its own worth.

entries.body:
A messenger bearing Camelot's white dragon seal intercepts you on the road, and what he carries is not merely a summons — it is a question from the king's council, passed by hand through three riders before reaching you. The council requires a judgment on a matter of precedence between two lords' claims, and the matter has been given to you because you are near enough to answer quickly.

The weight of Camelot is in the seal even when the king is not present. To answer this question is to answer it in Arthur's name, and Camelot's name suffers the consequence of any answer badly given.

responses.label: You may deliver your judgment and accept the responsibility that accompanies it.
You may investigate the matter further before committing to a judgment.

entries.body:
The precedence question is not simple: one lord holds his land by ancient custom, the other by recent grant from the crown, and the law of each age has answered such questions differently. You must give an answer that is just — and that can be defended as just to both men, which is the harder part.

The messenger waits with his wax and parchment. Whatever you say will be written.

resolutions.label: deliver a judgment that weighs the claims with fairness and clarity

success.body:
The judgment you give is not perfect — no judgment in such a matter can be — but it is honest, and its reasoning holds. The messenger records it and rides north. Word comes back weeks later that both lords accepted the ruling, not happily, but without appealing further.

Camelot's name has been served.

failure.body:
The judgment you give proves to hold a flaw the council later identifies — a misreading of the ancient custom's scope that allows one lord grounds for further appeal. No dishonour attaches to you; these are difficult waters. But the matter is reopened, and Camelot's council must spend time it does not easily spare.

You have learned what the law demands of those who speak in its name.

entries.body: The messenger is patient — he has ridden hard already and can wait another day — and there is a lord nearby whose tenure pre-dates both claimants and who may know the precedents. To delay is to take responsibility for the delay, but a judgment made on insufficient knowledge is no judgment at all.

resolutions.label: gather what evidence remains and reason from what can be established

success.body:
The old lord's memory is excellent — he recalls the original grant's conditions and the custom that preceded it — and what he tells you reshapes the question. The judgment you give with that knowledge is cleaner and more defensible than anything you could have offered the day before.

The messenger rides with it to Camelot, and the matter is closed.

failure.body:
The old lord's memory has its own gaps — age has blurred the very precedent you needed — and you must give your judgment with less certainty than you hoped for. It holds well enough, but its weakness is visible to anyone who looks closely, and someone will look closely.

You have done what you could with what was available.

entries.body:
The landscape changed without your noticing — the trees too still, the light at the wrong angle for the hour, the distant water visible through a gap that should not be there. A young man in a fishing boat on that water looks at you with eyes too old for his face, and when he speaks his voice carries across an impossible distance.

He invites you to the far shore. He does not explain what waits there. The Fisher King does not explain himself, and those who reach Corbenic already know that the Grail's demands are not announced in advance.

responses.label: You may accept the invitation and cross to the far shore with an open heart.
You may ask what question must be asked before you cross.

entries.body:
The crossing is short and long simultaneously — these waters operate outside ordinary measure. When you reach the far shore, the castle is there, its towers narrow against a sky that is neither day nor night. Inside, the Fisher King sits before a fire, diminished and radiant in the way of those whose wound is also their purpose.

The Grail passes through the hall. This is the moment. The question is in your chest already. Whether you can speak it clearly is the whole of the test.

resolutions.label: ask the question the Grail requires without flinching from its fullness

success.body:
The question comes from the place in you that does not calculate consequence — the place where compassion lives when it is not performing itself. 'Whom does the Grail serve?' The words are simple. The asking is not.

The Fisher King's face changes. The wound does not close — that is not yet — but the hall becomes briefly luminous, and what you receive is not a reward in any ordinary sense. It is a weight added to your purpose.

failure.body:
The question does not come. It is there — you can feel its shape — but something holds it, some caution or unworthiness or simply the vast difficulty of asking the right thing at the right moment. The Grail passes. The Fisher King looks at you with neither reproach nor pity.

You cross back to the ordinary shore. The castle is gone when you look. You were close.

entries.body:
The young man on the boat — or the simulacrum of a young man; it is hard to know what the Grail Castle shows you and what it is — pauses at your question. The question about the question is itself a question of a kind, and Corbenic has no interest in those who arrive already knowing what they are doing.

He asks you, instead, what brought you to this shore to begin with. Not your journey. Your reason.

resolutions.label: speak your true reason for seeking Corbenic, whatever that reason is

success.body:
The true reason is not heroic — it rarely is. It is something smaller and more personal: a wound of your own, a seeking that has not yet found its object, a grief that has not yet been given the right shape. You say it plainly. The boatman nods and takes up his pole.

The crossing is made, and the castle does not disappear before you reach it.

failure.body:
The reason you give is the performed reason — the chivalric reason, the one that sounds right — and the boatman receives it with a patience that is its own verdict. The boat does not move. After a long time, the light shifts, the water changes colour, and the far shore is an ordinary bank with no castle on it.

Corbenic has not denied you. It has simply asked you to be more honest with yourself before you return.

entries.body:
The invitation arrives as a sealed letter carried by a boy with no retinue, and the seal is one you have seen described but never seen in person — Morgan le Fay's own mark. The letter is short: you are expected at the castle's lower gate by nightfall, and the hospitality offered is genuine, as far as it goes. That final clause is Morgan's.

The castle is visible from where you stand, its grey towers neither welcoming nor threatening — simply there, with the patience of a place that has many arrangements running simultaneously.

responses.label: You may ride to the lower gate and accept the hospitality on Morgan's stated terms.
You may approach with caution and assess the enchantments before entering.

entries.body:
The gate is open as promised, and a steward of perfect courtesy leads you to a hall where the meal is already laid. Morgan le Fay sits at the head of the table — not in the throne of a ruler but in the ease of a scholar at her work — and she looks at you with an interest that is neither warm nor cold.

She has prepared a test, as she always does. She will not name it. That is also part of the test.

resolutions.label: engage with her hospitality fully and meet whatever it conceals without dissembling

success.body:
The test reveals itself gradually — in the questions she asks over the meal, in the objects she places before you as though incidentally, in the small choices she offers that are not small. You meet each one without pretending it is other than what it is, and Morgan, who has seen every variety of self-deception a court can produce, registers this with a fractional relaxation.

She gives you a piece of knowledge about yourself that you did not have before. It is not comfortable, but it is accurate.

failure.body:
One of the choices reveals you — not to Morgan, who already knew, but to yourself. A preference you disguised, a value you did not know you were acting from. Morgan does not exploit this. She simply acknowledges it with the courtesy of one who has done you the service of showing it.

You leave her hospitality with an accurate if uncomfortable new understanding.

entries.body: The enchantments on Morgan's castle are not aggressive — she has no need for aggression — but they are many and layered, each one serving a different purpose. Some turn visitors back. Some record. Some alter what a visitor sees without altering what is there. The ability to read these distinctions is half the art of moving through the castle with any accuracy.

resolutions.label: discern the castle's enchantments and navigate them with clear sight

success.body:
The reading takes time and attention — Morgan's work is not careless — but the patterns reveal themselves to someone who knows where to look. You enter the castle knowing what is actual and what is presented for your benefit, which is not the same thing.

Morgan meets you with the particular approval she reserves for those who arrive without being fooled. She is a better host to such guests.

failure.body:
The enchantments are more intricate than your reading anticipated, and you enter having identified the outermost layer only. Inside, you spend the visit navigating a space that is subtly different from the space you think you are in, and the difference matters in small ways that accumulate.

Morgan is not unkind about it. She has seen this before.

entries.body:
The ship came down from the north without apparent urgency, and the knight who steps ashore is of Orkney's stock unmistakably — the broad build, the direct eyes, the quality of silence that is not unfriendliness but the northern code's insistence that words should mean something before they are spent. He has been at sea for twelve days on business he has not been asked to explain.

He asks for one thing: to know whether you are a knight whose word, once given, remains given. He frames it as a matter of courtesy, but the question has a different kind of weight.

responses.label: You may answer his question directly and stand by what you say.
You may ask what he requires before answering — fair terms on both sides.

entries.body:
To answer a northern knight's question about honour is to accept that the answer will be taken as a binding statement. He listens to your answer with the completeness of a man who does not take notes because he does not forget things, and when you are done he nods once.

He then makes a request. He needs a companion for a difficult errand — one that requires both strength and the willingness to be in the wrong place at the wrong time for a cause that has not yet been explained to you.

resolutions.label: commit to the errand on the strength of the Orkney oath between you

success.body:
The errand is exactly as advertised: difficult, unglamorous, and undertaken in conditions that test the commitment you gave at the shore. You complete it. The Orkney knight does not praise you, because in the northern code, completing what you said you would complete does not require praise. He clasps your arm once.

The word of an Orkney knight, once given, now runs both ways. This has value.

failure.body:
The errand breaks against conditions neither of you anticipated — weather, an unexpected party, a road that has closed since the intelligence was gathered. The Orkney knight does not fault you: the failure was the errand's, not yours. But the errand remains incomplete.

You have shown enough that the northern door is not closed. That is not nothing.

entries.body:
The Orkney knight receives your counter-question with a moment's consideration — it is not the northern custom, but it is fair, and the northern code understands fair. He tells you what the errand is, which is a cold piece of work in difficult country, and what he is offering in return, which is the standing of a knight whose oath is now in your keeping.

This is the northern negotiation: frank, without ornament.

resolutions.label: negotiate the terms cleanly and reach an honest compact

success.body:
The negotiation is brief because you both know the shape of a fair arrangement — the terms that leave neither party diminished. What you agree to is harder than you might have accepted without asking, but it is also cleaner: specific, measurable, and true on both sides.

The Orkney knight shakes on it with the gravity of a man who considers that gesture a legal instrument.

failure.body:
The negotiation reveals a gap in your understanding of what the errand requires — a detail you should have pressed for but did not — and the compact is made with that gap in it. The errand is harder than it needed to be, and the arrangement's edge favours the Orkney knight rather than the balance you intended.

Fair enough: you learn to press harder on the detail next time.

entries.body:
The plain around the stones is empty at this hour, and the stones themselves are doing what they do — which is nothing visible, and therefore everything. The sound the wind makes through the lintels is not music and not language, but it is structured, and anyone who stands here long enough begins to hear the structure.

A pilgrim is seated against the outermost stone, wrapped in a travel cloak, and he has been here long enough that the structure has reached him. He looks up at your approach and says, without preamble, that the stones spoke to him this morning. He does not know what they said.

responses.label: You may stand among the stones yourself and listen for what they carry.
You may help the pilgrim interpret what he received — the stones speak to every listener differently.

entries.body:
Among the stones the quality of attention required is different from what a knight ordinarily brings to a situation — not alert vigilance, but a stilled receptiveness, the kind that requires you to stand without purpose for long enough that purpose itself becomes unnecessary.

The stones do carry something. Whether you can receive it is a question of what you have learned to hear.

resolutions.label: stand in stillness among the stones and receive what they are able to give

success.body:
The reception does not happen all at once — it accumulates, the way cold accumulates in stone overnight. What arrives is not prophecy in the sense of a vision with clear content, but a sense of the realm's long arc: where it has been, what it has survived, where the stresses are that will matter in the days ahead.

You leave the stones carrying that sense, which is Merlin's gift in another form.

failure.body:
The stillness is harder than you anticipated — the mind's business keeps arriving — and after a long time among the stones you step away without clear reception, only the sense of having been in a very patient presence that was neither hurried nor disappointed by your limitations.

The patience itself instructs.

entries.body:
The pilgrim's experience has the quality of genuine reception — the incoherence, the physical effects, the inability to translate into words — and he has come from a long way to stand here, which suggests a preparation that makes the experience plausible. What he received is incomplete, as such things often are. The stones give the pieces; the interpretation is the listener's work.

You sit with him and begin to ask what details he remembers.

resolutions.label: reason through his experience carefully and help him find what it means

success.body:
The details he remembers are scattered but specific — specific enough that, assembled, they suggest a pattern. The interpretation you reach together is not certain, but it is coherent: a warning about a road, or a person, or an approach to a problem, phrased in the stones' oblique idiom.

The pilgrim thanks you with a precision that suggests the interpretation has confirmed something he already half-knew.

failure.body:
The details resist assembly — some are contradictory, some are too symbolic to anchor — and the interpretation you reach is more your own reasoning than the stones' communication. The pilgrim receives it politely, but his eyes suggest he knows the difference.

You have tried honestly. Sometimes the stones simply do not yield.

entries.body:
The abbey's outer wall is visible from the crest of the hill, and coming down the road you meet one of its monks travelling in the other direction — a young man carrying a manuscript case with the careful grip of someone transporting something irreplaceable. He has been three weeks away from the abbey on a copying commission, and he is three weeks from home.

The manuscript case bears a crack in the leather, and the road ahead of him is a river ford that rises in rain. The sky to the west is darkening.

responses.label: You may escort him to the ford and ensure the crossing is made safely.
You may find a different route for him — longer, but dry — and ride with him to the abbey.

entries.body:
The ford is navigable — barely — and the rain has not yet arrived, but the path through the crossing requires reading the water's depth correctly, choosing the line that keeps the manuscript case out of the current. The monk is strong enough and willing, but he has not forded many rivers with his hands occupied.

You must manage both the crossing and the protection of what is carried.

resolutions.label: lead the ford crossing and keep the manuscript safe through it

success.body:
The crossing is wet and close — the water comes higher than the shallow line you had chosen — but you get the monk and his case to the far side before the rain reaches the ford. The case is damp at its edges only. The manuscript, when the monk checks it with shaking hands, is unharmed.

The monk thanks you with the gratitude of a man who understands exactly what was at stake.

failure.body:
The line you chose is not deep enough to be the deep one, and you are mid-ford when you understand your error. You manage the monk's safety entirely — that is never in question — but the manuscript case goes fully under for a moment, and several pages of the interior are damaged beyond legible recovery.

The monk does not fault you. The road, the rain, and the ford are not your fault. But the pages are lost.

entries.body:
The longer route adds two hours and requires passage through a village where the abbey has standing relations — the abbot's name opens what needs opening there, and the monk carries a letter with that authority. But two hours on an autumn road are not nothing, and the monk has already been three weeks away from his work.

You must find the route and keep good pace without pushing a monk who has been walking for three weeks.

resolutions.label: navigate the route and bring him to the abbey in good time and good condition

success.body:
The route you choose threads between the worst of the rising ground and arrives at the abbey's rear gate by vespers — late, but within the hour of it. The manuscript is dry. The monk is tired but complete. The abbot himself meets you at the gate, recognising the manuscript case before he recognises the monk.

The abbey's welcome is warm, and the abbot's library is open to you before you sleep.

failure.body:
The route takes a wrong turning in the dark of a beech-wood, and the correction costs an extra hour that arrives at the abbey well past vespers. The manuscript is safe, the monk is unharmed, but the gatekeeper is less warmly disposed to late arrivals than the abbot would be.

You are given shelter and no more than that. The manuscript's safety is its own satisfaction.

entries.body:
The cave's mouth is visible from the road — barely, set into a hillside that has been growing over the entrance for years. Those who know where to look know this hill; those who do not have passed it a hundred times without finding it. You found it because something drew your eye to the particular shape of moss on the particular stone.

Inside, deeper than the entrance suggests, something is present that is not air and not silence. Those who have studied such matters call it Merlin's residue — what remains when a great working has been done in a place and the worker is no longer present.

responses.label: You may go deeper into the cave and address what is present there.
You may examine the cave's outer chamber for what Merlin left that can be read and learned.

entries.body:
The deeper chamber is lit by nothing and visible by something that is not light — the old word for it is 'gnosis', the knowing that requires no exterior source. What is present here is not Merlin in any simple sense, but it is not nothing; it is the residue of a mind that worked here for decades, and it has opinions.

The opinions press at you without words. They require answers the cave has been waiting to receive from the right questioner.

resolutions.label: answer the cave's pressing with what you truly know and truly believe

success.body:
The pressing is a test of whether you know the difference between what you have been told and what you have understood, between what you believe and what you merely repeat. The answers you give from that distinction satisfy the residue in a way that the clever answers would not have.

What you receive in return is not knowledge so much as a re-arrangement of knowledge you already had — a sudden coherence of what had been separate, a gift from a teacher who is no longer present in any ordinary sense.

failure.body:
The pressing reveals that you know less of the distinction between understanding and repetition than you had supposed. The residue does not dismiss you — it simply stops pressing, the way a question stops when the questioner sees the answer is not available.

You leave the deeper chamber with a clear sense of what you would need to know before you returned.

entries.body:
The outer chamber holds what a scholar would recognise as a working space: marks on the stone that are not purely decorative, a shelf of sorts carved into the rock bearing several objects long since drained of immediate power but still legible to someone who knows the grammar of such things.

Merlin was a systematic worker. What he left in the outer chamber, he left to be found by someone able to read it.

resolutions.label: study the outer chamber's marks and objects and extract what understanding remains

success.body:
The grammar of the marks takes an hour to work through, and the objects yield their purposes to patient examination — each was a tool for a specific kind of working, and understanding the tool tells you something about the working. What you learn is not a spell or a prophecy, but a methodology: how Merlin approached the problems that required his particular gifts.

The methodology has uses that extend beyond magic, for any problem that requires structured thought.

failure.body:
The marks resist your reading — not because they are obscure in principle, but because the grammar requires a background you do not have. You understand fragments: a word here, a purpose there, enough to know that what you are missing is significant.

You leave with the fragments, which are more than you arrived with.

entries.body:
The chapel is further from any road than any chapel should reasonably be — it has been there since before the current road, and the road chose not to acknowledge it. Moss covers the roof and the walls and the lintel-stone above the door, and something is engraved in that stone that has not worn away despite everything else wearing away.

A very large man in green sits on a stone beside the chapel door, resting his chin in one hand, and his axe is embedded in the ground beside him as though it has always been there. He looks up at your approach without surprise.

responses.label: You may greet him as an equal and ask the terms of his presence here.
You may examine the engraving on the lintel and read what the chapel demands before speaking to the man.

entries.body:
The Green Knight receives your greeting with a formality that is also a slight mockery of formality — the northern courtesy performed one degree too exactly to be entirely respectful. He explains, as though it is a matter of public record, that the chapel requires a bargain of a specific kind: a knight may strike a blow now, and receive one in return, here, in one year's time.

He says he has been waiting today for someone. He does not say they were waiting for you specifically.

resolutions.label: accept the terms of the bargain and stand to the covenant

success.body:
You accept with the directness the Green Knight's code requires — neither bravado nor reluctance, simply the clean acknowledgment of a covenant entered into with open eyes. His expression shifts into something that might be respect, or might be the northern equivalent of it.

What the year holds is unknown. But the covenant has been made honestly, and that is the thing the chapel is here to test.

failure.body:
The acceptance is made, but something in your manner — a hesitation, an involuntary qualifier — tells the Green Knight that the covenant has been entered with a reservation. He accepts it anyway, as the chapel's terms require. But he tells you, without cruelty, that the reservation will cost you something when the year is out.

The covenant stands. You have a year to make it true.

entries.body:
The engraving on the lintel is in a language that is neither Latin nor the old British tongue but something between them — a formal register, used for covenants and contracts in the age before written law. The chapel's dedication is to the keeping of one's word, specifically, and the conditions of that keeping are written out with legal precision.

To read this before speaking to the Green Knight is to understand what the chapel will and will not permit.

resolutions.label: read and understand the chapel's covenant-law before engaging with the Green Knight

success.body:
The engraving is detailed and specific — it names exceptions, qualifications, the conditions under which the bargain is binding and those under which it is void. The knowledge of these specifics gives you a different kind of standing when you turn to face the Green Knight, who sees that you have done your preparation and whose manner shifts accordingly.

The bargain you negotiate from this position is one you understand on both sides.

failure.body:
The language resists you — the formal register is archaic beyond your schooling — and you get the broad outlines but not the specifics. You know the shape of the bargain without knowing its edges, which is better than nothing but not the advantage you hoped for.

The Green Knight engages with you anyway. The chapel's business must be transacted.

entries.body:
The lake is still enough to be a mirror, and in the mirror something moves that does not move above the surface. Then — without the surface breaking, which should be impossible — a hand emerges and holds up a sword, and the sword is of a quality that has no name in the world above the water.

A voice that is not carried by air says you may come further, or you may speak from the shore. Either is accepted. The invitation is genuine. Faerie bargains often are, which is not the same thing as them being simple.

responses.label: You may enter the water and cross into the realm below.
You may speak to the voice from the shore and negotiate from there.

entries.body:
The water closes over you without coldness — it is the wrong temperature for water, as everything in faerie is the wrong version of itself in ways you cannot object to, only note. The palace below is lit by no visible source and furnished in the manner of an age that has not existed above the surface for centuries.

The Lady of the Lake receives you in a hall where time is slower by the degree that makes negotiations possible. She has something she is willing to give, and something she requires in return.

resolutions.label: accept the faerie exchange and hold to your part of the bargain

success.body:
The exchange is made in the faerie manner — not with the bluntness of a commercial transaction, but with the elaborate indirection of a gift given in expectation of a gift returned, both parties maintaining the fiction that nothing has been asked for. You navigate this with the ease of someone who understands that the form of the negotiation is not separate from the content.

What you receive from the Lady is real and lasting. What you give is less than you expected to give.

failure.body:
The indirection of the faerie exchange proves more demanding than your courtly training anticipated — what you offer is not wrong, but it is offered in a manner too direct, too transactional, and the Lady receives it with a disappointment that is also a degree of respect for the honesty involved.

The exchange is made on lesser terms. You return to the surface with something, and an understanding of what you would need to do differently.

entries.body:
From the shore the voice is clear enough — clearer than it should be across the water — and the negotiation proceeds in plain terms, which is unusual for faerie dealings and suggests that the Lady of the Lake, in this instance, prefers efficiency to elaborate form. She has something lost that she knows you are looking for. She has conditions for its return.

The conditions are not onerous. That is what makes them worth examining carefully.

resolutions.label: examine the conditions carefully and ensure what is agreed is what is meant

success.body:
The careful examination reveals two places where the conditions' plain language has a second reading that is not plain at all — faerie contracts are not dishonest, but they are precise, and imprecision is the negotiator's fault, not the faerie's. You press for clarification on both points and receive it.

The agreement made on those terms is solid, and what is returned to you is exactly what was described.

failure.body:
The examination finds one ambiguity but misses the second, and when the agreement is made the second ambiguity resolves in the Lady's favour, which is entirely within the agreement's terms. What you receive is what was offered, but the conditions cost you more than a more careful reading would have required.

The thing lost is returned nonetheless. The lesson about precision is the additional cost.

entries.body:
The mist comes without warning — not the sea-fog of the channel coast but something older, something that moves with deliberate purpose. When it lifts, the isle is before you: green hills descending to still water, apple trees in blossom though the season does not warrant it, and on the shore three women in grey watching your approach without surprise.

Avalon is not what you expected. There is no grandeur to it, no walls, no banners. The power here is the power of things that have always been — the healing that precedes medicine, the knowledge that precedes learning. One of the three women steps forward. She knows your name, though you have not given it.

The women offer you a choice of how to receive what the isle can give. Both paths lead inward, but they lead to different inward places.

responses.label: You may seek the isle's healing for a wound or weariness that has long resisted mending.
You may ask the women to teach you something of the isle's older knowledge.

entries.body: The woman who leads you to the pool does not speak during the walk. The pool itself is neither large nor remarkable — dark water, stones at its edge worn smooth by long use. She gestures, and you understand: enter it, or place your hands in it, or simply stand at its edge and be still. The isle will know what you need.

resolutions.label: open yourself fully to the isle's healing and let it work without directing it

success.body:
The pool knows. Whatever has been wrong — in body, or in the quality of attention you bring to riding and thinking and keeping faith — it is less wrong now. You emerge from the water with the specific sense of something correctly set.

The woman who led you here touches your shoulder once, which is, on Avalon, the equivalent of high ceremony.

failure.body:
The pool receives you but does not entirely know what to do with you — the part of you that needs directing has followed you into the water and the isle cannot heal what insists on supervising its own healing.

Still, the water is cold and clean, and you emerge with something you did not name diminished.

entries.body: The eldest of the three women leads you to a place where the apple trees grow in a circle that is too precise to be accidental. She does not offer instruction in the conventional sense — she places certain objects in your hands, speaks certain words in the old language, and watches to see whether you can hold what she is showing you.

resolutions.label: receive the elder's teaching and work to comprehend its form

success.body:
You do not understand everything she shows you — no one could, on a first visit. But the core of it takes hold: a way of perceiving that is neither sight nor hearing but something that draws on both and adds a third thing for which there is no word in the common tongue.

You carry this back from Avalon. It will inform your judgements in ways you will not always be able to name.

failure.body:
The teaching passes through you without entirely staying — you catch pieces of it, the shape without the substance, the form without the force. The woman is not disappointed. She seems to have expected something between full comprehension and nothing.

You leave the circle with more than you came with, even if less than was offered.

entries.body:
The Grail has been seen at Camelot, and something in the world's spiritual architecture has shifted. Even Avalon is different — the mist that parts for you has a different quality to it, not welcoming but testing, as if the isle is asking whether you are here for the right reasons.

The three women are present as before, but they do not come forward. They watch you from the orchard's shadow. One of them speaks at last: the isle knows of the Quest, and those who come here in this age come either because they are broken by it and need healing, or because they seek counsel about what they are truly pursuing. Which are you?

The question is not hostile. It is honest, in the manner of all things on Avalon.

responses.label: You may honestly present your wounds from the Quest's road and seek restoration.
You may ask the isle for counsel about the Grail itself and what the Quest truly demands.

entries.body: The woman who tends to you in the healing garden does not distinguish between wounds of the body and wounds of the spirit — on Avalon, she says, they are the same wound showing different faces. She asks you to account for what the Quest's road has done to you. Not to justify it. Simply to account for it, with the honesty the isle requires.

resolutions.label: account honestly for how the Quest has shaped you — its costs and its gifts both

success.body:
The honesty is itself a kind of healing. The woman listens to all of it — the moments that should not have happened, the moments that almost did — and when you finish she touches the water of the pool with one hand and gestures for you to do the same.

Avalon accepts your account and gives in return a restoration that the Quest's road had made you doubt was still available to you.

failure.body:
The account you give is honest in parts but not wholly — there are things on the Quest's road that you are not yet ready to say aloud, even to Avalon, even to a woman who has heard more difficult accounts than yours.

The isle heals what you allow it to reach.

entries.body: The eldest woman listens to your question about the Grail with an expression that is not quite recognition and not quite surprise. The isle knows of the Grail — the two things are old enough to have known each other, and their relationship is complicated. She will tell you what Avalon knows of it. But Avalon's knowledge of the Grail is not the same as the Quest's knowledge, and you should understand the difference before she begins.

resolutions.label: listen carefully to Avalon's older and different understanding of what the Grail truly is

success.body:
What she tells you is not what the hermits on the Quest's road have been saying. The Grail, from Avalon's perspective, is not a test of spiritual worthiness but a question about what a knight does with power that exceeds understanding — a question the Fisher King has been sitting with for a very long time.

You carry this different understanding forward. It clarifies certain things that had been difficult on the road.

failure.body:
The difference between Avalon's understanding and the Quest's understanding proves harder to hold than you expected — the two frameworks fight for precedence and you leave with a confused version of both.

This is not nothing. Confusion of this particular kind is more useful than certainty of the wrong kind.

entries.body:
You find the path to Avalon — you are not certain you could explain how — in the grey autumn of the realm's last season. The isle receives you without the formality it once had. The apple trees are heavy with fruit that no one is gathering. The three women are all present but they do not speak of the future, only of what they are doing, which is preparing.

Avalon knows what is coming. It has always known. The end of Arthur's age was written into the isle's purpose from the beginning — it was made for exactly this, the receiving of what Britain cannot keep.

The eldest woman asks you why you have come, in this of all seasons. The question is not a challenge but a genuine one. Even Avalon does not presume to know everything about what a living knight needs.

responses.label: You may tell her you have come to make your peace with what is ending.
You may tell her you have come to ask whether anything of the age can be preserved.

entries.body:
The eldest woman accepts this without ceremony. Making peace with what is ending is, she says, one of the harder arts — harder than warfare, harder than healing. Most people never manage it in time. The fact that you have sought the isle for this purpose rather than for advantage or extension means something.

She shows you a place on the hillside where the view is clear across the water toward Britain. She does not say anything. The view speaks for itself, or does not, depending on what you bring to it.

resolutions.label: sit on the hillside and find what peace with the ending the view can give you

success.body:
The peace is not comfortable — that was never what peace with endings offers. What it offers is the ability to look at the ending directly, without the flinching that turns men desperate and causes them to do things they will not be able to account for.

You leave the hillside able to ride into what remains with your eyes open.

failure.body:
The peace is partial — you find some of it on the hillside, and some of it escapes you. The water is still and the view is clear and your heart is still louder than both.

This is further than most people get. Partial peace is still peace.

entries.body:
The woman considers the question for a long time before answering. What can be preserved of an age is always different from what those living in it want preserved. The stories are preserved. The impulse that made the fellowship is preserved, in the way that all impulses toward the good are preserved — changed, scattered, but not destroyed. What cannot be preserved is the specific form, the Round Table as an object in a specific hall.

She asks you whether you are asking about preservation in general, or about a specific thing you are trying to save.

resolutions.label: name the specific thing you are trying to preserve and ask Avalon's help with it

success.body:
The island's answer is practical rather than mystical: what can be preserved is what is committed to the keeping of people and places that will outlast the current ruin. Avalon offers, in return for the naming, a quality of clarity about what that thing is and who can keep it.

You leave with a specific purpose and the means to pursue it — not comfort, but something more useful.

failure.body:
The naming is harder than expected — you reach for the specific thing and find that it is already partially abstract in your mind, a feeling rather than a named object. Avalon can only work with what is named.

You leave with a better understanding of what you are trying to preserve, even if the means to preserve it did not come.

entries.body:
Benwick sits on a hill above a river valley where vineyards descend in neat rows to the water. The castle is older than it looks — the Frankish stonework overlays something Roman — and the court inside it has a formality that Camelot's court, for all its splendour, does not quite match. King Ban's household has been training knights since before Arthur's father was born.

You are received with correct courtesy — neither warmly nor coldly, but with the precise measure of honour that Benwick assigns to a knight of your standing. In this court, standing is everything. What you do here will either establish or confirm what Benwick already thinks of Britain's knights.

Two opportunities present themselves: the chance to test yourself against Benwick's martial training, or the chance to demonstrate the courtly qualities that the French court prizes above battlefield prowess.

responses.label: You may present yourself to Benwick's master-at-arms and request to train in the Frankish method.
You may seek an audience with King Ban himself and engage the court in counsel.

entries.body:
The master-at-arms is a man of fifty with the compact build of someone who has been training every day for thirty years. He looks at you with the professional detachment of a man who has assessed many knights and found most of them competent in the ways their training produced, and deficient in the ways their training did not address.

The Frankish method emphasises discipline of form over strength of arm. It is, he explains, not harder than the British method — it is differently hard. He will train you through its first principles and see what you make of them.

resolutions.label: undergo the master-at-arms's training and adapt to the Frankish method's disciplines

success.body:
The Frankish method corrects something in your riding that the British training never addressed — a tendency to lead with the right shoulder, almost imperceptible, that the master identifies in the first ten minutes and spends the afternoon correcting.

You ride differently leaving Benwick than you rode arriving. The master says nothing complimentary, which in Benwick's terms is high praise.

failure.body:
The Frankish method's demands sit awkwardly against your established habits — you spend the afternoon fighting the corrections as much as applying them, and the master knows it.

He says: return when the habits from before are less insistent. He means it as encouragement. In Benwick, it passes for encouragement.

entries.body:
King Ban is a tall man whose age shows in his beard rather than his bearing. He receives you in a council room rather than the great hall, which means he intends to actually speak with you rather than perform royal hospitality. A map of Britain is on the table between you. He has questions about Arthur's court, about the northern situation, about whether the fellowship is as strong as the stories that reach Benwick claim.

The questions are not hostile. They are the questions of a king who has committed his kingdom to an alliance and wants to know whether his assessment of that alliance was correct.

resolutions.label: answer Ban's questions with honest counsel and engage him as an equal in the discussion of strategy

success.body:
The conversation lasts longer than the audience was scheduled to last — Ban's chamberlain appears twice to signal that other business waits, and Ban waves him away twice.

What you and Ban produce together is better than either of you would have reached alone. The alliance between Benwick and Camelot has been strengthened not through ceremony but through the harder work of honest counsel.

failure.body:
The conversation goes well until the moment it doesn't — a question about internal politics at Camelot that you answer more defensively than honestly, and Ban notices. He closes the map and the audience becomes formal again.

You leave with the alliance intact and your standing in Benwick slightly below where it was when you arrived.

entries.body:
Benwick's great hall is quieter than it was. Lancelot du Lac was born here, trained here, left here to become the greatest knight in Christendom — and his absence has a specific quality in a kingdom that shaped him. The vineyards are still producing, the training grounds still active, but there is a held breath to the place, a sense of waiting for word that does not come.

King Ban's steward — Ban himself has aged considerably since the Quest's departure — receives you in the hall. He speaks of the Quest with the particular anxiety of a man who cannot go on it himself and cannot stop his best knights from having gone. He asks whether you have word of any of Benwick's knights who ride under the Grail's pull.

You have the option to offer what you know, or to address the more practical concerns that a kingdom without its best knights now faces.

responses.label: You may share whatever word you have of the knights who left Benwick for the Quest.
You may offer your service to Benwick's practical needs in its knights' absence.

entries.body:
The steward listens to your account with the focused attention of a man filing each piece of information against a map in his head. He has been collecting these fragments — a sighting here, a report from a passing traveller — and assembling them into something he is not certain he wants to see completed.

What you tell him matters. He knows that. He will not tell you how it matters, because Benwick does not do that. But it matters.
