resolutions.label: give a complete and honest account of all you know of Benwick's knights on the Quest

success.body:
Your account is complete, honest, and — this is the harder part — presented without softening the portions that the steward would prefer to have been different. He sits with it for a moment before thanking you with the formal courtesy Benwick reserves for serious debts.

Word of your honesty will travel back through Benwick's network of contacts. Your name is now associated with something more than knightly competence.

failure.body:
The account is mostly complete but you soften one portion — the part about a specific knight the steward clearly cares about — and the steward knows you have softened it. He accepts the softened version without comment.

Kindness of that kind is also a form of honour, even if incomplete.

entries.body:
The steward is relieved to be asked about practical matters — the spiritual weight of the Quest's absence has been sitting on his shoulders for months, and a question about what Benwick actually needs right now is a gift he was not expecting.

The most pressing need, he explains, is a border dispute with a neighbouring lord who has taken advantage of the knights' departure to press a claim that was adjudicated against him three years ago. It has not yet become armed conflict. It might.

resolutions.label: undertake to resolve the border dispute through negotiation before it becomes war

success.body:
The neighbouring lord proves more reasonable than the steward feared — he was pressing the claim because he expected no one to push back, not because he actually wanted war. Your intervention, which offers him a dignified way to withdraw without losing face, is accepted.

Benwick's border is quiet when you leave. The steward's gratitude is practical and specific, which is Benwick's highest register of thanks.

failure.body:
The neighbouring lord is less reasonable than expected, and the negotiation reaches an impasse that you cannot break. You leave Benwick with the dispute unresolved but with a clearer understanding of the specific terms that would resolve it.

The steward thanks you for the attempt. In Benwick, attempting things that matter is not nothing.

entries.body:
Benwick wears its grief openly now. Lancelot went to war on Britain's behalf — or on a principle's behalf, which in the end may be the same thing — and the wars followed him across the water. The vineyards have been tended by men too old for fighting. The training grounds are quieter than a place of this size should be.

The kingdom that shaped the greatest knight in the world is watching that greatness destroy it. This is the cost of a certain kind of excellence, and Benwick is paying it without complaint, because complaint was never the Frankish way.

You are received by the steward's successor, a young man who did not have the old steward's experience but has his dignity. He asks you directly: have you come to help, or to witness?

responses.label: You may answer that you have come to help and offer what a single knight can do.
You may answer honestly that you have come to understand what happened here before it is lost.

entries.body:
The young steward takes you at your word — in the Final Wars, taking people at their word is an act of courage, not naivety. What Benwick needs is specific: there are supply lines from the interior of France that have been disrupted by men who are not quite an army but are effective enough, and a knight who can travel fast and resolve conflicts without unnecessary killing is worth more right now than a hundred soldiers.

He shows you the map. He is direct about the odds.

resolutions.label: take the supply road alone and clear the disruption by force or persuasion as needed

success.body:
The disruption is human-sized — men who are hungry and frightened and have decided that taking from travelling merchants is a solution to their problem. You persuade two-thirds of them out of it and defeat the remainder efficiently enough that no one dies who does not have to.

The supply line is open. Benwick's steward clasps your hand when you return, which is, in the current age, more than ceremony.

failure.body:
The disruption proves more organised than the map suggested — what looked like a scattered opportunism turns out to have a commander behind it, someone who knows the road better than you do.

You return having made the situation no worse and having mapped its true dimensions, which is the beginning of a solution.

entries.body:
The steward nods at your honesty and leads you to a room where a scribe is at work on something that is clearly not administrative correspondence. The scribe, he explains, is recording everything — the training methods, the lineages of the knights who trained here, the specific things Lancelot learned in this hall and at what age.

The kingdom may not survive the wars. What can be preserved is the knowledge of what it was. You can help with that.

resolutions.label: work with the scribe to record what you yourself know of Lancelot and Benwick's tradition

success.body:
Your knowledge of Lancelot — what you observed, what he told you in unguarded moments, what can be inferred from watching him fight — adds dimensions to the record that the scribe's other sources did not have.

The record grows more complete. Whatever else is lost, this is saved.

failure.body:
Your account is offered in good faith but is thinner than you expected — you knew Lancelot in the way knights know each other, which is to say well and also not at all, and the scribe's questions reveal the gaps.

What you offer is honest, and honest things are worth recording even when incomplete.

entries.body:
The stones at Callanish have stood on this northern headland since before any chronicle was kept — tall, grey, aligned with something in the sky that the astronomers at Camelot are still debating. The wind here is constant. The grass between the stones is short and tough. There are no pilgrims, no keepers, no one. The stones do not require attendance.

Merlin has spoken of these stones in terms that he did not use for most things — with a quality of deference that was unusual from him, as though he were in the presence of an older teacher. You understand something of that now, standing among them. These stones are not impressed by Camelot.

The stones' power presents itself in two forms: as a place to receive knowledge that cannot be learned elsewhere, or as a place to ask a question about what lies ahead.

responses.label: You may stand within the stone circle and open yourself to what the old powers offer.
You may bring a specific question to the stones and attempt to read an answer in their alignment.

entries.body: Within the circle, the wind drops to almost nothing — not because the circle blocks it, which physically it could not, but because the stones seem to create a different relationship with the air around them. You stand at the centre and wait. The old powers do not rush. They also do not exactly speak. What they do is something between weather and language that you will need time to understand.

resolutions.label: stand at the stones' centre and receive whatever the old powers choose to give

success.body:
The knowledge that comes is not propositional — not a list of facts or a vision of specific events. It is more like a recalibration of the senses toward the older rhythms of the land: the way a storm reads differently to someone who knows what the stones know, the way a road's character can be felt in the soil.

You leave Callanish reading the land differently than you did when you arrived.

failure.body:
The stones give you the beginning of something and then fall silent — perhaps you moved, perhaps your attention was divided, perhaps the old powers decided you were not yet ready for the full version.

You carry a fragment of the recalibration. It is enough to know the fragment is there.

entries.body:
The stones are aligned with the midsummer sunrise and the midwinter moon and, possibly, other things that the Callanish builders intended and the ages since have obscured. The alignment is a form of attention — the stones, built by someone, are looking at something. If you are skilled enough to read what they are looking at, you can ask a question along the same line of sight.

The question, the hermits who know the old ways have said, must be sincere. The stones do not answer performed questions.

resolutions.label: bring a sincere question to the stones and read the answer in their alignment

success.body:
The answer comes in the form of a quality of certainty rather than specific information — a knowing of the kind that precedes the words for it. Your question, whatever it was, receives a response that you will be able to understand more fully in time.

You leave the stones with the answer not entirely unpacked but present.

failure.body:
The question was sincere but the skill to read the alignment was not equal to it — the stones are looking at something you cannot yet follow.

You leave Callanish knowing the limit of your current reading and understanding, for the first time precisely, what would be needed to exceed it.

entries.body:
The stones are the same as they have always been. But the air around them is not. The Grail's appearance at Camelot sent a pulse through the spiritual architecture of Britain, and the old places that were already resonant have responded to it. Callanish is more active than you have ever felt it — the wind speaks more insistently, the light through the standing stones falls at slightly different angles than the season explains.

You are not alone at the stones for once — a hermit has made camp at their outer edge, a young man who came north to pray and has not been able to leave because, he says, the stones will not release him until he has understood what they are showing him. He looks neither distressed nor particularly holy. He looks like someone doing hard intellectual work.

He invites you to share what the stones' current awakening is showing each of you.

responses.label: You may enter the circle and experience the heightened resonance directly.
You may confer with the hermit about what the old powers and the Grail's power are saying to each other.

entries.body: The circle's resonance is different in this age — the frequency that the Quest has awakened in the land's spiritual body is in conversation with the older frequency the stones have always produced. Standing within the circle during the Grail's age, you feel them as two voices in counterpoint, neither drowning the other, both saying something that separately they could not.

resolutions.label: hold the two frequencies together without forcing a resolution between them

success.body:
The two voices do not merge. They do not need to merge. What you achieve is the capacity to hear both at once without either cancelling the other — a quality of perception that the hermit, watching from the edge, recognises and marks in the look he gives you when you emerge.

The Grail's meaning and the land's meaning are not the same thing. Knowing that is worth knowing.

failure.body:
The two frequencies are harder to hold simultaneously than you expected — you end up hearing each in isolation, alternating, which is informative but not the full achievement.

The hermit says: that is how most people manage it. His tone suggests this is not a reprimand.

entries.body:
The hermit's name is Brother Coel — not a common name, and he does not offer its provenance. He has been sitting with the stones' current speech for six weeks and has assembled a working theory: the Grail's appearance awakened something in the old places because both the Grail and the old places are pointing at the same underlying reality, from different directions.

He wants to know whether your experience of the Quest's road confirms or contradicts this. He is asking in good faith. He is not attached to being right.

resolutions.label: share your experience of the Quest's road honestly and reason with the hermit toward his theory's truth

success.body:
The conversation produces a refinement of Brother Coel's theory that he marks as significant — your experience of the road's spiritual tests adds the experiential dimension that his contemplative practice was missing.

He gives you the theory's refined version before you leave. It is useful in a way that is difficult to name but will make itself felt in subsequent decisions.

failure.body:
Your account of the Quest's road is honest but Coel's theory requires more theological precision than you can supply — you are a knight who has experienced the road, not a scholar who can analyse it.

Coel thanks you anyway. Experience without analysis is still data, he says, and data is never nothing.

entries.body:
The stones at Callanish are unchanged. This is the point of them — kingdoms rise and break, and the stones register neither as meaningful events. They were here before Camelot. They will be here after the last horn has been blown and the last oath dissolved.

You have come here because you needed something that outlasts kingdoms. The stones do not offer comfort, which is a form of respect — they do not pretend that what is ending is not ending, or that endurance is the same as consolation. They offer only what they have always offered: the unambiguous presence of something older and more patient than human suffering.

The wind is cold. The stones are grey. The sky above them is vast and does not care about the Round Table. You understand, standing there, why you came.

responses.label: You may ask the stones what of the Arthurian age will endure beyond its breaking.
You may bring your grief to the stones without a question and let them receive it.

entries.body:
The stones do not answer in words. You did not expect them to. What they offer is a form of attention that, if you can sustain it long enough, becomes a kind of answer — not the specific list you might wish for, but a sense of the shape of what persists. Not who. Not where. The kind of thing, the quality.

You will need to be willing to accept that the answer is not more specific than that.

resolutions.label: sustain the attention the stones require long enough to receive what they can show

success.body:
The answer, when it comes, is not comfortable but is clear: what persists is not the institutions but the acts that the institutions made possible. The Round Table will not endure. The impulse to make a Round Table — to create structures of shared honour — endures in every person the age touched.

You carry this back into the world with you. It is enough.

failure.body:
The cold and the wind defeat you before the stones are done — you break the attention too early, and the sense of the answer disperses without completing.

But the beginning of it is there, and the beginning is not nothing: something endures. The specifics you will have to work out on your own.

entries.body:
The stones receive grief the way they receive everything else — without comment. Their silence is not indifference. It is the silence of something that has received a great deal of human grief over a long time and has learned not to diminish it with consolation.

Stand among them. Let the cold in. The stones know what you are doing and they have made space for it.

resolutions.label: stand in the grief without managing it and let the stones' patience hold it with you

success.body:
The grief does not go away. But after a long time standing in the cold wind among the grey stones, it is grief rather than the thing that was blocking you from grief — it has become something you can carry rather than something you are submerged in.

The stones did not remove anything. They held the space while you completed the work.

failure.body:
The cold is too cold and the grief is too present and you are not able to hold both simultaneously without breaking the stillness — you leave before the stones have done their work.

But you came. Standing among ancient stones in the cold with your grief is not nothing, even incomplete.

entries.body:
The towers of Camelot are white against a sky that deserves them. The road to the gate is busy — riders returning from patrols, merchants presenting goods for the court's approval, heralds from distant lords whose names you are still learning. The Round Table is new enough that people still feel the thrill of proximity to it, and the thrill translates into a kind of aliveness that fills the castle's corridors with energy.

King Arthur holds court in the great hall. Merlin stands at the king's left hand, which is where he stands when something is being decided. The Round Table itself is in the hall beneath the great windows, every seat occupied, every knight present. This is Camelot as it was meant to be.

Two possibilities present themselves, each drawing on different strengths the court requires.

responses.label: You may approach the Round Table and offer your deeds for the fellowship's account.
You may seek audience with Merlin and engage his counsel on a matter that requires more than arms.

entries.body:
The herald calls your name. The hall falls to a attentive silence — not hostile, not expectant, simply the silence of people who take their attention seriously. The king is watching you with the quality of focus that made Camelot what it is: the specific interest of a man who has made it his business to know what each of his knights is and what they are becoming.

You will speak your deeds. This is not performance. This is the Round Table's mechanism for accountability — what you have done is stated in the presence of witnesses, and the fellowship receives it and weighs it.

resolutions.label: state your deeds before the Round Table with honesty and appropriate measure

success.body:
The account is received well — not because it is impressive, but because it is honest. The king asks one question, which is always a sign that he has listened carefully, and your answer to it produces a nod that means he has seen the thing you were really doing, beneath the surface of the deeds.

Your place at the fellowship is confirmed in the eyes of those who matter here.

failure.body:
The account veers toward the impressive rather than the honest at one moment — an instinct that is understandable in the great hall's atmosphere — and Merlin's eyes narrow slightly, which is worse than an accusation.

The fellowship receives the account. What it thinks of the accounting quality is unspoken but present in the room.

entries.body:
Merlin is older than he looks and younger than he acts, and both of these are by design. He receives you in the library tower — a room full of books that are not arranged by any logic you can discern — and he listens to your matter with the attention of a man who is simultaneously thinking about something else, which is how Merlin always listens.

The matter you bring is the one that has been at the edge of your thinking for longer than you have been willing to admit. Merlin already knows this. He generally does.

resolutions.label: present your matter clearly and engage Merlin's counsel without hedging the difficult parts

success.body:
Merlin's response is oblique, which is always the sign that he is taking the matter seriously — when he gives direct answers, he has already decided the matter is beneath his full attention.

The oblique response, when you have had time to work through it, is a complete answer. You leave the library tower with what you came for and a number of additional questions that are actually gifts, because the questions are the right ones.

failure.body:
You hedge the difficult parts, which means Merlin receives a partial version of the matter and gives a partial response. He is not fooled. He is simply declining to extract what you are not ready to offer.

He says: come back when you are ready to ask the whole question. He will wait.

entries.body:
Camelot is half itself. The white towers are the same, but the hall they top is too large for the court that remains in it, and the Round Table — still magnificent, still polished — has too many empty chairs. Arthur keeps court with the knights who stayed, and keeps it with the full ceremony as though absence is not a comment on the fellowship.

The king is older than he was. Not in body — Arthur is still the warrior who drew the sword — but in a quality of the eyes that was not there before the Quest's departure. He knows what the going away cost. He knew before they left. He sits with the knowledge of it every day in this half-empty hall, and he keeps court anyway.

You have come in this difficult season. Two things are needed here.

responses.label: You may take your place at the Round Table and serve the court that remains.
You may seek private audience with the king and offer what a knight can offer a king who is carrying too much alone.

entries.body:
The steward of the Round Table shows you to your seat with the same ceremony as if every other seat were occupied. The business of court continues: land disputes from the western provinces, a formal complaint from a lord whose rights have been infringed, the question of a young man who wishes to be made a knight and must be assessed by the knights present.

The work of court does not stop because a hundred and fifty knights are riding after a vision. It reduces, perhaps, to what is essential.

resolutions.label: take up the court's business fully and serve in the place where you are needed

success.body:
The business is handled. The land disputes are adjudicated fairly; the lord's complaint is received and addressed without causing a larger rupture; the young man who wants to be a knight receives a serious assessment rather than a ceremony.

This is not glory. This is what makes Camelot continue being Camelot when glory has gone elsewhere.

failure.body:
The court's business proves more complex than the reduced circumstances suggested — the land dispute turns on an obscure point of tenure law, and your ruling is challenged by someone who knows the law more precisely than you do.

The steward resolves it after your ruling, quietly, which saves the court's dignity and your own. The work was attempted. That counts for something in the half-empty hall.

entries.body:
The king receives you in his private chamber after the court day is done. This is not an audience — there is no ceremony and no herald. He is sitting by the window that faces west, which is the direction the knights went when they departed. He does not ask you why you have come. He waits for you to begin.

What a knight can offer a king in this position is not advice — Arthur has better counsellors for that — but a certain quality of honest presence. To be with the king as a person rather than as a symbol. He rarely has this.

resolutions.label: remain with the king in honest presence and speak only when there is something real to say

success.body:
What passes between you is mostly silence with a few words in it. The king speaks of Guinevere and stops. He speaks of Lancelot and stops. He speaks of the morning the Quest's company rode out, and does not stop — he describes the sight of them going, a hundred and fifty knights in the early light, and how the sight was exactly the sight of everything he had made being true, and also the sight of losing it.

You say almost nothing, and that is exactly what was needed.

failure.body:
The silence becomes too heavy and you fill it with words that are well-meant but wrong — practical suggestions, assessments of the Quest's likely duration, things that would be reasonable in a council but are wrong in a private chamber with a king looking west.

He thanks you and the audience becomes formal again. You did not hurt him. But you did not help him as much as he needed.

entries.body:
Camelot's gates are open but the gate-guard has the look of men who have been watching the road for trouble that has not yet arrived. Mordred's claim to the regency is a fact in the official correspondence and a rumour in the castle corridors. Some lords have sent their oaths to Mordred. Some have not. The court that remains is reduced, careful, and very aware of what the wrong step would cost.

You ride in and the gate-guard knows your name. That is either reassuring or dangerous, depending on who put it there.

The situation in Camelot requires action, and you are one knight. What you can do is specific and limited and potentially decisive.

responses.label: You may seek out those in the court who have held faith with the king and strengthen their resolve.
You may attempt to learn what Mordred's people are actually planning before it is too late to act.

entries.body:
The faithful remnant of Arthur's court are not hard to find — they are the ones who look the most tired, because holding faith in a besieged position is exhausting work. You know some of them from better days. The ones you do not know recognise the quality of resolve you have come with, and resolve, in Camelot as it now is, is a currency more valuable than gold.

What they need is not new information or tactical advice. They need someone who has chosen the same thing they have chosen to stand in the room with them.

resolutions.label: stand with the faithful remnant and speak plainly about why the king's cause is still worth holding

success.body:
The words you find are not eloquent — this is not the time for eloquence — but they are true, and truth in a court full of careful speech is a physical presence in a room.

The remnant is steadied. Not saved — steadied. It is what a single knight can do, and in the Final Wars, it is enough to matter.

failure.body:
The words come out as encouragement rather than truth — you lift the spirits of the room without quite addressing what is actually at stake, and the remnant is lifted and then, as you leave, slowly settles back to where it was.

You were there. That was not nothing, even if the words did not find their mark.

entries.body:
Mordred's people in Camelot are not marked — they wear the same livery as everyone else, attend the same halls, speak with the same deference. But people whose allegiance has shifted have a quality to how they move through a room that, if you know what to look for, can be read.

You are trying to learn something specific: not who has shifted — that is too many — but what the plan is and when it is to be executed. That is the harder problem.

resolutions.label: move through the castle's corridors and gather what can be gathered through observation and careful questioning

success.body:
The plan is not entirely what you expected. The timing is fixed to Arthur's movements, not to any external event. This means there is a window — a specific and limited window — that the faithful can use if they know about it.

You know about it. You go immediately to share it with the people who can use it.

failure.body:
You gather pieces but not enough of them — the plan's shape stays unclear, its timing undiscoverable. What you do learn is that the situation is more advanced than the faithful know, which is information, even if it is not the information you sought.

You share what you have. Incomplete information is still information.

entries.body:
The road to Corbenic does not appear on maps. It appears when it appears, and those who have not yet been sent for do not find it regardless of how carefully they seek it. That you are standing before the castle gates means the castle was ready for you — or ready to test whether you were ready for it.

The Fisher King's castle is older than any other structure you have seen. The stone is a colour that changes with the light, and the silence around it has weight. The land in the castle's immediate vicinity is wrong in a way that is difficult to name — the grass grows but incorrectly, the river nearby runs but too quietly, the trees are present but static in a wind that should move them.

The Fisher King is wounded. The wound does not heal. The question that heals it has not yet been asked. A servant at the gate offers you entry, with the specific gravity of a man who knows what hangs on this.

responses.label: You may enter the castle and present yourself to the Fisher King directly.
You may sit with the wounded land first and try to understand the nature of the wound before entering.

entries.body:
The Fisher King lies on a litter in his great hall — not incapacitated, not dying, simply unable to leave the castle or be whole within it. The Grail and the bleeding Lance are in the hall with him. The Grail produces a light that has no source. The Lance bleeds without ceasing onto a cloth that never becomes fully soaked.

He looks at you with eyes that have been waiting for something for a very long time. You are perhaps not the thing he is waiting for. But you are here, and that is not nothing.

resolutions.label: speak with the Fisher King honestly about what you have seen and sought on the roads of Britain

success.body:
The Fisher King listens to your account with an attention that is unlike anything you have received in any court. He asks a few questions — specific ones, about particular moments — and your answers seem to satisfy something in him, though not the central thing.

You have not asked the question that heals. But you have visited Corbenic with your full honesty, and that is not without its own meaning here.

failure.body:
In the Fisher King's presence, with the Grail's light filling the room, you find that the account you have to give is smaller than you imagined it. The roads of Britain have not, in this first age, produced the kind of material that Corbenic was built to receive.

He receives you anyway, with the patience of someone who has been waiting a very long time and does not expect today to be the end of the waiting.

entries.body:
The waste land around Corbenic is not dramatic — it is not burned or barren or obviously ruined. It is more subtle than that: a quality of diminishment, a sense that the land is present but not fully alive. The grass grows but does not grow with vigour. The river runs but does not run with the energy that water should have. The trees are there but not quite there.

To understand this, you will need to look at it carefully and without flinching.

resolutions.label: move through the waste land with careful attention and read the nature of the wound

success.body:
The wound in the land is the Fisher King's wound made external — the land suffers because the king suffers, and the land's suffering has the same shape as his, which is: something is withheld that should not be withheld, and the withholding is not the land's fault.

You enter Corbenic with this understanding. The Fisher King sees that you have it, and something in his expression shifts.

failure.body:
The waste land resists your reading — it is neither obviously ruined nor obviously whole, and the middle state defeats the diagnostic tools you have available.

You enter Corbenic without the understanding you sought. The Fisher King receives you without the shift in expression that understanding would have produced. He has waited before.

entries.body:
The road to Corbenic appears before you in the grey morning, where no road was the evening before. The Quest's undertow has brought you here — this is the destination, the thing behind the longing that has been shaping every turn of the road since Camelot.

The Fisher King's hall is lit by the Grail's own light, which is the colour of nothing you can name. The Lance bleeds without ceasing on its cloth. The Fisher King looks at you as he has looked at every knight who has arrived here — with exhausted hope, the hope of a man who has been waiting for the right person to ask the right question for a very long time.

You know, from the hermits and the road and the long nights of the Quest, what the question is. The question is: whom does the Grail serve? The difficulty is not knowing the question. The difficulty is whether you are ready to mean it.

responses.label: You may ask the Fisher King the question that the Grail demands, with your whole heart.
You may first make your confession of what the Quest has cost you before asking.

entries.body:
The Fisher King's hall is very still. The Grail's light pulses once, slowly, as though it is drawing a breath. He is looking at you with a quality of attention that has been refined by decades of waiting into something indistinguishable from hope.

The question must be asked now. Not rehearsed, not approached, not prepared further. Asked.

resolutions.label: ask the question — whom does the Grail serve? — with complete sincerity and without condition

success.body:
The question leaves you with a quality of completeness — as though every road of the Quest led to this moment and the moment is exactly the right size for all of them.

The Fisher King's face changes. The wound closes — not gradually, not partially, but entirely, in the moment the question is received in its full sincerity. The waste land outside begins, immediately, to be different. The Grail's light fills the hall.

Whom does it serve? Everyone. Always. This is the answer, and you asked it truly enough to receive it.

failure.body:
The question is asked, and it is sincere — but not entirely, not all the way through. Some part of you held something back, some corner of the heart that was still managing rather than offering, and the Grail receives a question that is almost whole.

The Fisher King's face changes, slightly. Not the full healing. But something has moved. He places his hand on your arm and says: you have been here. That is not nothing.

entries.body:
The eldest of the hermits who served as guide told you this: arrive at Corbenic with a clean account. Not clean in the sense of innocent — the Quest's road has made innocence impossible — but clean in the sense of fully acknowledged, everything named and owned.

You kneel in the Fisher King's hall and make the confession that the road has generated. The Grail's light is present for it. The Lance bleeds. The Fisher King listens with his full attention.

resolutions.label: make a full and honest confession before the Grail and then ask the question from that cleared place

success.body:
The confession takes time — longer than you expected, because the road produced more than you had counted. When it is done, the hall is quieter than it was before, and the question rises from that quietness naturally, without effort: whom does the Grail serve?

The Fisher King's healing is complete. The question, asked from a cleared place, reaches everything it was intended to reach.

failure.body:
The confession is made but not completed — there is one thing that stops short of full acknowledgment, a thing the road produced that you are not yet ready to name in the Grail's light.

The Fisher King waits. He has waited longer for less. The question you ask from the incomplete confession is received — heard — but the healing that full sincerity would have produced does not come entirely.

He says: return. The question is the right one. The door stays open.

entries.body:
The road to Corbenic appears one last time in the broken age — as though the castle knows you have come, and has decided to receive you despite everything.

The change inside is stark. The Grail's light is dimmer than the stories described it — or perhaps the stories described it in the age when Perceval or Galahad asked the question and it blazed, and what remains now is the ordinary radiance of after. The Fisher King is healed — the Quest's culmination accomplished that — but the healing of a king does not heal everything that the wound made waste.

He receives you in the hall without ceremony. He is an old man now, fully present in a way the wound never allowed him to be, and his full presence has a quality that is both wonderful and sad.

responses.label: You may speak with the healed Fisher King about what remains after the Grail's purpose is fulfilled.
You may walk the waste land and assess what the healing has and has not restored.

entries.body:
The Fisher King speaks of the healing the way a man speaks of a great illness survived — with gratitude, and with the specific sorrow of understanding, now that it is over, how long it went on.

He asks about the world outside. He has been here, inside the castle's logic, for so long that the Final Wars are abstract to him — he knows Arthur and Mordred in the way he knows all things, but at a distance. He wants your account of what the age has become.

resolutions.label: give the Fisher King a full and honest account of Britain in the Final Wars

success.body:
The account you give is complete — the glory that was, the fracture that came, the specific costs of Mordred's ambition and Lancelot's grief. The Fisher King listens with the attention of a man who has been waiting to understand the world outside for a very long time.

He offers in return a perspective on the Grail's meaning that no living knight has heard from this source. What the Grail was for. What it accomplished and what it could not. You carry this back into the Final Wars with you.

failure.body:
The account is honest but incomplete — the Final Wars are difficult to describe to someone who has not been in them, and what you produce is approximate rather than adequate.

The Fisher King receives it with gratitude anyway. Any account of the outside world is more than he had.

entries.body:
The waste land around Corbenic has partially recovered — the grass grows with more vigour, the river runs with proper energy, the trees have returned to themselves. But partial is the word. There are places where the waste has set in too deep for the Fisher King's healing alone to reach. These places need tending that is different from the king's healing — more specific, more local, more patient.

You can provide some of that. It is slow work, and you will not finish it today.

resolutions.label: work through the waste land and apply what skill and patience you have to the recovery

success.body:
The work is genuinely useful — you can feel the difference between land that has been tended and land that has been left, and the tended places respond to the attention with the slow gratitude of things that have been waiting for it.

You do not restore the waste land. You restore a part of it, and the part is specific and real and will continue recovering after you leave.

failure.body:
The waste land's recovery is further from completion than your skills can reach — the damage is too old and too deep for what you can offer in the time you have.

You work anyway, for the time you have. The land receives the attention even when the attention is not equal to the wound.

entries.body:
Morgan le Fay's castle does not appear the same way twice. The approach is different each time — sometimes through forest, sometimes along a cliff road, sometimes across a ford that was not there before — and the castle at the end of the approach is always exactly where it should be and never exactly where you expected.

She receives you in a hall that is decorated with the results of previous knights' visits: a sword here, a broken shield there, a tapestry depicting a scene you are almost certain you have not yet lived through but which looks uncomfortably familiar. Morgan herself is not what the rumours described. She is more specific, more amused, and more attentive.

She has a test for you. She always has a test. What she tests depends on what she has already determined you need to be tested on.

responses.label: You may accept whatever test she has prepared without asking what it is first.
You may negotiate the terms of the test — ask what she is testing for and why.

entries.body:
Morgan's test, which she does not describe in advance, is in three parts. The first part is a question about something you are ashamed of. The second part is a demonstration of the thing you are most certain of in yourself. The third part is a moment of choice between two things, neither of which is obviously wrong.

She observes all three with the attention of someone conducting a study. She takes notes. This is the most unsettling part.

resolutions.label: complete all three parts of the test with complete honesty and without strategic calculation

success.body:
Morgan reviews her notes before speaking. What she tells you about yourself is the kind of thing you could not have told yourself — not because it is unknown to you, but because you have been looking at it from the wrong angle.

The test's result is a corrected perception of your own character. Morgan is the only person in Britain who can provide this. You are grateful, in the complicated way that one is grateful for Morgan's gifts.

failure.body:
The strategic calculation enters in the second part — you present the thing you are most certain of in a form that is shaped by what you want Morgan to conclude rather than what is actually there.

She notes this in her notes. She tells you: the test reveals what you could not hide, as well as what you tried to show. Both things are informative.

entries.body:
Morgan receives the question — what is she testing for and why — with an expression that is close to approval. Most knights, she says, accept the test without asking. The asking is itself a kind of answer to one of the test's questions.

She tells you: she tests for the distance between what a knight believes about himself and what is actually there. The why is that the distance, if large enough, will eventually cause catastrophic failure at a moment that cannot be anticipated. She tests to give you the chance to close the distance before that moment arrives.

The test is the same regardless of whether you knew what it was. She begins.

resolutions.label: engage the test with the understanding of its purpose and apply that understanding throughout

success.body:
Knowing the test's purpose changes your engagement with it — not because you perform better, but because the understanding allows a quality of honest attention that the test is designed to produce but rarely receives.

Morgan's assessment at the end is more nuanced than her standard report. She says: you understood what was being measured and were honest with the measurement. That is not common.

The distance between belief and reality, in your case, is smaller than average. She tells you this without warmth but without withholding.

failure.body:
The understanding of the test's purpose proves harder to apply than to hold theoretically — knowing what Morgan is measuring does not, in the event, make it easier to be fully honest about the measurement.

She notes this in her notes. The test revealed, she says, the gap between knowing what honesty requires and being able to practise it under observation. This too is a useful finding.

entries.body:
Morgan le Fay's castle in the age of the Grail has a different quality — the enchantments that she has woven through its fabric are responsive to the Quest's undertow, and the hall's decorations include, since the Grail's appearance at Camelot, new additions that she has not explained to her guests.

She receives you with the same precise attention as always, but the question she asks before beginning the test is different from the one she usually asks. She wants to know: what are you actually seeking? Not the Grail — she says the Grail's name dismissively, as though the Grail is the surface problem — but the thing the Grail is standing in for. The thing beneath the Grail's longing.

The question is the test. How you answer it will determine what she tests you on.

responses.label: You may answer her question honestly, even if the honest answer is surprising to you.
You may turn the question back on her and ask what she observes you to be seeking.

entries.body:
Answering Morgan's question honestly means sitting with it first — not rushing to the first answer that presents itself, which will be the performed answer, but finding the thing that is actually there.

She waits with the patience of someone who is in no hurry. Her castle is outside the ordinary flow of the Quest's urgency. There is time here to find the honest answer.

resolutions.label: take the time to find and speak the honest answer to what you are truly seeking beneath the Grail's pull

success.body:
The honest answer, when you find it, is not what you expected it to be. It is smaller and more specific than the Grail and more immediately yours — a thing you lost, or failed at, or could not accept about yourself, and have been travelling under the Grail's flag in order to recover or resolve.

Morgan listens to it with a quality of stillness that means she already knew and is now respecting that you found it yourself. Her test, when it comes, is designed precisely around this specific thing.

failure.body:
The honest answer escapes you — you find an answer that feels honest but is still one layer above the actual thing, and Morgan's expression when you speak it tells you this without words.

She does not correct you. She designs the test around the answer you gave, which will eventually, through the test's mechanism, reveal the layer below.

entries.body:
Morgan receives the reversal with what is very nearly delight — not because she is caught off-guard by it, but because so few knights think to try it. She sits back and gives the question her genuine attention.

Her answer is precise: she observes you to be seeking proof that what you believe about yourself is true. Not the Grail as a spiritual achievement — you could find that on the road. The Grail as a mirror that will show you the reflection that your own doubts have been obscuring.

She asks: was she right?

resolutions.label: respond honestly to Morgan's assessment and engage the test it implies about self-knowledge

success.body:
Your honest response confirms her assessment more completely than a simple yes — you add dimensions that her observation caught and others that it missed, and the conversation that results is more reciprocal than Morgan's tests usually are.

She says at the end: I test knights in this castle, but I do not often converse with them. You have been more useful to my understanding than I expected.

This is, from Morgan le Fay, extraordinary.

failure.body:
The honest response proves harder than the reversal that preceded it — you reached for honesty and found defensiveness instead, and Morgan's assessment was accurate enough that receiving it fully was more than you managed today.

She notes this with patience. The test continues regardless of whether you are ready for it.

entries.body:
Morgan le Fay's castle in the final age has a different function. The tests are still present — she cannot stop being what she is — but the enchantments on the walls have been rearranged, and there is a room in the south tower that was not there before, being prepared for something.

She receives you with less ceremony than she has shown in other ages. The amused quality is diminished. She says, without preamble: Arthur is coming. Not yet, but soon. She has been preparing the reception for him since before the wound at Camlann was dealt — she knew, she says, that it was coming.

She does not ask you to help her prepare. But she is not not-asking, either.

responses.label: You may offer to help Morgan prepare for Arthur's arrival at the isle's end.
You may ask Morgan what she knows of what is coming and whether it can still be changed.

entries.body:
What Morgan needs is specific. The room in the south tower requires certain objects brought from certain places — not magical objects, but ordinary ones that carry specific significance. One is from Camelot. One is from the battlefield where the wound will be dealt. One is from a place in Arthur's personal history that she names and that you recognise.

She asks for these things without explaining their function. If you want to know the function, you will have to ask.

resolutions.label: undertake to gather the objects Morgan requires without asking for justification

success.body:
The gathering takes longer than a single journey — the objects are in places that require travel, and some of the places are dangerous in the Final Wars. But you complete the task and return to Morgan's castle with all three.

She receives them with care rather than ceremony. She places each in the south tower room in a specific arrangement. She says, after: what you have done here will be known, though not spoken of. That is Morgan's version of permanent gratitude.

failure.body:
Two of the three objects are gathered. The third — from the battlefield, which you could not yet reach — remains absent.

Morgan accepts two of three without complaint. She has methods for the third that do not require your intervention. You have done what you could in the time the Final Wars allow.

entries.body:
Morgan looks at you for a long moment before answering. What she knows of what is coming is very specific — she has been watching the threads of the age since before Arthur drew the sword, and the thread-ends are clear to her in this final season.

Can it be changed? She pauses again. Changed how much, she asks. What would the change preserve, and what would it cost to preserve it? She has thought about this. She has not been able to decide whether the cost is one that any living person has the right to pay on Arthur's behalf.

resolutions.label: engage Morgan's question honestly — what would you preserve, and what cost would you accept?

success.body:
The conversation becomes a genuine one — Morgan is not certain, and your engagement with the question at its full depth provides her with a perspective she has not had.

What she tells you in return is what she knows of the ending's shape and what is actually inevitable versus what is chosen. The distinction matters. Some things that look fixed are not fixed. She names them.

You leave Morgan's castle with specific knowledge of where a knight's action can still make a difference. You go immediately to act on it.

failure.body:
The question proves beyond what you can answer at its full depth — the costs and the things to preserve are too entangled in this final age for any clear accounting.

Morgan nods. She expected something like this. She gives you the part of what she knows that does not require the full accounting: the one thing in the coming weeks that a knight's action can change, which is small but real.

entries.body:
The sea-wind reaches you before Orkney does — salt and cold, carrying the cry of gannets and the smell of stone beaches. The island rises from the water like a shield cast down by a giant, treeless and proud, and the hall of Lot's sons stands on the headland above the bay as though daring the ocean to take it.

Gawain's brothers are here — Gareth at the horse-lines, Gaheris on the rampart watching you arrive — but the welcome is not a simple thing. Orkney gives its respect to those who earn it, and the Orkney code is not Camelot's. What you do here will be seen and weighed.

responses.label: You may challenge one of Lot's sons in the yard and prove yourself by the Orkney measure.
You may seek an audience with the lord of the hall and speak to the bond between Orkney and Camelot.

entries.body: Gareth steps forward before his brothers can — he is the youngest of them, and gentle by nature, but he was trained by Lancelot and there is nothing soft in his swordwork. The yard is cobblestone, swept clean, and Agravaine watches from the wall with his arms folded and his opinion already formed.

resolutions.label: meet Gareth in the yard and fight with skill and honour both

success.body:
The bout is close — Gareth presses hard and you press back, and there is a moment when it could go either way. It goes your way. You yield the final pass with grace, and Gareth grins in a manner that makes the whole cold yard briefly warmer.

Agravaine uncrosses his arms. In Orkney, that is applause.

failure.body:
Gareth is better than you expected, or you are less sharp than usual — either way the bout is a loss, though a clean one. He offers his hand after, which is the Orkney way, and the loss is seen as the prelude to the rematch, not the end of things.

You leave the yard with the shape of his technique still in your hands.

entries.body: The lord of the hall is not Lot — Lot is dead, years past, slain in the wars that preceded Arthur's peace — but his absence is present in every hanging and every stone. The man who leads here now answers to Camelot because the sons made their oaths, but Orkney's pride is a thing that does not bend easily, and the hall remembers what it was before the fellowship.

resolutions.label: speak to the compact between Orkney and the Round Table with care and precision

success.body:
You find the words that honour both things — Arthur's court and the island's own dignity — and the hall receives them well. A feast is ordered. The Orkney men are proud, yes, but they are not unreasonable; they needed only to be addressed as what they are.

Before you leave, a gift is pressed into your hand: a clasp of northern silver, the Orkney dragon worked small and precise.

failure.body:
The phrasing goes wrong — you make Arthur's name too large, or Orkney's too small, and the room cools. Nothing is broken, but the warmth does not come. The lord of the hall nods and the meal is provided and nothing more is offered.

You have learned the measure of the thing you did not find.

entries.body:
The Orkney sons have ridden for the Grail — all save Agravaine, who looked at the vow and looked at his own nature and did not make it. The hall is emptier without Gawain's voice and Gareth's warmth, and the people who remain on the island are waiting in a particular way: they do not know what the Quest will return to them, or whether it will return them at all.

Agravaine is lord here now, in practice if not in name. He walks the rampart above the sea with the face of a man deciding something important, and it is not yet decided.

responses.label: You may speak with Agravaine about what he withholds from the Quest and what he guards instead.
You may walk among the Orkney people and learn what the island holds that the Quest cannot take from it.

entries.body: Agravaine does not want to be found in a good light — he has arranged his face and his manner against it, and he will resist your sympathy on principle. But there is something genuine here, beneath the pride: he is the son who stayed, and staying is its own kind of choice, and he has not been asked to account for it before.

resolutions.label: draw out Agravaine's true account of why he remained — with patience and without judgment

success.body:
He speaks, finally, in the oblique way of a man who has not practised saying true things. He stayed because someone had to. The Quest promises the Grail, but the Grail does not feed the island, does not keep the sea-roads safe, does not send rain in the right season. Someone had to keep Orkney.

You listen to all of it. When you leave, something in his face has shifted — not quite open, but less defended.

failure.body:
He shuts before you can reach anything true — your question goes wrong somewhere, or his suspicion rises faster than your patience. He gives you the correct words and the correct courtesies and nothing real.

But you have learned the shape of what he will not say, and that has its own value.

entries.body: The island people — the fishers, the farmers on the thin inland soil, the women who mend the nets and watch the weather — have their own relationship with what happens in the south. The Grail Quest is a thing lords' sons do. The sea is a thing that simply is. You sit with them at their work and you listen.

resolutions.label: learn what the island knows that no chronicle would think to record

success.body:
What they know is considerable: the breeding grounds of seals that signal storms, the currents that carry news faster than horses, the standing stones on the island's high ground that are older than Lot and older than the Romans and have always been watched for what they show. An old woman tells you the stones moved once, when a king was made in the south.

You record all of it as best you can. Britain is larger than its courts.

failure.body:
The island people are polite and guarded — you are clearly from the south, clearly a knight, and some of what they know is not for southerners. You receive hospitality and the surface of things.

The surface is still honest, and honest things repay attention.

entries.body:
Orkney knows what it bred. The word has come north in pieces — Mordred's regency, Arthur's accusation, the war that broke what Arthur had made — and the island receives it with a silence that is not peace. These were Lot's sons. This is Lot's blood.

The hall is divided: some who follow Mordred's letters south, some who refuse to. The harbour-chain is down. You have come to a kingdom reckoning with what it made, and there is no welcome waiting — only the question of what you are here for.

responses.label: You may speak to those Orkney men who still hold to the old oaths and lend what strength you have.
You may seek the truth of what Orkney knew of Mordred's plans before the end came.

entries.body: There are men here who rode with Arthur in the early years — not the brothers, but older men, the generation of Lot's warband who survived Camlann's eve and came home to find their sons had made a different choice. They are not broken, but they are diminished, and they are looking for a reason to hold on to what they were.

resolutions.label: stand among the faithful remnant and give your honour as a warrant for their own

success.body:
You give them not a speech but an accounting — what you have seen, what was lost, what the Round Table was before it scattered. You make no promises you cannot keep. The old men listen with the attention of people who have heard enough promises and know how to tell the difference.

When you finish, one of them stands. Then another. It is not an army — but it is the thing that matters: the refusal to be entirely done.

failure.body:
The words are right, but the moment is wrong — or you have not yet earned the standing to be heard in this particular hall, at this particular hour. The old men hear you out and sit in their silence after.

They are not unmoved. They simply cannot move yet. You have planted something, even if you will not see it flower.

entries.body: There are women in the hall who knew Mordred as a boy — who watched him grow up in the shadow of his origin, knowing what he knew, patient in a way that made even Agravaine uncomfortable. Some of them knew what was being planned, in outline if not in detail. Some of them are ready to say so.

resolutions.label: piece together what was known and when from those willing to speak it aloud

success.body:
The picture that emerges is partial but coherent: Mordred planned for a long time, and some in Orkney knew the shape of his ambition without knowing the scale of the wound it would make. They were not all willing. They were not all unwilling.

You carry this knowledge carefully. It is not evidence that changes what happened — but it is truth, and truth of this kind is rare enough to matter.

failure.body:
The walls in Orkney are thick and the people are older in their caution than a single visit can shift. You hear what they choose to give you, which is not nothing, but it is not the full account.

You have a partial map. Partial maps are still maps.

entries.body:
The stones stand on the plain as they have stood since Merlin set them here — brought from Ireland, from a place that was already old when the Romans came, arranged in their dance on the Salisbury plain where the dead kings are buried. From the outside the ring looks like stone. From the inside it looks like something that is paying attention.

Merlin came with you as far as the plain's edge and then stopped, and said nothing, and let you go in alone. That is information.

responses.label: You may stand at the altar stone and open yourself to what the stones have to say.
You may walk the ring and learn its structure — the heelstone, the trilithons, the axis toward midsummer dawn.

entries.body: The altar stone is flat and grey and older than its name — no altar was ever used here, in the Christian sense, but the stone receives what is placed before it with something that functions like reverence. You place yourself before it. The wind inside the ring is different from the wind outside it: it moves without changing direction.

resolutions.label: remain still at the altar stone and receive whatever the stones will give

success.body:
What comes is not a vision exactly — not images or voices — but a quality of knowing: the names of three things that will matter before the year is out, given not as words but as certainties that settle into you like stones dropped into deep water.

Merlin, when you return to the plain's edge, looks at your face and nods once. 'You heard something,' he says. He does not ask what.

failure.body:
The stones receive you without giving back — you stand at the altar stone until your knees ache and the wind has chilled you through, and nothing comes that you can name. But when you leave the ring, the plain outside seems more legible than before: the light falls differently on it, and you can see where the burials are without being told.

Something was given. It was given quietly.

entries.body: The structure of the ring is also a text, if you know how to read it. The heelstone marks the midsummer sunrise — walk the axis from the heelstone to the altar stone and you are walking the path the sun takes at its height. The trilithons are arranged not at random but in a grammar: the tallest at the south-west, the axis resolving toward them at midwinter.

resolutions.label: read the structure of the ring and understand what it was built to say

success.body:
The stones speak of covenant — not with the dead kings buried beneath the plain, though they are here too, but with time itself: a promise that the sun's turning will be witnessed, that the realm will hold its frame against the dark. Britain is the promise as much as the land.

You understand, standing in the axis at the right angle, why Merlin built this here and why it matters that someone stand in it who knows what they are standing in.

failure.body:
The grammar is more complex than you can follow in a single visit — the stones speak in a language that rewards years of attention, not hours. You see the outline of the structure but not its meaning.

Merlin fills in what you missed, when you return to the plain's edge. He is generous with his knowledge, this once.

entries.body:
Nimue has taken Merlin — or Merlin chose to go with her, for there are both tellings — and Stonehenge stands without its maker for the first time since he set the stones in their positions. The stones have not changed. They do not require Merlin to be what they are. But the quality of their attention has shifted: subtler now, asking more of whoever enters the ring.

A monk is camped at the ring's edge, copying something into a small book. He looks up at you and then back at his work without greeting. The Quest has reached even here — the sacred has been redistributed, and Stonehenge is finding its new relationship with what has always made it sacred.

responses.label: You may enter the ring and seek what the stones say now that Merlin's voice no longer mediates them.
You may speak with the monk and learn what he has been recording about the stones since Merlin's departure.

entries.body: Without Merlin, the ring speaks more slowly and requires more. The altar stone is the same stone it has always been, but the receptiveness that was so present before is now conditional — it will give, but only to the right quality of attention. The Grail's light has touched even this place: there is a hunger in the stones for something they cannot name.

resolutions.label: bring the stones what they are hungry for — a pure and undefended attention

success.body:
What you offer is not a prayer exactly — it is a quality of presence, stripped of ambition, of petition, of the desire to receive something in return. The stones answer this with something that has never been given in words and cannot be: a direct encounter with what the ring was built to hold.

You emerge from the ring knowing something permanent. You could not say what, even to yourself. But it is there.

failure.body:
The quality shifts on you — what began as genuine attention acquires, somewhere in the standing, the form of a desire, and the stones feel the difference and say nothing. You leave the ring with the same measure you brought in.

The stones are patient. They will be here when you have learned to bring the right thing.

entries.body: The monk's name is Brother Caius, and he has been here for three months. He is not, strictly speaking, on a pilgrimage — he came because the Grail Quest sent his abbot to pray and his prior to ride out and his scriptorium to chaos, and someone had to go and record what was happening at the old sacred places in the new sacred time.

resolutions.label: assist Brother Caius in his recording and contribute what you know of the stones to his account

success.body:
What Caius has is incomplete — he knows the Christian frame but not the older one, and the stones require both. You add what you know of Merlin's intention, the axis of the ring, the relationship between the stones and the buried kings. Together the account becomes something no single observer could have made.

Caius looks at what you have made together and says, quietly, 'This is better than what I came here to write.'

failure.body:
What you add does not quite fit his frame — your knowledge of the older matters and his of the Christian are not, on this attempt, reconcilable. The account remains his, useful in its limitations.

But the conversation itself was worth the having, and Caius shares what his own research has turned up.

entries.body:
The armies have not reached Salisbury plain yet, but they will — the plain is where these things end, in this land and in every age. Stonehenge stands in the middle of it without concern. It stood before Arthur and it will stand after.

You come to the ring alone — there is no one else on the plain this late in the season, this late in the age. The stones make no distinction between the ages of men. The midsummer axis is the midsummer axis whether Britain holds or breaks.

responses.label: You may stand in the ring and ask the stones whether the realm can be preserved.
You may make an offering at the altar stone for those who will not survive what is coming.

entries.body: The question is put honestly: not 'will the realm survive' — the ring does not deal in hope or despair — but 'what of Britain persists, and where does it persist.' The stones are older than the question. They have held this ground through the end of things before.

resolutions.label: put the question to the stones and hold still for the answer

success.body:
The answer comes in the form of the stones themselves — the ring, the axis, the buried kings beneath the plain. Britain persists where the covenant is kept: in the witnessed turning of the sun, in the tended graves, in the stories told accurately. The realm is a practice, not a territory. It cannot be taken.

You carry this back down the plain with the clarity of something that was always true and is only now legible to you.

failure.body:
The stones give you the silence they give to everyone who asks the wrong version of the question. You asked about survival when you should have asked about persistence; you asked about the kingdom when you should have asked about the land.

The plain is still the plain. The stones are still here. That is a partial answer.

entries.body: The names gather as you walk toward the ring: Gareth, slain by Lancelot in a moment no one intended. Lamorak, taken in treachery. The nameless men on both sides of the war, whose deaths will not be recorded anywhere. You arrive at the altar stone with a weight of names that has nowhere to go but here.

resolutions.label: make the offering with the full weight of grief behind it, without diminishment

success.body:
The offering is received. Not acknowledged in any visible way — the stones do not move, the wind does not change — but received in the sense that the weight shifts: what you brought in as grief comes out as something that has been witnessed. The names have been given a place.

You stand in the ring a long time after, and the plain is quieter than it was.

failure.body:
The grief comes out wrong — too much pride in it, or too much anger, or the names get tangled with your own resentments — and the stones receive it neutrally, neither turning it away nor transforming it. You leave the ring carrying what you brought.

The names were spoken aloud. That is not nothing.

entries.body:
The Abbey of Kells stands behind its stone walls with the composure of a place that has decided what it is and has not been persuaded otherwise. Within, the sound of sung offices carries through the cloister at the canonical hours, and between them the scriptorium is busy: monks bending over vellum, grinding pigments, ruling lines with practised hands. The illuminated books being made here are unlike anything produced elsewhere in Britain or Ireland.

The guest-master receives you correctly and instals you in the guest quarters with a jug of water and a candle. The hospitality is genuine and complete, and the abbey expects nothing from you in return except the correct forms of reverence.

responses.label: You may ask permission to spend time in the scriptorium and learn from what is being made there.
You may seek an audience with the abbot and speak of what the abbey and the fellowship might offer each other.

entries.body: The brother who oversees the scriptorium — Brother Donal, a small man with ink-stained hands and very direct eyes — is willing to have you sit and observe, but observing is not what the scriptorium is for. It is for making. He places a brush in your hand with a look that says: let us see what you are.

resolutions.label: work alongside the monks and contribute something worthy to the illuminated work

success.body:
What you produce is not at the standard of Brother Donal's best work — but it is honest work, made with genuine attention, and the section you illuminate is accepted and incorporated. A small dragon in the margin of the Gospel of John, gold and blue, watches the text from outside its frame.

Donal looks at it for a long moment and says: 'You paid attention.' This is, in the scriptorium, the highest praise.

failure.body:
Your hand is too coarse for the work, or your attention too divided — the illumination goes wrong and Donal takes it gently from you and sets it aside without comment. You watch instead, and watching teaches its own things.

The afternoon in the scriptorium is not wasted. Nothing spent in this room is wasted.

entries.body: The abbot is an old man who has governed this abbey through plague and raid and two changes of the secular power, and he knows the difference between a visit that wants something and a visit that offers something. He sees which kind you are before you have said three sentences, and adjusts his manner accordingly.

resolutions.label: make the case for a bond between Camelot and Kells that honours both without diminishing either

success.body:
The abbot listens with the patience of a man who has all the time there is, and when you finish he asks two questions — not challenges, exactly, but the kind of question that sharpens an agreement into something that will hold. You answer both well.

The abbey will send a scholar to Camelot's court, and Camelot will provide escort for the next book-pilgrimage the abbey undertakes. Small things. Durable things.

failure.body:
The abbot is courteous and sees through to the gaps in your proposal before you can fill them. The thing you offer is not quite what the abbey needs, and the thing you ask is not quite what the fellowship can provide, and the middle ground is not found on this visit.

He offers you a book, regardless. A small one, a psalter, illuminated in the margin with a knight on a horse. A generous man.

entries.body:
The Abbey has been changed by the Quest in ways that are not immediately visible. The offices continue, the scriptorium continues, the hospitality continues — but in the chapter house there has been a conversation going on for six months that has not been resolved. The monks of Kells have their own understanding of the Grail, and it is not entirely the same as the understanding that sent the knights riding.

The abbot will receive you. He has been waiting, in the patient abbey way, for someone from the fellowship to come and engage with what his monks have been saying.

responses.label: You may enter the chapter house debate and hear what the monks hold about the Grail's nature.
You may ask to read the texts the monks have assembled bearing on the Grail's history and meaning.

entries.body: The chapter house debate has a shape: one side holds that the Grail is the chalice of the Last Supper, a physical relic, a thing that can be found and kept; the other side holds that the Grail is a quality of attention — that what the knights seek is not a cup but a capacity, and that seeking it as a physical thing is a category error. Brother Donal, who was so certain in the scriptorium, is less certain here.

resolutions.label: contribute to the debate with the knowledge a knight has that a monk cannot — the experience of the road

success.body:
What you bring is something the chapter house lacked: testimony. You have been on the roads of the Quest. You have seen what the hermits' tests look like from inside them, what a knight encounters when the forest closes around him. The chapter house receives this with the attention of people who have been arguing from principle and have met a practitioner.

The debate does not resolve — but it deepens. That is what chapter houses are for.

failure.body:
The monks are more practised at this kind of argument than you are — they know the technical terms, the authorities, the hierarchy of sources — and your testimony, though valuable, is not enough to move the debate in the direction you aimed at.

You listen to more of it than you contributed. Listening to very good thinking is its own education.

entries.body: The texts the monks have gathered — not only Christian sources, but older ones, translated from the Irish and the Welsh and something that may be older still — form a library within the library. The scriptorium has been putting off other commissions to copy them. There are things here about the Grail that none of the knights who rode have seen.

resolutions.label: read deeply in the assembled texts and find what they hold about the Grail that the Quest has not found

success.body:
What the texts hold is this: the Grail was in Ireland before it was in Britain, and before Ireland it was somewhere else, and it is not a thing that stays — it moves toward the most pressing need. It has been in Britain since Arthur drew Excalibur, and the texts suggest — obliquely, in the way old texts suggest things — that what called it here may now be releasing it.

You close the last text and sit for a while in the scriptorium's particular quiet.

failure.body:
The texts are in three languages and require a knowledge of the subtler Latin — not liturgical Latin but the classical variety — that you do not fully command. You get the outline but miss the crucial details that would make the outline cohere.

Brother Donal offers to give you a summary. It is generous and accurate, and it is not the same as reading.

entries.body:
The wars have not reached Kells yet. The sea between Britain and Ireland is still wide enough, and the raiders who come to these shores are a different kind of threat than Mordred's armies. But the abbey has heard the news from Britain — travellers come from the ports, monks return from pilgrimages — and the abbot has drawn his own conclusions.

He is copying, himself, in the scriptorium when you arrive. Not illuminating — preserving: making clean copies of the most irreplaceable texts, against the possibility that what reaches the Irish shore will not be merely news.

responses.label: You may assist in the preservation work and ensure the most vital texts survive whatever comes.
You may bring the abbey what you have witnessed on the roads of Britain and let them record it.

entries.body: The texts that must be preserved first are not the most beautiful — the illuminated books can be rebuilt, if imperfectly, from memory and skill. What must be preserved first are the unique ones: the translation of a Welsh text that exists in no other form, the record of a council that preceded Arthur's, a life of a saint who was never canonised but should have been.

resolutions.label: work through the night on the preservation copies, with all the care you can bring to bear

success.body:
By dawn you have made clean copies of four texts — not perfectly, but legibly, in a hand the monks can use as the basis for better copies later. The abbot comes to the scriptorium at Lauds and sees what has been done. He does not say thank you. He says: 'This will outlast both of us.' He means it as the highest form of thanks.

The copies are wrapped in oilskin and hidden in the abbey's deep cellar.

failure.body:
You work for several hours before your hands cramp and your eyes begin to blur and the quality of the copying falls below what the abbot will accept. He takes the work from you gently and thanks you for the hours given.

The hours given were real. And you have learned which texts are most at risk.

entries.body: The abbot wants what you have seen — not the versions that are already circulating, already being shaped into the form that will serve this faction or that, but the things you witnessed directly: the state of the roads, the condition of the people, the exact words that were said in the moments before the wars began. A primary account, before the accounts harden.

resolutions.label: give the abbot an honest account of what you saw, without softening what reflects badly on the fellowship

success.body:
You give him the account you would give if you knew it would outlast you and be read by people who would judge it against everything else that was recorded. You tell him what was done well and what was done badly, what the Round Table was at its height and what it failed to be. The abbot writes it down in his own precise hand.

It will go into the record. The record will outlast the age.

failure.body:
Somewhere in the telling you begin to shape it — to protect someone, or to explain away something that does not need explaining, or simply to make it more coherent than it was. The abbot notices. He writes down what you said, and the gap between what you said and what you saw is part of the record now too.

A partial truth is still more than nothing. He writes it down with care.

entries.body:
Merlin's cave is in the hillside above a valley where the wrong trees grow — yew and holly in the proportions of a deliberate planting, not wild growth — and the entrance is narrow enough that a man in armour must turn sideways to enter. Within, the rock is dry and the air holds the smell of old fire and something herbal that does not correspond to any plant in the valley.
