Merlin himself is inside. He was not here last week — the cave comes and goes in relation to him, as though it chooses to be findable when he is here and unfindable when he is not. He is sitting beside a small fire with a piece of worked iron in his hands, turning it end to end, and he looks up at you with the expression of a man who knew you were coming.

responses.label: You may ask Merlin directly what he foresees for the kingdom in the years ahead.
You may ask Merlin to teach you something of his craft — whatever he judges you ready to learn.

entries.body: Merlin does not answer the question immediately. He sets the piece of iron aside and feeds the fire and looks at it for a while in the manner of a man who has seen too many answers to be careless about giving them. Then he says: 'Ask me what you actually want to know.'

resolutions.label: ask Merlin what you truly want to know — not the general question but the personal one

success.body:
The true question, when it comes, surprises you a little — you did not know it was the question until you heard yourself asking it. Merlin hears it without surprise. He answers it directly and completely, without riddle, because the question has earned a direct answer.

What he tells you is true. It will remain true. It will be useful at the moment you most need it to be.

failure.body:
What comes out is still the general question — the kingdom, the future, something large enough to require a riddle in return. Merlin gives you the riddle with the patience of a teacher who knows the student will find the answer eventually, probably at an inconvenient moment.

The riddle is genuine. Riddles from Merlin always are.

entries.body: Merlin looks at you with a long, particular attention — the kind that takes inventory without asking permission — and then says: 'There is one thing I can teach you in a single afternoon that you will use for the rest of your life. Whether you should know it is a separate question, and I will answer it this way: you are here, and I am here, and that is sufficient answer.' He rises and moves to the back of the cave.

resolutions.label: learn what Merlin has to teach with the full attention such teaching deserves

success.body:
What he teaches you is not a spell in the theatrical sense — no words of power, no gestures that change the weather. It is a mode of attention: the ability to see the structure of a thing before you engage with it, to perceive the grain of the world and work with it rather than against it. Merlin's word for it is older than Latin and he does not translate it.

The cave feels smaller when you leave it — not because the cave has changed, but because you carry more of the world inside you now.

failure.body:
What Merlin teaches is real, but your capacity for it is currently less than the teaching requires. You receive the instruction and can perform a pale version of it — enough to be useful, not enough to be the thing itself. Merlin says: 'You will understand this better in ten years.' He does not say it as criticism.

The pale version is still more than you had.

entries.body:
Merlin is gone — taken by Nimue into the hill, or gone willingly, or gone in some third way that the different tellings do not agree on — and the cave is findable now without his presence, which it was not before. The yew and holly valley is the same. The entrance is the same. The smell of old fire and the herbal quality of the air are the same.

But the fire is not lit. The piece of worked iron is on the floor where he put it down for the last time. His books — small ones, thick ones, some that are more accurately described as bundles of bark with markings on them — are on the shelf he cut into the rock.

responses.label: You may read what Merlin left behind and see what counsel his books still hold.
You may bring something of your own to the cave and ask what remains of Merlin's presence to respond to it.

entries.body: The books are in several hands — Merlin's own, which is compressed and irregular, and others' which he has copied and annotated. There are marks in the margins that function as a commentary on the commentary: small corrections, disagreements with himself, questions he wrote down because he had not yet resolved them. The unresolved questions are the most interesting.

resolutions.label: read Merlin's books and find in them an answer to what the Quest most needs

success.body:
The answer is not in any single book but in the conversation between them — the margin notes arguing with the text, the cross-references between a Welsh fragment and a Latin one, the correction in a different ink that was made years later than the original. You hold the thread of it and follow it to its end.

What you find there is the shape of what the Grail is — not its nature, but its movement, where it goes and why. Merlin knew, and left the knowledge where someone patient enough could reach it.

failure.body:
The books are in languages and registers that resist you — not locked, but not easy, requiring a knowledge of the older traditions that you do not fully possess. You find the edges of what Merlin knew without finding the centre.

The edges are still Merlin's edges, which makes them more than most centres.

entries.body: The cave does not feel empty, exactly — Merlin was here long enough that the stone itself holds something of his attention. Whatever he brought to bear on the problems he worked through in this cave is still present in the walls, in the manner of an impression left by long pressure. The question is whether it is still responsive.

resolutions.label: bring your most pressing question to the cave and hold it here, waiting for an answer

success.body:
The cave responds — not as Merlin would have responded, which was always in words, always a little adversarial, always testing — but in the particular way that stone holds what was worked in it: an impression, a shape in the air that corresponds to the shape of the question and points, without speaking, toward the answer.

You stay until you understand what it is pointing at. When you leave, the piece of iron on the floor catches the light from the entrance, and it is the colour of the answer.

failure.body:
The cave receives your question and holds it — you can feel the question going into the stone — but gives nothing back that you can read. The impression is there but your capacity to receive it is insufficient, or the question is not precise enough, or both.

Sitting in Merlin's cave for an afternoon is not nothing. The yew trees in the valley below are the same yews he planted.

entries.body:
The valley is unchanged: the yew and holly, the narrow entrance, the smell of old fire. The cave is what it has been since Merlin's departure — accessible, quiet, holding what it holds. But you come to it now in the final age, and the questions you bring are different in weight than the questions of earlier visits.

The piece of iron is still on the floor. Someone has been here since you were last here, if you were ever here — the fire ring has been used, and the ash is not old. Merlin's absence has not made this cave merely empty. It has made it available in a way it was not when he occupied it.

responses.label: You may light the fire and sit in the cave and let what remains of Merlin's counsel come to you.
You may take from the cave what Merlin left for those who would come after him.

entries.body: The fire lights easily — the wood is dry, the stone around the fire ring channelled to draw the air through efficiently. Merlin arranged this cave for long occupation. The fire settles into a steady burn, and the cave takes on the quality it must have had when he was alive: a place for thinking hard thoughts in good conditions.

resolutions.label: sit by the fire in the cave and let the counsel that remains here speak to the age's need

success.body:
What comes is not Merlin — his voice, his manner, his deliberate obscurity — but something that was in him and is now in the cave: the long view. You see what is happening in Britain not as the catastrophe it appears from inside it, but as the turning of something that has turned before and will turn again. The kingdom breaks. The stories persist. The stories are the kingdom.

You carry the long view back down the valley with you. It does not make what is coming easier. It makes it comprehensible.

failure.body:
The fire burns and the cave is warm and the long view does not come — what comes instead is grief, which is not nothing, but is not what you needed. The cave holds your grief without transforming it.

You stay until the fire dies. The yew trees in the valley are still there when you come out.

entries.body: Among the books on the shelf is one that is different from the others — newer, and written in a hand that is Merlin's late hand, the one that wrote more slowly and with more precision than the earlier books, as though the writer knew the time for carelessness was past. It is addressed to no one and everyone. It is addressed to whoever comes after.

resolutions.label: take the book Merlin left for those who would need it, and commit to using it well

success.body:
The book is heavier than its size explains. What it contains is a map — not of Britain's geography but of its deep structure: the places where the old powers are strongest, the obligations that still hold between the land and those who tend it, the things that cannot be done and the things that must be. It is a guide to keeping what can be kept.

You accept it with both hands. You are the guarantee. You accept that.

failure.body:
The book is beyond you — not the language, which is legible enough, but the level of knowing it assumes in the reader. You can read the words but not understand what they require. It was written for someone further along than you are.

You leave it on the shelf, where it will wait for whoever is further along.

entries.body:
The Green Chapel is harder to find than a chapel should be — you are looking for a building and what you find is a mound: a hill grown over with green so thoroughly that the chapel within has been swallowed, its stones colonised by moss until the line between made and natural has been entirely erased. The entrance is an arch of living willow. Inside, the floor is grass.

The Green Knight is there. He is always there. He stands in the chapel's green-filtered light with his great axe across his shoulders and the green of him — skin, hair, the green of his armour — so normal in this place that it is the absence of green that would be startling. He looks at you without surprise.

responses.label: You may take up the Green Knight's bargain — a blow given now for a blow received in a year's time.
You may speak with the Green Knight about the nature of his covenant and what it is testing.

entries.body: The Green Knight lowers the axe from his shoulders and offers it to you, hilt-first, with the gravity of a man performing a ritual that has been performed before. The bargain is simple in form: you strike, and in one year you present yourself at this chapel to receive the return blow. The simplicity is deliberate. The difficulty is entirely inside it.

resolutions.label: take up the axe and make the blow and accept the full weight of the bargain

success.body:
The blow is struck cleanly. The Green Knight's head falls and rolls and he picks it up — this is the part that tests the nerve — and the head speaks from his hand, reminding you of the return. One year. The Green Chapel. The same axe.

You hand the axe back. You have made the bargain that Gawain made. What it requires is the next year of your life — the whole of it — lived with that appointment at its end.

failure.body:
You take the axe and your nerve fails in the moment of the blow — the strike goes wrong, either too hesitant or too fierce, and the Green Knight lifts his head from where it has landed and regards you with a patience that is worse than disapproval. The bargain is still on offer. He will wait.

He always waits. That is what the chapel is for.

entries.body: The Green Knight sets the axe aside — not down, exactly, but at rest, propped against the moss-covered altar — and his manner shifts from the ritual to something easier. He has been asked about the covenant before, and the question pleases him: it means the asker has been thinking about it rather than merely reacting to it.

resolutions.label: engage the Green Knight in a genuine account of what his test means and why it endures

success.body:
He explains — obliquely at first, then more directly as you follow the oblique parts — that the covenant is not about courage in the obvious sense. It is about the difference between a vow made when you understand the full cost and a vow made before you do. The year between the blow given and the blow received is where the vow is actually kept, or not kept. The axe is only an instrument.

You understand what Gawain faced, when you leave the chapel. You understand it differently than you did when you arrived.

failure.body:
The Green Knight is willing to explain, but the explanation requires a frame of mind you have not quite assembled — you hear the words and lose the thread, and at the end you have the surface of the thing but not the depth. He does not seem disappointed. He has had this conversation many times.

He gives you a piece of green ribbon from his belt. 'Come back,' he says, 'when you've thought about it more.'

entries.body:
The Green Chapel has not changed — it is not capable of changing in the ordinary sense, its green being the green of things that grow on a longer cycle than human seasons. The Green Knight receives you with the same composure he always has, and the axe is across his shoulders, but he does not immediately offer the bargain. He is watching you with a quality of attention that is different from the Age 1 welcome.

He says, after a moment: 'What is it that you are seeking?' He does not mean the Grail, exactly, or not only the Grail. He means the thing beneath the seeking.

responses.label: You may answer the Green Knight's question honestly and see what it leads to.
You may ask the Green Knight what he himself makes of the Grail Quest from his strange vantage.

entries.body: The honest answer to 'what are you seeking' is rarely the simple one. The Grail is part of it — the vision in the hall, the oath, the road — but beneath the Grail is something the knight sought before the Grail gave it a name. The Green Knight's question requires you to go down below the name.

resolutions.label: find and speak the true thing you are seeking — without the name the Quest has given it

success.body:
What comes out surprises you — not because it is shameful, but because it is simpler than the Quest's language allows for. The Green Knight listens without interrupting. When you finish, he nods once, which in this chapel is equivalent to a benediction.

'Then you may find it,' he says. 'Not today, perhaps. But it is findable.' He turns the axe in his hands and offers it again, which means: the bargain is available to you, strengthened.

failure.body:
What comes out is the Grail's name, again — the official version, the one the oath uses — and the Green Knight hears it with the patience of someone who has heard it often. He does not call it wrong. He simply says: 'Perhaps closer, next time.'

The chapel is still the chapel. The offer of the bargain still stands.

entries.body: The Green Knight's vantage on the Grail Quest is not a human vantage — he is older than the Quest and will persist after it, and his covenant is about honour in the raw form that precedes chivalry's codification of it. But he has been watching, from the chapel's green seclusion, and he has opinions.

resolutions.label: listen fully to what the Green Knight holds about the Quest and weigh it without defensiveness

success.body:
What he holds is this: the Quest is genuine, and the seeking is real, but the form it has taken — the knightly heroism, the individual quest, the single perfect knight — is a limitation that the Grail is trying to work around rather than endorse. Some of the knights who will fail will fail because they are too heroic, not because they are not heroic enough.

You carry this carefully. It is the kind of observation that changes how you see everything you have already seen.

failure.body:
The Green Knight's observations are sharp enough that they require a defencelessness you have not quite achieved — you hear the critique of the Quest's form and something in you resists it, which is understandable but which prevents you from receiving the full account.

You receive enough to be useful. His words will come back to you later, in a different context, and be useful then.

entries.body:
The Green Chapel is unaffected by the wars — it was never a political institution, never allied with any throne, and Mordred's armies would not know what to do with a hill that is also a chapel with a green man inside it. The Green Knight stands in his usual place with his axe and his patience and the green of him slightly brighter for the autumn light coming through the willow arch.

You have come alone. He knows this without being told — he always knows the condition you arrive in — and the bargain he offers first is the same as it always was. But the weight of the age is on everything, and in the Green Chapel it is visible: the moss is a little deeper, the willow a little more enveloping, as though the chapel is preparing to receive something.

responses.label: You may renew the covenant of the Green Chapel — take up the bargain in the final age and fulfil it.
You may ask the Green Knight to witness what you intend to do in the final days and hold your honour in trust.

entries.body: The bargain in the final age has the same form as it always did. The year is shorter than it was — everything is shorter now — but the structure is the same: blow given, blow received, the year between. What is different is what the year contains. You know what the year contains.

resolutions.label: take up the bargain in full knowledge of what the year will hold, and hold yourself to it

success.body:
The blow is struck and the bargain is made and the Green Knight's head speaks its reminder in the familiar way, but with a quality this time of something acknowledged — as though the chapel knows this bargain has been taken in extraordinary circumstances and honours that.

You will present yourself in a year. What happens in that year is the rest of the story. The bargain is what makes the rest worth telling.

failure.body:
The blow is made and the bargain is accepted, but something in the making is incomplete — the nerve holds but the will behind it has a crack in it, something less than total. The Green Knight feels it and his head, when it speaks, says: 'You must repair that, before the year is up.'

The bargain stands. The work it requires is inside you, not in any chapel.

entries.body: The Green Knight is a keeper of things — his chapel holds bargains, his memory holds the record of the honourable and the dishonourable, his axe has measured the courage of more knights than any tournament list. Asking him to hold something in trust is not a small request, and he receives it with the gravity it deserves.

resolutions.label: place your intention before the Green Knight and name what you will do and why

success.body:
You name it precisely: the thing you intend to do, the person it is for, the principle it serves. The Green Knight listens to all of it. When you finish, he lays the axe on the grass-floor of the chapel — the first time you have seen him set it down entirely — and places his green hand on your shoulder.

'I will know if you do it,' he says. 'And I will know if you do not.' It is a comfort, not a threat. You have given your word to the Green Knight, and his chapel will keep it.

failure.body:
The naming goes wrong — what comes out is the shape of the intention without the full commitment behind it, and the Green Knight hears the difference. He does not call it dishonest. He says: 'Come back when you know the full thing you mean to do.'

The chapel is still here. So are you. The full thing may yet become clear.

entries.body:
The lake is still at this hour, the surface holding the sky so perfectly that the distinction between above and below seems provisional — a question of convention rather than fact. The water is cold and clear to a depth that should not be clear: you can see, if the light is right and you look at the right angle, the suggestion of towers below the surface.

The Lady of the Lake received you before you could name what you came for — that is her quality, in this age, when her power is at its height. She is at the water's edge already, standing in a manner that makes the still water look active around her, and she has the expression of someone who knows why you are here even if you do not entirely know yourself.

responses.label: You may ask the Lady of the Lake to grant you passage into the Underwater Palace and show you what it holds.
You may ask the Lady to tell you the nature of what she keeps in trust below the surface.

entries.body: Passage into the Underwater Palace is not refused — the Lady of the Lake does not refuse in this age, she weighs — but it is not given simply. What she asks of those who wish to enter is not a sacrifice but a quality: the ability to pass between states without losing what you are. The water is the threshold. Thresholds require the right preparation.

resolutions.label: prepare yourself for the crossing and enter the water with the correct quality of attention

success.body:
The palace below is colder than cold and lit by a light that has no visible source — it is simply present, the way light is present in dreams. The Lady moves through it as though the water is not there, and the things she shows you are the things that are being kept here: promises given and received, gifts that cannot be undone, the objects of covenant that define what Britain is.

You return to the surface with the memory of it intact, which is not always the case with what happens below the water.

failure.body:
You enter the water and the palace is there — you can see it, can feel its presence — but the crossing completes only partially. You are in the water but not in the palace; you receive the impression of what it holds but not the direct encounter. The Lady returns you to the surface gently.

The impression alone is considerable. You know what is kept there, in outline.

entries.body: The Lady of the Lake keeps what was given to her — by covenant, by gift, by the necessity of preservation — and the accounting of it is not a secret, exactly, but it is not public knowledge either. She tells it to those who ask in the right manner: not with demand, not with desperation, but with the quality of a person who needs to understand in order to act rightly.

resolutions.label: ask about what the palace holds with the genuine purpose of acting rightly on the knowledge

success.body:
She tells you of things kept that you knew nothing of: a covenant between the first king and the water-realm that predates Arthur, a gift given by Merlin in his earliest years that he never spoke of, the promise the lake received at Arthur's birth that the lake will be asked to fulfil at his end. This last she tells you without elaboration.

You sit with this knowledge on the bank. The still water holds the sky.

failure.body:
The Lady hears your purpose and weighs it and finds it genuine but insufficient — the right motivation but the wrong level of readiness. She gives you what she can give someone at your current level of understanding, which is partial but real.

She says: 'Come back when you know what question this answer leads to.'

entries.body:
The lake has a quality it did not have before the Quest began — the Grail's light has been moving through the world, even under the water, and the Underwater Palace has been changed by it in a way the Lady of the Lake received with neither resistance nor approval but with the interest of a witness to a significant event. The towers below the surface have an additional quality of light that was not there in the Golden Age.

She receives you at the water's edge as before, but her manner has the quality of someone who has been having a very long conversation with something she cannot fully name. The Quest, for her, has been an experience of what she holds being illuminated from an unexpected direction.

responses.label: You may enter the palace and see how the Grail's light has touched what is kept below.
You may speak with the Lady about what it means that the Grail has reached even the water-realm.

entries.body: The Grail's light below the water is different from what it is above — refracted, deepened, present in things it does not illuminate directly. The palace in the Grail Age holds the same objects of covenant and promise and gift, but each of them is more visible than before: the light makes their meaning legible in a way that the ordinary light of the palace did not.

resolutions.label: move through the illuminated palace and read the meaning of what is kept there

success.body:
What you read in the objects — the covenant, the gifts, the promises the lake received — is the structure of what Britain was built on, seen from the side that is not usually visible. The Grail's light does not add to what is there; it reveals what was already there, which is more than sufficient.

You understand, ascending through the water, that Britain was always more than its kings and its knights. The lake knew this. You know it now.

failure.body:
The illuminated palace is too much to take in at once — the Grail's light makes everything equally legible, which is its own kind of illegibility, and you cannot organise what you are seeing into an account you can hold. You return to the surface with fragments.

Fragments from the Underwater Palace are still more than most people ever see.

entries.body: The Lady's account of the Grail's arrival in her domain is precise and unsentimental — she is not prone to sentiment, and the Grail did not ask her permission, and she has had time to think about what it means that the light came through the water as well as the air. Her conclusion is not the same as the hermits' conclusion or the monks' conclusion, and she is aware of this.

resolutions.label: engage the Lady's account fully and bring to bear what you know from the road above

success.body:
The conversation that develops is the conversation between above and below, between the road and the water, between the knightly experience of the Quest and the lake's experience of it. The Lady's account gains from your testimony; your understanding of the Quest gains from her vantage.

You spend a long time at the water's edge. The conversation produces something that neither of you could have made alone.

failure.body:
The frame that the Lady uses — the water's vantage, the deep-time of what the lake holds — does not fit easily with the frame of the quest above, and the conversation finds no bridge. You speak past each other with mutual respect and no resolution.

But the mutual respect is real, and she names it as such.

entries.body:
You come to the lake alone, and the Lady of the Lake is there, and the stillness of the water is the stillness of something that has been waiting without impatience. She knows what is coming — or she knows what she has been told will come, which is the same thing at this level of the world — and the Underwater Palace has been arranged accordingly.

Excalibur was given to Arthur at this lake. The sword will be returned to this lake. The Lady receives you with the manner of someone at the end of a very long custody — not grief, not relief, but the quality of attention that belongs to the completion of something undertaken with full seriousness.

responses.label: You may enter the palace one last time and witness what it has kept and what it will receive.
You may speak with the Lady about what comes after — what the lake will hold when the age has ended.

entries.body: The palace in the final age holds everything it has always held, and it holds one thing more: a space, deliberately kept empty, that is the shape of a sword. The empty space is as much a presence as the objects around it — the palace has been waiting for the sword's return since the sword was given, and the waiting has given the space a quality of its own.

resolutions.label: descend into the palace and witness what it holds and what it waits for

success.body:
The palace receives you as it has always received those the Lady brings below — with the cold and the sourceless light and the objects of covenant arranged in their positions. But the empty space shaped like a sword is the thing you cannot look away from. It is the shape of everything that was promised and given and will be returned.

You understand, ascending, that the return of Excalibur will not be a defeat. It will be the completion of a covenant made before the age began.

failure.body:
The palace is accessible — you make the crossing — but the empty space shaped like a sword is too much to look at directly. You spend your time in the palace looking at the other objects, the older things, the covenants that preceded Arthur, and receive what you can receive.

The older things are still older than your capacity to fully understand them. But they are there.

entries.body: The Lady of the Lake does not speak of after-time easily — the water-realm is a realm of holding, not of projection, and what comes after is not her domain. But she will speak of it now, in this age, to someone who asks rightly. The lake will receive what it is given. It will hold what it holds. It was there before the age and will be there after.

resolutions.label: ask the Lady what the lake will hold and keep when Britain's age has passed

success.body:
She speaks slowly, as though each sentence is a commitment: the lake will hold Excalibur until someone worthy comes for it — not in this age, perhaps not in the next, but the sword does not decay and the lake does not forget. The promises made at the age's beginning are not cancelled by the age's ending. They persist in the deep water, waiting.

You leave the lake with the quality of someone who has been given a reason to believe that the wreckage of the present is not the whole truth.

failure.body:
The Lady hears the question and considers it and says, finally: 'I do not speak of after-time to those who are still in time.' It is not a refusal exactly — it is the correct answer to a question that has been asked from the wrong position.

She touches the surface of the water once, leaving a circle that spreads to the bank. The circle itself is the answer, if you can read it.
